Description: Examine system logs (e.g., /var/log/syslog, /var/log/cron) for evidence of upgrade.sh running under root, including exit status and error messages. Verify if the script generated any unexpected files, services, or configuration changes. If anomalies are found, remediate immediately and consider quarantine of affected host pending full forensic imaging.

Task #9  
Title: Investigate chmod 777 operation on mysql.ibd in db-prod auditd logs  
Description: Parse the auditd record at 02:21:40 to identify which command and which PID performed the chmod. Confirm whether the mysql user legitimately had objects with 777 permissions at that time or if this indicates tampering. Correlate with MySQL logs for any anomalous CREATE/ALTER TABLE activity or data export events that may indicate sabotage or exfiltration.

Task #10  
Title: Correlate suspicious PNG execution on WIN-01 with CVE-2025-12345  
Description: Acquire the PNG file used to trigger hidden.elev.exe on WIN-01 and analyze it for embedded Alternate Data Streams (ADS) or malformed container data that could exploit CVE-2025-12345. Obtain hidden.elev.exe for static malware analysis and confirm it matches behaviors documented for that vulnerability (e.g., privilege escalation via image loading or driver execution). Submit both files to external threat‑intel services.

Task #11  
Title: Search for persistence mechanisms across affected hosts  
Description: On srv-prod-01, db1, WEB01, DC01, PROD01, db-prod, and WIN-01, examine scheduled tasks (cron jobs, schtasks), startup folders, and registry Run keys for recently created entries or unfamiliar executables. Use timestamp filtering based on observed event times. Flag any persistence vectors that were installed without corresponding approval or known change management documentation.

Task #12  
Title: Conduct a cross-host threat hunting campaign for lateral movement indicators  
Description: Aggregate logs from all affected hosts and scan for patterns such as repeated Kerberos ticket requests to DC01, SMB traffic to REFS shares, PowerShell remoting invocations (Invoke-Command, New-PSSession), or creation of service accounts. Map any detected lateral movement to the MITRE ATT&CK techniques ID (e.g., T1078 Valid Accounts, T1040 Network Sniffing). Prioritize findings based on suspicious timing and frequency.

Task #13  
Title: Collect volatile memory artifacts from compromised hosts  
Description: Use tools like DumpIt, WinDbg, or Linux equivalents to capture memory samples from each affected machine at the approximate time of the alerts. Analyze memory for hidden processes, injected code segments, keyloggers, or credential caches. Leverage Volatility or Rekall frameworks for deep forensic analysis, focusing on mapped DLLs, network connections, and loaded drivers.

Task #14  
Title: Cross-correlate file hashes with external threat intelligence feeds  
Description: Submit SHA256 hashes of replaced binaries (/usr/bin/sshd, /opt/mssql/bin/sqlservr), hidden.elev.exe, upgrade.sh, and any other observed suspicious files to VirusTotal, Hybrid Analysis, and MISP. Document any matches against known malware families, IOC lists, or campaign trajectories and feed findings back into internal SOC playbooks.

Task #15  
Title: Document evidence chain of custody and investigative findings  
Description: For every artifact collected (binary images, log excerpts, memory dumps), record the collection time, tool or method used, person performing the action, and storage location. Include fingerprints (hashes) of each artifact to establish integrity. Summarize key findings, potential impact, and recommended containment or eradication steps to support future legal or compliance reviews.

Task #16  
Title: Preserve forensic integrity of affected hosts  
Description: Immediately initiate forensic imaging of all system drives on srv-prod-01, db1, WEB01, DC01, PROD01, db-prod, and WIN-01 to create bit‑for‑bit copies. Verify images with SHA256; store copies in a write‑once, read‑many (WORM) secure storage environment. Ensure that all evidence is handled according to the organization’s evidence‑handling policy before further analysis or remediation.

case_title: Suspicious privilege escalation attempt via local admin group modification on corporate workstation
case_description: Event ID 4624: user “jdoe” logged on locally to WKS01 at 10:15:32. Event ID 4670: “jdoe” attempted to grant Administrators group rights at 10:15:45. Process Monitor: cmd.exe launched from C:\Users\jdoe\AppData\Local\Temp with arguments "/c net localgroup administrators /add jdoe" at 10:15:46, executed with an elevated System token. Hidden kernel driver elevate.sys (MD5 9f8b4c2e…) loaded into svchost.exe at 10:16:02. Registry: HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Image File Execution Options\svchost.exe value “Debugger” set to “C:\ProgramData\SCTemp\escalate.exe.” New scheduled task MaliciousTask created under HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\ScheduledTasks, set to run as System at startup. Network capture shows outbound TLS connection to 198.51.100.50:443 from svcsvchost.exe at 10:17:10, handshake not matching corporate CA. Volatility analysis of memory dump at 10:18:23 reveals an embedded Administrator token in svcsvchost.exe. File artifacts: elevate.exe found in C:\Windows\Temp and C:\ProgramData\SCTemp, SHA256 d2f5a…. Process svchost.exe (PID 1524) elevated privileges observed at 10:18:00. No documented policy permits such group membership changes or driver loads. Observations suggest a PrivEsc attempt; success status or origin of privileges remains undetermined.
model_a_tasks:
Task #1  
Title: Confirm Administrators Group Membership Change  
Description: Retrieve the current local Administrators group membership on WKS01 (using `net localgroup administrators` or `Get-LocalGroupMember` in PowerShell). Verify whether user “jdoe” was added successfully during the 10:15:45 instance. Log any discrepancy between intended and actual group members.

Task #2  
Title: Validate Successful Privilege Escalation  
Description: Assess whether svchost.exe or elevate.sys retained System privileges after 10:18:00. Run a signed token inspection (`whoami /priv` inside the process context) or use Sysinternals Process Explorer to confirm privilege level. Document any evidence that the escalation was granted.

Task #3  
Title: Correlate Event 4670 with Group Membership Result  
Description: Cross‑reference the Windows Security log event (4670) for local group modification with the membership verification from Task #1. Confirm that the change was logged as successful rather than failed or denied.

Task #4  
Title: Examine Registry Alterations for Persistence  
Description: Inspect any “Image File Execution Options” entries for svchost.exe, specifically the “Debugger” value pointing to C:\ProgramData\SCTemp\escalate.exe. Verify the presence and integrity (MD5, SHA256) of the indicated “escalate.exe” file and determine if this entry was established by an elevated process.

Task #5  
Title: Audit Newly Created Scheduled Task (MaliciousTask)  
Description: Enumerate all scheduled tasks from HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\ScheduledTasks and evaluate the properties of MaliciousTask. Confirm the trigger (startup), user context (System), and path. Use `schtasks /query /TN "MaliciousTask" /v /fo LIST` to gather full details and check for signed binaries.

Task #6  
Title: Analyze Network Traffic to External IP  
Description: Decode the TLS session logged at 10:17:10 to 198.51.100.50:443. Verify certificate chain, handshake parameters, and payload. Compare the server’s certificate with corporate CA store; identify possible man‑in‑the‑middle or outbound C2 activity.

Task #7  
Title: Static and Dynamic Analysis of elevate.sys  
Description: Conduct a file integrity check against known good drivers. Use malware sandboxes and static analysis tools (e.g., IDA, PEiD) to identify obfuscation, code injection points, and potential persistence mechanisms. Flag any load-time detours or hidden behaviors.

Task #8  
Title: Verify File Presence and Integrity of elevate.exe  
Description: Search for all instances of elevate.exe in C:\Windows\Temp, C:\ProgramData\SCTemp, and other non‑standard locations. Compare SHA256 values across copies. Use antivirus and sandbox feeds to confirm if the binary is malicious or known to multiple hosts within the network.

Task #9  
Title: Review All Related Security Logs for Lateral Activity  
Description: Inspect Security (4624/4625), System, and Application logs for signs of additional logons, failed authentication attempts, or other Tactics & Techniques from the ATT&CK framework (e.g., remote admin tools usage, T1135). Look for patterns that might indicate the attacker has moved to other hosts.

Task #10  
Title: Conduct Memory Forensics for Embedded Tokens  
Description: Analyze the memory dump from 10:18:23 to enumerate all active tokens, especially the embedded Administrator token found in svchost.exe. Confirm that the token matches the System user’s SID and that there are no duplicate or spoofed tokens. Correlate with process lists to identify injection points.

Task #11  
Title: Isolate the Compromised Workstation  
Description: Quarantine WKS01 from the corporate network to prevent further spread or data exfiltration. Schedule a tree‑complete backup before isolation if it makes sense for forensic analysis; avoid touching OS without proper forensic procedures.

Task #12  
Title: Recommend Immediate Remediation and Policy Enforcement  
Description: Create a playbook that includes removal of elevate.sys, deletion or restoration of the “Debugger” registry entry, revocation of jdoe’s Administrators membership, and re‑application of group policy. Additionally, propose updating firewall rules to block outbound TLS to 198.51.100.50 and enhancing detection of unauthorized kernel driver loads.
model_b_tasks:
**Task #1**  
Title: Reconstruct a Detailed Timeline of Events  
Description: Collate and align timestamps from the Security event logs (Event ID 4624, 4670, 4728/4732), ProcMon entries, Volatility output, registry modifications, and the network capture. Use the SOAR timeline engine to string together the exact sequence from jdoe’s initial logon at 10:15:32 through the scheduled task execution at 10:18:00, highlighting gaps or overlaps that may indicate additional hidden activity.

---

**Task #2**  
Title: Verify whether jdoe was Added to the Administrators Group  
Description: Search for Event ID 4732 “Member added to local group” or 4728 “Member added to global group” with Target = Administrators and Owner = jdoe. Cross‑check the current local group membership on WKS01 with `net localgroup administrators` to confirm if the escalation succeeded. Flag success status for further containment steps.

---

**Task #3**  
Title: Investigate the Source of the System Token Granted to cmd.exe  
Description: Examine parent-child relationships in ProcMon and Windows Sysinternals Process Explorer to identify which process injected the System token into cmd.exe at 10:15:46. Look for evidence of token stealing (e.g., via mimikatz), abuse of SWbemServices GetObject permission, or delegation to a service that belongs to jdoe. If a legitimate service is implicated, assess its configuration and potential for exploitation.

---

**Task #4**  
Title: Static Analysis of elevate.sys Kernel Driver  
Description: Use IDA Pro or Ghidra to reverse engineer elevate.sys (MD5 9f8b4c2e…) launched into svchost.exe. Identify the kernel routines intercepted, hidden file mechanisms, and any privilege escalation hooks. Feed discovered YARA signatures into the SOAR platform and cross‑reference with Malware Information Sharing Platform and Threat Intelligence feeds for known malicious drivers.

---

**Task #5**  
Title: Expand the Registry Persistence Scan Beyond IFE Entry  
Description: Conduct a comprehensive registry search for suspicious Image File Execution Options, Run/RunOnce keys, Service configurations (`HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services`), and auto-start registry values (`HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run`, `HKLM\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\RunOnce`). Use the SOAR registry extractor to identify any other hidden “Debugger” pointers or undocumented driver loads that may facilitate persistence.

---

**Task #6**  
Title: Inspect, Document, and Safely Remove MaliciousTask Scheduled Task  
Description: Export the scheduled task configuration (`schtasks /Query /TN MaliciousTask /XML > MaliciousTask.xml`) to a SOAR artifact. Verify that it executes as System at startup, points to `escalate.exe`, and uses a meaningless name to evade policy filters. De-register the task (`schtasks /Delete /TN MaliciousTask /F`) while monitoring for automatic recreation, and flag any persistence attempts.

---

**Task #7**  
Title: Deep Analysis of the Outbound TLS Connection to 198.51.100.50  
Description: Parse the TLS handshake from the packet capture to identify server certificate details, cipher suite usage, and validate if the certificate chain is trusted by the corporate CA store. Perform regex matching on application data for signs of command-and-control traffic (e.g., “GET /cmd?” or “POST /data”). Use the SOAR network analyzer to ticket the IP as threat intelligence and add to the hygiene list.

---

**Task #8**  
Title: Verify the Sequence and Authenticity of Kernel Driver Loads  
Description: Scrutinize System Event Log IDs 7045 (Driver installed) and 7040/7047 (Driver started/stopped) around 10:16:02 to confirm legitimate loading of elevate.sys. Correlate with ProcMon to see if the loader added a hidden service entry or used the `ImageFileExecutionOptions` trick. Confirm whether the driver auto‑loads at every boot by checking `HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\elevate` configuration.

---

**Task #9**  
Title: Trace the Process Injection Chain Leading to Elevated svchost.exe  
Description: Use Volatility’s `malfind` and `pids` plugins to identify injected code segments within svchost.exe and any child processes started by cmd.exe. Look for known injection techniques (e.g., process hollowing, manual mapping) and verify that the elevated token originates from a compromised child process. Tag the injection chain for inclusion in the SOAR case file.

---

**Task #10**  
Title: Capture a Full Endpoint Forensic Image of WKS01  
Description: Deploy a live response agent to acquire a forensic disk image of WKS01 under chain-of-custody protocols. Preserve copies of all suspicious binaries (`elevate.sys`, `elevate.exe`, `escalate.exe`), Process Monitor logs, and registry hives (`SAM`, `SYSTEM`). Archive these artifacts in a locked SOAR evidence silo for offline analysis.

---

**Task #11**  
Title: Search for Credential Dumping Artifacts and Tools  
Description: Scan the memory dump and disk for known credential dumping tools (Mimikatz, mimikage, credump) and signatures of rundll32/regsvr32 abuse. Search for text files containing credential strings, Base64‑encoded passwords, or Windows LSASS memory copies. Cross‑reference findings with the SOAR IOC database and flag any exfiltration patterns regarding credential data.

---

**Task #12**  
Title: Review jdoe Account Abuse Patterns  
Description: Pull authentication logs (system, domain controllers) for jdoe’s account around the incident window. Examine logon times, source IP addresses, failure rates, and any anomalous helper service usage. Verify jdoe’s normal group memberships and privileges; assess whether jdoe is an anomalous object in the security baseline that could have been targeted or compromised.

---

**Task #13**  
Title: Threat Intelligence Lookup for Suspect Binaries and External IP  
Description: Submit the hashes of elevate.sys, elevate.exe, and esCatate.exe to commercial and open-source IOC feeds. Lookup 198.51.100.50 in threat intelligence platforms to determine reputational scores, historical association with malicious campaigns, and existing blocklists. Feed positive findings back into the SOAR playbook for automated blocking and alert correlation.

---

**Task #14**  
Title: Dynamic Sandbox Analysis of suspect binaries  
Description: Execute elevate.exe, elevate.sys (via a controlled driver load), and esCatate.exe in a Windows sandbox environment (Cuckoo, FireEye, etc.). Capture process creation, registry changes, file system writes, and network activity in real time. Compare sandbox output against the live environment to validate evasion tactics and to capture malicious behavior not evident in static analysis.

---

**Task #15**  
Title: Cleanup, Remediation, and Harden Policies Post‑Incident  
Description: Delete all copies of elevate.sys, elevate.exe, and esCatate.exe from the endpoint and network shares. Unregister MaliciousTask and any hidden service entries. Reset jdoe’s password, enforce MFA, and revoke any delegated permissions that allowed admin‑level changes. Tighten group policy to deny Local Group Membership modifications from standard users and blacklist loading of kernel drivers via IFE. Update SOAR playbooks to include detection of hidden “Debugger” values, disguised scheduled tasks, and kernel‑mode persistence. Document findings, TTP mapping (ATT&CK IDs T1548, T1053, etc.), and lessons learned in the incident report.

case_title: Internal network enumeration activity identified through abnormal scanning and discovery traffic
case_description: Network flow logs show outbound traffic from 10.10.1.10 to hosts 10.10.2.15, 10.10.3.30, and 10.10.4.45 on ports 21, 22, 23, 139, 445, 3389, 389, 5985, 1521, 1433, and 3306 between 13:05 and 13:20 UTC; corresponding NetFlow records indicate SYN packets followed by ACK/FIN storm on 445 and 389; SYSLOG entries reveal Windows Event ID 4624 at 13:05:30 for user admin@corp.local logging into workstation 10.10.1.10 (Logon Type 7); PowerShell execution logs indicate cmdlet Get-WMIObject executed from 10.10.1.10 with arguments enumerating Win32_NetworkAdapterConfiguration objects; Netcat output from 10.10.1.10 shows banner responses on port 80 of internal web servers containing “Apache/2.4.49 (Linux)”, and on port 443 returning “403 Forbidden”; DNS query logs from internal recursive server 10.10.0.5 contain frequent queries for subdomains “mail”, “ftp”, “vpn”, “rpc”, “asd”, and “krb5” with NXDOMAIN responses; SNMP trap logs show community strings “public” and “private” attempts against 10.10.1.50 and 10.10.1.51; Nmap scan report collected on 10.10.2.15 shows open ports 22, 80, 139, 445, 3306 with service banners; Winlogon audit logs on servers 10.10.2.15 and 10.10.3.30 show successful SMB “NTLM authentication” events for user “admin”; Apache access logs on web server 10.10.4.45 display lookups for /admin, /cgi-bin, /phpmyadmin, and /backups; Cisco ASA logs record 225 connection attempts from 10.10.1.10 to internal hosts on ports 3389, 1433, 1521, 3306 originating from a single source IP 10.10.1.10; Email server logs show outbound message from admin@corp.local to external address 192.0.2.10 “Re: System Information Request” with attachment “srv-info.txt” listing 5 internal host names and IPs; File integrity monitoring alerts on 10.10.1.10 detected creation of temp script “enum.ps1” with 12KB size containing code for NetUserGetInfo enumeration; Host-based IDS triggered alert ID 4527 for “Active Directory discovery” activity on workstation 10.10.1.10; External-facing firewall logs show event ID 3024 for exfiltration activity from internal IP 10.10.1.10 to external IP 198.51.100.10 over HTTPS with content-type “application/octet-stream” containing base64-encoded asset list.
model_a_tasks:
Task #1  
Title: Verify Legitimacy of the admin@corp.local Login  
Description: Review domain controller logs for the 13:05:30 event and confirm that the login time, source workstation (10.10.1.10), and user rights match known patterns. Cross‑check the user’s recent activity, password history, and any concurrent logins that could indicate credential compromise or reuse.

Task #2  
Title: Forensic Analysis of 10.10.1.10 Workstation  
Description: Conduct a memory capture and disk imaging of 10.10.1.10. Examine running processes, scheduled tasks, services, and startup items for malicious or unauthorized components. Verify the presence of any persistence mechanisms such as WMI event subscriptions or custom PowerShell scripts.

Task #3  
Title: Examine enum.ps1 Source Code  
Description: Retrieve the temp script (10.10.1.10\Temp\enum.ps1), review its contents, and compare it against known malicious scripts or legitimate network discovery utilities. Identify any obfuscated or encoded payloads, external downloads, or credential‑dumping commands.

Task #4  
Title: Correlate PowerShell and Netcat Activity  
Description: Map timestamped PowerShell commands (e.g., Get‑WMIObject calls) and Netcat banner traffic to the observed NetFlow SYN/ACK/FIN patterns. Confirm whether the enumeration originated from the workstation or if additional hosts were actors in the scanning process.

Task #5  
Title: Validate Exfiltration to 198.51.100.10  
Description: Capture the HTTPS flow from 10.10.1.10 to 198.51.100.10, decode the application/octet-stream payload, and parse the base64‑encoded asset list. Assess whether the exfil data includes sensitive information and evaluate whether the endpoint used a legitimate outbound channel.

Task #6  
Title: Investigate SMB “NTLM Authentication” Events  
Description: Examine the Winlogon audit logs for 10.10.2.15 and 10.10.3.30 to verify that admin users authenticated over SMB. Check for additional SMB commands (e.g., shared resource enumeration, file creation, or lateral movement indicators) and correlate with the source IP (10.10.1.10).

Task #7  
Title: Review Apache Access Logs for Exploit Attempts  
Description: Search for failed and successful HTTP requests to /admin, /cgi-bin, /phpmyadmin, and /backups on 10.10.4.45. Identify any suspicious POST payloads, session hijacking attempts, or enumeration scripts that might indicate web application exploitation.

Task #8  
Title: Analyze Cisco ASA 225 Connection Attempts  
Description: Inspect the ASA logs for event ID 225, focusing on attempts from 10.10.1.10 to internal hosts over high‑risk ports. Determine if the traffic was blocked, allowed, or throttled, and verify whether it aligns with legitimate remote administration or is symptomatic of lateral movement.

Task #9  
Title: Assess SNMP Probe Attempts  
Description: Review SNMP trap logs for community string usage (“public” and “private”) on 10.10.1.50 and 10.10.1.51. Confirm whether the probes succeeded, and cross‑reference the SNMP traffic with NetFlow to ensure no configuration data was extracted or altered.

Task #10  
Title: Investigate DNS NXDOMAIN Query Patterns  
Description: Correlate the high volume of NXDOMAIN responses for subdomains (mail, ftp, vpn, rpc, asd, krb5) from 10.10.0.5 to possible enumeration of internal services. Use DNS logs to map queries to initiating hosts and verify whether these originated from 10.10.1.10 or other compromised machines.

Task #11  
Title: Evaluate Host‑Based IDS Alert 4527 for Ad‑Domain Discovery  
Description: Drill into the Triggered ID 4527 logs to determine the exact queries and responses related to Active Directory discovery. Cross‑reference with domain controller logs for any anomalous LDAP or Kerberos requests originating from 10.10.1.10.

Task #12  
Title: Review Email Attachment “srv-info.txt”  
Description: Open and parse the outbound email that contained the “srv-info.txt” attachment. Verify the list of internal hostnames and IPs against the network inventory; confirm if the attachment was transmitted by a legitimate user or was part of an exfiltration vector.

Task #13  
Title: Mitigation & Containment Plan for 10.10.1.10  
Description: Based on findings, isolate the workstation from the network, reset the admin@corp.local password, revoke any additional privileges, and apply security patches. Update network ACLs to block known malicious IPs and monitor for repeat scanning activity.  

Task #14  
Title: Threat Intelligence Check on External IPs  
Description: Perform a WHOIS and reverse DNS lookup on 198.51.100.10 and 192.0.2.10. Compare results against known malicious IP blacklists, evaluate whether these endpoints are command‑and‑control servers or exfiltration hosts, and adjust firewall policies accordingly.  

Task #15  
Title: Conduct Comprehensive Network Triage  
Description: Combine all logs (NetFlow, Syslog, PE, IDS, DNS, SMTP) into a single timeline to detect lateral movement patterns, establish a chain of compromise, and identify the earliest indicator of compromise. Use this timeline to prioritize remediation of affected systems.
model_b_tasks:
Task #1
Title: Verify activity on suspected workstation 10.10.1.10
Description: Initiate collection of system logs from 10.10.1.10 covering 13:00-13:30 UTC, focusing on Windows Event Logs (System, Security), PowerShell logs, and BIOS/DRTM logs. Enumerate running processes, startup items, scheduled tasks, and services active during this window. Flag any unknown binaries, scripts, or network connections for deeper analysis.

Task #2
Title: Review Windows authentication events for suspicious admin logons
Description: Extract Security Event ID 4624 logs for successful logons to 10.10.1.10 around 13:05:30 UTC, filtering for Logon Type 7 (cleared) and user admin@corp.local. Verify source IP addresses, workstation names, and privilege levels associated with these events. Flag any repeated or anomalous logons that deviate from baseline authentication patterns.

Task #3
Title: Analyze PowerShell execution logs for enumeration commands
Description: Enable and capture PowerShell Transcript or Script Block Logging on 10.10.1.10 to record all PowerShell cmdlets executed between 13:00-13:30 UTC. Parse logs for evidence of WMI queries (Get-WMIObject), AD enumeration cmdlets (Get-ADComputer, Get-ADUser), or network configuration queries (Get-NetIPAddress, Get-NetAdapter). Assess usage of encoded commands or obfuscated scripts that may conceal malicious intent.

Task #4
Title: Examine file integrity monitoring alerts for temp script creation
Description: Investigate FIM alerts indicating creation of enum.ps1 on 10.10.1.10 by reviewing file creation timestamps, owner, parent directory, and file attributes. Open and analyze script content for functionality such as user enumeration, network scans, and persistence attempts. Cross-check registry Run keys, scheduled tasks, and startup folders for entries referencing enum.ps1 or any related binaries.

Task #5
Title: Correlate outbound network flows for scanning activity
Description: Retrieve NetFlow records for traffic from 10.10.1.10 to internal hosts on ports 21, 22, 23, 139, 445, 3389, 389, 5985, 1521, 1433, 3306 between 13:05-13:20 UTC. Look for patterns of SYN packets followed by ACK/FIN storms, especially on 445 and 389. Compare observed traffic with Nmap scan results on target hosts to confirm scanning behavior.

Task #6
Title: Investigate DNS query patterns for enumeration
Description: Analyze recursive DNS logs at 10.10.0.5 for high‑frequency queries to obscure subdomains (mail, ftp, vpn, rpc, asd, krb5) yielding NXDOMAIN responses. Correlate query volume and timing with outbound scanning activity. Determine if these queries target internal services or domain infrastructure, and assess whether they reflect legitimate operations or reconnaissance attempts.

Task #7
Title: Assess SNMP trap logs for unauthorized community string attempts
Description: Review SNMP trap records indicating attempts to use community strings “public” and “private” against 10.10.1.50 and 10.10.1.51. Verify authentication results, SNMP version used (v1/v2c), and whether attempts succeeded or failed. Based on findings, audit SNMP configuration across the network for default or weak community strings and adjust security settings accordingly.

Task #8
Title: Correlate email server logs for exfiltration attachment
Description: Retrieve SMTP logs for outgoing email “Re: System Information Request” sent from admin@corp.local to 192.0.2.10 between 13:15-13:30 UTC. Inspect message headers for abnormalities, verify attachment srv-info.txt content for internal host enumeration, and confirm whether email was manually authored or injected by malware. Check for any bounce‑back or delivery failure notifications that may hint at abuse detection.

Task #9
Title: Deep packet inspection of outbound exfiltration traffic
Description: Capture network packets from 10.10.1.10 to external IP 198.51.100.10 (HTTPS) around 13:25 UTC. Inspect TLS handshake details, certificate chain, hostname, and cipher suites for evidence of legitimate or malicious C2 endpoint. Decode HTTP payload to reveal base64‑encoded asset lists, assess encoding transformations, and map exfiltration size and frequency to enumeration activity.

Task #10
Title: Review web server access logs for admin interface probing
Description: Analyze Apache access logs on 10.10.4.45 for request paths /admin, /cgi‑bin, /phpmyadmin, and /backups during 13:00-13:30 UTC. Record source IPs, timestamps, response codes, user agents, and referers. Identify any successful access attempts, error patterns, or repeated probing indicative of credential guessing or exploitation of known vulnerabilities.

Task #11
Title: Analyze SMB authentication logs for lateral movement
Description: Scrutinize Winlogon audit logs on servers 10.10.2.15 and 10.10.3.30 for SMB “NTLM authentication” events involving user admin. Note source IPs, timestamps, authentication success/failure, and compare with NetFlow records for SMB traffic. Flag any anomalous authentication patterns that could signal lateral movement facilitated by compromised credentials.

Task #12
Title: Investigate Active Directory discovery activity
Description: Query domain controller event logs for enumeration of domain objects (users, computers, groups) around 13:05 UTC. Examine PowerShell and LDAP query logs for Get‑AD* calls, NetDiag operations, and other discovery mechanisms. Determine scope of enumeration—whether it targeted all domain resources or specific OUs—and assess potential gains for adversary.

Task #13
Title: Conduct threat hunting for known attacker tool signatures
Description: Deploy SOAR platform hunts for signatures associated with reconnaissance tools such as Mimikatz, Empire, SharpHound, or PowerUp.ps1 across logs from 10.10.1.10. Search extracted command lines, script contents, and network patterns for matchable hash or string artifacts. Include IOC feeds for new malware variants detected during enumeration. Flag any matches for containment and eradication actions.

Task #14
Title: Validate presence of enumeration utilities on Windows host
Description: Perform file system search on 10.10.1.10 for known tool binaries (nmap.exe, netcat.exe, enum4linux, PowerUp.ps1, SharpHound.exe). Inspect Windows Services, Startup Folder, Registry Run keys, and scheduled tasks for entries referencing suspicious tools. Document any unauthorized installations and recommend removal or lock‑down based on IT asset inventory.

Task #15
Title: Identify potential lateral movement via credential reuse
Description: Analyze PowerShell logs for evidence of credential dumping (e.g., Mimikatz SharpDump) and pass‑the‑hash techniques. Cross‑reference discovered credentials with SMB authentication logs to see if hashes were used to access other internal hosts. If reuse detected, immediately invalidate related credentials and assess impact on critical assets.

Task #16
Title: Initiate containment of compromised workstation
Description: Place 10.10.1.10 into network quarantine using segmentation policy or manually disable network interfaces. Ensure that outbound traffic to potential C2 or exfiltration endpoints is blocked. Log containment actions and confirm isolation by attempting to initiate external connections from the host.

Task #17
Title: Plan eradication and remediation steps for onboard malware
Description: Create a forensic image of 10.10.1.10 for evidence preservation. Compile list of malicious files, registry entries, scheduled tasks, and startup items discovered. Outline step‑by‑step removal procedures: delete scripts and binaries, unload malicious services, clear registry Run keys, and reset any altered system configurations.

Task #18
Title: Reset credentials of compromised accounts
Description: Force password reset for admin@corp.local and any other privileged accounts potentially compromised. Enforce MFA for all privileged‑level access and restrict login workstations to authorized devices. Review group membership changes to ensure no over‑privileged users were added inadvertently.

Task #19
Title: Conduct patch management audit of involved hosts
Description: Inventory OS and application versions on 10.10.1.10, 10.10.2.15, 10.10.3.30, 10.10.4.45. Cross‑check with vendor patch status for known vulnerabilities (SMB v1, RDP insecure configs, outdated DB drivers). Prioritize patch deployment for services discovered during enumeration as potential exploitation vectors.

Task #20
Title: Update IOC repository with newly discovered indicators
Description: Store internal host IPs, domain names, discovered service banners, PowerShell cmdlets, base64 payload patterns, and enumeration scripts as IOCs. Tag these with MITRE ATT&CK Tactic/Technique IDs: Discovery (T1087, T1046), Exfiltration (T1041), Credentials In Use (T1078), Lateral Movement (T1021). Push updates to SOAR IOC feeds for automated detection.

Task #21

case_title: Suspicious TCP SYN port scan detected on internal network hosts from external IP 203.0.113.45
case_description:
- Firewall log at 2024‑03‑10T00:12:43Z: DENY 203.0.113.45 → 192.168.1.10 port 80/TCP; DENY 203.0.113.45 → 192.168.1.11 port 443/TCP.  
- Snort IDS alert (ID 101) “TCP SYN Scan” triggered for source 203.0.113.45 targeting 192.168.1.12, dest port 22.  
- NetFlow export: 2,000 TCP SYN packets from 203.0.113.45 to 192.168.1.10‑192.168.1.20 between 2024‑03‑10T00:12:00Z and 2024‑03‑10T00:15:00Z.  
- Syslog entries: “ICMP echo request from 203.0.113.45 to 192.168.1.15” at 2024‑03‑10T00:13:05Z, repeatedly 30 times.  
- Windows Event ID 5156 logs report attempted connections from 203.0.113.45 on ports 21, 23, 25, 53, 139, 445 to internal shares.  
- SMTP gateway log shows a single connection attempt from 203.0.113.45 on port 25 that returned a TCP reset.  
- Nmap detection string “Nmap scan: 192.168.1.0/24 from 203.0.113.45” logged in SNORT rule 5001 event.  
- SIEM correlation rule “Possible port scan activity” flagged 3 events from 203.0.113.45 within a 5‑minute window.

case_title: Suspicious Exploitation Attempt via Remote Desktop Services on Windows Server 2019
case_description: Process chain: session ID 1024, user: jsmith, launched at 2024-03-05 02:12:45: cmd.exe → powershell.exe → rundll32.exe (attacker.dll) ; Registry modification: HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Winlogon\BackgroundStartup added “attacker.dll” (SHA256: 3d5f9b1a2c...) ; File artifact: C:\Users\Public\attacker.dll, size 84,632 bytes, last accessed 2024-03-05 02:12:30 ; Security event ID 4625: 4 failed logon attempts from IP 192.168.1.45 within 60 seconds; Sysmon event ID 1: image file executed with command line “rundll32.exe attacker.dll,DoStuff” ; Network traffic: outbound TLS to 203.0.113.45:443 with ALPN h2, packet payload containing base64 sequence “c2Vjb25kLWRvbWFn”; Email artifact: Outlook attachment report.pdf flagged as suspicious, path C:\Users\Public\Documents\report.pdf, file size 73,210 bytes, SHA256: 6b8e… ; Authentication log: user jsmith added to Domain Admins group at 2024-03-05 02:13:02 without proper approval; syslog event: “unusual privilege escalation” from local SYSTEM process; Unusual process runtime: rundll32.exe running for 2 hours with CPU > 95%; Missing log: no audit log entry for registry key creation.
model_a_tasks:
Task #1  
Title: Verify attacker.dll integrity and malicious content  
Description: Compare the SHA256 of C:\Users\Public\attacker.dll against known malicious hashes and run it through AV/antimalware engines. Load the DLL in a sandboxed environment to capture its imported/exported functions, identify the payload of “DoStuff”, and confirm whether the DLL is packed or obfuscated.  
  
Task #2  
Title: Confirm registry persistence via BackgroundStartup key  
Description: Inspect HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Winlogon\BackgroundStartup for the “attacker.dll” entry, verify its last‑modified timestamp, confirm that the key remains after a clean reboot, and review the ACLs to ensure they are not set to full control for the SYSTEM or Admins group.  
  
Task #3  
Title: Reconstruct the cmd→PowerShell→rundll32 execution chain  
Description: Use Sysinternals Procmon or PowerShell logging to trace the exact command lines and flags used by cmd.exe, powershell.exe, and rundll32.exe at the recorded timestamps. Verify that no additional bootstrap script or obfuscation layer exists and that all components are launched by jsmith.  
  
Task #4  
Title: Decode and analyze outbound TLS payload to 203.0.113.45  
Description: Decode the Base64 sequence “c2Vjb25kLWRvbWFn” to reveal the payload sent to the C2 server. Correlate the decoded data with known C2 commands or data exfiltration patterns, and identify the application or process responsible for the TLS session (e.g., rundll32 or a custom binary).  
  
Task #5  
Title: Examine the suspicious Outlook attachment report.pdf  
Description: Extract the PDF from C:\Users\Public\Documents\report.pdf, run it through an AV engine, and analyze for embedded JavaScript, malicious macros, or exploit code. Check email headers for spoofing or compromised accounts, and determine if opening the file correlates with the execution of attacker.dll.  
  
Task #6  
Title: Investigate jsmith’s domain admin privilege escalation  
Description: Review security and directory service logs around 2024‑03‑05 02:13:02 to confirm the legitimacy of the Domain Admins addition, identify who authorized the change, and search for evidence of lateral movement or credential theft that could have enabled the compromise.  
  
Task #7  
Title: Audit missing registry modification logs  
Description: Verify current audit policy for registry key creation events. Investigate whether log tampering occurred by cross‑checking EDR/file‑integrity monitoring data, and if no audit entry was generated legitimately, flag it as a security incident.  
  
Task #8  
Title: Profile anomalous rundll32.exe runtime behavior  
Description: Using Process Explorer or DupeGuru, monitor the rundll32.exe instance that ran for 2 hours at >95 % CPU. Capture its memory image, enumerate loaded modules, and identify any hidden threads or injected code that may stem from attacker.dll’s execution.  
  
Task #9  
Title: Validate legitimacy of RDP session ID 1024 for jsmith  
Description: Review RDP logs (Event ID 1149/24) to confirm session 1024 was authenticated, verify the source IP, and check for any backdoor usage or credential dumping activities during that session. Cross‑link with the timestamp of the attacker.dll execution to see if RDP was the vector.

Description: Add 192.168.1.45 to an external block list to prevent further failed logons, and block outbound traffic to 203.0.113.45:443. Deploy IDS/IPS rules to alert on any subsequent TLS traffic containing similar Base64 payloads or other anomalous patterns.

case_title: Suspicious enumeration activity detected on the corporate network from an unknown workstation targeting domain infrastructure
case_description:
– Sysmon EventID 1: Image C:\\Windows\\System32\\cmd.exe, command "net view \\\\DC01" executed by user Guest on workstation-01 from IP 10.20.0.45  
– Windows Security Event 4624: Logon type 3 (network) for account Guest on host DC01, source IP 10.20.0.45, logon successful  
– Splunk alert: Port‑scan detection on internal host 10.20.0.10, ports 135–445 scanned from source 10.20.0.45, 120 packets in 10 s  
– DNS logs: 10.20.0.45 issued 120 queries for subdomains of *.internal.example.com in a 5‑minute window, 30 per minute  
– Endpoint protection log: Detection of O365 macro “EnumUsers.ps1” downloaded to C:\\Users\\Guest\\Downloads, flagged as suspicious  
– Netcat activity: Unknown device MAC AA:BB:CC:DD:EE:FF connected to VLAN 10, performed SMB share enumeration commands (smbclient “-L” on multiple internal servers)  
– PowerShell Sysmon EventID 1: Image C:\\Windows\\System32\\WindowsPowerShell\\v1.0\\powershell.exe, command line “-NoProfile -ExecutionPolicy Bypass -Command Get‑ADUser -Filter *” executed on host DC01 at 09:47 UTC by user Guest from 10.20.0.45  
– Windows Event 4663: Failed attempts to access NTFS share “\\Server01\SharedData” by UNC path from IP 10.20.0.45, 15 attempts in 3 min  
– Network Flow logs: TCP SYN packets from 10.20.0.45 to 10.20.0.25:139, 10.20.0.26:445 with flow duration <1 s, 10 occurrences   
– Intrusion detection system (Snort) log: Alert ID 12345 “Active Directory enumeration” detected on host DC01, source 10.20.0.45, destination 10.20.0.1 (DC01)   
– LogCorrelator alert: Repeated “whoami” and “net user” commands executed by a non‑administrative local account on workstation-01, 5 executions in 15 min.
model_a_tasks:
Task #1  
Title: Validate Workstation-01 Identity and Ownership  
Description: Cross‑reference the MAC address AA:BB:CC:DD:EE:FF, hostname workstation-01, and IP 10.20.0.45 against the asset inventory to confirm legitimate ownership. Verify that this IP has not been reassigned or spoofed, and capture the matching asset record for red‑team comparison.  

Task #2  
Title: Examine the Guest Account Permissions  
Description: Retrieve the Guest AD account attributes, group memberships, and access rights. Look for any elevated or anomalous privileges, as well as recent changes to the account or related inheritance policies that could indicate compromise.  

Task #3  
Title: Perform a Full Endpoint Security Scan of Workstation‑01  
Description: Initiate an EDR/AV scan on workstation‑01, collect memory dumps, and list running processes, scheduled tasks, and startup items. Search for known malicious binaries and suspicious PowerShell/Netcat activity that might have been installed after the enumeration scripts ran.  

Task #4  
Title: Isolate and Sandbox the O365 Macro “EnumUsers.ps1”  
Description: Copy the macro to a controlled virtual environment and execute it in a sandbox. Inspect script contents for Get‑ADUser calls, credential dumping, or persistence mechanisms, and compare its hash with threat‑intel databases for known malicious artifacts.  

Task #5  
Title: Correlate DNS Query Patterns for Subdomain Enumeration  
Description: Aggregate the 120 DNS queries for *.internal.example.com from 10.20.0.45 within the 5‑minute window. Match queried subdomains against approved naming conventions; flag any unknown or spoofed subdomains that could indicate DNS tunneling or domain enumeration.  

Task #6  
Title: Investigate Port‑Scan Detected on 10.20.0.10  
Description: Review the Splunk port‑scan alert and list all internal hosts that were probed on ports 135–445. Cross‑check with known authorized scan windows; determine whether the scan correlates with legitimate vulnerability assessment activity or is a malicious reconnaissance attempt.  

Task #7  
Title: Scrutinize DC01 Logon Events for Guest Activity  
Description: Pull Windows Security Event 4624 logon type 3 entries for Guest on DC01. Verify source IP, port numbers, and login timestamps. Identify any simultaneous logons from unrelated accounts that could signify credential reuse or lateral movement.  

Task #8  
Title: Examine Failed NTFS Share Access Attempts (4663)  
Description: Investigate the 15 failed attempts to access \\Server01\SharedData from 10.20.0.45. Confirm that Guest lacks proper permissions, and check for patterns of brute‑force or enumeration attempts.  

Task #9  
Title: Analyze Network Flow Logs for Lateral Movement  
Description: Expand the network flow inspection beyond the 10.20.0.45–10.20.0.25:139 and 10.20.0.26:445 entries. Identify any other internal targets contacted within <1 s durations, and correlate those with subsequent successful logons or data access events.  

Task #10  
Title: Check for Privilege Escalation or Unauthorized Account Creation  
Description: Correlate all Sysmon Event 1 entries on DC01 and system logs for evidence of privilege escalation (e.g., creation of new local admin users, addition to security groups). Look for any Group Policy changes or registry edits that could support the attacker’s inventory actions.  

Task #11  
Title: Verify Exfiltration or Sensitive Data Access  
Description: Search file integrity monitoring and audit logs for any copying of user lists or sensitive configuration files. Review SMB traffic for unusually large outbound transfers to external IP addresses, and inspect any scheduled tasks that might exfiltrate data.  

Task #12  
Title: Consolidate Findings and Recommend Containment Measures  
Description: Compile evidence from the above tasks into a comprehensive report. If compromise is confirmed, recommend isolating workstation‑01, resetting the Guest password, patching vulnerable services, and tightening audit policies. Update IDS/IPS signatures and alerting rules to detect future enumeration patterns.
model_b_tasks:
Task #1  
Title: Verify workstation‑01 asset legitimacy  
Description: Cross‑reference the device “workstation-01” and its MAC address (AA:BB:CC:DD:EE:FF) against the approved asset inventory. Confirm that the Guest account is a sanctioned login and that the device should have access to the corporate network. If the asset is unregistered or unauthorized, add it to a watchlist and coordinate with the network operations team to isolate the workstation for further investigation.

Task #2  
Title: Analyze cmd.exe “net view” enumeration data  
Description: Extract Sysmon EventID 1 log entries on workstation‑01 showing “net view \\DC01”. Compile a list of all SMB shares and server names returned by this command. Cross‑reference these findings with the known domain infrastructure to determine whether the enumerated resources were legitimate or newly discovered during the alert timeframe.

Task #3  
Title: Inspect PowerShell AD enumeration activity  
Description: Examine Sysmon EventID 1 logs for PowerShell commands executed on DC01. Validate that the command line “Get‑ADUser ‑Filter *” was indeed run by the Guest account from IP 10.20.0.45. Retrieve the command output from AD logs and review it for signs of suspicious user enumeration, such as detection of service accounts or privileged roles.

Task #4  
Title: Correlate port‑scanning activity with enumeration  
Description: Merge Splunk port‑scan detection data (ports 135–445 on internal host 10.20.0.10) with network flow logs showing SYN packets from 10.20.0.45 to multiple SMB ports. Confirm that the scans correspond temporally with the enumeration actions (cmd.exe, PowerShell, Netcat). Identify all internal hosts and services that were targeted during the scanning window.

Task #5  
Title: Investigate suspicious O365 macro file  
Description: Retrieve the O365 macro “EnumUsers.ps1” flagged in the endpoint protection log from the Guest user’s Downloads folder. Sandbox the script to observe its behavior in a controlled environment. Look for functions that harvest credentials (e.g., Mimikatz modules), spread laterally, or establish persistence mechanisms on the host.

Task #6  
Title: Verify SMB share enumeration via Netcat  
Description: Review network flow and Netcat session logs from the unknown device (MAC AA:BB:CC:DD:EE:FF) recorded on VLAN 10. Identify SMB enumeration commands issued (smbclient “-L”). Cross‑check the enumerated servers and shares against the domain inventory to detect illicit reconnaissance attempts.

Task #7  
Title: Assess failed SMB share access attempts  
Description: Search Windows Event 4663 logs on DC01 for failed access attempts to the NTFS share “\\Server01\SharedData” originating from 10.20.0.45. Quantify these failures over the 3‑minute window and inspect ACLs on the share for misconfigurations that might allow Guest access. Determine whether the failures were due to brute‑force attempts or legitimate enumeration.

Task #8  
Title: Check for subsequent privileged account usage  
Description: Filter authentication logs from DC01 for positive network logons (Event 4624, LogonType 3/10) during the investigation period that involve privileged domains (Domain Admins, Enterprise Admins). Correlate source IP 10.20.0.45 with any successful privileged logins. Investigate whether suspicious enumeration actions led to credential compromise.

Task #9  
Title: Capture real‑time forensic evidence  
Description: Execute SOAR automation to isolate workstation‑01 and DC01 from the corporate network, preventing further lateral movement. Initiate live memory capture and volatile data collection on both hosts. Preserve Sysmon logs, PowerShell histories, malicious macro samples, and Windows audit logs for in‑depth forensic analysis.

Task #10  
Title: Initiate incident escalation  
Description: Compile a consolidated report summarizing the enumeration activity, port scans, malicious macro detection, and any subsequent privileged account use. Create an escalation ticket in the SOAR platform for the Incident Response Team, attaching all relevant artifacts. Include recommended containment actions, such as blocking IP 10.20.0.45, purging the O365 macro from the guest policy, and resetting Guest account credentials.

case_title: Suspicious LDAP enumeration attempt detected from external IP 203.0.113.45 targeting internal Active Directory
case_description:
- Source IP: 203.0.113.45 (unregistered external address)  
- Destination IP: 10.10.10.5 (internal AD server)  
- Service: LDAP on port 389 (and 636 for LDAPS)  
- Timestamp: 2026-03-10 08:55:12 UTC to 2026-03-10 09:10:47 UTC (continuous activity)  
- LDAP queries: repeated simple bind attempts followed by search requests with base DN "dc=corp,dc=example,dc=com" and filter "(sAMAccountName=*)" retrieving user objects; returned attributes: sAMAccountName, displayName, objectSid  
- List of queried user accounts (partial): jsmith, ada, wflores, pking, admin, domainadministrator, guest, etc.  
- Authentication logs: 32 failed bind attempts with status "invalid credentials" (no successful authentication observed)  
- Netlogon logs: no sign of successful authentication, only failed attempts logged as "3 Failed LDAP bind"  
- Windows Event ID 4625: multiple failed logon attempts from 203.0.113.45 with TargetUserName "*", LogonType 3 (network)  
- Network flow: high packet rate (~5 packets per second) on port 389/636 toward AD server, primarily from 203.0.113.45, no outbound traffic noted  
- User-agent and client info: empty TLS handshake, simple LDAP client string "LDAPClient v1.0" observed in Wireshark capture  
- Behavioral pattern: rapid sequential queries (every 1–2 seconds) with no such pattern in baseline traffic; no user agent fingerprint tying to known legitimate tools  
- Observed response size: 1200 bytes for each enumerate query, consistent with a bulk user list retrieval  
- No known legitimate service from 203.0.113.45 in network registry or allowed processes.
model_a_tasks:
Task #1  
Title: Verify firewall ACLs permitting LDAP traffic from 203.0.113.45  
Description: Review the perimeter firewall logs and ACL rules to confirm whether inbound LDAP connections (ports 389/636) are allowed from the external IP 203.0.113.45. Identify any recent changes or misconfigurations that might have unintentionally opened the service to the public network and ensure this traffic was correctly logged.

Task #2  
Title: Retrieve LDAP audit logs from the domain controller for the event window  
Description: Extract the epoch‑stamped LDAP trace logs from the AD server (10.10.10.5) for 2026‑03‑10 08:55:12 UTC – 09:10:47 UTC. Focus on bind requests, search operations, and returned attributes to rebuild the attacker’s query timeline.

Task #3  
Title: Correlate Windows Security events (4625) for the same timeframe  
Description: Pull Event ID 4625 logs from all domain controllers during the attack window, filtering on Source IP = 203.0.113.45 and LogonType = 3. Map each failed login to the corresponding LDAP bind attempt to confirm the correlation between network traffic and authentication failures.

Task #4  
Title: Inspect Netlogon logs for “Failed LDAP bind” events  
Description: Scan the Netlogon audit logs for a matching period, isolating any “Failed LDAP bind” messages from 203.0.113.45. Compare counts with the LDAP logs to ensure no successful bind escaped detection.

Task #5  
Title: Conduct threat‑intelligence lookup on 203.0.113.45  
Description: Query commercial and open‑source threat‑intel platforms for the IP’s reputation, known malicious usage, and historical abuse patterns. Document any matches to APT groups or known credential‑stealing operations.

Task #6  
Title: Resolve reverse DNS and geolocation for the source IP  
Description: Perform reverse DNS lookup and IP geolocation to ascertain the hosting provider, region, and possible affiliation. This context assists in determining if the IP is a known malicious actor or a legitimate, yet unexpected, entry point.

Task #7  
Title: Validate the “LDAPClient v1.0” client string against malware signatures  
Description: Search malware and IOC databases for the exact client identification string “LDAPClient v1.0.” Determine if it correlates with any known malicious tools or custom scripts used for enumeration.

Task #8  
Title: Deep‑packet analyze the captured traffic between source and AD server  
Description: Open the captured Wireshark session, filter by the IP/port pair, and dissect each LDAP operation. Verify that no credential material (e.g., passwords or clear‑text credentials) was transmitted, and assess the volume of data returned per query.

Task #9  
Title: Review AD audit logs for anomalous account changes during the event window  
Description: Examine AD change‑tracking logs (Event IDs 4720, 4722, 4723, 4724, 4725, 4726) for any creation, modification, or privilege elevation of user or group objects that could have been conducted after enumeration or due to credential theft.

Task #10  
Title: Search for lateral movement indicators following enumeration  
Description: Look for new SMB sessions, RDP connections, or other network activity originating from internal hosts to external addresses after the enumeration window. Identify any processes running under discovered user principals that might signal lateral exploitation.

Task #11

Description: Query firewall and IDS logs to identify any outbound traffic from the domain controllers or other critical servers to 203.0.113.45 or other external destinations during or shortly after the enumeration. Confirm whether the attacker attempted exfiltration.

Task #12

Description: Based on findings from threat intel, logs, and any outgoing malicious activity, decide on an immediate network blocker (e.g., firewall deny rule). Document the containment action, justification, and any exceptional consideration for false positives.

Task #13

Description: Secure the captured PCAP, log files, and any extracted forensic artifacts. Create timestamped, hash‑verified copies and record handling details in the incident record to satisfy compliance and potential legal review.

Task #14  
Title: Summarize findings and recommend remediation steps  
Description: Compile a concise report outlining attack vector, scope, potential impact, and recommended action items such as granulating LDAP policies, strengthening monitoring, and training staff on anomalous enumeration patterns. Submit to the relevant incident response team for follow‑up.

Description: Compare the recorded LDAP query logs, captured packet traces, and Windows Security events (4625) to confirm that the enumeration attempts indeed succeeded in retransmitting user data, despite all bind attempts reporting “invalid credentials.” Verify that the search requests are legitimate and not spoofed or false positives introduced by monitoring filters.

Task #7

Description: Inspect Netflow/sFlow records for any outbound traffic from the AD server originating after the enumeration window. Look for SMB, WinRM, or DNS queries that might indicate lateral movement or credential exfiltration triggered by the enumeration data.

Task #8

Description: Run automated Tomahawk (?) playbooks on domain controllers to search for processes like “mimikatz.exe,” “sekurlsa.dll,” or PowerShell scripts indicating credential dumping. Prioritize evidence appearing within ±15 minutes of the enumeration activity.

Task #9  
Title: Block External Source IP 203.0.113.45 at Perimeter Firewall  
Description: Use the SOAR automation framework to push a new deny rule for IP 203.0.113.45 targeting ports 389 and 636 across all ingress points in the DMZ. Confirm rule deployment and receipt via firewall logs.

Task #10  
Title: Notify Privileged Account Owners of Enumeration Exposure  
Description: Trigger an automated notification to the custodians of accounts enumerated during the activity (admin, domainadministrator, guest). Include a brief assessment of potential risk and immediate remediation steps (e.g., password change).

Task #11  
Title: Generate Incident Summary Report for Review Board  
Description: Collate investigation findings—including timeline, LDAP query details, threat intel context, and mitigation actions—into a structured report. Attach evidence artifacts (packet captures, log excerpts) and provide a risk rating per NIST SP 800‑83 guidelines.

Task #12  
Title: Update SIEM LDAP Correlation Rules  
Description: Incorporate the identified enumeration pattern (high‑frequency search requests with “(sAMAccountName=*)” filter) into the SIEM correlation language. Set a threshold to trigger alerts when enumeration exceeds baseline activity by >5× over a 15‑minute window.

Task #13  
Title: Harden LDAP Bind Requirements on AD Servers  
Description: Enforce LDAP referrals restrictions and require Signed LDAP over SSL/TLS. Disable anonymous binds entirely and ensure smart card or certificate‑based authentication is required for all client connections outside the corporate network.

Task #14  
Title: Schedule Continuous Monitoring for LDAP Enumeration from External IPs  
Description: Deploy a recurring SOAR playbook that scans logs daily for LDAP search patterns exceeding baseline metrics from any external IPs. Configure automatic escalation to SOC analysts if thresholds are breached, accompanied by enriched threat intel context.

case_title: Security team observed an exploitation attempt targeting Apache Struts 2 S2-045 vulnerability via crafted SOAP requests on internal web server
case_description: Firewall logs show inbound traffic from 203.0.113.45 to 192.168.2.10:80 with repeated SYN/ACK and IDS alert “possible exploitation attempt”; Web server logs record POST requests to /services/Controller with SOAP bodies containing injection string ${command@classpath://cmd} and mis‑encoded XML; Windows Security logs indicate multiple Event ID 4625 (failed logon) and Event ID 4688 for cmd.exe /c powershell -NoProfile -EncodedCommand Q…; Sysmon event 11 logs creation of C:\Windows\Temp\svchost.exe (SHA‑256 8f2c5e…) followed by Sysmon event 12 execution; Antivirus scan flags this file as trojan:Win32/Generic; Network egress includes outbound connection to 66.168.1.22:443 and DNS resolution for c2.b2ver.net; Vulnerability scan from OWASP ZAP lists S2‑045 on the target as High; Observed anomalies include frequent malformed SOAP traffic, repeated failed logon attempts, creation of suspicious binaries, and outbound connections to an unknown C2 domain. No confirmed privilege escalation or data exfiltration has yet been observed.
model_a_tasks:
Task #1  
Title: Review inbound firewall activity  
Description: Inspect the inbound firewall logs for traffic from 203.0.113.45 to 192.168.2.10:80. Identify patterns such as SYN/ACK floods, rate‑limited requests, or repeated connection attempts. Correlate the timing of these attempts with the first recorded web server and Sysmon events to establish a timeline of the attack.

Task #2  
Title: Correlate SOAP injection payloads with web logs  
Description: Extract all POST requests to /services/Controller that contain the injection string `${command@classpath://cmd}`. Match those requests to the corresponding timestamps in the web server logs and the Sysmon events for C:\Windows\Temp\svchost.exe to confirm whether the injection was successfully processed.

Task #3  
Title: Verify Struts 2 S2‑045 vulnerability on the target  
Description: Run a dedicated Struts vulnerability scanner or send a crafted request to verify that the application is indeed exposed to the S2‑045 flaw. Document the Struts version, build number, and any mitigations (e.g., `disableXMLSerialization` flag) that might affect exploitation.

Task #4  
Title: Decode and analyze the encoded PowerShell command  
Description: Reconstruct the Base64‑encoded PowerShell payload from Sysmon Event ID 4688. Decode it locally and examine the command or script for malicious intent, exfiltration goals, or persistence instructions. Archive the decoded payload for future evidence.

Task #5  
Title: Examine Sysmon event 11 and 12 for malicious activity  
Description: Aggregate image creation (Event 11) and image load (Event 12) events related to `C:\Windows\Temp\svchost.exe`. Review parent process information, command line arguments, and that the binary’s execution is not legitimate. Verify that no unexpected scheduled tasks or services were launched from this binary.

Task #6  
Title: Compare suspicious file hash with malware databases  
Description: Search the SHA‑256 hash `8f2c5e…` in public and private threat intelligence feeds (CAPE, VirusTotal, Hybrid Analysis). If known, download the unpacked sample and analyze its behavior. If unknown, proceed with sandboxing to determine malicious functions.

Task #7  
Title: Identify persistence mechanisms used by the attacker  
Description: Scan the Windows registry for malicious Run keys, Startup folders, or scheduled tasks that invoke `svchost.exe`. Search for hidden or obfuscated services in `services.msc`. Verify whether the evoked binary has self‑repair or auto‑recovery mechanisms.

Task #8  
Title: Investigate failed and potentially successful logons  
Description: Filter Windows Security logs for Event ID 4625 around the time of the attack, then check Event ID 4624 for any succeeding successful logons to the system. If a successful logon exists, record the account, workstation, and logon type to assess privilege escalation or lateral movement.

Task #9  
Title: Monitor outbound C2 traffic for data exfiltration  
Description: Capture packets to 66.168.1.22:443 and perform DNS queries for c2.b2ver.net. Inspect TLS handshakes, certificates, and encrypted payloads for indicators of data exfiltration or command‑and‑control communication. Compare observed communication patterns to known C2 templates.

Task #10  
Title: Run a full malware scan on the impacted host  
Description: Execute a multi‑engine AV scan (Windows Defender, CrowdStrike, ESET) against the entire system volume and any removable media. Flag and isolate any additional suspicious binaries, especially those with names like “svchost”. Verify that AV reports are logged with file hashes and detection confidence.

Task #11  
Title: Create forensic copies of the host for evidence preservation  
Description: Image the entire system volume and create a RAM dump during a live capture window. Sign the images, record timestamps, and store them in a secure, read‑only repository for later deep analysis or legal evidence.

Task #12  
Title: Evaluate containment and remediation steps  
Description: Based on findings, isolate the affected server from the internal network, patch the Struts vulnerability immediately, and apply hardening guidelines (e.g., disable XML serialization, apply least‑privilege). Update firewall rules to block the attacker’s IP and sanitize any recovered credentials or compromised accounts. Return the host to operation only after validating that all malicious artifacts are removed.

case_title: Suspicious privilege escalation attempt via UAC bypass and scheduled task creation detected
case_description: Event ID 4672: elevated privilege logon for user jdoe; creation of scheduled task “ElevateTask” with SYSTEM context; registry key HKLM\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run modified to add “EvilApp” pointing to C:\Temp\eVIL.exe; PowerShell script executed to set HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Policies\System\EnableLUA to 0; network connection from 192.168.10.12 to internal web server on port 8080; suspicious file C:\Temp\eVIL.exe with altered timestamp and hash mismatch; Sysmon event 1: CreateProcess with arguments “/c start /b C:\Windows\System32\svchost.exe -k EvilService”; addition of user jdoe to Local Administrators group via net localgroup; Windows Security Log Event ID 4829 indicating privilege escalation; command-line arguments for cmd.exe show impersonation token usage; abnormal scheduled start times and elevated privilege process spawning.
model_a_tasks:
Task #1  
Title: Validate the integrity of C:\Temp\eVIL.exe  
Description: Calculate the current hash of C:\Temp\eVIL.exe and compare it with known good hashes from a trusted repository or baseline. Verify the digital signature, if present, and check for any embedded certificates or mismatches. Perform a full malware scan using multiple AV engines and a sandbox analysis to identify any malicious behavior. Document any anomalies and retain the file for further forensic processing.

Task #2  
Title: Examine the properties of the “ElevateTask” scheduled task  
Description: Export the task definition using `schtasks /query /TN ElevateTask /XML`. Review the trigger times, action command line, user context, and settings for forced elevation or hidden execution. Cross‑reference the task’s creation timestamp with event logs 4672, 4829, and 4728 to ensure consistency. Identify any UAC bypass patterns such as `runas` switches or SYSTEM privileges.

Task #3  
Title: Assess changes to HKLM\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run  
Description: Export the registry hive to capture the Run key before and after the modification. Verify the “EvilApp” entry points to the executable’s true path and confirm that file metadata (creation, modification dates) matches expected values. Check whether the entry was added by an authorized process or by a script, and look for any auto‑delete or persistence cleanup tasks associated with it.

Task #4  
Title: Review the EnableLUA registry setting modification  
Description: Capture the value of HKLM\SYSTEM\ControlSet\Control\Policies\System\EnableLUA from before and after the event. Determine whether the change was made by a legitimate installer or a custom script. Correlate with PowerShell logs to confirm that the command line explicitly set EnableLUA to 0. Verify that the ChangeLog or Event ID 4674 logs reflect this alteration.

Task #5  
Title: Audit jdoe’s addition to the Local Administrators group  
Description: Retrieve Event IDs 4728 (member added), 4720 (user account enabled), and 4732 (group membership changed) for the timeframe surrounding the escalation. Confirm the user’s prior group membership and role, then confirm whether the addition was an administrative action or an escalated privilege request. Search for any associated scripts or scheduled tasks that performed the group modification.

Task #6  
Title: Analyze the PowerShell script for UAC bypass techniques  
Description: Pull the full PowerShell command from the event logs or safe mode execution history. Deconstruct the script to identify functions that manipulate security policies, registry entries, or scheduled tasks. Evaluate whether the script relies on standard UAC bypass modules (e.g., “Invoke-UACBypass”) and check for obfuscation or encryption. Replace the script with a hash and send for sandbox execution.

Task #7  
Title: Correlate the 192.168.10.12 network connection to internal activity  
Description: Inspect firewall, IDS/IPS, and internal web server logs for inbound sessions from 192.168.10.12 on port 8080. Identify any HTTP requests, POST payloads, or unusual WMI calls originating from this IP. Determine whether the connection was legitimate, part of a command and control session, or used to download the malware binary.

Task #8  
Title: Investigate Sysmon event ID 1 related to svchost execution  
Description: Extract the command line arguments (`/c start /b C:\Windows\System32\svchost.exe -k EvilService`) from the Sysmon log. Confirm that the process was launched from a malicious context and that no legitimate service `EvilService` exists. Review registry service entries and the services.msc list to discover any service registrations pointing to `eVIL.exe`.

Task #9  
Title: Validate cmd.exe impersonation token usage  
Description: Examine the `cmd.exe` event logs for command lines that contain `runas` or `start /i` with elevated tokens. Identify the credentials or token source used for the impersonation. Correlate with the context of the scheduled task and UAC bypass to confirm if the impersonation was part of the privilege escalation chain.

Task #10  
Title: Perform a host-wide persistence indicator scan  
Description: Search for newly created or modified scheduled tasks, registry Run keys, system startup folders, and authorized loader modules. Examine the Windows Security and Application logs for any anomalous startup events or execution of unknown binaries. Catalog all discovered indicators and compare them against known APT persistence frameworks such as Persistence Path or T1070.001.
model_b_tasks:
Task #1  
Title: Verify existence and definition of scheduled task “ElevateTask”  
Description: Use the Task Scheduler console or schtasks.exe (eg. `schtasks /Query /TN "ElevateTask" /V /FO LIST`) to retrieve the full definition of the task. Confirm that it runs with SYSTEM security context, note its trigger schedule, action command, and any associated conditions. Export the task definition to XML (`schtasks /Query /TN "ElevateTask" /XML > ElevateTask.xml`) for evidence and double‑check event IDs 4698/4702 if available for creation/modification timestamps.

Task #2  
Title: Examine the suspicious file C:\Temp\eVIL.exe  
Description: Compute its MD5/SHA256 hashes locally and compare to values stored in the Security Log event mentioning “EvilApp”. Submit the file to VirusTotal and run enterprise AV engines if available. Inspect the binary header, analyze sections for packing or obfuscation, and dump strings for indicators of malicious activity (reverse shell, persistence code). Consider sandbox execution (e.g., Cuckoo) to observe runtime behaviour and capture peer IP addresses.

Task #3  
Title: Decompile and analyze the UAC bypass PowerShell script  
Description: Locate the base64‑encoded or inline PowerShell command from the Security Log that modifies `HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Policies\System\EnableLUA`. Decode any obfuscation (`[System.Convert]::FromBase64String`) and review the script logic. Confirm it ran successfully by checking registry value after execution (`reg query HKLM\System\CurrentControlSet\Control\Policies\System /v EnableLUA`). Document whether it used signed scripts, persisted settings, or employed $ExecutionContext tricks.

Task #4  
Title: Verify modification of the Run registry key to add “EvilApp”  
Description: Query `HKLM\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run` using regedit or `reg query` to list all values. Confirm that “EvilApp” exists pointing to `C:\Temp\eVIL.exe`. Compare current key state against known clean baselines (if available) or against earlier snapshot data. Check for unauthorized user rights or integrity timestamp alterations that might indicate registry tampering.

Task #5  
Title: Analyze Sysmon event ID 1 for svchost.exe launch under EvilService  
Description: Retrieve Sysmon logs surrounding the timestamp of the “ElevateTask” execution. Focus on event ID 1 records where `Image=C:\Windows\system32\svchost.exe` and `CommandLine=/c start /b C:\Windows\system32\svchost.exe -k EvilService`. Map the parent/child process chain: EvilApp → cmd.exe → svchost.exe. Verify that EvilService – a service configuration – was indeed created or modified by inspecting `sc query EvilService` or the registry at `HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\EvilService`.

Task #6  
Title: Audit addition of user jdoe to Local Administrators group  
Description: Execute `net localgroup Administrators` on the host to list current members. Filter Security Log for event ID 4728 (add user to local group) around the time of jdoe’s privilege escalation. Note the subject user who added jdoe, the target group, and the operation source. Validate whether the addition was legitimate (e.g., by a domain admin) or performed through token impersonation.

Task #7  
Title: Investigate cmd.exe impersonation token usage  
Description: Search Security Log for event ID 4688 with `ImageName=cmd.exe` and `CreationFlags` indicating impersonation (`DWORD` 0x00000008). Extract the corresponding `CommandLine` to confirm presence of `/C` or `/K` flags that could be used to run elevated commands. Trace back the originating process and user context to identify how the impersonation token was acquired (e.g., via `LogonType=2` or `3`).

Task #8  
Title: Analyze anomalous network connection from 192.168.10.12 to internal web server port 8080  
Description: Review firewall logs, NetFlow, or passive packet captures for outbound traffic from 192.168.10.12 to the destination internal web server on TCP/8080. Inspect HTTP headers, URLs, and payloads for evidence of data staging, command delivery, or exfiltration. Map the network connection to the source process (`Get-NetTCPConnection | Where-Object {$_.LocalAddress -eq "192.168.10.12" -and $_.LocalPort -eq 8080}`) to determine if it was part of the privilege escalation process.

Task #9  
Title: Search for additional privilege escalation activities beyond jdoe  
Description: Filter Security Log for events 4703 (deleted object), 4704 (changed ACLs), 4823/4826 (process blocked due to insufficient privilege), and 4688 for process creations involving tools like `psexec`, `rundll32`, `wmic`. Examine Scheduled Task logs for other abnormal tasks, and review WMI queries (`wmic /namespace:\\root\cimv2 PATH Win32_Process GET ProcessId,CommandLine`). Check for scripts or service registrations that may have been created concurrently.

Task #10  
Title: Build and analyse incident timeline across all suspicious events  
Description: Leverage the SOAR platform’s timeline engine to collate timestamps from Event IDs 4672, 4698/4702, 4728, 4829, Sysmon ID 1, and process creation logs. Sequence the events to illustrate the adversary’s workflow: initial UAC bypass → scheduled task persistence → registry run key persistence → SYSTEM‑level svchost launch → admin group elevation. Identify overlapping windows that suggest coordinated actions and pinning points for containment.

Task #11  
Title: Compile and format Indicators of Compromise (IOCs)  
Description: From the investigation log, extract file hashes, registry paths/values, scheduled task names, PowerShell command fragments, service names, network destination IP/port, and user accounts involved. Format these IOCs according to MITRE ATT&CK tactics/techniques (e.g., T1086 PowerShell, T1053 Scheduled Task, T1068 Exploitation for Privilege Escalation). Export to CSV/TXT and push to internal IOC feed for broader detection.

Task #12  
Title: Recommend containment actions for the identified malicious activity  
Description: Provide a step‑by‑step remediation checklist:  
- Revoke jdoe administrative privileges (`net localgroup Administrators jdoe /delete`).  
- Delete scheduled task “ElevateTask” (`schtasks /Delete /TN "ElevateTask" /F`).  
- Remove “EvilApp” registry entry (`reg delete HKLM\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run /v EvilApp /f`).  
- Delete or quarantine C:\Temp\eVIL.exe and other residual files.  
- Re‑enable UAC (`Set-ItemProperty HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Policies\System EnableLUA 1`).  
- Verify EvilService does not exist or has been terminated, and remove any registry keys under `HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\EvilService`.  
- Run full endpoint malware scan and collect memory dumps for deeper artifact analysis.

Task #13  
Title: Examine Event ID 4829 privilege escalation entries in detail  
Description: Isolate Security Log events with ID 4829 that occurred within the same timeframe as the scheduled task. Capture the `SubjectUserSid`, `TargetUserSid`, `ProcessName`, and `AdditionalInformation` fields to reconstruct the exact chain of privilege escalation (e.g., token impersonation, privilege gain). Correlate with cmd.exe process entries to confirm who performed the action and whether it was legitimate. Use this analysis to rule out false positives and to strengthen evidence for malicious intent.
