case_title: Suspicious LDAP enumeration activity detected from an internal workstation
case_description: PowerShell process executed “Get-ADUser -Filter *” (Sysmon event ID 1) repeated 7 times between 02:30‑02:35 UTC; Windows Event 4624: successful logon to domain controller from 10.8.12.31 using user “anonymous” occurring 12 times; Sysmon DNS logs: 10.8.12.31 queried “dc.domain.local” 15 times within 5 minutes; Snort rule 123456 triggered for LDAP traffic from 10.8.12.31 to 10.8.1.10 port 389 flagged enumeration payload; NetFlow: >30 SMB connections from 10.8.12.31 to 10.8.2.5‑10.8.2.10 on port 445; File system change: new script “EnumAD.ps1” created in C:\Temp by SYSTEM on 10.8.12.31; WMI event log: multiple queries for Win32_UserAccount performed 20 times from 10.8.12.31; Syslog entry: ‘whoami’ command executed by user “administrator” on host 10.8.12.31 at 03:12 UTC; Malware scan on host 10.8.12.31 flagged binary “enum4linux.exe” as suspicious.
model_a_tasks:
Task #1  
Title: Compile a comprehensive event timeline for 10.8.12.31 (02:00‑04:00 UTC)  
Description: Pull all relevant logs—including Windows Event IDs 4624/4634, Sysmon events, WMI logs, Syslog, NetFlow, and Snort alerts—for the specified period. Sort entries chronologically to map the sequence of activities and identify any anomalies or gaps in the log data.  

Task #2  
Title: Verify the integrity and creator of the “EnumAD.ps1” script  
Description: Retrieve the script from disk or system backup, check its file attributes and ACLs, compare its hash to a known baseline, and confirm whether it was created by SYSTEM or another account. Detect any modifications or embedded malicious payloads.  

Task #3  
Title: Analyze the PowerShell script’s payload and outputs  
Description: Execute the script in a controlled environment or parse its content to identify the exact PowerShell commands (e.g., Get-ADUser, WMI queries). Capture any output objects and compare them to legitimate enumeration patterns to confirm malicious intent.  

Task #4  
Title: Investigate the “enum4linux.exe” binary flagged by the malware scan  
Description: Obtain the binary’s hash, examine its file properties, and, if resources permit, run the file in a sandbox to observe its behavior. Cross‑reference with signatures in security feeds to determine if it is a known adversarial tool.  

Task #5  
Title: Correlate LDAP enumeration traffic with domain controllers  
Description: Map the DNS queries for “dc.domain.local” and the LDAP traffic to 10.8.1.10 port 389 against the domain controller inventory. Verify whether the enumeration targeted a specific DC or the entire domain, and check for any unusual LDAP bind attempts.  

Task #6  
Title: Validate the legitimacy of “anonymous” logons to the domain controller  
Description: Scrutinize the Windows 4624 entries to confirm authentication method, source workstation, and ticket granting context. Determine if these logons were a side effect of enumeration or indicative of credential compromise or rogue service accounts.  

Task #7  
Title: Examine WMI query activity from 10.8.12.31  
Description: Drill down into the WMI event logs to identify the exact queries, timestamps, and parameters used. Compare these with the script’s expected WMI requests to confirm whether the script triggered them or if a separate tool was involved.  

Task #8  
Title: Inspect SMB connections to hosts 10.8.2.5‑10.8.2.10  
Description: Use NetFlow data or host‑side SMB logs to determine which processes initiated SMB sessions, the shared resources accessed, and any file copy operations. Evaluate whether these connections point to lateral movement or data exfiltration attempts.  

Task #9  
Title: Review and analyze the Snort rule 123456 trigger for LDAP traffic  
Description: Retrieve the packet captures or log snippets that triggered the rule. Confirm that the payload matches known enumeration or injection patterns, and assess whether the traffic originated from the script or a separate tool.  

Task #10  
Title: Investigate the “whoami” execution by administrator on 10.8.12.31  
Description: Verify the context of the command, ensuring the administrator account had necessary privileges and that no privilege escalation occurred beforehand. Document whether this action was part of normal operations or an attacker‑initiated check.  

Task #11  
Title: Isolate workstation 10.8.12.31 from the network  
Description: Remove the host from any active ACLs or segmentation boundaries to prevent further enumeration or lateral movement. Ensure isolation does not disrupt critical operations for other users.  

Task #12  
Title: Acquire a memory dump of 10.8.12.31 for forensic analysis  
Description: Use approved forensic tools to capture a live memory image. Analyze the dump for hidden processes, injected code, and in‑memory copies of the PowerShell script and enum4linux binary.  

Task #13  
Title: Search for evidence of data exfiltration or additional lateral movement  
Description: Cross‑reference outbound NetFlow flows, firewall logs, and destination hosts for any anomalous traffic after the enumeration window. Check for large file transfers, uncommon protocols, or connections to external IP addresses.  

Task #14  
Title: Verify privilege escalation attempts on the host  
Description: Inspect system logs, Event ID 4672, and security policy changes for any attempts to obtain higher privileges or create new administrative accounts. Confirm whether the administrator account or any service accounts were abused.  

Task #15  
Title: Compile findings into an incident report for remediative action  
Description: Consolidate evidence, timelines, and analysis results into a structured report. Highlight confirmed malicious activities, gaps in detection, and recommend mitigations such as tightening enumeration privileges, updating FIM baselines, and reinforcing WMI monitoring.
model_b_tasks:
Task #1  
Title: Reconstruct a comprehensive timeline of LDAP enumeration events  
Description: Align Sysmon event ID 1 logs, Windows Event 4624 entries, Sysmon DNS logs, Snort alert 123456, NetFlow SMB connections, PowerShell script creation, whoami command, and malware scan results to map the sequence from initial PowerShell execution through LDAP queries, SMB lateral attempts, and potential exfiltration. Include timestamps, source/destination IPs, users, and parent process information.

Task #2  
Title: Validate the legitimacy of the “anonymous” DC logon instances  
Description: Query domain controllers for the “anonymous” account to confirm whether it is a legitimate service account, a default unused account, or a compromised credential. Cross‑reference the 12 logons at 10.8.12.31 with approved service account logs and check for any elevation or privilege assignments that should not exist.

Task #3  
Title: Deep‑down PowerShell execution analysis  
Description: Extract command line arguments, working directory, session ID, and parent process for each PowerShell instance that executed “Get‑ADUser -Filter *”. Compute the scriptblock hash and search for it in malware databases. Determine whether the executions were user‑initiated or automatically invoked by a malicious script.

Task #4  
Title: Retrieve and analyze the “EnumAD.ps1” script content  
Description: Obtain the full contents of C:\Temp\EnumAD.ps1 created by SYSTEM on 10.8.12.31. Review for LDAP enumeration functions, credential dumping routines, persistence implants, or obfuscation techniques. Compare the script against known malicious repositories (Malpedia, VirusTotal) using its hash.

Task #5  
Title: Confirm elevation and execution context of the malicious script  
Description: Verify that EnumAD.ps1 ran under the SYSTEM account as indicated by Sysmon logs. Investigate any privilege escalation attempts that allowed SYSTEM execution, such as abuse of Startup folder, scheduled task creation, or MSI deployment. Cross‑check EDR event logs for privilege escalation exploits.

Task #6  
Title: Investigate SMB traffic for lateral movement or exfiltration indicators  
Description: Scrutinize NetFlow and SMB logs for excessive connections from 10.8.12.31 to 10.8.2.5‑10.8.2.10, large file transfers, or use of administrative shares. Correlate with host file system events to detect suspicious file copying or sharing activities that may indicate lateral movement or data staging.

Task #7  
Title: Assess WMI query activity and potential misuse  
Description: Analyze WMI event logs for frequent queries to Win32_UserAccount and other classes executed by 10.8.12.31. Identify initiating processes and validate whether the queries match legitimate monitoring or reconnaissance patterns attributable to MITRE ATT&CK T1047. Flag anomalous payloads or repeated class queries.

Task #8  
Title: Analyze malware scan results for “enum4linux.exe”  
Description: Gather scanner metadata for enum4linux.exe flagged on 10.8.12.31, including SHA256 hash, detection score, family classification, and source URLs. Query reputation databases for prior sightings. Verify its execution context, parent process, and any registry entries it may have created.

Task #9  
Title: Correlate enumerated AD accounts against privileged account lists  
Description: Extract the list of AD users returned by the “Get‑ADUser” queries (via script output or memory forensics). Cross‑reference with the organization’s privileged account list to identify potential targeting or exploitation of high-value accounts. Document any newly discovered service or user accounts.

Task #10  
Title: Search for signs of persistence mechanisms installed by the script  
Description: Inspect startup folders, registry Run keys, scheduled tasks, services, and DLL search order hijacking entries created on 10.8.12.31 by the malicious script. Validate whether any persistence methods involve modifying HKCU or HKLM Run keys, adding scheduled tasks with “SYSTEM” context, or installing a loadable driver. Correlate with Windows Defender logs for persistence detection.

Task #11  
Title: Conduct host memory and process forensics for additional RAT artifacts  
Description: Generate a memory dump from 10.8.12.31 and analyze with Volatility or Rekall for hidden processes, injected code, or registered callbacks. Search for known RAT modules such as RATKit, MirrorImage, or custom backdoors that may have been loaded after EnumAD.ps1 execution. Verify integrity of critical system directories and binaries for tampering.

Task #12  
Title: Define containment, eradication, and post‑incident actions  
Description: Based on findings, recommend isolating 10.8.12.31 from all network segments, blocking LDAP and SMB traffic from/to the host. Terminate all malicious PowerShell and related processes. Remove Enumerator scripts and persistence entries. Reset credentials for any accounts (including “anonymous” and “administrator”) that were used by the attacker. Propose system rebuild or image restoration if integrity cannot be assured, and initiate a broader network sweep for lateral movement indicators. Ensure all users receive a notification and password reset instructions if threatened credentials were touched.

case_title: Unusual outbound network scanning activity detected on corporate network
case_description: Source IP 172.16.5.123 attempted TCP SYN packets to ports 22, 80, 443, 3389 on target range 10.0.0.0/8; approximately 2000 SYN packets observed within a 5‑minute window (2023‑07‑17 02:00‑02:05 UTC); firewall logs show 50 denied connection attempts from the source; IDS raised a “PortScan” alert; internal host 10.1.42.8 logged 500 ICMP echo requests in the same period; no TCP handshakes or established sessions; no authentication successes recorded; system logs indicate no user logins during the timeframe; no malware or malicious payloads detected; source IP reputation unknown; packet payloads were empty; no user context could be linked to the activity.
model_a_tasks:
Task #1  
Title: Identify the source host responsible for the scanning activity  
Description: Query the asset inventory, DHCP lease logs, and network device tables to map 172.16.5.123 to its physical machine, hostname, and owner. Verify the device’s role (workstation, server, appliance) and cross‑reference with IT roster records to establish whether it belongs to a legitimate user or system.

Task #2  
Title: Verify internal device logs for anomalous processes  
Description: Log into or collect logs from 172.16.5.123 to inspect running processes, scheduled tasks, and startup items during the 02:00‑02:05 UTC window. Look for known scanning tools (e.g., nmap, Masscan) or custom scripts, and check timestamps against the detected SYN burst.

Task #3  
Title: Correlate firewall‑denial and IDS‑alert logs  
Description: Pull the firewall logs for the 5‑minute window and align denied TCP SYN attempts with the IDS “PortScan” alert. Verify that the 50 denied attempts correspond to the 2000 SYNs reported, ensuring that the incident detection and firewall enforcement match.

Task #4  
Title: Inspect 10.1.42.8 host for inbound and outbound activity  
Description: Access system logs of 10.1.42.8 to confirm the 500 ICMP echo requests and to check for any service replies or abnormal inbound connections. Verify no unauthorized connections were established during the scanning period.

Task #5  
Title: Compare observed scanning pattern to authorized vulnerability scans  
Description: Retrieve scheduled vulnerability scan logs and configurations from the security tools. Check if any legitimate scan (e.g., port sweep of 22, 80, 443, 3389 on 10.0.0.0/8) aligns with the 2000 SYNs and the timeline, to rule out a legitimate scan mis‑identified as malicious.

Task #6  
Title: Search for credential theft or lateral‑movement attempts  
Description: Query authentication logs (Active Directory, RDP, SSH) for login attempts originating from 172.16.5.123 during the scan window. Investigate any successful or failed logins, especially involving privileged accounts, that might indicate credential misuse.

Task #7  
Title: Conduct endpoint malware scan on source host  
Description: Deploy an EDR or antivirus scan to 172.16.5.123, reviewing detections of malware, backdoors, or reconnaissance tools. Examine file hashes, registry entries, and modification timestamps for indicators of compromise.

Task #8  
Title: Enrich source IP with threat intelligence  
Description: Query external and internal threat‑intelligence feeds for the private IP 172.16.5.123, looking for historical malicious activity, malware signatures, or prior compromise indicators. Use this insight to inform containment decisions.

Task #9  
Title: Gather and analyze deep packet captures for the event window  
Description: Capture or retrieve packet captures from network taps or IDS covering 02:00‑02:05 UTC. Inspect the TCP SYN packets, sequence numbers, and any hidden data to confirm that the activity was purely reconnaissance and that no payload was transmitted.

Task #10  
Title: Validate network access policy compliance  
Description: Perform a policy audit to confirm that the scanning activity does not violate internal segmentation rules or permitted port access. Document any policy breaches and recommend remediation if the activity is unauthorized.

Task #11  
Title: Block or isolate source IP if malicious activity is confirmed  
Description: If the investigation confirms malicious intent, update firewall rules to block 172.16.5.123 from initiating outbound connections to the corporate network or quarantine the device pending remediation. Notify IT Operations and update incident response playbooks.

Task #12  
Title: Report findings and update the knowledge base  
Description: Compile the investigation results, remediation actions, and new indicators discovered. Add the case to the organization’s knowledge base and adjust future detection rules or playbooks to reduce recurrence of similar scanning events.
model_b_tasks:
Task #1  
Title: Confirm host roles of 172.16.5.123 and 10.1.42.8  
Description: Query the corporate CMDB and network inventory to establish the intended purpose of each host. Verify hostnames, owner, department, and whether either machine is listed as an authorized vulnerability scanner, backup server, or standard workstation. Flag any unrecognized or orphaned assets for immediate review.

Task #2  
Title: Correlate host‑side logs with firewall and IDS entries  
Description: Extract Windows Security Event logs (ID 4624, 4625, 4634), Sysmon network connection events (3, 10), and Linux syslog for the 02:00‑02:05 UTC window. Align these with the firewall denied attempts and IDS “PortScan” alert timestamps. Look for gaps or mismatches that might indicate log filtering or tampering.

Task #3  
Title: Review egress firewall rules for both hosts  
Description: Inspect the egress ACLs applied to 172.16.5.123 and 10.1.42.8. Confirm whether outbound traffic to ports 22, 80, 443, 3389 toward the 10.0.0.0/8 range is permitted and under what conditions. Flag any overly permissive or misconfigured rules that could enable unauthorized scanning.

case_title: Multiple enumeration attempts discovered on internal network via SMB and LDAP queries
case_description: Network logs show repeated SMB share queries (\"net view \\\\10.0.1.10\") from 10.0.2.45 between 02:12 and 02:25 UTC; Kerberos authentication failures for user \"svc_sync\" and one successful logon to the Admins group at 02:07 UTC; DNS zone transfer attempts from internal workstation 10.0.2.45 to domain controller; LDAP queries on 10.0.2.45 retrieving all users from OU=Engineering,DC=corp,DC=local; Windows Event 4624 successes and 4625 failures correlated to 10.0.2.45 at 02:15 UTC; PowerShell script executed from host 10.0.2.45 using 'Get-ChildItem \\10.0.1.10\Finance -Recurse' at 02:20 UTC; suspicious process 'net.exe' enumerating directory listings noted in audit logs at 04:00 UTC; high number of outbound TCP SYN packets to port 445 from 10.0.2.45 to external IP 203.168.1.35.
model_a_tasks:
Task #1  
Title: Collect and correlate all authentication and enumeration logs  
Description: Pull Windows Event logs 4624/4625, Sysmon, DNS, LDAP, SMB, and firewall logs from 10.0.2.45, the domain controller(s), and the Finance share server. Align timestamps between 00:00–06:00 UTC to construct a detailed activity timeline and identify any gaps or overlaps that could indicate insider or lateral movement.

Task #2  
Title: Examine SMB enumeration and access patterns  
Description: Review SMB server logs for “net view” and “Get-ChildItem” interactions originating from 10.0.2.45. Verify the ACLs on shares accessed, confirm whether enumerated data matches legitimate operational needs, and look for anomalous permissions or repeating IP‑targeted scans.

Task #3  
Title: Investigate DNS zone transfer attempts  
Description: Audit DNS server logs for zone transfer (AXFR) requests from 10.0.2.45. Validate whether this was authenticated or spoofed, assess the legitimacy of the source, and cross‑check for any subsequent data exfiltration activity to the external IP 203.168.1.35.

Task #4  
Title: Parse and analyze the PowerShell enumeration script  
Description: Retrieve the full PowerShell session history and script code executed on 10.0.2.45 (including “Get-ChildItem \\10.0.1.10\Finance ‑Recurse”). Determine whether the script references known malicious indicators, downloads additional code, or contains obfuscated sections that may indicate backdoor installation.

Task #5  
Title: Verify integrity and security of the svc_sync account  
Description: Conduct an account hygiene check for svc_sync: review its last successful logon, group memberships, password age, and any changes to ACLs. Search for failed Kerberos requests to gauge whether an attacker attempted credential reuse or brute‑force on this service account.

Task #6  
Title: Identify signs of credential dumping or LSASS compromise  
Description: Scan Sysmon and Windows Security logs for events 1004 (process creation of “cmd.exe”/“powershell.exe”), 4105 (image load), or 4755/4756 (lateral privilege escalation). Correlate with any memory‑dumping utilities (e.g., mimikatz) or suspicious DLL loads that could indicate credential theft.

Task #7  
Title: Monitor outbound SMB traffic to external IP 203.168.1.35  
Description: Isolate network traffic to port 445 from 10.0.2.45 and 203.168.1.35. Capture packet payloads to confirm if SMB traffic is normal file transfer or a stealth exfiltration channel. Check for anomalous file names, sizes, or repeated connections that exceed typical usage.

Task #8  
Title: Conduct host‑based forensic analysis on 10.0.2.45  
Description: Acquire a memory image, disk snapshot, and process list from the workstation. Run static and dynamic malware scanners, compare process IDs with known benign services, and inspect scheduled tasks or startup items for persistence mechanisms.

Task #9  
Title: Validate group membership escalation attempts  
Description: Cross‑examine Windows Event 4624 for logons to the Admins group and check related GPOs. Assess whether the successful Admins logon aligns with authorized administrative sessions or if the account was hijacked or spoofed by the enumerating host.

Task #10  
Title: Implement remediation and containment measures  
Description: Isolate 10.0.2.45 from the network, enforce a mandatory password reset for all service accounts, revoke any temporary or suspicious SMB share permissions, and apply the latest security baseline updates. Configure monitoring to alert on future LDAP or SMB enumeration from this host or similar patterns.

Description: Locate user profile(s) associated with workstation 10.0.2.45 in the domain inventory. Collect user attributes, last login times, and group memberships to establish baseline activity for the host and account.

case_title: Suspicious exploitation attempt targeting the legacy web application via crafted SQL injection payloads originates from external IP 203.0.113.45 and triggers IDS alert ID 5021.
case_description:
- IDS alert ID 5021, severity high, timestamp 2026‑03‑09T12:45:00Z, detection: possible RCE via CVE‑2025‑1234.  
- Source IP 203.0.113.45, destination 10.10.1.5:443, 120 requests in 2 minutes, 20 bytes each.  
- WAF log: 350 blocked SQL injection payloads; sample payload “SELECT * FROM users WHERE '1'='1';”.  
- Endpoint logs: WinEvent 4626 (authentication failure) for user admin on Windows Server 2023.  
- Antivirus detection: ESET flagged 1.2 MB binary “malicious.exe” in C:\Temp as Trojan.Generic.  
- Email alert: “Intrusion detection: Potential exploitation attempt”, priority medium.  
- System logs show 5 failed admin login attempts from 203.0.113.45 in 3 minutes.  
- Network flow: high packet volume to port 443, anomaly score 9/10.

Task #1  
Title: Verify CVE‑2025‑1234 Vulnerability Status on Server 10.10.1.5

Cross‑reference these patterns with the IDS alert timestamp to determine if the same or similar payloads triggered the IDS alarm.

Task #4

Description: Copy the 1.2 MB file located at C:\Temp to a secure, isolated sandbox environment. Perform static analysis with ESET and dynamic analysis to observe file behavior, registry changes, and network contacts. Record all indicators of compromise for future correlation with other logs.

Task #5  
Title: Inspect Network Flow for Port 443 Anomalies  
Description: Review the high‑volume traffic directed at 10.10.1.5:443 during the alert period. Inspect packet payloads for abnormal request sizes, exfiltration patterns, or command‑and‑control traffic. Compare the flow data to a baseline of normal HTTPS activity to isolate suspicious behavior.

Task #6  
Title: Conduct Internal Vulnerability Scan on 10.10.1.5  
Description: Run a comprehensive vulnerability assessment targeting SSL/TLS endpoints and CVE‑2025‑1234. Verify if the application’s web servers remain exposed and identify any misconfigurations that could enable exploitation. Log findings and recommend immediate patches or mitigations.

Description: Search all security sensors for other indicators associated with IP 203.0.113.45, including DNS queries, system alerts, and file integrity changes. Look for evidence of lateral movement, data exfiltration, or additional malware artifacts. Document any new findings to inform broader incident response.

case_title: Suspicious LDAP enumeration activity detected from external IP 203.0.113.45 targeting domain user accounts.
case_description:
• Artifact: Windows Security event 4768 – Kerberos Ticket Granting Ticket request for account "TEST\\admin" from source IP 203.0.113.45 at 2024‑03‑10T02:15:32Z.  
• Artifact: NetStream capture shows 312 LDAP search queries for attribute sAMAccountName to target 192.168.1.10:389 between 02:10 and 02:30 UTC.  
• Artifact: DNS logs record 58 NXDOMAIN responses for subdomains within domain.local from 203.0.113.45.  
• Observation: Query pattern indicates enumeration of user accounts by iterating through sAMAccountName values, no successful authentication returned.  
• Observation: High volume of LDAP requests originates from a single external IP with previous scanning activity (incident 2024‑02‑28).  
• Artifact: Splunk alert “High LDAP request density” triggered on 2024‑03‑10 02:20:05, correlating with observed traffic.
model_a_tasks:
Task #1  
Title: Verify the malicious nature of source IP 203.0.113.45  
Description: Query threat‑intelligence feeds, external reputation services, and internal firewall logs to determine if 203.0.113.45 is known for reconnaissance or lateral movement. Confirm whether the IP is static, part of a known botnet or malicious infrastructure, and update threat intel datastore accordingly.  

Task #2  
Title: Correlate Windows Security event 4768 with LDAP traffic  
Description: Pull the exact 4768 Kerberos TGT request event and match its timestamp, DNS domain, and source IP against the NetStream LDAP queries. Ensure both activities originate from the same IP and correlate the event times to confirm a single attacker’s actions.  

Task #3  
Title: Export and parse all LDAP search queries from NetStream capture  
Description: De‑serialize the 312 LDAP search requests, extract filters, attributes, and timestamps. Identify enumeration patterns (e.g., sequential sAMAccountName values) and flag any unusual filters or repeated attempts that indicate malicious enumeration or probing.  

Task #4  
Title: Investigate NXDOMAIN DNS responses for subdomain enumeration  
Description: List the 58 failed DNS queries, correlate requested subdomains with the domain `domain.local`, and search the patterns against known domain generation algorithms or malicious subdomain lists. Determine whether the queries are part of a broader reconnaissance effort.  

Task #5  
Title: Search for successful authentication events from 203.0.113.45  
Description: Query Event ID 4624, 4625, and 4626 logs on all domain controllers for the 02:10‑02:30 UTC window. Verify whether any logons succeeded, were interactive, remote, or use privileged accounts; absence of success confirms only reconnaissance.  

Task #6  
Title: Inspect Kerberos service tickets for evidence of spoofing or pass‑the‑ticket  
Description: Review Event IDs 4774/4775, 4777/4778, and any Ticket‑Granting Service logs for suspicious ticket requests or unusual service principals. Look for indicators that a valid ticket was captured, stored, or replayed by the attacker.  

Task #7  
Title: Cross‑reference activity with the prior February 28, 2024 incident  
Description: Retrieve logs, alerts, and investigations conducted on that earlier scanning event. Compare IP addresses, scanning patterns, DNS probes, and LDAP queries to identify consistent behavior or a broader adversary campaign.  

Task #8  
Title: Audit administrative privileges for the TEST\\admin account  
Description: Run PowerShell AD queries (Get-ADUser, Get-ADGroupMember) to list the admin’s effective group memberships, GPO edits, and any privileged roles. Confirm whether the account has elevated permissions that could be exploited if compromised.  

Task #9  
Title: Block and log traffic from 203.0.113.45 to internal LDAP/ADC ports  
Description: Update firewall/IPS rules to drop packets to 192.168.1.10:389 (LDAP) and 192.168.1.10:445/DC and toggle logging. Notify network operations of the block and monitor for any subsequent attempts from this IP or its peers.  

Task #10  
Title: Refine the Splunk “High LDAP request density” alert rule  
Description: Review the current threshold and correlation logic of the Splunk rule, adjust the sensitivity to reduce false positives while ensuring enumeration patterns are still flagged. Document the rule changes and notify the SOC team for ongoing tuning.
model_b_tasks:
Task #1  
Title: Correlate Kerberos TGT request with LDAP enumeration activity  
Description: Cross‑reference the Windows Security event 4768 for account "TEST\\admin" (timestamp 2024‑03‑10T02:15:32Z, source IP 203.0.113.45) with the NetStream LDAP search timeline (02:10‑02:30 UTC). Verify both originate from the same external IP and assess whether the Kerberos ticket was successfully issued. Check additional Kerberos events (4769, 4771) for service tickets or pre‑auth failures that might indicate credential reuse or compromise. Determine how long the obtained TGT is valid to gauge potential risk window.

Task #2  
Title: Evaluate AD anonymous LDAP query permissions  
Description: Review the domain controller’s configuration to confirm whether anonymous LDAP queries are enabled (NTDS setting "Allow anonymous LDAP queries"). Attempt an anonymous search on a test LDAP client to validate. If anonymous queries are permitted, this misconfiguration could explain enumeration success. Recommend disabling anonymous LDAP queries or restricting them to specific hosts using firewall/NTDS ACL controls. If anonymous queries are disabled, investigate how enumeration succeeded – likely via the "TEST\\admin" bind – and assess that account’s privilege level.

Task #3  
Title: Investigate "TEST\\admin" account for compromise  
Description: Search Windows Event Logs on all domain controllers for logon (4624/4625), account lockouts (4745), privileged usage (4670, 4672) associated with "TEST\\admin" around the alert time. Cross‑reference with Splunk pass‑the‑ticket or cloning alerts. Examine AD attributes for password age, last set, and change timestamps to spot anomalous inactivity or recent resets. If compromise indicators emerge, initiate containment: disable the account, reset its password, and audit group memberships to curtail lateral movement potential.

Task #4  
Title: Scrutinize LDAP search query patterns for enumeration tactics  
Description: Parse the NetStream LDAP capture to extract all search filters targeting sAMAccountName. Visualize query progression (e.g., sequential names, basename + index). Dump the resulting attribute values, if available, and cross‑check against the domain’s full sAMAccountName list using PowerShell (Get‑ADUser). Identify whether queries succeeded without authentication or used weak binds. Flag any patterns indicative of automated enumeration tools (e.g., BloodHound, ldapsearch loops). Document the enumeration scope, including total accounts discovered and any privileged accounts enumerated.

Task #5  
Title: Investigate DNS NXDOMAIN responses as part of reconnaissance  
Description: Review 58 NXDOMAIN entries logged for domain.local subdomains. Catalog queried hostnames, identify patterns (e.g., active directory service SRV records like _ldap._tcp, _kerberos._udp), and evaluate whether they target AD components (global catalog, DNS replication zones). Determine if the attacker attempted zone transfers (AXFR/IXFR) or merely enumerated nonexistent subdomains. Correlate these queries with LDAP search basenames to assess depth of reconnaissance. Use threat intelligence to compare patterns against known reconnaissance scripts.

Task #6  
Title: Enrich investigation with external threat intelligence on 203.0.113.45  
Description: Conduct WHOIS lookup for 203.0.113.45 to obtain registry, ASN, and owner information. Cross‑check the IP against CTI feeds (MISP, AbuseIPDB, SANS ISC, IBM X‑Force). Flag if the IP is listed for scanning, malware C&C, or known to belong to threat actor groups. If identified as malicious or suspicious, coordinate with network defenders to block the IP at perimeter firewalls, and consider reporting to law enforcement or CERT authorities. Store CTI findings as evidence for future investigations.

Task #7  
Title: Map external IP 203.0.113.45 to internal host and activity  
Description: Use net flow, firewall logs, and internal IDS observations to trace inbound traffic from 203.0.113.45 to determine the internal endpoint involved. Identify whether the host is a legitimate RDP/SSH target, a compromised workstation, or a perimeter device. Perform host‑based forensic analysis on the mapped system: collection of event logs (Sysmon, Windows Security), runninq processes, scheduled tasks, registry changes, and installed software. Verify presence of enumeration tools (ldapsearch, BloodHound, PowerShell scripts). If compromise is confirmed, isolate the host, preserve volatile memory, and collect disk forensic artifacts.

Task #8  
Title: Determine outcome of LDAP enumeration queries  
Description: Reconcile LDAP search results (packet payloads or resultant user objects) against the expected domain account list. Classify each query as successful (valid sAMAccountName returned), partially successful (error codes but some data), or blocked (authentication failures, no access). Compute enumeration rate per minute to compare against baseline behavior. If queries succeeded without proper binds, confirm no privilege escalation potential. Highlight enumeration of privileged accounts (e.g., members of Domain Admins, Account Operators) as high‑risk findings.

Task #9  
Title: Seek evidence of lateral movement attempts from external IP  
Description: Examine SMB (445), RPC (135), or WMI (3268) connection logs on domain controllers and critical servers following the enumeration period. Look for trail entries showing shared folder access, remote command execution, or file copy operations originating from 203.0.113.45. Check for subsequent Kerberos service ticket requests (4769 events) to other domain controllers or services by "TEST\\admin" or newly discovered accounts. If lateral movement indicators are found, map the infection path, identify compromised hosts, and trigger containment protocols for affected systems and accounts.

Task #10  
Title: Analyze Splunk “High LDAP request density” alert context and thresholds  
Description: Review the Splunk alert configuration to understand the trigger condition (e.g., >X LDAP requests/min). Compare the alert activity window (02:20:05 UTC) against NetStream data to evaluate accuracy and granularity of detection. Identify false positives if legitimate traffic contributed to the spike (e.g., scheduled backups). Refine alert thresholds based on baseline LDAP traffic profiles, and consider adding detection for enumeration patterns (sequential sAMAccountName requests) to improve specificity.

Task #11  
Title: Profile enumeration behavior to refine future detection rules  
Description: Distill key signatures from the LDAP queries: filter syntax (sAMAccountName equal or near), base DN (e.g., "DC=domain,DC=local"), usage of LDAP paging control, request frequency, and source IP behavior. Compare against known enumeration utilities to identify potential tool fingerprints. Share these signatures with the SOC data analytics team to automate detection via Splunk, ELK, or Zeek custom scripts. Recommend rule adjustments such as flagging rapid sequential sAMAccountName queries from a single external IP.

Task #12  
Title: Draft containment and remediation recommendations for observed activity  
Description: Synthesize findings to produce actionable mitigation steps:
- Block IP 203.0.113.45 at perimeter firewall and update threat intel lists.
- Disable or restrict anonymous LDAP queries on domain controllers.
- Reset and enforce MFA on compromised "TEST\\admin" account; review its privileges.
- Patch identified AD servers for known LDAP enumeration vulnerabilities.
- Deploy continuous LDAP traffic monitoring and baseline modeling.
- Educate staff on suspicious LDAP access patterns and reporting procedures.
Document containment actions taken, evidence recovered, and next steps (eradication, recovery, lessons learned).

Task #13  
Title: Acquire forensic packet traces for deep analysis  
Description: Coordinate with network security to retrieve packet captures covering 02:10‑02:30 UTC from ingress points seeing traffic from 203.0.113.45. Store captures in tamper‑proof evidence repository with proper chain‑of‑custody labeling. Use forensic tools (Zeek, Suricata, Wireshark) to decode LDAP traffic, extract search filters, returned objects, and authentication methods. Preserve DNS query/response logs and any parsed LDAP payloads for correlation with SIEM evidence.

Task #14  
Title: Assess risk of LDAP amplification exploitation in current environment  
Description: Verify whether domain controllers are configured to limit LDAP query sizes, disable recursion, and enforce rate limiting. Perform controlled LDAP amplification tests (using ldapsearch with large filter expressions) to ensure no excessive responses that could be abused for DoS. If vulnerabilities are found, apply configuration hardening or patch updates to mitigate amplification vectors. Document findings and remediation actions.

Task #15  
Title: Summarize investigation findings and risks for executive stakeholders  
Description: Compile a concise report outlining the incident timeline, key investigation outcomes, enumeration extent (312 LDAP searches, sAMAccountName targets), compromised "TEST\\admin" account status, and any lateral movement evidence. Quantify potential impact in terms of identity theft risk, privileged account enumeration, and system compromise likelihood. Present recommended remediation actions (IP blocking, AD hardening, MFA rollout) and highlight the need for ongoing monitoring enhancements. Provide a risk assessment rating (e.g., High) and a suggested post‑incident response plan. Ensure report is delivered within 48 hours of alert kickoff as per SOC SOP.

case_title: Detected Network Scanning Attempt from External IP 198.51.100.7 Targeting Multiple Internal Hosts
case_description: 1) Firewall log entry FW01: 2026-03-09T14:23:10Z, src 198.51.100.7, dst 10.10.10.5, ports 22,23,80,443,3389,3306,445, action ALLOW, flagged SYN; 2) IDS alert ID 20260019: Port Scan, source 198.51.100.7, target 10.10.10.5, severity High, detection: TCP SYN scan; 3) NetFlow record: 78 packets, 0.15 MB during 14:22-14:24 UTC, all SYN; 4) Packet capture pcap_20260309_142310.pcap: shows SYN packets from 198.51.100.7 to 10.10.10.5 for those ports, no ACKs; 5) Endpoint syslog on 10.10.10.5: no login attempts or errors; 6) Endpoint EDR logs: no suspicious processes; 7) No established connections observed; 8) Internal monitoring spike in inbound SYN rate at 14:23 UTC; 9) Routing table confirms gateway 10.10.1.1 used for inbound traffic.
model_a_tasks:
Task #1  
Title: Verify firewall rule permitting internal host access  
Description: Inspect FW01 rule that allows inbound traffic from 198.51.100.7 to 10.10.10.5 on ports 22,23,80,443,3389,3306,445 to confirm whether this is intentional or misconfigured, check rule priority, and determine if a valid business requirement justifies the permit.  

Task #2  
Title: Cross‑check IDS detection with packet capture  
Description: Correlate IDS alert ID 20260019 with pcap_20260309_142310.pcap to confirm that the flagged SYN scan matches the captured packets and that the rule is generating a true positive rather than a false alarm.  

Task #3  
Title: Expand NetFlow analysis to other internal hosts  
Description: Retrieve NetFlow records for the 10.10.10.0/24 subnet during 14:22–14:24 UTC to identify any additional hosts that received SYNs from 198.51.100.7, thereby assessing the full scope of the reconnaissance.  

Task #4  
Title: Confirm inbound traffic routing via gateway  
Description: Verify that inbound traffic from 198.51.100.7 traverses the expected gateway 10.10.1.1 by inspecting routing tables and ACLs, ruling out routing anomalies that could allow the scan to reach multiple internal hosts.  

Task #5  
Title: Perform threat‑intel lookup on source IP  
Description: Query reputable threat‑intel sources (AbuseIPDB, VirusTotal, etc.) for 198.51.100.7 to gather historical activity, reputation score, and any known malicious behavior associated with the IP.  

Task #6  
Title: Search for post‑scan exploit attempts  
Description: Examine logs on 10.10.10.5 and other internal hosts for authentication failures, failed RDP connections, SMB access, and web service errors after 14:24 UTC to determine if the scanner pursued exploitation of vulnerabilities.  

Task #7  
Title: Review endpoint host integrity and configuration  
Description: Run file integrity and configuration checks on 10.10.10.5 to detect any unauthorized changes, backdoors, or installed binaries that could result from the scan, notwithstanding the lack of EDR alerts.  

Task #8  
Title: Validate firewall logging consistency  
Description: Compare firewall log entries, IDS alerts, and pcap timestamps to ensure consistent recording of the SYNs as ALLOW rather than dropped or denied, reducing the possibility of log tampering or mis‑logging.  

Task #9  
Title: Assess internal network segmentation health  
Description: Confirm that 10.10.10.5 is correctly segmented, internal, and not exposed via DMZ or NAT whitelisting, and that the inbound ports list does not inadvertently expose critical services to external traffic.  

Task #10  
Title: Implement temporary block for source IP  
Description: Configure the firewall to rate‑limit or block inbound traffic from 198.51.100.7 until investigation completes, mitigating potential follow‑up reconnaissance or exploitation while preserving legitimate traffic paths.  

Task #11  
Title: Strengthen port‑scan detection rules  
Description: Review and fine‑tune IDS signatures for TCP SYN‑scans, potentially adding flow‑based heuristics, and ensure alerts are escalated for high‑severity scans to improve visibility and response.  

Task #12  
Title: Compile and document investigation findings  
Description: Summarize evidence (firewall logs, IDS alerts, NetFlow data, pcap, threat‑intel, host integrity checks) in a case report, noting any remedial actions, and provide recommendations for future prevention and detection.

case_description: Event ID 4672: SeTcbPrivilege granted to user ‘svcUser’ on host srv-prod‑01 at 09:12:04 UTC; File C:\Users\svcUser\AppData\Local\Temp\payload.exe (SHA256: 3b1c7f73e4a1c2d4f5e6) downloaded from external IP 203.0.113.10; Process creation: payload.exe (PID 2024) spawned from cmd.exe /c start payload.exe with arguments –NoProfile –NonInteractive –ExecutionPolicy Bypass; Outbound TCP connection from srv-prod‑01 to 203.0.113.45:443 observed by SysMon (HTTPS handshake); Registry modification: HKLM\Software\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Winlogon\SpecialAccounts\Reserved added key ‘svcUser’ (Event 4657); Scheduled task “Escalate” created to run payload.exe on system boot (Security log); Mimikatz.exe executed with argument “sekurlsa::logonpasswords” recorded by SysMon (Process creation); Command “net localgroup administrators svcUser /add” executed on srv-prod‑01 (Audit log).

case_title: Suspicious privilege escalation attempt via PowerShell script and rogue local admin account creation
case_description: Artifact: Windows Event ID 4624 logon success for user 'EvilUser' from 192.168.1.5 at 2024-03-08T09:42:17Z; Artifact: PowerShell command line executed 'powershell.exe -NoProfile -ExecutionPolicy Bypass -File C:\Temp\Invoke-Mimikatz.ps1' at 2024-03-08T09:43:02Z; Artifact: New local user 'TempAdmin' added to Administrators group at 2024-03-08T09:44:15Z; Artifact: Scheduled task 'OwnAdInstaller' created to run as SYSTEM at 2024-03-08T09:45:30Z; Observation: Hash of script matches known ATT&CK T1086 pattern; Observation: elevated PowerShell session lacks authentication token; Observation: network traffic from internal host to external IP 203.0.113.42 on port 445 at 2024-03-08T09:46:05Z; Observation: Endpoint protection flagged unknown signing certificate for script; Observation: Sysmon logs missing evidence of process termination after execution; Observation: no known malware signature detected in endpoint protection database.
model_a_tasks:
Task #1  
Title: Verify the context of the EvilUser logon  
Description: Retrieve Windows Security Event logs (Event ID 4624) for EvilUser, noting logon type, source IP (192.168.1.5), authentication package, and domain. Check if EvilUser is a legitimate internal account and if the logon occurs during normal business hours; flag any anomalous source IPs or credential reuse that could suggest compromise.  

Task #2  
Title: Capture PowerShell execution details  
Description: Pull Sysmon Event ID 1 and Windows PowerShell event ID 4104 logs around 09:43:02 Z to confirm the command line, process ID, parent process, and execution policy. Verify that the PowerShell instance ran with “-NoProfile –ExecutionPolicy Bypass –File C:\Temp\Invoke‑Mimikatz.ps1” as documented and gather any additional arguments supplied.  

Task #3  
Title: Acquire the Invoke‑Mimikatz.ps1 script for analysis  
Description: Retrieve the script file from C:\Temp\Invoke‑Mimikatz.ps1, calculate its hash, and compare it to the documented hash. Conduct a static analysis (e.g., byte‑by‑byte string de‑obfuscation, regex search for “Invoke‑Mimikatz” or known credential‑dumping calls) and identify any embedded payloads or scripts designed to evade detection.  

Task #4  
Title: Cross‑reference script hash with known threat signatures  
Description: Compare the script’s hash against the MITRE ATT&CK T1086 reference database and third‑party threat‑intel feeds. Confirm whether the hash matches a known variant of Mimikatz or if there are any deviations that might signal a new or modified script.  

Task #5  
Title: Investigate Sysmon evidence of stealth during execution  
Description: Review Sysmon logs for missing Process Termination (Event 4105) events following the PowerShell run; look for indications of process‑hollowing, DLL injection, or other tampering. Correlate with baseline Sysmon activity to identify anomalies that suggest the attacker deliberately avoided logging.  

Task #6  
Title: Validate local user and administrator group modifications  
Description: Retrieve Security Event logs (Event 4720 for user creation, Event 4728 for group membership changes) to confirm that “TempAdmin” was created and added to the Administrators group at 09:44:15 Z. Verify the account’s last logon, password age, and any subsequent logon attempts to assess exploitation of the new privilege.  

Task #7  
Title: Examine scheduled task “OwnAdInstaller” properties  
Description: Pull Task Scheduler logs and use PowerShell (Get‑ScheduledTask) to retrieve the task’s configuration—trigger, action, start‑up type, and service account. Confirm that the task runs as SYSTEM, the binary path, and that it is set to trigger at system startup or after logon. Check for any hidden tasks or tampered permissions that might indicate persistence.  

Task #8  
Title: Analyze SMB traffic to external IP 203.0.113.42  
Description: Extract NetFlow or packet capture data for the period around 09:46:05 Z, focusing on SMB (port 445) connections to 203.0.113.42. Inspect SMB commands, file names, and any packets containing credential or key material to determine if the traffic represents legitimate file sharing, exfiltration, or C2 activity.  

Task #9  
Title: Correlate endpoint protection alerts concerning unknown signing certificate  
Description: Retrieve the endpoint protection logs that flagged the script’s signing certificate. Resolve certificate details (issuer, serial number, revocation status) and cross‑reference against a list of trusted certificates in the environment. Assess whether the certificate is self‑signed or compromised, and whether it was allowed to execute or was quarantined.  

Task #10  
Title: Verify the absence of process termination evidence and potential tampering  
Description: Cross‑reference event logs and Sysmon data to confirm no Process Termination events (Event 4105) for the malicious PowerShell run. Investigate whether the attacker may have performed anti‑forensic actions—such as clearing Security logs, disabling audit policies, or modifying Sysmon configuration—to conceal activity.  

Task #11  
Title: Map activity against MITRE ATT&CK tactics and techniques  
Description: Place each observed artifact (logon, PowerShell execution, user creation, scheduled task, SMB traffic) into the ATT&CK matrix, labeling techniques such as T1086 (PowerShell), T1053.005 (Scheduled Task), T1069 (Permission Groups Discovery), T1078 (Valid Accounts), and T1105 (Ingress Tool Transfer). Identify any coverage gaps in detection or logging.  

Task #12  
Title: Recommend containment and remediation actions  
Description: Based on findings, advise disabling or deleting the “OwnAdInstaller” scheduled task, removing the rogue “TempAdmin” account and revoking its admin rights, blocking outbound traffic to 203.0.113.42, and updating endpoint detection rules to flag the script hash and PowerShell patterns. Also recommend tightening PowerShell logging (Set‑ExecutionPolicy Restricted for scripts, enforce signed‑script checks) and reinforcing audit policies.
model_b_tasks:
Task #1  
Title: Validate EvilUser credential legitimacy  
Description: Cross‑reference the logon event for EvilUser at 09:42:17Z from 192.168.1.5 with Kerberos, NTLM, and MFA logs to determine whether the credential was compromised or legitimately used. Look for unusual time‑of‑day patterns, forwarded credentials, or password policy violations that may indicate credential theft.  

Task #2  
Title: Verify authenticity of PowerShell script execution  
Description: Examine the command line capture of PowerShell—`-NoProfile -ExecutionPolicy Bypass -File C:\Temp\Invoke-Mimikatz.ps1`—to assess whether the bypass flag and file path are typical of legitimate admin scripts or suspicious of a malicious payload. Compare against documented standard operating procedures for PowerShell usage.  

Task #3  
Title: Confirm rogue local admin account properties  
Description: Retrieve all metadata about TempAdmin (creation time, password age, last logon, associated device). Evaluate whether the account has been used by legitimate administrators or appears solely in anomalous activity logs.  

Task #4  
Title: Retrieve scheduled task configuration  
Description: Query Task Scheduler on host 192.168.1.5 for the XML definition of OwnAdInstaller. Note user context, triggers, actions, persistence flags, and disable conditions. Verify whether the task is configured to run as SYSTEM and whether it points to any executables that could maintain persistence.  

Task #5  
Title: Investigate Sysmon process termination logs  
Description: Filter Sysmon event logs around 09:43:02Z–09:45:30Z for ProcessTerminate events corresponding to powershell.exe and Invoke-Mimikatz.ps1. The absence of termination events may suggest Sysmon was disabled or tampered with during execution.  

Task #6  
Title: Search registry for persistence entries  
Description: Enumerate Run keys (HKLM\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run, HKCU\...Run) and Service Control Manager registry paths for references to C:\Temp\Invoke-Mimikatz.ps1 or OwnAdInstaller. Persistence via the registry could confirm ongoing attacker foothold.  

Task #7  
Title: Deep packet inspection of SMB traffic  
Description: Collect NetFlow or full packet capture around 09:46:05Z for traffic from 192.168.1.5 to 203.0.113.42 on port 445. Inspect SMB sessions for unusual shares, authentication anomalies, or hidden payloads that could indicate lateral movement or exfiltration channels.  

Task #8  
Title: Correlate traffic with threat intelligence  
Description: Submit IP 203.0.113.42 to threat intelligence services (AlienVault OTX, ThreatCrowd, VirusTotal, Microsoft Defender ATP). Gather reputation data, associated malware families, and recent activity timestamps to determine if the remote host serves as a command‑and‑control endpoint or file‑sharing server.  

Task #9  
Title: Sandbox analysis of PowerShell script  
Description: Execute Invoke-Mimikatz.ps1 in a virtualized test environment. Monitor for credential dumping, persistence installation, or command execution against system processes. Document all malicious behavior and capture hashes of any derived binaries for IOC creation.  

Task #10  
Title: Hash analysis against ATT&CK T1086 library  
Description: Compare the script’s hash to documented malicious signatures associated with ATT&CK technique T1086 (PowerShell). A match indicates the use of a known adversary toolset, aiding attribution and informing detection rule refinement.  

Task #11  
Title: Memory forensics for Mimikatz detection  
Description: Run volatility or Rekall on the forensic memory dump gathered in Task #12. Search for DLL listings and loaded modules containing “mimikatz” or “sekurlsa”. Additionally, scan memory regions for suspicious strings (e.g., credential extraction patterns) that may evidence in‑memory Mimikatz activity.  

Task #12  
Title: Acquire forensic memory dump  
Description: Use FTK Imager or Windows built‑in task manager “Dump” feature to capture a volatile memory image of host 192.168.1.5. Ensure timestamping captures the state of the system during the privilege escalation window (09:42–09:46 UTC).  

Task #13  
Title: Create disk image of the host  
Description: Obtain a sector‑by‑sector copy of all storage devices on host 192.168.1.5 using ddrescue or FTK Imager. Preserve boot sectors, file system metadata, and hidden data to facilitate full forensic reconstruction of attacker activities.  

Task #14  
Title: Scan for additional rogue accounts  
Description: Enumerate all local users with administrative privileges on host 192.168.1.5 and cross‑reference against the authorized roster. Flag any accounts created near the time of compromise without legitimate traceable purpose, and investigate associated service bindings.  

Task #15  
Title: Validate Sysinternals tool usage  
Description: Examine Process Monitor logs for the period of interest to detect usage patterns of Sysinternals utilities, particularly those that can modify registry, create services, or export credentials. Correlate with PowerShell or scheduled task activity to detect coordinated adversary tool adoption.  

Task #16  
Title: Remove rogue TempAdmin account  
Description: Immediately delete the TempAdmin account from the Administrators group on host 192.168.1.5. Reset passwords for any services linked to this account and audit for residual references in registry or service configurations.  

Task #17  
Title: Delete scheduled task OwnAdInstaller  
Description: Remove OwnAdInstaller from Task Scheduler and clear any residual XML configuration. Verify that the associated executable, if any, has been purged and check for dangling registry entries that could re‑create the task.  

Task #18  
Title: Block IP 192.168.1.5 at network perimeter  
Description: Add a deny rule for 192.168.1.5 in firewall ACLs and IDS signatures. Monitor for subsequent connection attempts to confirm containment. Document response actions for future reference.  

Task #19  
Title: Query threat intelligence for 203.0.113.42  
Description: Submit IP 203.0.113.42 to TI services to retrieve reputation data, documented malware families, and recent activity logs. Assess whether the IP has been associated with known IOC strings or threat actor campaigns matching the current intrusion vector.  

Task #20  
Title: Update endpoint protection signatures  
Description: Create a custom signature for the unknown signing certificate flagged on Invoke-Mimikatz.ps1. Deploy this signature across all endpoints and configure heuristics for unsigned PowerShell scripts that attempt policy bypass.  

Task #21  
Title: Harden PowerShell execution policies  
Description: Enforce a system‑wide “AllSigned” or “ConstrainedLanguage” execution policy to reduce the risk of malicious script execution. Enable logging of all PowerShell activations in Sysmon (EventID 4104) for forensic traceability.  

Task #22  
Title: Draft containment playbook  
Description: Compile detailed steps for handling rogue local admin account creation, including inventory, deprovisioning, ticketing, and privileged access management policy updates. Incorporate lessons learned from this incident to improve SOPs for future escalation attempts.  

Task #23  
Title: Prepare post‑mortem timeline  
Description: Collate timestamps from authentication logs, PowerShell execution, account creation, scheduled task installation, and network traffic. Create a chronological reconstruction of attacker actions, highlight gaps in logging, and recommend enhancements for continuous monitoring.  

Task #24  
Title: Configure Sysmon to log process termination  
Description: Update Sysmon configuration to ensure that ProcessTerminate events are captured for all significant processes moving forward. Validate configuration changes by inducing benign terminations and confirming event generation.  

Task #25  
Title: Set detection rule for suspicious PowerShell usage  
Description: Build an alert for executions of PowerShell with "-ExecutionPolicy Bypass" or "-NoProfile" switches, especially when coupled with file paths like C:\Temp\Invoke-Mimikatz.ps1. Incorporate signature checks for known ATT&CK T1086 patterns.  

Task #26  
Title: Enable scheduled task logging  
Description: Activate detailed logging for scheduled task creations and modifications in Windows Event logs (Event ID 4698, 4699, 4702). Include task name, user context, triggers, and actions in detection correlators to surface potential persistence mechanisms early.

- NetFlow record: 1,245 packets, 5 MB volume from 203.0.113.45 to the 10.10.5.0/24 subnet over an 8‑minute interval.  
- Nmap scan report dated 2026‑03‑08 13:46:01 indicating a SYN scan of ports 1–1024 on host 10.10.5.23.  
- Syslog from 10.10.5.23 (timestamp 13:47:03): multiple TCP connection attempts to ports 445, 3389, 3306 returned RST.  
- ThreatIntel feed entry: IP 203.0.113.45 listed in prior reports for port‑scanning activity.

case_title: Unexpected modifications to critical system configuration file detected.
case_description: File path: C:\Windows\System32\config\system modified at 2026-03-10 02:15:43 UTC; Process: C:\Windows\SysWOW64\malware.exe (PID 4520) executed by SYSTEM; Windows Security Event ID 4663 logged on 2026-03-10 02:15:44 UTC reporting modification; Sysmon Event 13: creation of C:\Windows\Temp\bad.dll at 2026-03-10 02:13:12 UTC by cmd.exe; file hash changed from original SHA256 1234567890abcdef… to SHA256 fedcba0987654321…; ACL change: added Users group with FullControl; process tree: malware.exe → cmd.exe → wmic.exe; network activity: outbound connection from malware.exe to 203.0.113.45:443, downloading 512 KB blob into C:\Windows\Temp\bad.dll; discrepancy: modification time outside normal backup window; unexpected file size increase of 128 KB and changed file type for system file.
model_a_tasks:

Title: Verify the integrity of C:\Windows\System32\config\system  
Description: Retrieve the original SHA256 hash and metadata (size, timestamps, ACLs) from the backup repository or version‑controlled baseline. Compare the current hash (fedcba0987654321…) and file size to confirm the unauthorized modification and identify potential tampering.

Task #3

Description: Correlate the ACL modification event with the file modification timestamp. Confirm whether adding the Users group with FullControl aligns with documented update procedures or is a suspicious privilege escalation attempt.

Task #5

Task #11  
Title: Collect forensic evidence for legal and analysis purposes  
Description: Secure a memory dump, disk image, and all relevant logs from the compromised host. Document chain‑of‑custody procedures to maintain evidence integrity for potential further investigation or legal action.

Task #12  
Title: Recommend containment, eradication, and recovery actions  
Description: Based on findings, propose isolation of affected systems, removal of malicious binaries (malware.exe, bad.dll), restoration of the system file from a clean backup, application of necessary patches, and monitoring for re‑occurrence. Suggest updates to security controls (IDS signatures, file integrity monitoring, IAM policies) to prevent similar incidents.
model_b_tasks:
**Task #1**  
**Title:** Verify File Integrity Against Known Good Baseline  
**Description:** Retrieve the original SHA256 hash of `C:\Windows\System32\config\system` from your backup repository or hash database and compare it to the current hash (`SHA256 fedcba0987654321…`). Additionally, confirm that the file size increased by exactly 128 KB and that its file type has indeed changed from the expected registry format. Document any deviations and flag the file as needing restoration.

---

**Task #2**  
**Title:** Correlate Sysmon Process Creation Logs  
**Description:** Query Sysmon for Event ID 1 entries that correspond to `malware.exe` (PID 4520), `cmd.exe`, and `wmic.exe`. Build the full parent-child relationship chain: `malware.exe → cmd.exe → wmic.exe`. Note all timestamps to ensure consistency with the Windows Security Event ID 4663 timing. Export these logs for deeper analysis and cross‑reference with Sysmon Event 13 for the creation of `C:\Windows\Temp\bad.dll`.

---

**Task #3**  
**Title:** Conduct Static Analysis of malware.exe  
**Description:** Run the binary at `C:\Windows\SysWOW64\malware.exe` through static analysis tools such as PEiD, CFF Explorer, and YARA. Extract all strings, embedded resources, and examine sections for common packers or obfuscation techniques. Submit the file hash to multiple online malware scanners (VirusTotal, Hybrid Analysis) and evaluate any returned detections. Determine if the binary is known or previously observed in your environment.

---

**Task #4**  
**Title:** Review Command Line Arguments of cmd.exe  
**Description:** Inspect Sysmon Event ID 10 or any configured “Command Line” audit logs to capture the exact arguments passed to `cmd.exe` when it invoked `wmic.exe`. Identify any suspicious flags (e.g., `wmic process call create`, `wmic computersystem get /format:list`) or scripts executed. Correlate the command line timeline with the creation time of `bad.dll` (02:13:12 UTC) and the subsequent system file modification (02:15:43 UTC).

---

**Task #5**  
**Title:** Analyze the Downloaded bad.dll  
**Description:** Confirm the SHA256 hash of `C:\Windows\Temp\bad.dll` and determine its MIME type using tools like `file` or Heuristic analysis. Run AV scans across all endpoint detection tools. If feasible, load the DLL into a sandbox environment (e.g., Cuckoo) to observe inject behavior, DLL hijacking attempts, or persistence mechanisms. Check for the presence of hidden sections or unusual imports that suggest malicious intent.

---

**Task #6**  
**Title:** Inspect Windows Security Event ID 4663 Details  
**Description:** Export the full Event ID 4663 record logged at 02:15:44 UTC. Note the Object Name (`C:\Windows\System32\config\system`), Object Type (File), Access Mask (WriteData), SDDL of the updated ACL, Caller Computer Name, and accessing SID. Verify that SYSTEM legitimately had permissions to modify the file and that the ACL change (adding Users group FullControl) is logged with the correct modifying SID and timestamp.

---

**Task #7**  
**Title:** Validate ACL Changes to System Configuration Files  
**Description:** Compare the current ACL of `C:\Windows\System32\config\system` against the baseline ACL stored in backup or source control. Document the addition of Users group with FullControl and identify the modifying SID. Search for any concurrent ACL changes on adjacent critical directories (e.g., `C:\Windows\System32\config\software`). Review any audit logs that may have logged pre-existing ACL modifications.

---

**Task #8**  
**Title:** Verify Persistence Mechanisms Established by malware.exe  
**Description:** Search registry hives for Run and RunOnce keys under `HKLM\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run` and `RunOnce`. Examine Startup Folder contents and scheduled tasks (Task Scheduler) for entries pointing to `malware.exe` or its command chain. Look for hidden services or WMI Event Consumers that could restart the process automatically. Flag any newly created persistence points for removal.

---

**Task #9**  
**Title:** Analyze Network Traffic Associated with the Attack  
**Description:** Use packet capture tools (Netmon, Wireshark) to isolate the TLS session from `malware.exe` to `203.0.113.45:443`. Verify the Server Name Indication (SNI), certificate chain, and size of the downloaded blob (~512 KB). Save the decrypted payload for forensic analysis. Confirm whether the blob corresponds to `bad.dll` or an unrelated exfiltration. Cross‑reference timestamps to align with Sysmon and Security logs.

---

**Task #10**  
**Title:** Cross-Compare Modification Time Against Backup Window  
**Description:** Retrieve backup schedules for critical system configuration files from your backup management system. Confirm that the 02:15:43 UTC modification timestamp falls outside the normal backup window, indicating unauthorized changes. Document any backup events that occurred just before or after the modification time for further scrutiny.

---

**Task #11**  
**Title:** Isolate Infected Processes and Files  
**Description:** Immediately stop `malware.exe`, its child `cmd.exe`, and spawned `wmic.exe`. Use `wmic process where name="malware.exe" delete` with administrative rights to ensure termination. Copy the binaries (`malware.exe`, `bad.dll`) and any related artifacts (temporary files, registry keys) to writable, non‑testable media for forensic preservation. Take memory snapshots of the infected system to capture in‑memory artifacts.

---

**Task #12**  
**Title:** Restore System Configuration File to Known Good State  
**Description:** Retrieve a clean copy of `C:\Windows\System32\config\system` from backup or a known good image. Verify integrity with hash comparison. Replace the compromised file and re‑apply the original ACLs to remove Users group FullControl. Restart or reload the Windows Management Instrumentation Service (`winmgmt`) to ensure it reads the rebuilt configuration file.

---

**Task #13**  
**Title:** Harden File Integrity Monitoring and ACLs  
**Description:** Update your file integrity monitoring (FIM) policy to include high‑risk directories such as `C:\Windows\System32\config`. Force audit logging of all modifications to ACLs (Object Access: Microsoft Windows security/auditing). Tighten ACLs to exclude Users group FullControl from sensitive system directories. Deploy or re‑enable Windows Advanced Audit Policies for object integrity and access.

---

**Task #14**  
**Title:** Communicate Findings, Containment, and Remediation Plan  
**Description:** Draft a detailed incident report covering timeline, impacted assets, technical analysis results, containment actions taken, and remediation steps completed. Share this with the Incident Response Team, system owners, and compliance officers. Include recommendations for patching vulnerable services, reviewing privileged account assignments, and enhancing detection signatures.

---

**Task #15**  
**Title:** Conduct Post‑Incident Forensics and Lessons Learned Review  
**Description:** Analyze memory dumps and volatile artifacts to identify the initial compromise vector (e.g., malicious email attachment, remote desktop session, software vulnerability). Evaluate why SYSTEM executed the malware, checking for compromised scheduled tasks, services, or defective user privilege escalations. Update response playbooks, train staff on new indicators of compromise, and adjust proactive controls based on insights gained.

---
